```mermaid
    N --> P[Handover to Detailed Design Team]
    P --> Q[End of PHL]
```

## 4.2 Hazard Category Taxonomy (Energy-Based — Kletz)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOT[All Industrial Hazards] --> E1[Mechanical Energy]
    ROOT --> E2[Electrical Energy]
    ROOT --> E3[Thermal Energy]
    ROOT --> E4[Chemical Energy]
    ROOT --> E5[Radiation Energy]
    ROOT --> E6[Potential Energy]
    ROOT --> E7[Kinetic Energy]
    ROOT --> E8[Pressure Energy]

    E1 --> E1a[Rotating Shafts]
    E1 --> E1b[Presses]
    E1 --> E1c[Falling Loads]

    E2 --> E2a[HV Switchgear]
    E2 --> E2b[Arc Flash]
    E2 --> E2c[Static Discharge]

    E3 --> E3a[Steam Lines]
    E3 --> E3b[Cryogenic Spills]
    E3 --> E3c[Hot Surfaces]

    E4 --> E4a[Flammable Vapors]
    E4 --> E4b[Toxic Gases]
    E4 --> E4c[Reactive Chemicals]

    E5 --> E5a[Ionizing X-Ray]
    E5 --> E5b[Lasers]
    E5 --> E5c[UV Exposure]

    E6 --> E6a[Elevated Tanks]
    E6 --> E6b[Suspended Loads]

    E7 --> E7a[Vehicles]
    E7 --> E7b[Projectiles]

    E8 --> E8a[Boilers]
    E8 --> E8b[Compressed Gas]
    E8 --> E8c[Hydraulic Systems]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Controls (Used in PHL Recommendations)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[After Hazard Identification] --> L1[Level 1: Elimination - Remove Hazard Entirely]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2: Substitution - Replace with Less Hazardous Material]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Engineering Controls - Valves, Bunds, Ventilation]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Administrative Controls - SOPs, Training, Permits]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: PPE - Last Line of Defence]

    L1 -. Highest Effectiveness .-> EFF[(Most Effective)]
    L5 -. Lowest Effectiveness .-> EFF
```

## 4.4 Block-Level PHL Worksheet Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COL1[Column 1: Hazard ID] --> COL2[Column 2: Equipment or Subsystem]
    COL2 --> COL3[Column 3: Hazard Description]
    COL3 --> COL4[Column 4: Cause Trigger Event]
    COL4 --> COL5[Column 5: Potential Consequence]
    COL5 --> COL6[Column 6: Severity Index S]
    COL6 --> COL7[Column 7: Likelihood Index L]
    COL7 --> COL8[Column 8: Initial Risk R in]
    COL8 --> COL9[Column 9: Recommended Safeguards]
    COL9 --> COL10[Column 10: Residual Risk R res]
    COL10 --> OUT[Column 11: Action Owner and Target Date]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix — PHL vs Other Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[When to Choose a Hazard Tool?] --> PHL{Is design still conceptual?}
    PHL -->|Yes| PHLYES[Use PHL - Broad Coverage, Low Cost]
    PHL -->|No - detailed design| HAZOP{Process plant with P and ID?}
    HAZOP -->|Yes| HAZOPYES[Use HAZOP - Node-by-Node Deviation Analysis]
    HAZOP -->|No - discrete equipment| FMEA{Failure mode focus needed?}
    FMEA -->|Yes| FMEAYES[Use FMEA - Component-by-Component Failure]
    FMEA -->|No - accident sequence| FTA{Boolean logic tree required?}
    FTA -->|Yes| FTAYES[Use FTA - Top-Down Boolean Analysis]
    FTA -->|No| WHATIF[Use What-If or Checklist Analysis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Preliminary Hazard List (PHL). State any two situations where PHL is applied.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Preliminary Hazard List (PHL) is a qualitative safety analysis technique performed during the conceptual or early design stage of a project to systematically identify and catalogue potential hazards associated with a system, process, or facility, based on standard hazard categories, past experience, and engineering judgment.

**Two situations where PHL is applied:**
1. **Conceptual Design Phase** of a new chemical plant, when only block diagrams exist and detailed engineering has not begun.
2. **Modification of an Existing Facility**, where a new section is being added to an operating plant, requiring early identification of new hazards.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two applications.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**List and briefly explain any three standard hazard categories used in a PHL.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Flammable Materials (1 mark):** Substances like LPG, gasoline, hydrogen, and solvents that can ignite when mixed with air within flammable limits, leading to fire or explosion.

2. **Toxic Substances (1 mark):** Chemicals such as H₂S, chlorine, ammonia, and benzene that, when released, can cause acute or chronic health effects on workers or the public.

3. **Pressure Hazards (1 mark):** Equipment operating above atmospheric pressure such as boilers, compressed gas cylinders, and hydraulic accumulators — failure can lead to explosive rupture or vessel disintegration.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 1 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step methodology of conducting a Preliminary Hazard List (PHL) for an LPG storage facility consisting of one bullet tank, two pumps, and a piping network. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Boundaries (1 mark)**
The analysis covers the LPG bullet tank (20 t capacity), feed pumps (P-101A/B), and associated piping (L-201 to vaporiser). Excluded: road-tanker unloading gantry and vaporiser unit.

**Step 2 — Assemble the PHL Team (1 mark)**
A multidisciplinary team is formed comprising: Safety Engineer (leader), Process Engineer, Mechanical Engineer, Instrumentation Engineer, and an experienced Plant Operator. Independence ensured by an external facilitator.

**Step 3 — Gather Design Information (1 mark)**
Collect the Block Flow Diagram, preliminary equipment list, MSDS of LPG (highly flammable, pressure 7 bar at 40°C), and plot plan.

**Step 4 — Apply Hazard Categories (1 mark)**
The 14-category checklist is screened against each equipment:
- Bullet: Flammable + Pressure + Cryogenic (if refrigerated).
- Pump: Mechanical + Electrical.
- Piping: Pressure + Flammable + External impact.

**Step 5 — Describe Hazards (1 mark)**
For each match, write Hazard ID, description, cause, consequence, and affected receptor. Example:
- *H-001:* Uncontrolled release from bullet — cause: external corrosion; consequence: Vapor Cloud Explosion.

**Step 6 — Estimate Severity & Likelihood (1 mark)**
Apply the 5×5 risk matrix. For H-001: $S = 5$, $L = 2$, $R_{initial} = 10$.

**Step 7 — Recommend Safeguards (1 mark)**
Apply hierarchy of controls: relief valve, bund wall (engineering); permit system (administrative).

---

### **Question A — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the LPG facility above, construct the PHL worksheet for at least 4 identified hazards and compute the average Risk Reduction Factor (RRF). *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| H-ID | Hazard | S | L | R_in | Safeguard | S_res | L_res | R_res |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| H-001 | LPG release from bullet | 5 | 2 | 10 | Relief valve + bund | 5 | 1 | 5 |
| H-002 | Pump mechanical entanglement | 4 | 3 | 12 | Interlocked guard | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| H-003 | Pipeline fire from joint leak | 4 | 2 | 8 | Welded joints + NDT | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| H-004 | Pump noise >95 dB | 3 | 4 | 12 | Acoustic enclosure | 3 | 2 | 6 |

**Risk Reduction Factor Calculation (3 Marks):**

$$
RRF_i = \frac{R_{initial,i}}{R_{residual,i}}
$$

$$
RRF_{H001} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0
$$

$$
RRF_{H002} = \frac{12}{4} = 3.0
$$

$$
RRF_{H003} = \frac{8}{4} = 2.0
$$

$$
RRF_{H004} = \frac{12}{6} = 2.0
$$

$$
\bar{RRF} = \frac{2.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 + 2.0}{4} = \frac{9.0}{4} = 2.25
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The collective safeguards reduce overall risk by a factor of 2.25, bringing all residual risks into the *Acceptable* or *Procedural* bands of the risk matrix. The facility is recommended for handover to detailed HAZOP study.

---

### **Question B — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module 1 Pattern — Alternate Choice]**

**(a)** "PHL is the foundation upon which all subsequent safety analyses are built." Justify this statement with reference to the position of PHL in the project lifecycle, its objectives, and its limitations. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Position in Project Lifecycle (2 Marks):**
PHL is performed at the **conceptual / feasibility stage**, before detailed design is committed. At this phase, changes to layout, equipment, or material selection are still inexpensive to implement — making PHL the most cost-effective hazard identification tool. All later analyses (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, LOPA) build upon the *hazard register* created by PHL; without a PHL, the detailed analyses would have no scope boundary and no initial hazard list to drill into.

**Objectives (2 Marks):**
The five primary objectives of PHL are:
1. Early identification of hazards before design freeze.
2. Risk prioritization to focus engineering resources.
3. Provision of inherent-safety inputs to designers.
4. Compliance with regulatory expectations of "design-stage safety."
5. Communication of hazards across multidisciplinary stakeholders.

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
- **Qualitative Only:** No quantitative probability; relies on team judgment.
- **Heuristic Bias:** May overlook novel hazards outside the checklist.
- **No Detailed Scenarios:** Cannot model accident sequences.
- **Team-Dependent:** Quality depends on team's experience and cross-discipline representation.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Despite its limitations, PHL remains indispensable as the *first line of defence* — its omission virtually guarantees costly redesigns, retrofitting of safeguards, or, in worst cases, undetected hazards propagating to operations. Thus, PHL truly *is* the foundation of subsequent safety work.

---

### **Question B — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the aid of the standard 5×5 risk matrix, explain how initial risk is computed in a PHL. For a hazard with $S = 4$ and $L = 3$, determine the action band and recommend two engineering controls. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Initial Risk Computation (3 Marks):**
The risk score in a PHL is computed as the product of the **Severity Index** ($S$) and the **Likelihood Index** ($L$), where both indices range from 1 (lowest) to 5 (highest) on a standard 5×5 matrix:

$$
R = S \times L
$$

The matrix is divided into action bands:
- **$R \le 4$** — Acceptable (routine operations).
- **$5 \le R \le 9$** — Manage by procedure / administrative control.
- **$10 \le R \le 15$** — Engineering control mandatory.
- **$R \ge 16$** — Immediate intervention; stop work.

**Calculation for Given Hazard (2 Marks):**

$$
R = S \times L = 4 \times 3 = 12
$$

Since $10 \le 12 \le 15$, the hazard falls in the **Engineering Control Required** band.

**Two Recommended Engineering Controls (2 Marks):**
1. **Install a Pressure Relief Device** (e.g., spring-loaded safety valve) sized for the maximum credible overpressure scenario, venting to a safe location or flare system.
2. **Provide Secondary Containment** (e.g., bund wall of 110% tank capacity) to prevent uncontrolled spread of released material, paired with **leak detection instrumentation** (e.g., hydrocarbon gas detector).

**Conclusion:** With these engineering controls, the likelihood $L$ is expected to drop to $L_{residual} = 1$, yielding $R_{residual} = 4 \times 1 = 4$, which is *Acceptable*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PHL with HAZOP:** Examiners report 20% of students wrongly describe HAZOP keywords (deviations, guide words) when the question is on PHL. Remember: PHL is *broad and conceptual*; HAZOP is *node-based and parametric*.
>
> 2. **Skipping the Severity/Likelihood Tables:** Even in descriptive answers, examiners expect the **5×5 numerical scale**. Writing "high severity" without a number loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Omitting the Hierarchy of Controls:** When asked for safeguards, simply listing "relief valve" or "training" is incomplete. Always state the *level* of the hierarchy (Engineering vs Administrative vs PPE).
>
> 4. **Forgetting Team Composition:** Many students forget that PHL is *team-based*. Stating "the engineer prepares the PHL" loses a mark.
>
> 5. **No Calculation in Risk Questions:** When the question asks to "compute," step-by-step arithmetic is mandatory. Skipping to a final answer without showing $R = S \times L$ loses at least 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Inconsistent Units / Indices:** Mixing up the Severity and Likelihood values (e.g., quoting $L = 5$ when $S = 5$) is a frequent silent error — always recheck before writing the final number.
>
> 7. **No Conclusion / Recommendation:** A 14-mark answer must end with a clear **concluding statement** about the acceptability of residual risk. Ending abruptly after the table loses 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PHL = Preliminary Hazard List** (also called PHA — Preliminary Hazard Analysis).
- It is the **earliest, broadest, qualitative** hazard identification tool, performed at the **conceptual design phase**.
- PHL uses a **multidisciplinary team** — never a one-person exercise.
- It is **energy-based** (Kletz) and **checklist-driven** (CCPS / MIL-STD-882).
- The output is a **PHL worksheet** containing: Hazard ID, Description, Cause, Consequence, S, L, R_in, Safeguards, S_res, L_res, R_res, Action Owner.
- Initial Risk: $R_{initial} = S \times L$ on a **5×5 matrix**.
- Action bands: **≤4 Acceptable**, **5–9 Procedural**, **10–15 Engineering**, **≥16 Stop**.
- The **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE) guides safeguard selection.
- The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** quantifies safeguard effectiveness: $RRF = R_{initial} / R_{residual}$.
- PHL is the **gateway** to more detailed tools: Checklist → What-If → HAZOP / FMEA / FTA.
- **Limitations:** Qualitative only, dependent on team experience, may miss novel hazards, no detailed accident-sequence modelling.
- **Real-world users:** Chemical, Petrochemical, Oil & Gas, Nuclear, Pharmaceutical, Aerospace (per MIL-STD-882 and NASA-STD-8719).
- **Mandatory deliverable** for KTU Module 1 — examiners expect a numerical 5×5 matrix and explicit safeguard hierarchy in any 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a systematic, structured, and qualitative risk-assessment technique used to identify potential hazards, operability problems, and deviations from design intent in process plants (chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, pharmaceutical, and nuclear facilities). It was originally developed by the **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI)** in the United Kingdom during the **1960s** under the leadership of Herbert Lawley, and later codified by the **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)** and international standards bodies such as **IEC 61882**.

The HAZOP methodology rigorously examines every section ("node") of a process flow diagram (PFD) or piping & instrumentation diagram (P&ID) by applying a fixed set of **Guide Words** to a list of **Process Parameters**, generating meaningful **Deviations**. Each deviation is then interrogated for **Causes**, **Consequences**, **Existing Safeguards**, and **Recommendations**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Doctor's Check-Up for a Chemical Plant"**

Imagine you take your car to a mechanic. Instead of waiting for a part to break, the mechanic performs a **systematic inspection** by asking a fixed set of questions for every system:

- *"What if there is NO oil in the engine?"* → **Guide Word: NO/NONE**
- *"What if there is MORE oil than usual?"* → **Guide Word: MORE**
- *"What if there is LESS brake fluid?"* → **Guide Word: LESS**
- *"What if water gets INTO the fuel?"* → **Guide Word: AS WELL AS / CONTAMINATION**

The mechanic then writes down: **Cause → Consequence → Action**. This is exactly what HAZOP does for an industrial plant. The plant is divided into sections (nodes), and a multidisciplinary team asks "What if…?" questions using a standardized vocabulary to find hidden dangers **before** accidents occur.

## 1.3 The Seven Pillars of HAZOP Terminology

| # | Term | KTU-Standard Meaning |
|---|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Node** | A specific section of the process (e.g., a pump, vessel, line, heat exchanger) selected for study |
| 2 | **Intention** | The design operating condition of the node (flow rate, temperature, pressure) |
| 3 | **Deviation** | A departure from the design intention, generated by combining a Guide Word + a Parameter |
| 4 | **Cause** | The realistic reason why the deviation might occur |
| 5 | **Consequence** | The resulting hazardous outcome (fire, explosion, toxic release, environmental damage) |
| 6 | **Safeguard** | Existing protection (relief valve, alarm, interlock) |
| 7 | **Action** | New recommendation to eliminate or mitigate the risk |

## 1.4 Origin & Applicability Scope

- **Year of Origin:** **1960s (Mid-1960s)**
- **Originating Organization:** **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), UK**
- **Primary Domain:** Chemical & Petrochemical Process Plants
- **Extended Domains (Modern Use):** Pharmaceutical, Oil & Gas (Onshore/Offshore), Nuclear, Water Treatment, Food Processing, Mining, and even Software/Firmware Control Systems
- **Regulatory Recognition:** Mandatory under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM), Seveso III Directive (EU), COMAH Regulations (UK), and Indian Factories Act (under major hazard installations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** HAZOP is classified under **Qualitative Safety Analysis** because it relies on engineering judgment and team expertise rather than numerical probabilities (as opposed to Quantitative Risk Assessment / QRA). It is **the most widely used Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** tool in industry worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The HAZOP Guide Word × Parameter Matrix — The Heart of HAZOP

The fundamental "engine" of HAZOP is the application of a set of **standardized Guide Words** to each **Process Parameter**. The most commonly used set, as defined by **IEC 61882** and **KTU 2024 syllabus**, is:

### 2.1.1 Standard HAZOP Guide Words

| Guide Word | Meaning | Deviation Generated |
|------------|---------|---------------------|
| **NO / NOT / NONE** | Complete negation of the design intention | No Flow, No Pressure, No Level |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | More Flow, More Temperature, More Pressure |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Less Flow, Less Temperature, Less Pressure |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite of intention | Reverse Flow |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional condition occurring alongside | Flow + Contaminant, Phase + Two-Phase |
| **PART OF** | Only a portion of the intention achieved | Reduced Composition (partial mixing) |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution — different event occurs | Maintenance during operation, wrong material |
| **LATE / EARLY** (Sequential HAZOP) | Timing-related deviations | Late opening of valve |

### 2.1.2 Standard HAZOP Process Parameters

| Parameter | Applies To |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Flow** | Liquids, gases, slurries in pipes |
| **Pressure** | Vessels, pipelines, headers |
| **Temperature** | Reactors, heat exchangers, storage tanks |
| **Level** | Vessels, drums, tanks, columns |
| **Composition / Concentration** | Mixers, reactors, distillation columns |
| **Phase** | Multi-phase systems, boilers |
| **Viscosity, pH, Time, Sequence** | Special cases |

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> HAZOP is qualitative, but it integrates with downstream quantitative methods. The following table consolidates the **key equations and metrics** that KTU examiners expect students to know in combination with HAZOP studies.

| Concept / Metric | Formula / Notation | Description |
|------------------|--------------------|-------------|
| **Deviation** | $D = G \times P$ | Deviation $D$ = Guide Word $G$ applied to Process Parameter $P$ |
| **Risk Priority** | $R = S \times L$ | $R$ = Risk Score, $S$ = Severity, $L$ = Likelihood (qualitative 1–5 scale) |
| **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$ | Mean Time Between Failures in **hours** (used to quantify safeguards) |
| **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** | $1 \le SIL \le 4$ | Determined via IEC 61508 from $PFD_{avg}$ (Probability of Failure on Demand) |
| **PFD for SIL-1** | $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$ | Low demand mode |
| **PFD for SIL-2** | $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$ | Low demand mode |
| **PFD for SIL-3** | $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$ | Low demand mode |
| **PFD for SIL-4** | $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}$ | Low demand mode |
| **Node Length (typical)** | $L_{node} \approx 3$ to **10 m** of pipework | Rule of thumb for dividing P&ID |
| **HAZOP Team Size** | $N_{team} = 5$ to **7 members** | KTU syllabus recommendation |

> **Note on $\vert$ symbol:** All vertical absolute-value bars in formulas have been written using `\vert` or written out explicitly to avoid breaking KTU markdown table syntax.

## 2.3 The HAZOP Procedure — 10 Sequential Logic Steps

1. **Define Scope & Objectives** — Specify which plant sections, lifecycle phase, and study goals.
2. **Select Multidisciplinary Team** — Process engineer, control/instrumentation engineer, safety engineer, operations/maintenance representative, team leader/facilitator.
3. **Gather Documentation** — P&IDs, PFDs, material balances, operating procedures, MSDS, layout drawings.
4. **Divide Plant into Nodes** — Each node = a logical process section with defined design intention.
5. **Apply Guide Words to Parameters** — Systematically iterate every Guide Word × Parameter combination.
6. **Identify Deviations** — Document any meaningful deviation from design intent.
7. **Determine Causes** — For each deviation, brainstorm all realistic initiating events (equipment failure, human error, external event).
8. **Evaluate Consequences** — Identify worst-case outcomes (toxic release, fire, explosion, environmental impact, operability loss).
9. **Review Existing Safeguards** — Identify protections already in place (relief valves, alarms, interlocks, procedures).
10. **Recommend Actions** — Suggest engineering, procedural, or training improvements; assign responsibility and target date.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design Phase:** Prevents costly retrofits by catching design errors early (saves **30–50%** of capital cost overruns).
- **Operational Phase:** Forms the basis of **Management of Change (MOC)** reviews.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory prerequisite for plant commissioning under Seveso III / OSHA PSM.
- **Insurance & Liability:** Accepted by Lloyds, Munich Re, and major reinsurers as evidence of due diligence.
- **Incident Investigation:** Post-incident HAZOP-style "what-if" reconstructions are standard in Bhopal, Flixborough, and Texas City refinery investigations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common 7-mark question asks students to *list and explain all 7 guide words with an example deviation each*. Memorize the deviations, not just the guide words.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step HAZOP Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example: HAZOP Study on a Centrifugal Pump delivering Water from a Storage Tank to a Header**
>
> *System Description:* A centrifugal pump (P-101) draws water from atmospheric storage tank (T-101) and discharges through a control valve (FCV-101) and a check valve (CV-101) to a pressurized process header. The line has a pressure indicator (PI), flow indicator (FI), and a pressure safety valve (PSV-101) set at **10 bar(g)**.
>
> *Design Intention of the node:* Transfer water at a steady flow rate of **50 m³/h** at a discharge pressure of **5 bar(g)** and temperature of **30 °C**.

## 3.1 Step A — Node Selection and Design Intention

$$
\text{Node} = \text{Pump P-101 discharge line (from pump suction to CV-101)}
$$

$$
\text{Design Intention} = \begin{cases}
F = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \\
P = 5 \text{ bar(g)} \\
T = 30 \text{ °C}
\end{cases}
$$

**Explanation:** The "intention" is the agreed design operating state. Every deviation is then measured against this baseline. Without a clear intention, the team cannot judge what is "abnormal."

## 3.2 Step B — Generate Deviations

Applying the standard guide words to the parameter **FLOW**, we obtain the deviations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviation 1:} \quad & \text{NO FLOW} \; (\text{Guide Word: NO} + \text{Parameter: FLOW}) \\
\text{Deviation 2:} \quad & \text{MORE FLOW} \; (\text{Guide Word: MORE} + \text{Parameter: FLOW}) \\
\text{Deviation 3:} \quad & \text{LESS FLOW} \; (\text{Guide Word: LESS} + \text{Parameter: FLOW}) \\
\text{Deviation 4:} \quad & \text{REVERSE FLOW} \; (\text{Guide Word: REVERSE} + \text{Parameter: FLOW}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The systematic pairing produces deviations even if some are physically impossible — they are still documented to demonstrate completeness of the study.

## 3.3 Step C — Cause → Consequence → Safeguard → Action Matrix

### HAZOP Worksheet Excerpt (Pump P-101 Discharge Line)

| Deviation | Cause(s) | Consequence(s) | Existing Safeguard | Recommendation (Action) |
|-----------|----------|----------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| **NO FLOW** | Pump failure (mechanical seal leak), suction strainer blocked, suction tank empty, FCV-101 closed in error, power failure | Pump overheating → seal damage → water leak; downstream process unit loses feedstock → plant shutdown | Low-flow alarm FAL-101, pump trip on no-flow, manual operating procedure | Install low-low flow trip with auto-restart lockout; add strainer DP indication |
| **MORE FLOW** | FCV-101 fails open; downstream valve closed by operator; pump operating on wrong impeller | Pipe erosion, flange leak, downstream equipment over-rated | PI high alarm; FCV-101 has pneumatic fail-closed action | Verify FCV-101 fail-safe direction during commissioning; install flow HI-HI trip |
| **LESS FLOW** | Partial blockage in strainer, partial pump cavitation, partial FCV-101 closure | Reduced throughput → product quality deviation; pump operates away from BEP (Best Efficiency Point) → vibration | FI low alarm; PI readout; routine vibration monitoring | Add remote blockage indication; conduct quarterly pump performance trend review |
| **REVERSE FLOW** | Pump stops while downstream header remains pressurized; check valve (CV-101) fails open | Header fluid backflows → storage tank overflow → slip hazard, environmental spill | CV-101 (check valve), suction-side level switch LSL | Install a second check valve (double block) or a pressure-balanced zero-leakage valve |

## 3.4 Step D — Quantitative Severity & Likelihood Scoring

For each consequence, the team assigns qualitative scores that can be tabulated using the matrix:

$$
R = S \times L
$$

| Deviation | Severity $S$ (1–5) | Likelihood $L$ (1–5) | Risk Score $R = S \times L$ |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------|
| NO FLOW | 3 | 3 | **9** |
| MORE FLOW | 4 | 2 | **8** |
| LESS FLOW | 2 | 4 | **8** |
| REVERSE FLOW | 5 | 1 | **5** |

**Explanation of scoring scale (1–5):**

- **Severity (S):** 1 = Negligible, 2 = Minor, 3 = Moderate, 4 = Major, 5 = Catastrophic
- **Likelihood (L):** 1 = Rare (< 1 in 1000 yrs), 2 = Unlikely (1 in 100 yrs), 3 = Possible (1 in 10 yrs), 4 = Likely (1 in 1 yr), 5 = Frequent (> 1 per year)

**Risk Acceptance Threshold (typical company standard):** $R_{accept} = 4$ (i.e., $R > 4 \rightarrow$ mandatory action).

## 3.5 Step E — Closing the Loop: Action Tracking

Each recommendation must be entered into a **HAZOP Action Register** with:

- Action Number
- Description
- Responsible Person
- Target Closure Date
- Verification Sign-Off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Note:** A HAZOP study is **NOT complete** until all actions are closed, verified, and signed off. Auditors (under OSHA PSM, Seveso III, or KTU ESE viva) will specifically check the **Action Close-Out Log**.

## 3.6 HAZOP vs Other PHA Methods — KTU Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | HAZOP | What-If | FMEA | Fault Tree |
|-----------|-------|---------|------|------------|
| **Type** | Qualitative | Qualitative | Qualitative | Quantitative |
| **Team Required** | Yes (5–7) | Yes (2–4) | No (single analyst possible) | Yes (specialist) |
| **Scope** | Process deviations | Broad brainstorming | Component failure | Top event analysis |
| **Documentation Detail** | Very high | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Numerical Output** | None (unless Risk Matrix added) | None | RPN | Probability of top event |
| **Best Suited For** | Continuous chemical processes | Early design, novel processes | Mechanical/electrical equipment | Complex systems, rare events |
| **Time Required** | High (1–6 weeks) | Low (1–3 days) | Medium | Very High |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example: SIL Determination for a HAZOP Recommendation

Suppose HAZOP identifies that **MORE FLOW** must be mitigated by a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). The team performs a **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** and finds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Tolerable Frequency &= 10^{-5} \text{ /year} \\
\text{Initiating Event Frequency} &= 10^{-1} \text{ /year} \\
\text{IPL Credits (independent layers)} &= 10^{-2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the required **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of the SIF is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{required} &= \frac{\text{Tolerable Frequency}}{\text{Initiating Frequency} \times \prod(\text{IPL})} \\
&= \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-1} \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} \\
&= 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** From IEC 61508, $PFD_{avg}$ in the range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ corresponds to **SIL-1**. Therefore, the SIF must achieve SIL-1, meaning the safety instrumented system must have an architecture compliant with SIL-1 (e.g., 1oo1 or 1oo2 with proof test interval as per manufacturer data).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show units in intermediate steps and state the IEC 61508 reference range in the final answer. Marks are awarded for correctly mapping $PFD_{avg}$ to a SIL level.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAZOP Study Lifecycle — Procedural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage1 Define Scope Objectives] --> B[Stage2 Select Multidisciplinary Team]
    B --> C[Stage3 Gather P and ID PFD MSDS Documentation]
    C --> D[Stage4 Divide Plant into Logical Nodes]
    D --> E[Stage5 Apply Guide Words to Process Parameters]
    E --> F[Stage6 Identify Meaningful Deviations]
    F --> G[Stage7 Determine Causes for Each Deviation]
    G --> H[Stage8 Evaluate Consequences Severity]
    H --> I[Stage9 Review Existing Safeguards IPLs]
    I --> J[Stage10 Recommend Actions and Assign Responsibility]
    J --> K{All Actions Closed and Verified?}
    K -->|Yes| L[HAZOP Study Complete and Archived]
    K -->|No| M[Re-review in next HAZOP cycle or Management of Change]
    M --> D
```

## 4.2 Guide Word × Parameter Deviation Generation — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Guide Word Library] --> C[Deviation Generator Matrix]
    B[Process Parameter Library] --> C
    C --> D[Deviation Set]
    D --> E[Cause Analysis]
    E --> F[Consequence Evaluation]
    F --> G[Safeguard Review]
    G --> H[Action Recommendation]
    H --> I[Action Close Out Register]
```

## 4.3 HAZOP Team Composition — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TL[Team Leader and Facilitator HAZOP Trained] --> SC[Scribe and Recorder Documents Worksheet]
    TL --> PE[Process Engineer]
    TL --> IE[Instrumentation and Control Engineer]
    TL --> SE[Safety and Loss Prevention Engineer]
    TL --> OP[Operations and Maintenance Representative]
    PE --> DOC[HAZOP Worksheet]
    IE --> DOC
    SE --> DOC
    OP --> DOC
    SC --> DOC
```

## 4.4 HAZOP Worksheet — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Node Identification] --> B[Design Intention Statement]
    B --> C[Deviation Generation Guide Word plus Parameter]
    C --> D[Cause Identification]
    D --> E[Consequence Evaluation]
    E --> F[Safeguard Listing]
    F --> G[Action Recommendation]
    G --> H[Responsibility Assignment]
    H --> I[Action Closeout Verification]
```

## 4.5 Risk Matrix Used in HAZOP Severity-Likelihood Scoring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Likelihood 1 Rare] --> N1[Negligible Zone 1]
    L2[Likelihood 2 Unlikely] --> N2[Low Zone 2 to 4]
    L3[Likelihood 3 Possible] --> M1[Medium Zone 5 to 9]
    L4[Likelihood 4 Likely] --> M2[Medium Zone 5 to 9]
    L5[Likelihood 5 Frequent] --> H1[High Zone 10 to 14]
    S1[Severity 1 Minor] --> N1
    S2[Severity 2 Marginal] --> N2
    S3[Severity 3 Moderate] --> M1
    S4[Severity 4 Major] --> M2
    S5[Severity 5 Catastrophic] --> H1
    N1 --> Accept[Acceptable No Action]
    N2 --> Monitor[Monitor and Review]
    M1 --> Reduce[Reduce Risk ALARP]
    M2 --> Reduce
    H1 --> Stop[Stop and Mandatory Mitigation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** Each block in a HAZOP flowchart maps to one row in the HAZOP worksheet. Examiners expect students to be able to *trace a deviation from generation all the way to action close-out* on such a diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**Q: Define HAZOP. Who developed it, and in which decade?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** is a structured, team-based, qualitative safety analysis technique used to identify hazards and operability problems in process plants by systematically applying standardized Guide Words to Process Parameters at designated nodes of the plant.
>
> HAZOP was developed by **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), United Kingdom**, during the **1960s** (mid-1960s), and was later formalized by the **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)**. It is recognized internationally by standards such as **IEC 61882** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 PSM**.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**Q: List any six standard HAZOP Guide Words with one example deviation each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Guide Word | Example Deviation |
|------------|-------------------|
| **NO / NOT / NONE** | No Flow in the discharge line |
| **MORE** | More Pressure inside the reactor |
| **LESS** | Less Temperature at the heat exchanger outlet |
| **REVERSE** | Reverse Flow in the pump discharge |
| **AS WELL AS** | Flow + Contaminant (air ingress into oil line) |
| **PART OF** | Reduced Concentration of acid in the mixing tank |
| **OTHER THAN** | Maintenance carried out during normal operation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**Q: A centrifugal pump delivers methanol from a storage tank to a distillation column through a control valve. A flow indicator (FI), pressure indicator (PI), and a high-pressure alarm (PAH) are installed on the discharge line.**

**Required:**

**(a)** Explain the **complete HAZOP procedure** (all 10 steps) to be followed for analyzing this system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Develop a **HAZOP worksheet** for the parameter **FLOW** at the pump discharge node, identifying all possible deviations (using standard guide words), their causes, consequences, and recommended actions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a)

The HAZOP procedure consists of **10 systematic steps**:

**Step 1 — Define Scope & Objectives:** Specify that the study covers the pump discharge line, focusing on flow and pressure deviations, with the objective of identifying hazards prior to commissioning.

**Step 2 — Select Multidisciplinary Team:** Form a team of 5–7 members including a Team Leader (HAZOP-trained facilitator), Process Engineer, Instrumentation Engineer, Safety Engineer, Operations/Maintenance representative, and a Scribe.

**Step 3 — Gather Documentation:** Collect the latest P&ID, PFD, Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS) for methanol, pump data sheet, control valve specification, instrument loop diagrams, and operating procedures.

**Step 4 — Divide Plant into Nodes:** Identify the node as the pump discharge line from the pump outlet flange to the control valve inlet.

**Step 5 — Apply Guide Words to Parameters:** Systematically apply each Guide Word (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) to each Process Parameter (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase).

**Step 6 — Identify Deviations:** For Flow: NO FLOW, MORE FLOW, LESS FLOW, REVERSE FLOW.

**Step 7 — Determine Causes:** Pump mechanical failure, control valve stuck closed, line blockage, suction tank empty.

**Step 8 — Evaluate Consequences:** Loss of feedstock → column flooding; pump overheating → seal failure → methanol release → fire hazard.

**Step 9 — Review Existing Safeguards:** Flow low alarm (FAL), pressure high alarm (PAH), pump trip on low suction, ESD system.

**Step 10 — Recommend Actions & Assign Responsibility:** Install flow low-low trip, add remote block valve with position indication, conduct quarterly pump vibration analysis, update operating procedure for startup sequence. Actions entered in **Action Register** with target dates and responsible persons.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b)

**HAZOP Worksheet — Node: Pump P-201 Discharge Line — Parameter: FLOW**

| Guide Word | Deviation | Cause(s) | Consequence(s) | Existing Safeguard | Recommendation |
|------------|-----------|----------|----------------|--------------------|----------------|
| **NO** | No Flow | Pump failure, blocked strainer, FCV closed, suction empty | Pump cavitation damage, downstream column loses feed → off-spec product, methanol leak from seal → fire | FAL-101 low-flow alarm, suction level switch, ESD-1 | Install flow low-low trip with auto-restart lockout; add strainer DP transmitter |
| **MORE** | More Flow | FCV fails open, downstream valve closed, pump impeller oversized | Pipe erosion, flange leak, downstream column flooding → overpressure | FCV fail-closed, PI high alarm | Add flow HI-HI trip; verify FCV air supply regulator |
| **LESS** | Less Flow | Partial strainer blockage, partial FCV closure, pump cavitation start | Reduced throughput, product quality deviation, pump vibration | FI low alarm, vibration monitoring | Install remote blockage indicator; review pump curve at operating point |
| **REVERSE** | Reverse Flow | Pump stops, check valve fails open, downstream header pressure > suction | Backflow → storage tank overflow → methanol spill → fire, environmental contamination | Check valve, suction line level switch | Install double-block valve arrangement or zero-leakage valve; add tank HI-HI level trip |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative to Question A**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**Q: A heat exchanger cools hot oil from a reactor using cooling water. The cooling water inlet has a flow control valve (FCV), and the oil outlet temperature is monitored by TI with a high-temperature alarm (TAH).**

**Required:**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **HAZOP study methodology** showing the sequence from node definition to action close-out. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Perform a HAZOP study for the parameter **TEMPERATURE** at the oil outlet node. List deviations, causes, consequences, safeguards, and at least four recommended actions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a)

The HAZOP methodology can be represented as a 10-step block diagram:

```
[Define Scope] → [Form Team] → [Gather Documents]
            ↓
[Divide Plant into Nodes]
            ↓
[Apply Guide Words to Parameters]
            ↓
[Identify Deviations] → [Find Causes] → [Evaluate Consequences]
            ↓
[Review Safeguards] → [Recommend Actions]
            ↓
[Document in Action Register]
            ↓
{Action Closed?} → Yes → [Archive & Sign-off]
                → No  → [Re-cycle in next HAZOP review or MOC]
```

This block diagram corresponds exactly to the 10 procedural steps described in Section 2.3 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b)

**Node: Oil Outlet of Heat Exchanger E-101**
**Design Intention: Oil Outlet Temperature = 80 °C, Flow = 30 m³/h, Pressure = 4 bar(g)**

| Guide Word | Deviation | Cause(s) | Consequence(s) | Existing Safeguard | Recommended Action |
|------------|-----------|----------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **MORE** | More Temperature (oil outlet > 80 °C) | Cooling water flow loss, FCV fails closed, fouling on water side, pump trip on cooling water | Downstream unit receives hot oil → thermal damage to gaskets, fire risk, product degradation | TAH high-temperature alarm, TI local indicator | Install temperature HI-HI trip to close hot oil block valve; add cooling water flow low alarm |
| **LESS** | Less Temperature (oil outlet < 80 °C) | Excess cooling water flow, FCV fails open, ambient cold weather | Oil viscosity rises → downstream pump cavitation; energy waste | TI low indication | Add temperature low alarm; insulate outdoor piping |
| **REVERSE** | Reverse flow of cooling water | Cross-connection, pump failure with check valve failure | Oil-side contamination; possible pressure surge | Check valve, flow indicator | Verify check valve annually; install double block-and-bleed |
| **AS WELL AS** | Temperature + Phase change (two-phase flow) | Water leakage into oil side due to tube rupture | Tube bundle corrosion; water hammer in oil line | Leak detection not installed | Install online tube integrity monitoring; install interlock to isolate exchanger on leak detection |
| **OTHER THAN** | Wrong oil grade flowing (not the design oil) | Operator error during campaign change, valve misalignment | Heat transfer coefficient different → uncontrolled outlet temperature | Operating procedure, line labelling | Add positive material identification (PMI) at line entry; update operator training |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in HAZOP Questions**
>
> 1. **Memorizing Guide Words without Examples** — Examiners award marks only when a deviation is shown (e.g., "NO FLOW" not just "NO"). Always state the deviation in full.
> 2. **Confusing Cause with Consequence** — "Pump fails" is a CAUSE; "Pump overheats and seal ruptures, releasing methanol" is a CONSEQUENCE. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction for **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting Safeguards** — Students often skip the "Existing Safeguards" column. This is a guaranteed mark-loser; HAZOP without safeguard review is incomplete.
> 4. **Forgetting Risk Scoring** — Modern KTU questions require Risk Score $R = S \times L$ to be computed and compared against the company's acceptance threshold.
> 5. **Skipping the Action Close-Out Loop** — A HAZOP study is not closed until actions are verified. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for mentioning action tracking.
> 6. **Wrong Origin of HAZOP** — Several textbooks incorrectly credit "British Chemical Industry" generically. The correct attribution is **ICI (Imperial Chemical Industries), UK, 1960s**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HAZOP (Module 1, OESFT837)**
>
> - **Full Form:** **H**azard **a**nd **O**perability **P**roblem Study (NOT "Analysis" in older references — KTU 2024 uses "Study").
> - **Origin:** **ICI, UK, 1960s** — Herbert Lawley is widely credited as the principal developer.
> - **Type of Analysis:** **Qualitative**, structured, team-based.
> - **Standard Reference:** **IEC 61882:2016** "Hazard and operability studies (HAZOP studies) — Application guide."
> - **Team Size:** **5 to 7 members**, led by an independent, HAZOP-trained facilitator.
> - **Core Tool:** Guide Words × Process Parameters → Deviations.
> - **7 Standard Guide Words:** **NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN** (Plus late/early for sequential HAZOP).
> - **6 Common Parameters:** **Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase.**
> - **Worksheet Columns:** Node, Intention, Deviation, Cause, Consequence, Safeguard, Action, Responsibility.
> - **Risk Equation:** $R = S \times L$ (1–5 scale each).
> - **Node Length Rule:** **3 to 10 metres** of pipework or one major equipment item per node.
> - **Output:** HAZOP Worksheet + Action Register → tracked to closure under Management of Change.
> - **Limitations:** Does NOT address occupancy, natural hazards, security threats, or rare multi-failure events (these require QRA, FTA, or bow-tie).
> - **Related Standards:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, Seveso III Directive 2012/18/EU, COMAH 2015 (UK).
> - **Downstream Tool:** HAZOP findings often feed into **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** for SIL determination per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line Memory Aid for Viva:**
> *"HAZOP = Nodes × Guide Words × Parameters → Deviations → Causes → Consequences → Safeguards → Actions → Closed-Loop."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) - Identification of Failure Modes
Application of Hazard Identification Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of Hazard Identification Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazard Identification Techniques (HITs)** are systematic, structured, and documented methodologies employed during the design, operation, and decommissioning phases of an engineering system to recognize, classify, and evaluate potential sources of harm (hazards) before they manifest into accidents or incidents. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, these techniques form the qualitative foundation of process safety management (PSM) and are the precursor to quantitative risk assessment (QRA).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 OBE framework**, hazard identification is mapped to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Identify and analyse hazards in process and industrial systems using qualitative and semi-quantitative techniques."* These techniques convert subjective engineering judgment into a defensible, auditable, and reproducible safety record.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Detective's Toolkit" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a detective arriving at a factory *before* a crime (accident) has occurred. You cannot simply guess where the thief might strike. Instead, you use different magnifying glasses (techniques):
>
> - **Checklist** → A standard list of known past crimes (history-based screening).
> - **HAZOP** → You walk through every pipe and valve, asking "What IF this valve is closed by mistake?" (deviation-based probing).
> - **FMEA** → You dismantle every component on paper and ask "How could this gear FAIL?" (component-level failure review).
> - **FTA** → You start with the disaster (e.g., explosion) and work backwards, like reverse-engineering the crime scene (top-down logic).
> - **What-If** → A free-form brainstorming session with experienced operators.
> - **Bow-Tie** → You draw the "story" of an accident: threats on the left, the incident in the middle, and consequences on the right.
>
> Each technique is a different lens; no single lens catches every hazard. That is why **application of multiple techniques** is mandated by ISO 12100 and OSHA 1910.119.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard metrics govern the *application* of hazard identification in industry:
> - **Hazard Identification Performance Standard:** Detection of 100% of Category A (catastrophic) hazards is mandatory (Target = 1.0).
> - **SIL (Safety Integrity Level):** Per **IEC 61508**, ranges from **1 to 4** (probability of failure on demand between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-5}$).
> - **Risk Priority Number (RPN) Threshold for Action:** Generally **RPN ≥ 100** triggers mandatory corrective action in FMEA.

| Symbol | Definition | Unit / Scale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S$ | Severity | 1 to 10 (dimensionless) |
| $O$ | Occurrence | 1 to 10 (dimensionless) |
| $D$ | Detection Difficulty | 1 to 10 (dimensionless) |
| $RPN$ | Risk Priority Number | Score 1 to 1000 |
| $PFD$ | Probability of Failure on Demand | Dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Hazard Identification Techniques

Hazard identification techniques are broadly classified into five families, each applied at a different stage of the project lifecycle:

- **Inherent Safety / Conceptual Stage**
  - **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
  - **What-If Analysis**
  - **Checklist Analysis**
- **Detailed Design Stage**
  - **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)**
  - **FMEA / FMECA**
- **Operational & Quantitative Stage**
  - **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
  - **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**
  - **Bow-Tie Analysis**
  - **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)**
- **Continuous / Lifecycle Stage**
  - **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)**
  - **Management Oversight and Risk Tree (MORT)**

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Technique

- **HAZOP** answers: *"What deviations from design intent can occur, their causes, and consequences?"* It is a **qualitative, deviation-based** technique.
- **FMEA** answers: *"How can each component fail, and what is the impact on the system?"* It is a **bottom-up, component-level** technique.
- **FTA** answers: *"What combinations of failures lead to the undesired top event?"* It is a **top-down, logical** technique.
- **What-If** answers: *"What unforeseen scenarios could lead to loss?"* It is a **brainstorming-based** technique.
- **Checklist** answers: *"Have we addressed all known historical hazard categories?"* It is a **compliance-based** technique.
- **Bow-Tie** answers: *"What are the threats, the central event, the preventive/reactive controls, and the consequences?"* It is a **risk-barrier** visualization technique.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Method | Symbolic Form | Application Context |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Ranking failure modes for action |
| 2 | Boolean OR (Fault Tree) | $P(A \cup B) = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$ | Top event probability for parallel paths |
| 3 | Boolean AND (Fault Tree) | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Top event probability for series paths |
| 4 | Cut-Set Probability | $P_{top} \approx \sum P(K_i) - \sum P(K_i \cap K_j)$ | Quantitative fault tree summation |
| 5 | SIL Assignment | $PFD_{avg}$ vs Target Range | Mapping RPN to IEC 61508 SIL |
| 6 | Risk Score (Matrix) | $R = L \times S$ | Semi-quantitative risk classification |
| 7 | Event Tree Outcome Probability | $P(O_k) = P(I) \cdot \prod_{i} P(C_i)$ | Success/failure branch sequence |
| 8 | Hazard Rate (Weibull) | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Component failure rate vs time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, writing the formula *and* explaining what each variable means (e.g., $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence) guarantees at least 1 mark even if the numerical substitution is wrong.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** HAZOP is **mandatory** during Pre-Start-up Safety Review (PSSR).
- **Nuclear Power:** FTA is the gold standard (e.g., WASH-1400 Reactor Safety Study).
- **Aerospace & Automotive:** FMEA / FMECA is required by ISO 26262 (Automotive SIL) and SAE ARP 4761 (Aviation).
- **Construction & Maintenance:** Job Safety Analysis (JSA) is required before any non-routine work.
- **Process Plants:** Bow-Tie analysis is mandated by the **COMAH Regulations (UK)** and **CCPS Guidelines (USA)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN) — Exhaustive Derivation

The RPN is a semi-quantitative score used to prioritize failure modes. The derivation from severity, occurrence, and detection follows:

**Step 1: Define the three independent ordinal scales.**

The severity scale $S$ ranges from 1 (no effect) to 10 (catastrophic, loss of life). The occurrence scale $O$ ranges from 1 (very unlikely, $\le 1$ in $10^6$) to 10 (very high, $\ge 1$ in 2). The detection scale $D$ ranges from 1 (almost certain detection) to 10 (impossible to detect before reaching the customer).

**Step 2: Establish the multiplicative aggregation model.**

Because the three parameters are independent contributors to the overall risk of a failure mode, the engineering community (SAE J1739, IEC 60812) adopted a multiplicative scoring model.

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 3: Calculate the theoretical range.**

Since the minimum value of each parameter is 1 and the maximum is 10:

$$RPN_{min} = 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1$$

$$RPN_{max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$$

**Step 4: Interpretation bands.**

$$
RPN\ Band = \begin{cases}
\text{Low} & \text{if } RPN \le 50 \\
\text{Medium} & \text{if } 51 \le RPN \le 150 \\
\text{High} & \text{if } RPN \ge 151
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5: Worked numerical example.**

Consider a pressure relief valve in a steam boiler:
- Severity $S = 9$ (potential for fatality if it fails to open)
- Occurrence $O = 4$ (occasional failure, ~1 in 200 demands)
- Detection $D = 6$ (manual testing only, not continuous)

$$RPN = 9 \times 4 \times 6 = 216$$

Since $RPN = 216 > 150$, this failure mode is classified as **High Risk** and requires immediate corrective action, such as installing a continuous online diagnostic monitor (reducing $D$ to 2) and a redundant relief valve (reducing $O$ to 1). Re-computing:

$$RPN_{new} = 9 \times 1 \times 2 = 18 \quad (\text{Low Risk, acceptable})$$

### 3.2 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Top Event Probability Derivation

The FTA quantifies the probability of a top event by combining the probabilities of basic events through AND/OR logic gates.

**Step 1: Identify the top event $T$.**

The top event is the undesired disaster at the apex of the tree (e.g., *Reactor Overpressure*).

**Step 2: Construct the logic gates.**

A fault tree uses AND gates (output occurs only if ALL inputs occur — series reliability) and OR gates (output occurs if ANY input occurs — parallel reliability).

**Step 3: Probability of an AND gate.**

For independent basic events $A$ and $B$:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

**Step 4: Probability of an OR gate.**

For independent basic events $A$ and $B$:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

Or equivalently:

$$P(A \cup B) = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$$

**Step 5: Worked example — Two redundant pumps failure.**

Two identical pumps (each with failure probability $P = 0.05$ per demand) operate in parallel. The system fails only if **both** pumps fail (AND gate, because the system needs at least one pump running for cooling).

$$P_{system\,fail} = 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0025 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

This is a $20\times$ reduction in failure probability compared to a single pump.

**Step 6: Worked example — Two independent causes of overpressure.**

The reactor can overpressure due to (i) block valve closed on the outlet, or (ii) cooling water pump failure. Each has probability $P = 0.01$ per year. The causes are connected by an **OR gate**.

$$P_{top} = 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.01) = 1 - 0.9801 = 0.0199 \approx 0.02 \text{ per year}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (When to Apply Which Technique)

| Technique | Best Lifecycle Stage | Type | Output | Key Limitation |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Checklist** | Concept / Pre-design | Qualitative | Compliance list | Misses novel hazards |
| **What-If** | Concept / Pre-design | Qualitative | Scenario list | Highly dependent on team experience |
| **PHA** | Concept / Pre-design | Qualitative | Hazard register | Coarse-grained |
| **HAZOP** | Detailed design | Qualitative | Deviation causes/consequences | Time-consuming, requires P&ID |
| **FMEA** | Detailed design / Operation | Semi-quantitative | RPN-ranked list | Can become a "paperwork exercise" |
| **FTA** | Detailed / Operation | Quantitative | Top event probability | Requires accurate failure data |
| **ETA** | Detailed / Operation | Quantitative | Outcome frequency | Cannot identify new initiating events |
| **Bow-Tie** | Operation | Qualitative + Quantitative | Threat-Consequence map with barriers | Static snapshot; does not show time dynamics |
| **JSA** | Construction / Maintenance | Qualitative | Step-by-step hazards per task | Only for defined tasks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "**HAZOP + LOPA + SIL**" triplet is the industry-standard application sequence in the process industries. HAZOP identifies the scenario, LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) semi-quantifies the independent protection layers, and SIL assigns the required safety integrity level per IEC 61511.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bow-Tie Analysis Schematic (Application View)

This diagram shows the **application of Bow-Tie** for a hydrocarbon storage tank fire scenario — a classic KTU 2024 exam diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node_threatA["THREAT A: Lightning Strike"]
    node_threatB["THREAT B: Static Electricity"]
    node_threatC["THREAT C: Hot Work Nearby"]
    node_barrierP1["PREVENTIVE BARRIER 1: Lightning Arrester"]
    node_barrierP2["PREVENTIVE BARRIER 2: Earthing and Bonding"]
    node_barrierP3["PREVENTIVE BARRIER 3: Hot Work Permit System"]
    node_central["CENTRAL EVENT: Tank Roof Fire Ignition"]
    node_barrierM1["MITIGATIVE BARRIER 1: Foam Pourer System"]
    node_barrierM2["MITIGATIVE BARRIER 2: Emergency Shutdown ESD"]
    node_barrierM3["MITIGATIVE BARRIER 3: Fire Water Deluge"]
    node_consA["CONSEQUENCE A: Pool Fire Escalation"]
    node_consB["CONSEQUENCE B: BLEVE Explosion"]
    node_consC["CONSEQUENCE C: Toxic Smoke Release"]

    node_threatA --> node_barrierP1 --> node_central
    node_threatB --> node_barrierP2 --> node_central
    node_threatC --> node_barrierP3 --> node_central
    node_central --> node_barrierM1 --> node_consA
    node_central --> node_barrierM2 --> node_consB
    node_central --> node_barrierM3 --> node_consC
```

### 4.2 HAZOP Application Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This flow maps the **practical application sequence** of a HAZOP study as required by IEC 61882.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node_start["Start: Define Scope and Objectives"]
    node_team["Assemble Multi-Disciplinary Team: Process, Mech, Instr, Safety, Ops"]
    node_data["Gather Data: P and ID, PFD, Operating Procedures"]
    node_node["Divide Plant into Study Nodes: e.g., Feed Section, Reactor, Distillation"]
    node_deviation["Apply Guide Words: NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN"]
    node_cause["For Each Deviation: Identify Causes"]
    node_conseq["For Each Deviation: Identify Consequences"]
    node_safeguard["List Existing Safeguards: Alarms, Interlocks, Reliefs"]
    node_recommend["Generate Recommendations and Action Items"]
    node_report["Compile HAZOP Report with Risk Matrix Ranking"]
    node_closeout["Close-Out: Verify Implementation of Recommendations"]

    node_start --> node_team --> node_data --> node_node --> node_deviation
    node_deviation --> node_cause --> node_conseq --> node_safeguard --> node_recommend --> node_report --> node_closeout
```

### 4.3 Fault Tree Analysis Application (OR-then-AND Structure)

This diagram represents a typical application of FTA to a **reactor overpressure** top event.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node_top["TOP EVENT: Reactor Overpressure"]
    node_or1["OR GATE G1"]
    node_and1["AND GATE G2"]
    node_and2["AND GATE G3"]
    node_basicA["A: Block Valve Closed"]
    node_basicB["B: Operator Error"]
    node_basicC["C: Cooling Pump Fails to Start"]
    node_basicD["D: Power Supply Fails"]
    node_basicE["E: Pressure Sensor Fault"]

    node_top --> node_or1
    node_or1 --> node_and1
    node_or1 --> node_and2
    node_and1 --> node_basicA
    node_and1 --> node_basicB
    node_and2 --> node_basicC
    node_and2 --> node_basicD
    node_and2 --> node_basicE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Tree:** Top event occurs if (A **AND** B) **OR** (C **AND** D **AND** E). This is a *minimal cut-set* representation; the smallest combination of basic events causing the top event is the **2nd-order cut set {A, B}**.

### 4.4 FMEA Worksheet Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node_item["Step 1: List System Item: e.g., Pressure Safety Valve PSV-101"]
    node_func["Step 2: Define Function: Open at Set Pressure 10 barg"]
    node_fail["Step 3: List Failure Modes: Fails to Open, Opens Early, Leaks"]
    node_effect["Step 4: Determine Effects on System"]
    node_score["Step 5: Score S, O, D for Each Failure"]
    node_rpn["Step 6: Compute RPN = S x O x D"]
    node_rank["Step 7: Rank and Prioritize"]
    node_action["Step 8: Recommend Action for RPN over 100"]

    node_item --> node_func --> node_fail --> node_effect --> node_score --> node_rpn --> node_rank --> node_action
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Hazard Identification. List any four commonly used hazard identification techniques in the process industry.*

**Model Answer:**
Hazard Identification is the systematic process of recognizing, listing, and characterizing sources of potential harm (hazards) in a system, facility, or activity to enable subsequent risk evaluation and control.

Four common techniques are:
1. **Checklist Analysis** – Uses a pre-defined list of known hazards.
2. **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** – Deviation-based study using guide words on P&IDs.
3. **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** – Component-level failure analysis using RPN.
4. **What-If Analysis** – Brainstorming-based scenario review by a multidisciplinary team.

*(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four techniques with one-line description.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Write the formula for Risk Priority Number (RPN) used in FMEA and state the action threshold.*

**Model Answer:**
The Risk Priority Number is calculated as the product of three independent scores:
$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$
where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection Difficulty (1–10).
The theoretical range is **1 to 1000**. The standard action threshold is **RPN ≥ 100**, which triggers mandatory corrective action and re-design.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **write only the definition** of HAZOP without applying it to a node (e.g., "no flow", "more pressure") → lose up to 4 marks.
> - In FMEA numericals, students **omit the unit-less nature** of RPN and wrongly state the result in % → lose 1 mark.
> - For FTA, students **assume OR and AND are interchangeable** → severe conceptual error, lose up to 5 marks. Remember: AND = both must fail (series); OR = any one fails (parallel).
> - Always **state assumptions** (independent events, demand mode, etc.) in quantitative FTA questions to earn the full 7 marks of part (a).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — HAZOP + FMEA Application** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** *Explain the application of HAZOP study for a centrifugal pump handling a flammable solvent. Use at least **four guide words** and tabulate the deviation, cause, consequence, and safeguards. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is applied by a multidisciplinary team using **IEC 61882** as the framework. The study nodes for a centrifugal pump are: *Suction Line, Pump Casing, Discharge Line, and Seal System.*

**Guide Words applied (selected four):**

| Guide Word | Deviation | Possible Cause | Consequence | Existing Safeguard |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **NO / NONE OF** | No Flow | Pump unprimed, suction line blocked, suction valve closed, mechanical seal failure | Pump cavitation, overheating, potential seal failure and solvent release | Low-flow alarm, suction strainer, priming vent valve |
| **MORE OF** | More Flow | Bypass valve stuck open, discharge valve opened beyond design | Pipe vibration, flange leak, possible rupture downstream | Flow transmitter with high-flow alarm, PSV on discharge line |
| **LESS OF** | Less Flow | Partial blockage in strainer, partial valve closure, worn impeller | Reduced cooling/transfer rate, possible pump damage, solvent accumulation in upstream equipment | Local flow indicator, scheduled maintenance log |
| **AS WELL AS** | Contamination of Solvent | Cross-connection with another service, leak from heat exchanger tube | Quality loss, possible reaction in downstream vessel, fire hazard | Block-and-bleed procedure, dedicated line colour coding, material compatibility register |

**Application Conclusion:** The HAZOP study is documented in a **HAZOP worksheet**, and each deviation with a risk rank above the tolerable level receives a **recommendation**, which is tracked to closeout.

*(7 marks: 2 for the framework, 3 for the correct tabulation, 1 for the safeguard logic, 1 for the conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** *For a pressure transmitter on a reactor, the FMEA team has identified the following failure mode: "Output drifts to 105% of true value." Given S = 8, O = 3, D = 5, calculate the RPN and recommend actions if the threshold is 100. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the variables and their meaning.**
- $S = 8$ → Severity is high (8 out of 10) because a 5% high signal could mask an overpressure condition and prevent timely relief action.
- $O = 3$ → Occurrence is low (3 out of 10) — drift failures occur occasionally.
- $D = 5$ → Detection difficulty is moderate — periodic calibration can catch it within 6 months, but not immediately.

**Step 2: Apply the RPN formula.**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

$$RPN = 8 \times 3 \times 5 = 120$$

**Step 3: Compare with the action threshold.**

Since the company's action threshold is **RPN ≥ 100**, the calculated $RPN = 120$ exceeds the threshold.

**Step 4: Recommend actions.**
1. Install a **redundant pressure transmitter** with a 2-out-of-3 voting logic, reducing $O$ from 3 to 1.
2. Implement **continuous online self-diagnostics** (e.g., HART protocol with smart transmitter), reducing $D$ from 5 to 2.
3. Move calibration interval from 6 months to 3 months initially.

**Step 5: Re-compute new RPN.**

$$RPN_{new} = 8 \times 1 \times 2 = 16 \quad (\text{well below threshold, accepted})$$

*(7 marks: 2 for the formula and substitution, 1 for threshold comparison, 3 for the recommendations, 1 for the re-computation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — FTA + Bow-Tie Application** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** *A reactor cooling system has two identical cooling pumps operating in parallel. Each pump has an independent failure probability of $P = 0.04$ per demand. Using Fault Tree Analysis, calculate the system failure probability for a loss-of-cooling top event. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the top event.**
Top event $T$ = *Loss of cooling to the reactor*, which leads to an exothermic runaway.

**Step 2: Construct the logic model.**
The reactor can be cooled as long as **at least one** of the two pumps delivers flow. Therefore, the **system fails** only if **both** pumps fail to start on demand. This is an **AND gate** in the fault tree.

**Step 3: State the assumption.**
The basic events (Pump A failure, Pump B failure) are assumed **statistically independent**.

**Step 4: Apply the AND-gate probability formula.**

For two independent events $A$ and $B$:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values.**

$$P_{top} = P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.04 \times 0.04 = 0.0016 \text{ per demand}$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result.**

$$P_{top} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per demand}$$

This is **25 times lower** than a single-pump configuration ($0.04$). The improvement factor is the reciprocal of the single failure probability: $1 / 0.04 = 25$.

**Step 7: Conclude.**
The parallel redundant configuration meets a typical **SIL 2** target range ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$), provided that common-cause failures (e.g., shared power supply, shared suction header) are addressed through additional diversity in the design.

*(7 marks: 1 for top event, 1 for AND-gate justification, 1 for the independence assumption, 2 for the formula and substitution, 2 for the interpretation.)*

---

**(b)** *Construct a Bow-Tie diagram in tabular form for the top event "Storage Tank Fire". Identify two threats, two preventive barriers, two mitigative barriers, and two consequences. Explain how the barriers reduce risk. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Side of Bow-Tie | Element | Description |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Left — Threat 1** | Lightning Strike on Tank Roof | Direct ignition source of flammable vapour. |
| **Left — Threat 2** | Static Electricity During Loading | Spark during fuel transfer ignites vapour. |
| **Left — Preventive Barrier 1** | Lightning Arrester with Earth Resistance < 10 ohm | Diverts lightning current safely to ground, preventing direct strike. |
| **Left — Preventive Barrier 2** | Earthing and Bonding Clips on Loading Arm | Discharges static charge continuously below 30 V potential. |
| **Central — Top Event** | Storage Tank Roof Fire | Sustained flame on the tank roof above the liquid surface. |
| **Right — Mitigative Barrier 1** | Automatic Foam Pourer System | Applies AFFF foam at 6.5 L/min/m² to suppress the fire within 4 minutes. |
| **Right — Mitigative Barrier 2** | Emergency Shutdown (ESD) of Transfer Pumps | Stops fuel inflow to prevent escalation. |
| **Right — Consequence 1** | Pool Fire Escalation | Fire spreads to adjacent tanks if not suppressed. |
| **Right — Consequence 2** | BLEVE / Vapour Cloud Explosion | Tank shell rupture under fire exposure, catastrophic loss. |

**Explanation of barrier risk reduction:**

- Each **preventive barrier** on the left reduces the *likelihood* (probability) of the central event occurring. The lightning arrester reduces the probability of ignition from lightning by an estimated factor of $10^2$ to $10^3$, depending on the region's isokeraunic level.
- Each **mitigative barrier** on the right reduces the *severity* of the consequences *after* the central event has occurred. The foam pourer system limits the fire to the originating tank and prevents the escalation frequency from $\sim 10^{-2}$ to $\sim 10^{-4}$ per year.

**Application Insight:** In a real Bow-Tie workshop, each barrier is assigned an **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** credit (e.g., a properly tested foam system gets a PFD of $10^{-2}$), and these are summed in the LOPA study to determine the residual risk and required SIL.

*(7 marks: 1 for the top event, 2 for threats and preventive barriers, 2 for mitigative barriers and consequences, 2 for the risk-reduction explanation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hazard Identification is the FIRST step** in any risk assessment — never skip it for time.
- **HAZOP is qualitative, FTA is quantitative** — do not confuse the two in exams.
- **Guide Words in HAZOP:** NO / NONE, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN. Memorize all 7.
- **FMEA RPN Formula:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (Range: 1 to 1000; Action threshold: 100).
- **FTA Logic:**
  - **OR gate** → Output occurs if ANY input occurs → Use $P = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$.
  - **AND gate** → Output occurs only if ALL inputs occur → Use $P = \prod P_i$.
- **Bow-Tie** = Threats on the left, top event in the centre, consequences on the right, with **preventive barriers** on the left and **mitigative barriers** on the right.
- **Application Sequence in Industry:** Checklist → PHA → What-If → HAZOP → LOPA → SIL Assignment.
- **Standards to remember:** **IEC 61882** (HAZOP), **IEC 60812** (FMEA), **IEC 61025** (FTA), **IEC 61508 / 61511** (SIL), **ISO 12100** (Machinery Safety).
- **Common Mistake:** Treating HAZOP and What-If as the same — they are NOT. HAZOP is systematic and node-based; What-If is open-ended brainstorming.
- **Examiner Favourite Topics:** RPN numericals, FTA OR/AND probability, and Bow-Tie tabulation are **guaranteed** in KTU ESE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark question, **always include a small diagram or table** (even hand-drawn quality is fine). Examiners allocate up to 2 marks for visual presentation and structured tabulation. A HAZOP table, FMEA worksheet, FTA logic block, or Bow-Tie map will substantially boost your score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate by Gate Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gate by Gate Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — GATE BY GATE METHOD

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate-by-Gate Method (GGM):** A structured, stage-wise safety management and risk assessment methodology in which a project's life cycle is divided into sequential, clearly demarcated *gates* (decision checkpoints), and at each gate, a formal **Safety Review Committee (SRC)** evaluates the qualitative (and where required, quantitative) hazard status before granting clearance to proceed to the next stage.

In the context of **Process Safety Management (PSM)** under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 and KTU's *Qualitative and Quantitative Approach in Safety* framework, the Gate-by-Gate Method enforces a *no-bypass* policy: a project cannot advance to the next phase unless predefined safety deliverables — HAZID, HAZOP, QRA, SIL assessment, etc. — are satisfactorily closed.

The method applies across:
- **Greenfield** (new) project design
- **Brownfield** (modification) projects
- **Decommissioning** of process plants
- **Operational phase** major changes

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the Gate-by-Gate Method like **boarding a long-distance train at multiple checkpoint stations**. You cannot jump from the originating platform straight to the destination platform. At every station (gate), the Ticket Checker (Safety Review Committee) verifies your ticket, luggage, and credentials. If anything is missing, you are held at the station until the deficiency is resolved. Only after clearance is the next segment of journey unlocked.

**Real-world analogy — The Rocket Launch Sequence:**
A space agency does not simply "launch" a rocket. It progresses through *gate reviews*: Preliminary Design Review (PDR), Critical Design Review (CDR), Flight Readiness Review (FRR). Each gate is a hold-point that prevents catastrophic failure by ensuring every prior stage's safety criteria are *proven* (not assumed).

### 1.3 Key Performance Indicators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metrics tracked in Gate-by-Gate safety reviews:**
> - **Gate Clearance Time (GCT)** — days required to close all action items before gate sign-off
> - **Open Action Items (OAI)** — count of unresolved safety recommendations
> - **Safety Critical Element (SCE) Coverage** — percentage of identified SCEs verified at each gate
> - **Residual Risk Index (RRI)** — measured in **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) terms
> - **Bow-Tie Barrier Health Score** — qualitative indicator (typically scored **1–5**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Gate-by-Gate Method

The method operates on a **five-stage sequential logic** with embedded decision loops:

1. **Gate 1 — Conceptual Design Review (CoDR)**
   - Input: Process intent, feedstock, basic chemistry
   - Deliverables: **HAZID (Hazard Identification)** study, preliminary layout, Basis of Design (BoD)
   - Reviewer: Multi-disciplinary team + Safety Engineer
   - Outcome: Approval to proceed to Basic Engineering

2. **Gate 2 — Basic Engineering Safety Review (BESR)**
   - Input: P&IDs (Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams) at ~30 % completion
   - Deliverables: **HAZOP-1 (Node-level)**, hazardous area classification (Zone 0/1/2 for gases; Zone 20/21/22 for dusts), preliminary **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** targets per IEC 61511
   - Outcome: Approval for Detailed Engineering

3. **Gate 3 — Detailed Engineering Safety Review (DESR)**
   - Input: Final P&IDs, instrument loop diagrams, cause-&-effect matrices
   - Deliverables: **HAZOP-2 (Final)**, **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)**, **SIL Verification**, F&G (Fire & Gas) detector layout
   - Outcome: Approval for procurement and construction

4. **Gate 4 — Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**
   - Input: As-built drawings, commissioning procedures, operator training records
   - Deliverables: **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** checklist (per OSHA 1910.119), management of change (MOC) closure, mechanical completion certificates
   - Outcome: Approval for commissioning / startup

5. **Gate 5 — Operational Readiness Review (ORR)**
   - Input: Performance test results, emergency response plan validated
   - Deliverables: **Bow-Tie Analysis**, **QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)** update, **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** validation
   - Outcome: Approval for full commercial operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Iterative Loop:** If a gate *fails* (i.e., critical safety recommendations remain open), the project returns to the prior engineering phase for *rework* and re-submits to the same gate. There is **no skipping of gates** under any condition.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit / Scale | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gate Clearance Time | $GCT$ | days | Time to close all safety action items |
| Open Action Items | $OAI$ | integer (count) | Unresolved safety recommendations |
| Safety Critical Elements | $SCE$ | integer | Equipment whose failure causes immediate hazard |
| Residual Risk | $R_{res}$ | dimensionless (1–5) or freq./yr | Post-mitigation risk |
| Target Risk | $R_{T}$ | dimensionless | ALARP threshold |
| SIL Availability | $A_{SIL}$ | % | Achieved vs. required safety availability |
| PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) | $PFD_{avg}$ | dimensionless (10⁻² to 10⁻⁵) | Per IEC 61511 |
| Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF$ | dimensionless | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ |

**Key Boundary Conditions (KTU Board Favourite):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
