The question *"What is the probability the lighter will fail within the first 1,000 flicks?"* is exactly the kind of question we ask when **quantifying a basic event** for a non-repairable component.
> - The *mission time* is your 1,000 flicks.
> - The *failure rate* (λ) captures how often it fails per flick.
> - The *unreliability* Q(t) is the chance it has already failed.
> - The *reliability* R(t) is the chance it is still working.

This intuition maps directly onto non-repairable engineered systems such as:
- **Fuses** in electrical panels (one-time operation, then replacement).
- **Squibs / initiators** in airbag systems.
- **Pressure relief disks** (burst at set pressure, then replace).
- **Solid-state relays** in one-shot safety circuits.
- **Emergency stop springs** designed to fail safe.

## 1.3 Core Reliability Metrics in Bold

The following three mutually exclusive events describe the lifetime **T** of a non-repairable component:

1. **Reliability Function: $R(t) = P(T > t)$** — probability the component survives past time **t**.
2. **Unreliability Function: $Q(t) = P(T \leq t)$** — probability the component has failed by time **t**.
3. **Probability Density Function: $f(t)$** — instantaneous likelihood of failure at exactly time **t**.

Key constant: **Mission time $t_m$** (operating horizon over which the component is required to function).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For non-repairable components, $R(t) + Q(t) = 1$. This complementarity is the single most-used identity in KTU board questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logical Framework

The quantification process for non-repairable components follows a strict hierarchy:

- **Step 1 — Define the Mission Time ($t_m$):** The total operating duration demanded of the component (e.g., 10,000 hours, 100 cycles, 1 year).
- **Step 2 — Establish the Failure Distribution:** Choose a model (Exponential, Weibull, Normal, Lognormal) based on the failure mechanism. For KTU Module 2, the **Exponential Distribution** dominates, with **Weibull Distribution** as the secondary model.
- **Step 3 — Determine the Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$:** For non-repairable components, this is the *instantaneous failure rate* (also called *hazard rate* or *force of mortality*).
- **Step 4 — Compute Reliability $R(t)$** by integrating the survival probability from 0 to t.
- **Step 5 — Compute Unreliability $Q(t)$** as $1 - R(t)$.
- **Step 6 — Compute MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** as the expected value of the lifetime.

### 2.1.1 Why These Steps Matter (The "Why" Behind the Math)

- **Why a distribution?** Because component lifetimes are *random variables*. We cannot say "the fuse will fail at exactly hour 1,032." We say "the fuse has a certain *probability* of failing by hour 1,032."
- **Why $\lambda(t)$?** Because in real life, components fail at different rates over their lifetimes — early "infant mortality" failures, constant "useful life" failures, and late "wear-out" failures. The failure rate function $\lambda(t)$ captures this variation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Conditions / Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Complementarity | $R(t) + Q(t) = 1$ | Always valid; dimensionless |
| 2 | PDF from Reliability | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{dQ(t)}{dt}$ | Units: per hour (or per cycle) |
| 3 | Hazard Rate Definition | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Units: failures/hour |
| 4 | Reliability from Hazard | $R(t) = \exp\left[-\displaystyle\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$ | Always valid |
| 5 | Unreliability | $Q(t) = 1 - \exp\left[-\displaystyle\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$ | Always valid |
| 6 | Exponential Distribution (constant $\lambda$) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Requires constant failure rate |
| 7 | Exponential Unreliability | $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Valid for $\lambda t \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Exponential PDF | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Valid for $t \geq 0$ |
| 9 | MTTF (Exponential) | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Units: hours (same as time) |
| 10 | Weibull Distribution | $R(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | Shape $\beta$, scale $\eta$ |
| 11 | Weibull Hazard | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Increasing if $\beta \gt 1$ |
| 12 | Weibull MTTF | $MTTF = \eta \,\Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | $\Gamma$ is the gamma function |
| 13 | Approximation (small $\lambda t$) | $Q(t) \approx \lambda t$ | Valid when $\lambda t \leq 0.1$ |
| 14 | Mission Time Multiplier | $R(n \cdot t) = [R(t)]^n$ | Independent, identical intervals |
| 15 | Frequency of Failure | $w = \lambda \cdot R(t)$ | Failures per hour (asymptotic: $\lambda$) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, all absolute-value and conditional notations are written using $\vert$ for clarity within markdown tables. For example, $\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ denotes the gamma function evaluated at the argument $(1 + 1/\beta)$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The quantification of basic events for non-repairable components is the **mathematical backbone** of:

- **Nuclear Power Plant Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA):** quantifying the unavailability of safety injection pumps, circuit breakers, etc.
- **Aerospace Reliability Engineering:** computing single-point failure probabilities for one-shot pyrotechnic devices.
- **Automotive Functional Safety (ISO 26262):** computing the Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH) and Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) of electronic control units.
- **Oil & Gas Safety Instrumented Systems (IEC 61511):** computing the average probability of failure on demand (PFDavg) for a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability from the Failure Rate Function

We start with the **conditional probability** that a component which has survived up to time $t$ will fail in the next infinitesimal interval $dt$:

$$P(t < T \leq t + dt \mid T > t) = \lambda(t) \, dt$$

This is the *operational definition* of the hazard rate $\lambda(t)$. We can rewrite this conditional probability as a ratio of unconditional probabilities:

$$P(t < T \leq t + dt \mid T > t) = \frac{P(t < T \leq t + dt)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{f(t) \, dt}{R(t)}$$

Equating the two expressions gives us the **fundamental reliability differential equation**:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t) \, dt$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$ and applying the initial condition $R(0) = 1$ (a brand-new non-repairable component is operational at time zero):

$$\int_0^t \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right]$$

> This is the **most general reliability expression** for any non-repairable component. Every other KTU formula is a special case of this equation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Exponential Distribution (Constant Failure Rate)

For a non-repairable component in its **useful life phase** (the flat region of the bath-tub curve), the failure rate is **constant**:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$$

Substituting this into the general reliability equation:

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_0^t \lambda \, d\tau\right] = \exp[-\lambda t]$$

The unreliability follows by complementarity:

$$Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

The probability density function is obtained by differentiating $Q(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$f(t) = \frac{dQ(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property of the Exponential Distribution:**
> For a non-repairable component with constant $\lambda$, the conditional probability of surviving an additional time $s$, given survival to time $t$, is independent of $t$:
> $$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = e^{-\lambda s}$$
> This is why the exponential model is so widely used in KTU board problems — it has a single parameter $\lambda$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) for the Exponential

The expected lifetime is the first moment of the PDF:

$$MTTF = E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Using integration by parts (let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$):

$$MTTF = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} + \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Evaluating the boundary term: as $t \to \infty$, the term $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential decay dominates polynomial growth), and at $t = 0$ the term is zero. Hence:

$$MTTF = 0 + \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Weibull Distribution as a Generalization

The Weibull distribution is obtained by allowing the failure rate to follow a power law in time:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

where $\beta > 0$ is the **shape parameter** and $\eta > 0$ is the **scale parameter**. Substituting into the general reliability equation:

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_0^t \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{\tau}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} d\tau\right]$$

Carrying out the integral (using the substitution $u = \tau/\eta$):

$$\int_0^t \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{\tau}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} d\tau = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$

Therefore:

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

The unreliability and MTTF follow directly:

$$Q(t) = 1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

$$MTTF = \eta \, \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case Check:** When $\beta = 1$, the Weibull reduces to the exponential with $\lambda = 1/\eta$. This is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Distribution Selection Guide

| Failure Behavior | Shape Parameter $\beta$ | Recommended Distribution | Why? |
|------------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|------|
| Constant failure rate | $\beta = 1$ | Exponential | Memoryless, well-understood |
| Increasing failure rate (wear-out) | $\beta > 1$ | Weibull (with $\beta \gt 1$) | Captures aging effects |
| Decreasing failure rate (infant mortality) | $\beta < 1$ | Weibull (with $\beta \lt 1$) | Captures early-life burn-in |
| Symmetric, life dominated by central value | $\beta \approx 3.4$ | Weibull ≈ Normal | Mechanical wear-out, narrow spread |
| Repair time / heavy-tailed | $\beta < 1$ | Lognormal | Maintenance intervals |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A non-repairable pressure switch has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. Compute the reliability, unreliability, and MTTF for a mission time of $t = 10,000$ hours.

**Solution:**

$$R(10{,}000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-6})(10{,}000)} = e^{-0.05}$$

$$R(10{,}000) = 0.9512$$

$$Q(10{,}000) = 1 - 0.9512 = 0.0488$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 200{,}000 \text{ hours} \approx 22.8 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The unreliability is approximately 4.88%, meaning the pressure switch has roughly a 1-in-20 chance of failing within the 10,000-hour mission.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Basic Event Quantification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Non-Repairable Component] --> B[Define Mission Time t subscript m]
    B --> C{Choose Failure Distribution}
    C -->|Constant Hazard| D[Exponential Model]
    C -->|Time-Varying Hazard| E[Weibull Model]
    D --> F[Apply lambda constant in R of t equals exp of minus lambda t]
    E --> G[Apply beta and eta in R of t equals exp of minus t over eta to the beta]
    F --> H[Compute Unreliability Q of t equals 1 minus R of t]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute PDF f of t equals lambda times R of t]
    I --> J[Compute MTTF equals 1 over lambda or eta Gamma]
    J --> K[Validate using Bath-Tub Curve Region Check]
    K --> L[Report Basic Event Probability to Fault Tree]
```

## 4.2 The Bath-Tub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Component Life

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infant Mortality Region] --> B[Useful Life Constant Lambda Region]
    B --> C[Wear-Out Region]
    A --> A1[Lambda decreasing t small Beta less than 1]
    B --> B1[Lambda constant Exponential Model]
    C --> C1[Lambda increasing t large Beta greater than 1]
```

## 4.3 Logical Decision Tree — Distribution Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Component Type] --> Q1{Is Hazard Rate Constant?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| ExpDist[Use Exponential Distribution]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does Hazard Increase with Time?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| WB1[Use Weibull with Beta greater than 1]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Does Hazard Decrease with Time?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| WB2[Use Weibull with Beta less than 1]
    Q3 -->|No| NorDist[Use Normal or Lognormal]
    ExpDist --> Out[Quantify Basic Event]
    WB1 --> Out
    WB2 --> Out
    NorDist --> Out
```

## 4.4 Mapping Quantification Output to Fault Tree Boolean Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BE1[Basic Event 1 P equals 1 minus exp of minus lambda 1 t] --> OR1[OR Gate]
    BE2[Basic Event 2 P equals 1 minus exp of minus lambda 2 t] --> OR1
    BE3[Basic Event 3 P equals 1 minus exp of minus lambda 3 t] --> OR1
    OR1 --> TOP[Top Event Probability Q top]
    BE4[Basic Event 4 P equals 1 minus exp of minus lambda 4 t] --> AND1[AND Gate]
    BE5[Basic Event 5 P equals 1 minus exp of minus lambda 5 t] --> AND1
    AND1 --> TOP
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the unreliability function $Q(t)$ for a non-repairable component and state the fundamental relationship between $R(t)$ and $Q(t)$. Why is the unreliability the appropriate metric for fault tree quantification rather than the failure rate alone?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **unreliability function** $Q(t)$ is the probability that a non-repairable component has failed by mission time $t$:

$$Q(t) = P(T \leq t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{(for constant } \lambda \text{)}$$

The **fundamental relationship** is the complementarity identity:

$$R(t) + Q(t) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q(t) = 1 - R(t)$$

**Why $Q(t)$ for FTA?** A fault tree's top event represents a *failed state* over the mission. We need the *probability of being in the failed state by time $t$*, not the *rate* at which failures occur. The failure rate $\lambda$ is a *differential* quantity (failures per hour), while $Q(t)$ is a *cumulative* probability (chance of being failed), which is what AND/OR gate algebra requires for the top event. Hence, $Q(t)$ is integrated over the mission time and represents the actual likelihood of failure manifestation in the fault tree. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*With the help of the reliability expression $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, show that the exponential distribution is the appropriate model for a non-repairable component operating in the useful-life phase of the bath-tub curve.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In the **useful-life phase** of the bath-tub curve, the component has:
1. Passed beyond infant mortality (no early failures),
2. Not yet entered the wear-out phase (no aging-related failures).

During this flat middle region, the **failure rate is approximately constant**: $\lambda(t) = \lambda$.

Substituting a constant $\lambda$ into the general reliability equation:

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_0^t \lambda \, d\tau\right] = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This yields the **exponential distribution**, characterized by:
- Constant failure rate (memoryless property),
- Single parameter $\lambda$ — easy to obtain from field failure data,
- Closed-form solutions for $R(t)$, $Q(t)$, $f(t)$, and MTTF.

Therefore, the exponential model is the **canonical KTU choice** for non-repairable components in their useful-life phase. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Exponential Quantification with Mission Time Aggregation

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A safety-critical non-repairable fuse is rated for a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ failures per hour. The system is required to operate continuously for a mission time of $t_m = 5,000$ hours. Compute the reliability $R(t_m)$, unreliability $Q(t_m)$, probability density $f(t_m)$, and the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of the fuse. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The same fuse is now required to operate over a mission consisting of **three independent, identical sub-missions** of 5,000 hours each, with the fuse being replaced (with a fresh unit) at the start of each sub-mission. Compute the system reliability over the entire 15,000-hour aggregated mission and comment on the engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Single-Mission Quantification (7 Marks)**

Given: $\lambda = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ /hr, $t_m = 5{,}000$ hr.

**Reliability:**

$$R(5{,}000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(3 \times 10^{-5})(5{,}000)} = e^{-0.15}$$

$$R(5{,}000) = 0.8607$$

**Unreliability:**

$$Q(5{,}000) = 1 - 0.8607 = 0.1393 \;\; (\approx 13.93\%)$$

**Probability Density Function at $t_m$:**

$$f(5{,}000) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t} = (3 \times 10^{-5})(0.8607) = 2.582 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{per hour}$$

**MTTF:**

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-5}} = 33{,}333.33 \;\; \text{hours} \;\; (\approx 3.8 \text{ years})$$

> The fuse has an approximately 14% chance of failing within a 5,000-hour mission. The expected lifetime is ~33,333 hours, well above the mission time, indicating the fuse is fit for purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Multi-Mission Aggregated Reliability (7 Marks)**

When the fuse is replaced with a fresh unit at the start of each sub-mission, the **sub-missions are independent and identically distributed**. The aggregated system reliability over $n$ identical sub-missions is:

$$R_{sys} = [R(t_{sub})]^n$$

Here, $n = 3$ and $t_{sub} = 5{,}000$ hours. Substituting:

$$R_{sys} = [0.8607]^3 = 0.6375$$

The system unreliability over the 15,000-hour mission is:

$$Q_{sys} = 1 - 0.6375 = 0.3625 \;\; (\approx 36.25\%)$$

> **Engineering Implication:** Although each sub-mission is identical and the fuse is "renewed" each time, the system unreliability **grows non-linearly** with the number of sub-missions. After 3 missions, the cumulative chance of at least one fuse failure reaches 36.25%. This underscores the principle that *replacement of non-repairable components resets the component's clock, but not the system's cumulative exposure profile.* For safety-critical systems, this drives the need for redundant channels, periodic proof tests, and conservative design margins. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Weibull Quantification and Distribution Comparison

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** A non-repairable mechanical seal used in a chemical reactor has a Weibull failure distribution with shape parameter $\beta = 2.5$ and scale parameter $\eta = 8,000$ hours. Compute the reliability, unreliability, hazard rate, and MTTF of the seal at mission time $t_m = 4,000$ hours. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the use of the **Weibull distribution** versus the **Exponential distribution** for quantifying basic events of non-repairable components. Under what conditions should each be preferred? Provide a specific example for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Weibull Quantification (7 Marks)**

Given: $\beta = 2.5$, $\eta = 8{,}000$ hr, $t_m = 4{,}000$ hr.

**Reliability (Weibull):**

$$R(4{,}000) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right] = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{4{,}000}{8{,}000}\right)^{2.5}\right]$$

$$R(4{,}000) = \exp\left[-(0.5)^{2.5}\right] = \exp[-(0.17678)] = 0.8379$$

**Unreliability:**

$$Q(4{,}000) = 1 - 0.8379 = 0.1621$$

**Hazard Rate at $t_m$:**

$$\lambda(4{,}000) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} = \frac{2.5}{8{,}000}\left(\frac{4{,}000}{8{,}000}\right)^{1.5}$$

$$\lambda(4{,}000) = (3.125 \times 10^{-4}) \times (0.35355) = 1.105 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{per hour}$$

**MTTF (using Gamma function):**

$$MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 8{,}000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)$$

Using the standard gamma function value $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8873$:

$$MTTF = 8{,}000 \times 0.8873 = 7{,}098.5 \;\; \text{hours}$$

> The seal has a 16.21% chance of failing by 4,000 hours, an expected lifetime of ~7,099 hours, and a *time-dependent* hazard rate that is *increasing* (since $\beta = 2.5 > 1$), confirming the wear-out regime. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Distribution Comparison (7 Marks)**

| Feature | Exponential | Weibull |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ | Constant | Time-dependent (power law) |
| Number of parameters | 1 ($\lambda$) | 2 ($\beta, \eta$) |
| Memoryless? | Yes | No (in general) |
| Reduces to exponential? | — | When $\beta = 1$ |
| Useful-life phase? | Excellent fit | General fit (any $\beta$) |
| Wear-out phase? | Poor fit | Excellent fit ($\beta > 1$) |
| Infant mortality? | Poor fit | Good fit ($\beta < 1$) |
| Data requirements | Low | Moderate to high |

**When to use Exponential:**
- **Condition:** Failure rate is approximately constant over the mission.
- **Example:** An electronic component in its useful life (e.g., a solid-state relay in continuous operation). The exponential's simplicity makes it the KTU default when only a single failure rate $\lambda$ is available.

**When to use Weibull:**
- **Condition:** Failure rate varies with time (wear-out, infant mortality, or general trend).
- **Example:** A mechanical bearing with $\beta > 1$ showing increasing wear-out, or a semiconductor with $\beta < 1$ showing early-life burn-in. The Weibull's flexibility makes it the preferred choice when field data is rich enough to estimate $\beta$ and $\eta$.

> **Engineering takeaway:** The exponential is a *special case* of the Weibull with $\beta = 1$. The KTU expects students to recognize when the simple exponential is justified and when the more flexible Weibull is required for accurate fault tree quantification. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the complementarity rule:** Writing $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $Q(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ *simultaneously* in the same solution. $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$, not $e^{-\lambda t}$.
> 2. **Unit confusion in MTTF:** Writing MTTF $= \lambda$ instead of $1/\lambda$. The units of MTTF are *hours*, not per hour.
> 3. **Ignoring the constant-failure-rate assumption:** Applying the exponential model to a wear-out phase (e.g., $\beta > 1$) and getting a non-physical answer. Always state the assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Misapplying Weibull's gamma function:** Using $\Gamma(\beta)$ instead of $\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ in the MTTF formula. The argument is $1 + 1/\beta$, not $\beta$.
> 5. **Skipping the small-$\lambda t$ approximation note:** For $Q(t) \approx \lambda t$, the validity condition $\lambda t \leq 0.1$ must be stated.
> 6. **In multi-mission problems, using $R_{sys} = R(t)$ directly instead of $[R(t)]^n$.** The exponent $n$ for $n$ independent sub-missions is frequently missed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Non-repairable component** = discarded or replaced upon failure (not restored).
- [x] **Reliability $R(t) = P(T > t)$**, **Unreliability $Q(t) = P(T \leq t)$**, and $R(t) + Q(t) = 1$.
- [x] **Hazard rate** $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ is the *instantaneous* failure rate, not the cumulative failure probability.
- [x] **General reliability formula:** $R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) d\tau\right]$ — always valid.
- [x] **Exponential model** = constant $\lambda$ → $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$, $MTTF = 1/\lambda$.
- [x] **Memoryless property** of the exponential: $P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = e^{-\lambda s}$.
- [x] **Weibull model** = $\lambda(t) = (\beta/\eta)(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$, $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta)^{\beta}]$, $MTTF = \eta\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- [x] **Special case:** Weibull with $\beta = 1$ reduces to the exponential with $\lambda = 1/\eta$.
- [x] **Small-$\lambda t$ approximation:** $Q(t) \approx \lambda t$ when $\lambda t \leq 0.1$.
- [x] **Multi-mission rule:** $R_{sys}(n \cdot t) = [R(t)]^n$ for $n$ independent, identical sub-missions with component renewal.
- [x] **Bath-tub curve:** Use exponential in the useful-life (flat) region, Weibull with $\beta > 1$ in wear-out, Weibull with $\beta < 1$ in infant mortality.
- [x] **PDF relation:** $f(t) = -dR/dt = dQ/dt = \lambda(t) R(t)$.
- [x] **Units check:** $\lambda$ is in *per hour*, $t$ is in *hours*, MTTF is in *hours*, $R$ and $Q$ are dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATITIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hazard Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Reliability Engineering** and **Probabilistic Safety Analysis**, the **Hazard Rate** (also termed the **Instantaneous Failure Rate**, **Force of Mortality**, or **Hazard Function**) is a foundational metric that quantifies the conditional probability of a component, system, or entity experiencing failure within an infinitesimally small time interval $\Delta t$, *given that it has survived up to the beginning of that interval*.

Mathematically, the hazard rate $h(t)$ is formally expressed as a limit definition:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}$$

Where $T$ denotes the random variable representing the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** of the component or system under observation. The hazard rate is typically expressed in units of **failures per unit time** (e.g., **failures/hour**, **failures/cycle**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The hazard rate function is the cornerstone for characterizing the dynamic risk profile of engineering systems across their operational life cycle. It is the mathematical link between *failure density* and *residual reliability*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Survivor at Risk" Concept

Imagine you are an **insurance actuary** studying the mortality of a cohort of 1,000 identical light bulbs installed at time $t = 0$.

- The **Reliability Function** $R(t)$ tells you: *"How many bulbs are still glowing at time $t$?"* — This is the *total survivors* in the cohort.
- The **Failure Density Function** $f(t)$ tells you: *"How many bulbs are failing *in total* around time $t$?"* — This counts the dead, including those who died long ago and just got "noticed."
- The **Hazard Rate** $h(t)$ tells you something far more pointed: *"Of the bulbs that are STILL ALIVE at time $t$, what fraction is about to die in the next instant?"* — It is the **risk faced by a survivor**, not a historical average.

### Real-World Engineering Analogy: The Highway Tunnel

Think of a long road tunnel with **5,000 vehicles entering** every day.
- The **reliability** $R(t)$ = the number of cars that have *successfully* passed through without incident by day $t$.
- The **hazard rate** $h(t)$ = for a car that has *already* made it safely to kilometer 4 of the tunnel, what is the instantaneous probability of an accident in the next 100 meters?

This conditional "risk-among-the-survivors" framing is precisely what makes the hazard rate the **dominant tool in safety analytics** for time-dependent risk.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbol | Standard Name | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $h(t)$ | Hazard Rate / Failure Rate | Instantaneous conditional failure rate at time $t$ |
| $R(t)$ | Reliability / Survival Function | Probability of survival up to time $t$ |
| $F(t)$ | Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) | Cumulative probability of failure by time $t$ |
| $f(t)$ | Probability Density Function (PDF) | Unconditional failure density at time $t$ |
| $H(t)$ | Cumulative Hazard Function | Total accumulated hazard up to time $t$ |
| $MTTF$ | Mean Time To Failure | Expected operational life before failure |
| $\lambda$ | Constant Failure Rate | Special case where $h(t) = \lambda = \text{const}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU safety curriculum, students must recognize that the hazard rate is **NOT** a probability (it can exceed 1) — it is a *rate* with units of inverse time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Operational Logic — Why and How

The hazard rate is derived from a fundamental **conditional probability** argument. Here is the step-by-step logical breakdown:

### Step 1: Establish the Survival Function
The **Reliability Function** is the probability that a system survives past time $t$:

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$

This forms the *denominator* in the conditional probability calculus.

### Step 2: Identify the Conditional Failure Probability
The probability that a system fails in the small window $[t, t+\Delta t]$, *given survival to $t$*:

$$P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t \mid T \geq t) = \frac{P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t)}{P(T \geq t)} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

### Step 3: Normalize by the Time Interval
Dividing by $\Delta t$ converts the probability into a *rate per unit time*:

$$\text{Finite Failure Rate} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)}$$

### Step 4: Take the Limit as $\Delta t \to 0$
As the window shrinks to an instant, the finite rate converges to the **instantaneous hazard rate**:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

This is the **operational master equation** of the hazard rate.

### Step 5: The Differential Form
Using $f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (since $F(t) + R(t) = 1$), we get an alternate form:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]$$

### Step 6: The Cumulative Hazard & Inversion
Integrating yields the **Cumulative Hazard Function**:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du = -\ln R(t)$$

This can be **inverted** to express reliability entirely in terms of the hazard rate — the cornerstone of reliability prediction:

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du\right)$$

## 2.2 The Three Behavioral Regimes of $h(t)$

The shape of the hazard rate across the life cycle gives rise to the famous **Bathtub Curve** of reliability engineering — a *must-know* for KTU Module 2:

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Decreasing $h(t)$):** Early-life failures due to manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, or substandard materials. Burn-in testing is used to weed out these weak units.
2. **Useful Life Region (Constant $h(t) = \lambda$):** Random, memoryless failures dominate. The **Exponential Distribution** governs this regime: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
3. **Wear-Out Region (Increasing $h(t)$):** Aging-induced failures from fatigue, corrosion, wear, and degradation. The **Weibull Distribution** (with shape parameter $\beta > 1$) models this regime.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Cheat Sheet — Hazard Rate Relations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Master definition (conditional density form) | failures/time |
| 2 | $h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$ | Logarithmic derivative form | failures/time |
| 3 | $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du$ | Cumulative hazard definition | dimensionless |
| 4 | $H(t) = -\ln R(t)$ | Logarithmic inversion | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ | Reliability from cumulative hazard | probability |
| 6 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential (constant $\lambda$) | probability |
| 7 | $h(t) = \lambda$ | Constant failure rate (memoryless) | failures/time |
| 8 | $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Weibull hazard rate | failures/time |
| 9 | $h(t) = \frac{\lambda \cdot t^{\lambda-1}}{\alpha}$ | Power-law (non-parametric) | failures/time |
| 10 | $MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ | Mean Time To Failure | time |
| 11 | $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | MTTF for exponential | time |
| 12 | $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$ | Density in terms of hazard | density |

**Critical Convention:** All $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation in derivations uses $\vert$ without spaces to preserve table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hazard rate is the **lingua franca** of quantitative safety in domains such as:
- **Nuclear Reactor Safety** (reliability block diagrams for emergency core cooling).
- **Aerospace** (Mean Time Between Failures — MTBF — calculations for avionics).
- **Automotive ISO 26262** (ASIL-rated electronic control units).
- **Pharmaceutical Shelf-Life** (Weibull modeling of drug degradation).
- **Public Health Epidemiology** (Cox Proportional Hazards Model for survival analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's safety curriculum, the hazard rate is the analytical bridge that transforms *historical failure data* into *forward-looking risk predictions*, enabling **proactive safety** rather than reactive accident response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Conditional Probability to $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$

We derive the master formula rigorously from first principles.

**Step 1.** Begin with the conditional probability that a failure occurs in $[t, t+\Delta t]$, given survival up to $t$:

$$P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t \mid T \geq t)$$

**Step 2.** Apply the definition of conditional probability $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B)/P(B)$. Since $\{t \leq T < t+\Delta t\}$ is a subset of $\{T \geq t\}$, their intersection is just $\{t \leq T < t+\Delta t\}$:

$$= \frac{P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t)}{P(T \geq t)}$$

**Step 3.** Express the numerator using the CDF: $P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t) = F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)$, and the denominator as the reliability: $P(T \geq t) = R(t)$:

$$= \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4.** Divide by $\Delta t$ to get the *average* rate over the interval:

$$\text{Avg Rate} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)}$$

**Step 5.** Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$. The numerator becomes the derivative of $F(t)$ (which is $f(t)$), and the denominator's $R(t)$ is unchanged:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Logarithmic Form $h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$

**Step 1.** Start from the master equation and substitute $f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt}$ (since $R + F = 1$ implies $\frac{dR}{dt} + \frac{dF}{dt} = 0$, so $\frac{dR}{dt} = -f(t)$):

$$h(t) = \frac{-dR/dt}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2.** Recognize the right-hand side as the derivative of the natural logarithm (by the chain rule, $\frac{d}{dt}\ln R = \frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{dt}$):

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]$$

**Step 3.** This differential form is *separable*. Multiply both sides by $dt$ and integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d}{du}\ln R(u) \, du = -\left[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\right]$$

**Step 4.** Apply the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (a brand-new system is initially reliable with probability 1), so $\ln R(0) = 0$:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 5.** Exponentiate to invert for $R(t)$:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du\right)}$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** between reliability and hazard rate.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for Exponential Hazard

**Step 1.** For the constant hazard regime $h(t) = \lambda$, the cumulative hazard is:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, du = \lambda t$$

**Step 2.** Hence the reliability is the **Exponential Survival Function**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3.** The MTTF is defined as the expected value of the failure time:

$$MTTF = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4.** Use the alternative form (valid for non-negative random variables):

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 6.** Evaluate the integral using the standard result $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} dt = 1/a$ for $a > 0$:

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

This shows that **the higher the hazard rate, the shorter the expected life** — an intuitive and exam-ready result.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hazard Rate of Common Distributions

| Distribution | PDF $f(t)$ | Reliability $R(t)$ | Hazard Rate $h(t)$ | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exponential | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ (constant) | Memoryless |
| Weibull ($\beta, \eta$) | $\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | $e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | $\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\beta < 1$: decreasing; $\beta = 1$: constant; $\beta > 1$: increasing |
| Normal ($\mu, \sigma$) | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(t-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $1 - \Phi\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\frac{\phi\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)}{\sigma \left[1-\Phi\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]}$ | Increasing (for $t > \mu$) |
| Lognormal | $\frac{1}{t\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(\ln t - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $1 - \Phi\left(\frac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Complex, increases then decreases | Unimodal hazard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern):** A pressure sensor has an exponential failure distribution with $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. Find the reliability at $t = 500$ hours and the MTTF.
>
> - $R(500) = e^{-0.001 \times 500} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$ (≈ **60.65%**)
> - $MTTF = 1/0.001 = 1000$ hours
> - Hazard at 500 hrs: $h(500) = 0.001$ failures/hour (constant)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Hazard Rate Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the **interactions** between the core reliability functions and how the hazard rate serves as the central analytical hub.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CDF F of t"] -->|Differentiate| B["PDF f of t"]
    C["Reliability R of t"] -->|dF plus dR equals 0| A
    B -->|Divide by R| D["Hazard Rate h of t"]
    C -->|Divide| D
    D -->|Integrate over 0 to t| E["Cumulative Hazard H of t"]
    E -->|Exponentiate with negative sign| C
    D -->|Integrate over 0 to infinity| F["MTTF Expected Life"]
    G["Constant Lambda"] -->|Special case| D
    H["Weibull Beta and Eta"] -->|General case| D
    G -->|Invert| F
```

## 4.2 The Bathtub Curve — Hazard Rate Across System Life Cycle

The hazard rate is **not constant** over a system's life. The classic "bathtub" profile is composed of three regions:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INF["INFANT MORTALITY"]
        A1["h decreasing"] --> A2["Defects and workmanship"]
    end
    subgraph USE["USEFUL LIFE"]
        B1["h constant equals lambda"] --> B2["Random memoryless failures"]
    end
    subgraph WEAR["WEAR OUT"]
        C1["h increasing"] --> C2["Fatigue corrosion aging"]
    end
    INF --> USE
    USE --> WEAR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When asked to *sketch and label* the bathtub curve in exams, always mark the three regions, indicate the typical time boundaries (e.g., burn-in period, useful life, wear-out), and identify the governing distribution in each region.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Distribution to Use?

A flowchart for selecting the appropriate hazard model based on observed system behavior:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start Observed Failure Data"] --> S1{"Hazard Rate Behavior"}
    S1 -->|Constant over time| S2["Exponential Distribution"]
    S1 -->|Decreasing then constant| S3["Weibull Beta less than 1 then equals 1"]
    S1 -->|Monotonically increasing| S4["Weibull Beta greater than 1"]
    S1 -->|Increases then decreases| S5["Lognormal Distribution"]
    S2 --> S6["Memoryless Property applies"]
    S3 --> S7["Infant mortality dominant"]
    S4 --> S8["Wear out dominant"]
    S5 --> S9["Probabilistic fatigue model"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Board Diagrams:** Use clean rectangular/oval node labels in Mermaid — avoid embedding bold, italics, or HTML tables inside node labels to ensure smooth rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

### Q1. Define the term *Hazard Rate*. How is it mathematically related to the Reliability Function $R(t)$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The hazard rate $h(t)$ is the **instantaneous failure rate** of a system at time $t$, conditional on its survival up to that time. It represents the conditional probability density of failure in the next instant, given survival.

The two key mathematical relationships are:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} \quad \text{and} \quad h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$$

From the second form, integrating gives $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$, where $H(t) = \int_0^t h(u)\,du$ is the cumulative hazard. **(3 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Q2. What is meant by the *Constant Failure Rate* region in the bathtub curve? State the corresponding reliability function and identify the distribution that governs it.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The constant failure rate region represents the **useful life phase** of a system where failures occur randomly with no memory of prior operation. The hazard rate is constant: $h(t) = \lambda$.

The governing distribution is the **Exponential Distribution**, with reliability function:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The defining property is **memorylessness**: $P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A Safety Study on Hydraulic Pump Reliability**

A hydraulic pump in an industrial press has been observed to fail in accordance with a **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta = 2.5$ and scale parameter $\eta = 800$ hours.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Derive the general expression for the Weibull **hazard rate** $h(t)$ and compute its value at $t = 400$ hours for the given pump.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

Compute the **reliability** $R(400)$ and the **cumulative hazard** $H(400)$ for the pump, and comment on what these metrics imply for the plant's preventive maintenance schedule.

---

**Model Solution A:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1.** The Weibull reliability function is:

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$$

**Step 2.** The Weibull PDF is obtained by differentiating $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$$

**Step 3.** The hazard rate is $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$. The exponential terms cancel, leaving:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $\beta = 2.5$, $\eta = 800$:

$$h(400) = \frac{2.5}{800}\left(\frac{400}{800}\right)^{2.5-1} = \frac{2.5}{800} \times (0.5)^{1.5}$$

**Step 5.** Compute $(0.5)^{1.5} = 0.5 \times \sqrt{0.5} = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536$:

$$h(400) = \frac{2.5}{800} \times 0.3536 = 0.003125 \times 0.3536 = 0.001105 \text{ failures/hour}$$

$$\boxed{h(400) \approx 1.105 \times 10^{-3} \text{ failures/hour}}$$ **(7 Marks — 1 for derivation, 1 for formula, 3 for substitution, 2 for final answer with units)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1.** Compute the exponent term:

$$\left(\frac{400}{800}\right)^{2.5} = (0.5)^{2.5}$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate $(0.5)^{2.5}$:
- $(0.5)^2 = 0.25$
- $(0.5)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.5} = 0.7071$
- Product: $0.25 \times 0.7071 = 0.1768$

**Step 3.** Reliability:

$$R(400) = e^{-0.1768} = 0.8380 \approx 83.80\%$$

**Step 4.** Cumulative hazard:

$$H(400) = -\ln R(400) = -\ln(0.8380) = 0.1768$$

(Equivalently, $H(400) = (400/800)^{2.5} = 0.1768$, confirming the result.)

**Step 5.** Maintenance Implications:

- **83.8% survival** at 400 hours is *acceptable* for continued operation, but the increasing hazard (since $\beta = 2.5 > 1$) signals **imminent wear-out**.
- The cumulative hazard of $0.1768$ is approaching the threshold where maintenance intervention becomes cost-effective (typically $H(t) \approx 0.2$–$0.5$).
- **Recommendation:** Schedule preventive replacement **before $t \approx 600$ hours**, where $R(600) = e^{-(0.75)^{2.5}} = e^{-0.4871} = 0.6145$ (61.5% — well into the wear-out region). **(7 Marks — 2 for reliability, 2 for cumulative hazard, 3 for engineering interpretation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Time-Dependent Failure Analysis of a Reactor Coolant Sensor**

The failure time $T$ of a reactor coolant temperature sensor follows an **Exponential distribution** with constant hazard rate $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Derive the **reliability function** $R(t)$ and the **probability density function** $f(t)$ of the sensor. Compute the probability that the sensor survives beyond $t = 2000$ hours.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

Compute the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**, the **cumulative hazard** $H(3000)$, and state the **reliability** of the sensor at $t = 3000$ hours. Interpret these values in the context of a 6-monthly (≈ 4380 hours) plant shutdown inspection cycle.

---

**Model Solution B:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1.** For constant hazard $h(t) = \lambda = 0.0005$, the cumulative hazard is $H(t) = \lambda t$.

**Step 2.** Reliability function:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0005 t}$$

**Step 3.** The PDF is $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$f(t) = 0.0005 \, e^{-0.0005 t}$$

**Step 4.** Probability of survival beyond 2000 hours:

$$R(2000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 2000} = e^{-1} = 0.3679 \approx 36.79\%$$ **(7 Marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1.** MTTF for exponential:

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2.** Cumulative hazard at 3000 hours:

$$H(3000) = \lambda \times 3000 = 0.0005 \times 3000 = 1.5$$

**Step 3.** Reliability at 3000 hours:

$$R(3000) = e^{-H(3000)} = e^{-1.5} = 0.2231 \approx 22.31\%$$

**Step 4.** Interpretation for the 4380-hour shutdown cycle:

- MTTF = 2000 hours is **substantially less** than the 4380-hour inspection interval — the sensor is expected to fail, on average, **more than twice per inspection cycle**.
- $H(3000) = 1.5$ indicates **1.5 expected failures** per sensor by the 3000-hour mark.
- $R(3000) \approx 22.3\%$ means there is a **77.7% probability** that at least one sensor has already failed before the next scheduled shutdown.
- **Safety Recommendation:** Reduce the inspection interval to **≤ 1500 hours** (i.e., quarterly) to keep $R(t) \geq 47\%$ at the time of inspection, or install **redundant sensors** in a 1-out-of-2 voting configuration. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Hazard Rate Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always state the hazard rate in *failures per unit time*; a bare number loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $h(t)$ with $f(t)$** — $f(t)$ is the unconditional density (counts all dead systems, past and present), while $h(t)$ is the conditional rate *among survivors*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Misapplying the bathtub curve** — In Part A questions, students often draw a *U-shape* and label only two regions. You must show **all three** (infant mortality, useful life, wear-out) and identify the governing distribution in each.
> 4. **Dropping the conditional logic** — When deriving $h(t)$, students frequently write $P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t) / \Delta t$ without the denominator $R(t)$, yielding $f(t)$ instead of $h(t)$. The conditional nature is the entire point of the question.
> 5. **Ignoring the constant rate assumption** — In exponential problems, students sometimes differentiate $R(t)$ but write $h(t) = -\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ (which is $f(t)$, not $h(t)$). Constant hazard = $\lambda$, *not* $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hazard Rate (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Instantaneous conditional failure rate at time $t$, given survival to $t$.
- **Master Formula:** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$.
- **Cumulative Hazard:** $H(t) = \int_0^t h(u)\,du = -\ln R(t)$.
- **Reliability Inversion:** $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ — *the most powerful single equation* in reliability.
- **Constant Hazard ⇒ Exponential Distribution** ⇒ $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $MTTF = 1/\lambda$, *memoryless* property.
- **Weibull Hazard:** $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$; $\beta < 1$ (decreasing, infant mortality), $\beta = 1$ (exponential), $\beta > 1$ (increasing, wear-out).
- **Units:** $h(t)$ is in *failures per unit time*; it is **not** a probability and can exceed 1.
- **Bathtub Curve:** Three regions — *infant mortality* (decreasing), *useful life* (constant), *wear-out* (increasing).
- **Engineering Use:** Forms the basis for **preventive maintenance scheduling**, **reliability block diagrams**, **SIL calculations** (IEC 61508/61511), and **failure mode analysis** in safety-critical systems.
- **MTTF Relationship:** $MTTF = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt = 1/\lambda$ for exponential models.
- **Always state units** and **always distinguish** between conditional ($h(t)$) and unconditional ($f(t)$) failure metrics in KTU board answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exponential Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exponential Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Exponential Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution that models the time elapsed between two successive events in a Poisson process, which occur independently and at a constant average rate. It is the most widely used distribution in **reliability engineering, safety analysis, and equipment life testing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow an Exponential Distribution with parameter $\lambda > 0$ if its Probability Density Function (PDF) is given by:
> 
> $$f(x) = \begin{cases} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, & x \geq 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$
> 
> We denote this as $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, where $\lambda$ is the **rate parameter** (also called the *failure rate* or *hazard rate* in safety studies).

The parameter $\lambda$ has units of **inverse time** (e.g., failures per hour, accidents per million man-hours).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used:**
> - **$\lambda$ (Lambda)** — Rate parameter, units: failures / unit time.
> - **$\mu = \frac{1}{\lambda}$** — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) or Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
> - **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — Average operational time between consecutive failures.
> - **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) — Average expected lifetime of a non-repairable component.
> - **CDF** — Cumulative Distribution Function (Reliability function complement).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a bus stop where buses arrive **completely randomly** (no schedule), averaging **one bus every 10 minutes** on average. The waiting time for the next bus follows an Exponential Distribution.

In the context of **safety engineering**:
- Think of a **pressure relief valve** in a chemical plant. Components fail "out of the blue" without any prior warning or wear-out pattern.
- The exponential distribution models the time until the **first failure** of a component that has **no memory** of past usage — its future failure probability is **independent of how long it has already survived**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Intuition:** The exponential distribution is the "memoryless" distribution. It does not care whether the equipment is brand new or has been running for 5 years — its probability of failing in the next instant is always the same.

## 1.3 Domain and Range

- **Domain (Support):** $x \in [0, \infty)$
- **Parameter Space:** $\lambda > 0$
- **Mode:** $x = 0$ (the distribution is monotonically decreasing)
- **Skewness:** Always **right-skewed (positive skew)** for any finite $\lambda$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Origin from the Poisson Process
The exponential distribution is derived from the Poisson process. If events (failures) occur according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$, then the **waiting time until the first event** is exponentially distributed.

### Step 2: Memoryless Property — The Defining Characteristic
The exponential distribution is the **only continuous distribution** with the memoryless property:

$$P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t), \quad \text{for all } s, t \geq 0$$

This means: a component that has already survived $s$ hours has the **same probability of failing in the next $t$ hours** as a brand-new component.

### Step 3: Relationship Between PDF and CDF
The CDF is obtained by integrating the PDF:

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{0}^{x} \lambda e^{-\lambda u}\, du = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$$

The **Reliability Function** (Survival Function) $R(x)$ is:

$$R(x) = P(X > x) = e^{-\lambda x}$$

### Step 4: Hazard Function (Failure Rate Function)
The **instantaneous hazard rate** $h(x)$ is defined as:

$$h(x) = \frac{f(x)}{R(x)} = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda x}}{e^{-\lambda x}} = \lambda$$

This is a **constant** — meaning the exponential distribution assumes a **constant failure rate** (the "useful life" phase of the bathtub curve).

### Step 5: Moments and Generating Functions
- **Mean:** $\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}$
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$
- **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \frac{1}{\lambda}$
- **Moment Generating Function:** $M_X(t) = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}$, for $t < \lambda$
- **Skewness:** $\gamma_1 = 2$ (a fixed constant, independent of $\lambda$)
- **Kurtosis:** $\gamma_2 = 9$ (excess kurtosis)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| PDF | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$ | Probability density function |
| CDF | $F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ | Cumulative probability up to $x$ |
| Reliability $R(x)$ | $R(x) = e^{-\lambda x}$ | Probability of survival beyond $x$ |
| Mean $\mathbb{E}[X]$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ | MTTF / MTBF |
| Variance $\text{Var}(X)$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | Spread of failure times |
| Std. Deviation | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Dispersion measure |
| MGF $M_X(t)$ | $\frac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}$ | Moment generating function |
| Hazard $h(x)$ | $\lambda$ | Constant failure rate |
| Memoryless | $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ | No aging effect |
| Skewness | $2$ | Fixed right-skew |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Utility:**
> - Used in **reliability block diagrams** for non-repairable systems.
> - Models **time-to-failure** of electronic components, light bulbs, and mechanical systems in their useful life phase.
> - Forms the basis of the **Poisson-Exponential duality** used in accident occurrence modeling (e.g., Heinrich's Domino Theory quantification).
> - Applied in **safety stock** calculations and **mean downtime** estimation.

## 2.3 Median and Quantiles
- **Median:** $x_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \frac{0.6931}{\lambda}$
- **General $p$-th Quantile:** $x_p = -\frac{\ln(1-p)}{\lambda}$

## 2.4 Sum of Independent Exponentials (Gamma Connection)
If $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ independently, then:

$$Y = X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n \sim \text{Gamma}(n, \lambda)$$

This is the foundation for modeling **time-to-$n$-th failure** in safety studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CDF from PDF

We begin with the PDF:
$$f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$

By definition of the CDF:

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(u)\, du$$

Since $f(u) = 0$ for $u < 0$, we adjust the lower limit:

$$F(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \lambda e^{-\lambda u}\, du$$

Performing the integration:

$$F(x) = \lambda \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda u}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{x} = \lambda \cdot \frac{-1}{\lambda} \left[ e^{-\lambda x} - e^{0} \right]$$

$$F(x) = -\left( e^{-\lambda x} - 1 \right) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$$

$$\boxed{F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0}$$

**Textual logic:** The exponential integral evaluates to a simple closed form because the integrand is its own derivative (up to a sign and constant). The negative sign cancels with the integration limits, yielding a clean survival-style expression.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean $\mathbb{E}[X]$

By definition:

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x)\, dx = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$$

Using integration by parts with $u = x$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} dx$:

$$du = dx, \quad v = -e^{-\lambda x}$$

Applying $\int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du$:

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \left[ -x e^{-\lambda x} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$$

Evaluating the boundary term: at $x = \infty$, $x e^{-\lambda x} \to 0$ (exponential dominates); at $x = 0$, $-x e^{-\lambda x} = 0$. So the boundary term vanishes:

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda x}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

**Textual logic:** The boundary term vanishes because exponential decay outpaces linear growth. The remaining integral is a standard exponential integral, yielding the mean directly as the reciprocal of the rate parameter.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Variance $\text{Var}(X)$

First, we compute $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$:

$$\mathbb{E}[X^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} x^2 \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$$

Using integration by parts twice (or the Gamma function identity $\int_0^\infty x^n e^{-\lambda x} dx = \frac{n!}{\lambda^{n+1}}$):

$$\mathbb{E}[X^2] = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}$$

Then:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2 = \frac{2}{\lambda^2} - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}, \quad \sigma = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

**Textual logic:** The variance equals the square of the mean — a unique property of the exponential distribution. This means the **coefficient of variation is always 1**, regardless of $\lambda$.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Memoryless Property

We need to prove:

$$P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$$

By definition of conditional probability:

$$P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = \frac{P(X > s + t \cap X > s)}{P(X > s)} = \frac{P(X > s + t)}{P(X > s)}$$

Since $s + t > s$, the intersection reduces to $P(X > s + t)$:

$$= \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = \frac{e^{-\lambda s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = P(X > t)$$

$$\boxed{P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)}$$

**Textual logic:** The cancellation of $e^{-\lambda s}$ in numerator and denominator is the mathematical heart of the memoryless property. The "history" of how long $X$ has already survived is completely erased.

## 3.5 Derivation of the MGF

By definition:

$$M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[e^{tX}] = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{tx} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = \lambda \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(\lambda - t)x}\, dx$$

This converges for $\lambda - t > 0$, i.e., $t < \lambda$:

$$M_X(t) = \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda - t} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}, \quad t < \lambda$$

$$\boxed{M_X(t) = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}}$$

**Textual logic:** Combining the exponentials in the integrand gives a single exponential whose integral evaluates to a rational function. This MGF can be used to derive all moments by differentiation.

## 3.6 Validation Check — Total Probability

We must verify that the PDF integrates to 1:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = \left[ -e^{-\lambda x} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - (-1) = 1 \checkmark$$

**Textual logic:** A valid PDF must integrate to unity. The exponential PDF passes this check, confirming it is properly normalized.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem:** A safety-critical sensor has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.002$ failures per hour. Find:
1. The MTTF.
2. The probability it survives beyond 1000 hours.
3. The probability it fails within the first 500 hours.

**Solution:**

**(1) MTTF:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ hours}$$

**(2) Survival beyond 1000 hours:**
$$R(1000) = e^{-0.002 \times 1000} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**(3) Failure within 500 hours:**
$$F(500) = 1 - e^{-0.002 \times 500} = 1 - e^{-1} \approx 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$$

**Interpretation:** The probability of failure within one MTTF is always $\approx 63.21\%$ (since $1 - e^{-1}$), a key reliability engineering benchmark.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reliability Modeling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System Input X follows Exp Lambda"] --> B["Compute PDF f of x"]
    A --> C["Compute CDF F of x"]
    A --> D["Compute Reliability R of x"]
    B --> E["Hazard Function h of x equals Lambda"]
    C --> F["Mean E of X equals 1 by Lambda"]
    D --> G["Variance equals 1 by Lambda squared"]
    E --> H["Memoryless Property Verified"]
    F --> I["MTTF Output for Safety Decision"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Safety Reliability Report"]
```

**Textual explanation of the diagram:** This flow represents the analytical pipeline a reliability engineer follows when assessing exponential-distributed failure data. Starting from the random variable specification, parallel computations yield the PDF, CDF, and Reliability function, which feed into the constant hazard rate check, moment calculations, and memoryless verification. The final MTTF output drives the safety report.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Bathtub Curve Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["Phase 1 Infant Mortality"]
        A1["Decreasing Hazard Lambda"]
    end
    subgraph P2["Phase 2 Useful Life"]
        A2["Constant Hazard Lambda - EXPONENTIAL APPLIES HERE"]
    end
    subgraph P3["Phase 3 Wear Out"]
        A3["Increasing Hazard Lambda"]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
```

**Textual explanation:** The exponential distribution is the canonical model for the **middle (useful life) phase** of the bathtub curve. During this phase, components fail randomly at a constant rate due to external shocks or stochastic loads, not due to aging.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Poisson-Exponential Duality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Poisson Process with rate Lambda events per unit time"] --> B["Time until 1st event"]
    A --> C["Time until nth event"]
    B --> D["Exponential Distribution Exp Lambda"]
    C --> E["Gamma Distribution Gamma n Lambda"]
    D --> F["MTTF equals 1 by Lambda"]
    E --> G["Mean equals n by Lambda"]
```

**Textual explanation:** This diagram captures the duality between the Poisson process (counting events) and the Exponential/Gamma distributions (measuring time). This duality is fundamental in **accident modeling** and **safety event analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Exponential Distribution. State its key properties.**

**Model Answer:**
The Exponential Distribution is a continuous probability distribution with PDF $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \geq 0$, where $\lambda > 0$ is the rate parameter.

**Key Properties:**
1. **Memoryless Property:** $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$
2. **Constant Hazard Rate:** $h(x) = \lambda$
3. **Mean = MTTF = $\frac{1}{\lambda}$**
4. **Variance = $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$**
5. **Skewness is fixed at 2** (independent of $\lambda$)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the relationship between the Exponential Distribution and the Poisson Process?**

**Model Answer:**
If events occur according to a **Poisson process** with constant rate $\lambda$ per unit time, then the **waiting time until the first event** follows an Exponential Distribution with parameter $\lambda$. This is the **Poisson-Exponential duality**. Equivalently, the number of events in time $t$ follows a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda t$, while the inter-arrival times between events are i.i.d. exponentials.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of the Exponential Distribution with parameter $\lambda$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A safety system component has an exponential time-to-failure distribution with mean of 2000 hours. Calculate:
- (i) The failure rate $\lambda$.
- (ii) The probability that the component will survive at least 1500 hours.
- (iii) The probability that it will fail between 1000 and 3000 hours. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Mean Derivation:**
$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$$

Using integration by parts with $u = x, dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} dx$:

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \left[ -x e^{-\lambda x} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Variance Derivation:**
$$\mathbb{E}[X^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} x^2 \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2 = \frac{2}{\lambda^2} - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: Mean MTTF = 2000 hours $\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{2000} = 0.0005$ per hour.

**(i) Failure rate:**
$$\lambda = 0.0005 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**(ii) Survival beyond 1500 hours:**
$$R(1500) = e^{-0.0005 \times 1500} = e^{-0.75} \approx 0.4724$$

**(iii) Failure between 1000 and 3000 hours:**
$$P(1000 \leq X \leq 3000) = F(3000) - F(1000)$$
$$= \left(1 - e^{-0.0005 \times 3000}\right) - \left(1 - e^{-0.0005 \times 1000}\right)$$
$$= e^{-0.5} - e^{-1.5}$$
$$= 0.6065 - 0.2231 = 0.3834$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** State and prove the memoryless property of the Exponential Distribution. Hence show that the exponential distribution is the only continuous distribution with this property. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a chemical plant, accidental releases occur according to a Poisson process with an average rate of 2 incidents per year. Find:
- (i) The probability that no incident occurs in the next 6 months.
- (ii) The probability that the time until the next incident is less than 3 months.
- (iii) The expected waiting time until the next incident. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Statement:** For $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$,
$$P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t), \quad \forall s, t \geq 0$$

**Proof:**
$$P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = \frac{P(X > s+t)}{P(X > s)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = P(X > t) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Uniqueness:** Suppose $X$ is memoryless. Let $G(x) = -\ln P(X > x)$. Then memoryless gives $G(s+t) = G(s) + G(t)$. This Cauchy-type functional equation with measurability has the unique continuous solution $G(x) = \lambda x$. Therefore $P(X > x) = e^{-\lambda x}$, which is uniquely the Exponential CDF.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $\lambda = 2$ incidents/year. Times are in years.

**(i) No incident in 6 months = 0.5 years:**
$$P(X > 0.5) = e^{-2 \times 0.5} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

**(ii) Time until next incident < 3 months = 0.25 years:**
$$P(X < 0.25) = 1 - e^{-2 \times 0.25} = 1 - e^{-0.5} \approx 0.3935$$

**(iii) Expected waiting time:**
$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ years} = 6 \text{ months}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing Mean and Rate:** Students frequently write $\lambda = \mathbb{E}[X]$ instead of $\lambda = \frac{1}{\mathbb{E}[X]}$. **Always remember:** $\lambda$ is the *rate* (per unit time), and MTTF is its reciprocal.
> 2. **Domain Errors:** Forgetting that $f(x) = 0$ for $x < 0$. Always state the piecewise definition in derivations.
> 3. **Boundary Terms in Integration by Parts:** Dropping the evaluation of the boundary term $\left[-x e^{-\lambda x}\right]_0^\infty$. Examiners allocate **1 mark** for explicitly showing this term equals zero.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing time units (e.g., using $\lambda$ in hours but $t$ in minutes). **Always convert all times to the same unit** before substitution.
> 5. **Probability Range:** $P(X > t)$ requires the reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, NOT $1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ (which is the CDF).
> 6. **Skewness Constant:** Some students compute skewness as a function of $\lambda$. The skewness of the exponential distribution is **always 2**, regardless of $\lambda$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ has PDF $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \geq 0$, with rate parameter $\lambda > 0$.
- **CDF:** $F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$
- **Reliability/Survival Function:** $R(x) = e^{-\lambda x}$
- **Mean (MTTF):** $\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}$
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$; Standard Deviation = $\frac{1}{\lambda}$
- **Memoryless Property:** $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ — the **defining characteristic**.
- **Constant Hazard Rate:** $h(x) = \lambda$ for all $x \geq 0$.
- **Skewness = 2** (always); **Kurtosis = 9** (excess).
- **Poisson-Exponential Duality:** Inter-arrival times in a Poisson process are exponential.
- **Sum of $n$ i.i.d. exponentials** $\Rightarrow$ **Gamma$(n, \lambda)$ distribution**.
- **Failure within one MTTF** has probability $\approx 63.21\%$ ($1 - e^{-1}$).
- **Median** = $\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$; **Mode** = $0$.
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Component reliability, accident modeling, downtime analysis, MTBF estimation, safety stock.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weibull Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weibull Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Weibull Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution widely used in **reliability engineering, survival analysis, and safety modeling** to describe the time-to-failure of components, systems, and materials. It was introduced by Swedish engineer **Waloddi Weibull** in 1939 and has since become one of the most versatile distributions in engineering statistics.

Mathematically, a random variable $T$ follows a **two-parameter Weibull distribution** if its cumulative distribution function (CDF) is given by:

$$
F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

where the parameters are defined as:
- $\beta$ (beta) = **Shape parameter** (dimensionless), $\beta > 0$
- $\eta$ (eta) = **Scale parameter** (units of time), $\eta > 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Weibull distribution is classified as a **three-parameter distribution** in the generalized form, where an additional **location parameter $\gamma$** is included to account for a guaranteed non-failure period. However, for the OESFT837 module, the focus is on the **two-parameter form**, which is the standard industry model.

## 1.2 Probability Density Function (PDF)

The probability density function is obtained by differentiating the CDF:

$$
f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

## 1.3 Reliability Function (Survival Function)

The **reliability function** $R(t)$ represents the probability that a component survives beyond time $t$:

$$
R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Chain of Weakest Links"**
>
> Imagine a chain made of $n$ metal links. The chain breaks when its **weakest link** fails. Each link has a slightly different strength due to microscopic material defects. The Weibull distribution mathematically models this scenario: it represents the **weakest-link distribution** of a large number of independent failure mechanisms.
>
> - If you test **roller bearings** in machinery, the Weibull distribution describes the time to first spalling.
> - If you measure the **fatigue life** of a metal specimen, Weibull captures the scatter.
> - If you analyze the **breaking strength of glass fibers**, the data fits Weibull remarkably well.
>
> The **shape parameter $\beta$** is the "personality" of the failure: it tells you whether failures occur early (infant mortality), randomly (useful life), or through wear-out (aging).

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are essential in KTU examinations:

- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** $E[T] = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$
- **Characteristic Life ($\eta$):** The time at which **63.2%** of the population has failed (since $F(\eta) = 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$).
- **Hazard Rate:** $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid:** At $t = \eta$, exactly $1 - 1/e \approx 63.21\%$ of components have failed. This is the **"63.2% rule"** of Weibull analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Parameters Explained

### (a) Shape Parameter $\beta$ — The "Failure Personality"

The shape parameter $\beta$ controls the **shape** of the hazard (failure rate) function:

| Value of $\beta$ | Failure Behavior | Bathtub Curve Phase | Real-World Example |
|:----------------:|:-----------------|:--------------------|:-------------------|
| $\beta < 1$      | Decreasing failure rate | Infant Mortality | Software bugs, early bearing failures |
| $\beta = 1$      | Constant failure rate (Exponential distribution) | Useful Life (Random) | Electronic components, random shocks |
| $\beta > 1$      | Increasing failure rate | Wear-Out Phase | Fatigue, corrosion, bearing wear |
| $\beta \approx 3.44$ | Approximates Normal distribution | — | Material strength variations |

### (b) Scale Parameter $\eta$ — The "Stretch Factor"

The scale parameter $\eta$ **stretches or compresses** the distribution along the time axis. It has the **same units as $t$** (hours, cycles, km, etc.). A larger $\eta$ means longer-lived components.

### (c) Location Parameter $\gamma$ (Three-Parameter Form)

In the generalized form:

$$
F(t) = 1 - e^{-((t-\gamma)/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq \gamma
$$

The location parameter $\gamma$ represents a **guaranteed survival period** before any failure can occur (e.g., burn-in period for electronics).

## 2.2 The Hazard Function — Heart of Reliability Analysis

The **instantaneous failure rate** (hazard function) is:

$$
h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

The **cumulative hazard function** is:

$$
H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x)\,dx = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hazard Function Matters in Safety Engineering:** In OESFT837, the hazard function allows safety engineers to predict **when** a component is most likely to fail, enabling **predictive maintenance scheduling** and **risk-based inspection planning**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq F \leq 1$ |
| 2 | PDF | $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Probability density |
| 3 | Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Probability of survival |
| 4 | Hazard Rate | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Failures per unit time |
| 5 | Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Mean (MTTF) | $\mu = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | Same units as $t$ |
| 7 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \eta^2 \left[\Gamma(1 + 2/\beta) - \Gamma^2(1 + 1/\beta)\right]$ | Squared units of $t$ |
| 8 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \eta\sqrt{\Gamma(1+2/\beta) - \Gamma^2(1+1/\beta)}$ | Same units as $t$ |
| 9 | Median | $t_{0.5} = \eta \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$ | 50% failure time |
| 10 | Mode | $t_{mode} = \eta \left(\dfrac{\beta - 1}{\beta}\right)^{1/\beta}$ for $\beta > 1$ | Most likely failure time |
| 11 | Characteristic Life | $F(\eta) = 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$ | 63.2% failure point |
| 12 | Special Case | $\beta = 1$ $\Rightarrow$ Exponential ($\lambda = 1/\eta$) | Memoryless property |

> [!NOTE]
> **Gamma Function Reminder:** $\Gamma(k) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{k-1} e^{-x} dx$; for integers, $\Gamma(n) = (n-1)!$. For example, $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.886$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace Safety:** Modeling fatigue life of turbine blades and airframe structures.
- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete strength variation and bridge cable failure.
- **Nuclear Safety:** Reliability of reactor components and pressure vessels.
- **Automotive Safety:** Brake pad wear, tire life, and airbag deployment timing.
- **Electronics:** Capacitor failure, semiconductor burnout, and solder joint fatigue.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

**Statement:** For a two-parameter Weibull distribution with shape $\beta$ and scale $\eta$, prove that:

$$
E[T] = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

**Proof:**

The expected value is defined as:

$$
E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt
$$

Substituting the Weibull PDF:

$$
E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the substitution $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$.

This implies:

$$
