u = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \eta \cdot u^{1/\beta}
$$

Differentiating both sides:

$$
du = \beta \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta}\, dt = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} dt
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} dt = du
$$

**Step 2:** When $t = 0$, $u = 0$. When $t \to \infty$, $u \to \infty$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the integral:

$$
E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(\eta \cdot u^{1/\beta}\right) e^{-u}\, du
$$

$$
E[T] = \eta \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/\beta} \cdot e^{-u}\, du
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the integral as the Gamma function:

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} u^{k-1} e^{-u}\, du = \Gamma(k)
$$

Here, $k - 1 = 1/\beta$, so $k = 1 + 1/\beta$.

**Step 5:** Final result:

$$
\boxed{E[T] = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}
$$

**Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Variance

**Statement:** Prove that:

$$
\text{Var}(T) = \eta^2 \left[ \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) \right]
$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Use the identity $\text{Var}(T) = E[T^2] - (E[T])^2$.

**Step 2:** Compute $E[T^2]$ using the same substitution $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$:

$$
E[T^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^2 \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt
$$

Since $t^2 = \eta^2 u^{2/\beta}$:

$$
E[T^2] = \eta^2 \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{2/\beta} e^{-u}\, du = \eta^2 \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Square the mean:

$$
(E[T])^2 = \eta^2 \cdot \Gamma^2\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract:

$$
\text{Var}(T) = \eta^2 \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \eta^2 \Gamma^2\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Var}(T) = \eta^2 \left[ \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) \right]}
$$

**Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hazard Function

**Statement:** Derive the hazard function $h(t)$ from the PDF and reliability function.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** By definition:

$$
h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expressions:

$$
h(t) = \frac{\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}
$$

**Step 3:** The exponentials cancel:

$$
h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Equivalently:

$$
\boxed{h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta}} t^{\beta-1}}
$$

**Q.E.D.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** When $\beta = 1$, $h(t) = 1/\eta$ (constant rate — exponential case). When $\beta > 1$, $h(t)$ increases monotonically (aging components). When $\beta < 1$, $h(t)$ decreases (burn-in phase).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A safety-critical sensor's failure time follows a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 2.5$ and $\eta = 1000$ hours. Calculate:
- (i) The reliability at $t = 500$ hours.
- (ii) The MTTF.
- (iii) The probability that it fails between 200 and 800 hours.

**Solution:**

**(i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours:**

$$
R(500) = e^{-(500/1000)^{2.5}} = e^{-(0.5)^{2.5}} = e^{-0.1768} \approx 0.8380
$$

**(ii) MTTF:**

Since $\Gamma(1 + 1/2.5) = \Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8874$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = 1000 \times 0.8874 \approx 887.4 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Probability of failure between 200 and 800 hours:**

$$
P(200 < T < 800) = F(800) - F(200)
$$

$$
F(800) = 1 - e^{-(0.8)^{2.5}} = 1 - e^{-0.5724} \approx 0.5642
$$

$$
F(200) = 1 - e^{-(0.2)^{2.5}} = 1 - e^{-0.01789} \approx 0.01773
$$

$$
P(200 < T < 800) = 0.5642 - 0.01773 \approx 0.5465
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Weibull ($\beta = 1$) | Weibull ($\beta = 2$) | Weibull ($\beta = 3.5$) |
|:---------|:---------------------|:----------------------|:------------------------|
| Distribution Type | Exponential | Rayleigh-like | Near-Normal |
| Mean ($\eta = 1$) | $\Gamma(2) = 1$ | $\Gamma(1.5) \approx 0.886$ | $\Gamma(1.286) \approx 0.901$ |
| Variance ($\eta = 1$) | $1$ | $0.215$ | $0.073$ |
| Skewness | $2$ | $0.631$ | $0.025$ |
| Hazard Behavior | Constant | Linearly increasing | Steeply increasing |
| Use Case | Random failures | Fatigue onset | Material strength |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Weibull Distribution — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random Variable T - Time to Failure] --> B{Weibull Model Selection}
    B --> C[Two Parameter Form]
    B --> D[Three Parameter Form]
    C --> E[Shape Parameter Beta]
    C --> F[Scale Parameter Eta]
    D --> G[Location Parameter Gamma Added]
    E --> H{Beta Value Analysis}
    H --> I[Beta less than 1: Decreasing Hazard]
    H --> J[Beta equals 1: Constant Hazard Exponential]
    H --> K[Beta greater than 1: Increasing Hazard Wear Out]
    I --> L[Infant Mortality Phase]
    J --> M[Useful Life Random Failures]
    K --> N[Aging and Wear Out Phase]
    F --> O[Characteristic Life at 63.2 percent Failure]
    G --> P[Guaranteed Survival Period]
    L --> Q[Predictive Maintenance Schedule]
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Safety Decision: Replace, Inspect, or Continue Operation]
```

## 4.2 PDF and Hazard Function Behavior Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input[Time t] --> P1[PDF Curve f of t]
    Input --> P2[Hazard Curve h of t]
    P1 --> S1[Beta less than 1: Peak at t equals 0]
    P1 --> S2[Beta equals 1: Decay from Maximum]
    P1 --> S3[Beta greater than 1: Bell Shaped Peak]
    P2 --> H1[Beta less than 1: Decreasing Curve]
    P2 --> H2[Beta equals 1: Horizontal Line Constant]
    P2 --> H3[Beta greater than 1: Monotonically Increasing]
    S1 --> Out[Reliability Assessment Output]
    S2 --> Out
    S3 --> Out
    H1 --> Out
    H2 --> Out
    H3 --> Out
```

## 4.3 Weibull Analysis Workflow in Safety Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Failure Time Data] --> B[Construct Weibull Plot]
    B --> C[Determine Shape Parameter Beta from Slope]
    C --> D[Determine Scale Parameter Eta from 63.2 percent Line]
    D --> E{Beta Interpretation}
    E -->|Beta less than 1| F[Infant Mortality: Improve Manufacturing QC]
    E -->|Beta equals 1| G[Random Failures: Redundancy and Inspection]
    E -->|Beta greater than 1| H[Wear Out: Schedule Preventive Replacement]
    F --> I[Compute MTTF and Reliability R of t]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Generate Safety Report and Maintenance Plan]
```

## 4.4 Parameter Effect Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ParamEffects["Effect of Parameters on Weibull Behavior"]
        BetaLow[Beta less than 1] --> Effect1[Failure Rate Decreases: Early Failures Dominate]
        BetaOne[Beta equals 1] --> Effect2[Failure Rate Constant: Memoryless Process]
        BetaHigh[Beta greater than 1] --> Effect3[Failure Rate Increases: Aging Components]
        EtaSmall[Eta Small] --> Effect4[Short Lived Components: Failures Occur Quickly]
        EtaLarge[Eta Large] --> Effect5[Long Lived Components: Failures Delayed]
        GammaParam[Gamma Positive] --> Effect6[Shifted Distribution: Guaranteed Non Failure Period]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Weibull distribution. State its probability density function and cumulative distribution function for the two-parameter case.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Weibull distribution** is a continuous probability distribution used to model time-to-failure data in reliability and safety engineering.

**Two-parameter Weibull CDF:**

$$
F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Two-parameter Weibull PDF:**

$$
f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

where $\beta > 0$ is the **shape parameter** and $\eta > 0$ is the **scale parameter**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the shape parameter $\beta$ in the Weibull distribution? Mention the three characteristic cases.**

**Model Answer:**

The shape parameter $\beta$ determines the **failure behavior pattern** of the component:

1. **$\beta < 1$:** Decreasing failure rate — represents **infant mortality** (early-life failures due to manufacturing defects).
2. **$\beta = 1$:** Constant failure rate — equivalent to the **exponential distribution**; represents random failures during useful life.
3. **$\beta > 1$:** Increasing failure rate — represents **wear-out failures** due to aging, fatigue, or corrosion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of a two-parameter Weibull distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The time to failure (in hours) of an industrial pump follows a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 1.8$ and $\eta = 5000$ hours. Compute: (i) the MTTF, (ii) the reliability at 2000 hours, and (iii) the probability that the pump fails before 1000 hours. Given $\Gamma(1.556) \approx 0.893$ and $\Gamma(1.111) \approx 0.951$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Mean Derivation:**

$$
E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt
$$

Let $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, then $du = \frac{\beta}{\eta}(t/\eta)^{\beta-1} dt$ and $t = \eta u^{1/\beta}$:

$$
E[T] = \eta \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/\beta} e^{-u} du = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

**Variance Derivation:**

$$
E[T^2] = \eta^2 \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Var}(T) = E[T^2] - (E[T])^2 = \eta^2\left[\Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]
$$

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**(i) MTTF:**

Since $1 + 1/1.8 = 1.5556$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = 5000 \times \Gamma(1.556) = 5000 \times 0.893 = 4465 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Reliability at $t = 2000$ hours:**

$$
R(2000) = e^{-(2000/5000)^{1.8}} = e^{-(0.4)^{1.8}} = e^{-0.1917} \approx 0.8256
$$

**(iii) Probability of failure before 1000 hours:**

$$
F(1000) = 1 - e^{-(1000/5000)^{1.8}} = 1 - e^{-(0.2)^{1.8}} = 1 - e^{-0.0613} \approx 0.0595
$$

So there is approximately a **5.95%** chance the pump fails before 1000 hours.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the **shape parameter $\beta$** and **scale parameter $\eta$** in the Weibull distribution. Discuss how varying $\beta$ affects the hazard function with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety valve's failure time follows a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 2.0$ and $\eta = 800$ hours. Find: (i) the mean time to failure, (ii) the variance, and (iii) the failure rate at $t = 400$ hours. Given $\Gamma(1.5) = 0.8862$ and $\Gamma(2) = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Shape parameter $\beta$** controls the **failure behavior pattern**:
- $\beta < 1$: Decreasing hazard (infant mortality phase).
- $\beta = 1$: Constant hazard (useful life — exponential).
- $\beta > 1$: Increasing hazard (wear-out phase).

**Scale parameter $\eta$** controls the **time scaling**:
- It is the **characteristic life**, i.e., the time at which 63.2% of the population has failed.
- A larger $\eta$ implies longer-lasting components.

**Hazard function** $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$:

- When $\beta < 1$: $h(t)$ decreases monotonically — failures slow down over time (defects weeded out).
- When $\beta = 1$: $h(t) = 1/\eta$ — constant, no memory of past operation.
- When $\beta > 1$: $h(t)$ increases monotonically — aging dominates, failures become more frequent.

The **bathtub curve** is reproduced when components experience all three phases sequentially (early $\beta < 1$, useful $\beta = 1$, wear-out $\beta > 1$).

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**(i) MTTF:**

$$
\text{MTTF} = 800 \times \Gamma(1 + 1/2) = 800 \times \Gamma(1.5) = 800 \times 0.8862 = 709.0 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Variance:**

$$
\text{Var}(T) = 800^2 \left[\Gamma(2) - \Gamma^2(1.5)\right] = 640000 \left[1 - (0.8862)^2\right]
$$

$$
\text{Var}(T) = 640000 \times (1 - 0.7854) = 640000 \times 0.2146 \approx 137344 \text{ hour}^2
$$

**Standard deviation:** $\sigma \approx 370.6$ hours.

**(iii) Failure rate at $t = 400$ hours:**

$$
h(400) = \frac{2}{800}\left(\frac{400}{800}\right)^{2-1} = \frac{2}{800} \times 0.5 = 0.00125 \text{ per hour}
$$

So the instantaneous failure rate at 400 hours is **0.00125 failures/hour** (or equivalently, a **MTBF of 800 hours** at that instant).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the shape and scale parameters:** Students often swap $\beta$ and $\eta$ in formulas. Remember: $\beta$ is **dimensionless** (shape), $\eta$ has **same units as $t$** (scale).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Gamma function:** When computing MTTF and variance, you **must use the Gamma function**. Writing $E[T] = \eta$ alone is correct **only when $\beta = 1$** (exponential case). KTU examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this error.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in the exponent:** The CDF is $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$, NOT $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ and NOT $1 - e^{+(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$. The exponent must be **negative**.
>
> 4. **Wrong interpretation of the 63.2% rule:** At $t = \eta$, **63.2% have failed**, so the reliability is **36.8%**, not 63.2%. Many students flip this.
>
> 5. **Hazard function confusion:** $h(t)$ is **not** the same as $f(t)$. Always divide by $R(t)$: $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.
>
> 6. **Not stating the assumptions:** Always mention "$\beta > 0$, $\eta > 0$, $t \geq 0$" in derivation-based questions to earn full marks on the boundary conditions.
>
> 7. **Forgetting units:** MTTF and $\eta$ have units (hours, cycles, km). Variance has **squared units**. Failing to mention units in numerical problems can cost **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Weibull is a **continuous, three-parameter** (often used as two-parameter) distribution for modeling **time-to-failure** in reliability engineering.
- **CDF:** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$
- **PDF:** $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$
- **Reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$
- **Hazard Rate:** $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$
- **MTTF:** $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$
- **Variance:** $\eta^2 \left[\Gamma(1 + 2/\beta) - \Gamma^2(1 + 1/\beta)\right]$
- **Special case** $\beta = 1$: Reduces to **Exponential distribution** with $\lambda = 1/\eta$.
- **Special case** $\beta = 2$: Known as **Rayleigh distribution** — used in vibration analysis and wind speed modeling.
- **Special case** $\beta \approx 3.44$: Closely approximates the **Normal distribution**.
- **63.2% Rule:** $F(\eta) = 1 - e^{-1} = 0.6321$ — always quote this in KTU answers.
- **Shape interpretation:** $\beta < 1$ (infant mortality), $\beta = 1$ (random), $\beta > 1$ (wear-out).
- **Engineering domains:** Aerospace, nuclear, automotive, civil, electronics, and biomedical reliability.
- **Parameter estimation methods:** Graphical (Weibull plot), Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), and Method of Moments.
- **Three-parameter form:** Includes location parameter $\gamma$ for guaranteed non-failure period; CDF becomes $F(t) = 1 - e^{-((t-\gamma)/\eta)^{\beta}}$.
- **Memoryless property:** Holds **only when $\beta = 1$** (exponential case).
- **Always specify** $\beta > 0$, $\eta > 0$, and $t \geq 0$ in answers.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** = MTTF for **non-repairable** systems in the Weibull context.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reliability Engineering # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reliability (R(t))** is defined as the probability that a system, component, or item will perform its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions (environmental, operational, and stress conditions) for a specified period of time **t**. Mathematically, it is the complement of the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the time-to-failure random variable **T**: $$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$ where: - $F(t)$ = Cumulative probability of failure by time $t$ - $T$ = Random variable representing time-to-failure The **Unreliability** or **Probability of Failure** is: $$Q(t) = 1 - R(t) = F(t)$$ ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Wristwatch Guarantee"** > Imagine a wristwatch manufacturer guaranteeing that 95 out of 100 watches will work flawlessly for at least 2 years. That "95% working for 2 years" is the **Reliability** at $t = 2$ years. If we treat each watch as a system, reliability answers the question: *"Out of a large batch of identical items, what fraction will survive until time t without failing?"* In safety engineering, reliability is a cornerstone metric because: - It quantifies the **trustworthiness** of safety-critical systems (nuclear reactors, aircraft, medical devices). - It forms the mathematical basis for **risk assessment** and **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** determination. - It allows engineers to predict failure patterns and plan **preventive maintenance**. ## 1.3 Reliability vs. Availability — The Critical Distinction > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **Reliability** and **Availability**. Do NOT use these terms interchangeably. | Aspect | Reliability $R(t)$ | Availability $A(t)$ | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Probability of performing function **without failure** for time $t$ | Probability that a system is **operational at a given instant** of time | | **Repair Considered?** | **No** — assumes non-repairable or considers mission time only | **Yes** — explicitly includes repair/downtime | | **Time Basis** | Mission time (0 to $t$) | Steady-state (long-term) | | **Range** | $0 \le R(t) \le 1$ (decreasing function) | $0 \le A \le 1$ (constant in steady state) | | **Formula** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential) | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) The following standard metrics are universally used in reliability engineering: - **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** — failures per unit time (e.g., **failures per million hours (fpmh)** or **FIT — Failures In Time, where 1 FIT = 1 failure per $10^9$ device-hours**) - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — average time between consecutive failures (repairable systems) - **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** — average time to first failure (non-repairable systems) - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — average downtime per failure event - **Availability (A)** — long-term fractional uptime # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Reliability Function — Mathematical Foundation The reliability function is derived from the probability density function (PDF) of time-to-failure, $f(t)$: $$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ Therefore: $$R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx$$ ### 2.1.1 The Exponential Reliability Model (Constant Failure Rate) When the failure rate $\lambda$ is **constant**, the times-to-failure follow an **exponential distribution**. This is the most widely used model in KTU problems: **Why exponential?** Because for many electronic and mechanical components during their useful life (middle of the bathtub curve), the failure rate is approximately constant, making the math tractable and widely applicable. $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ $$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$ $$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$ The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** for the exponential distribution is: $$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ ## 2.2 The Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time > [!NOTE] > The **Bathtub Curve** is a HIGH-YIELD topic for KTU 2024 exams. It represents the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ as a function of time and has three distinct regions: ### Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-In / Debugging Phase) - **Cause:** Manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, substandard components, improper installation. - **Behavior:** Failure rate is **high but decreasing** with time. - **Mitigation:** Factory burn-in testing, quality screening, incoming inspection. ### Region II — Useful Life (Normal Operating Phase) - **Cause:** Random, statistically independent failures due to stress fluctuations. - **Behavior:** Failure rate is **constant** (lowest, flat) — the exponential model applies here. - **Mitigation:** Preventive maintenance, redundancy, condition monitoring. ### Region III — Wear-Out Phase - **Cause:** Aging, corrosion, fatigue, wear, material degradation. - **Behavior:** Failure rate is **increasing** with time. - **Mitigation:** Replacement, refurbishment, scheduled overhaul. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.** | # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Conditions / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $0 \le R(t) \le 1$, dimensionless | | 2 | Unreliability | $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ | | 3 | PDF of Failure | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Units: failures per unit time | | 4 | Failure Rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Constant for exponential; units: $hr^{-1}$ | | 5 | MTTF (exponential) | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Non-repairable component, hours | | 6 | MTBF (exponential) | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} + MTTR \approx \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (when MTTR $\ll$ MTBF) | Repairable system, hours | | 7 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $0 \le A \le 1$ | | 8 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Excludes logistics/administrative delays | | 9 | Series System Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | ALL components must work | | 10 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$ | At least ONE must work | | 11 | Weibull Distribution Shape | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape parameter | | 12 | Hazard Function | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate at time $t$ | | 13 | Failure Rate (Weibull) | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Decreasing if $\beta < 1$, constant if $\beta = 1$, increasing if $\beta > 1$ | | 14 | Mission Time | $t_m = \dfrac{-\ln(R_{required})}{\lambda}$ | Time by which reliability drops to $R_{required}$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Reliability engineering is applied in: - **Aerospace:** Predicting failure probability of avionics, computing redundancy for fly-by-wire systems. - **Nuclear Power:** Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) — computing core damage frequency. - **Oil & Gas:** SIL determination per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** for Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS). - **Electronics:** Derating analysis, MTBF prediction per **MIL-HDBK-217** or **Telcordia SR-332**. - **Automotive:** ISO 26262 functional safety — calculating probability of dangerous failure per hour (PFH). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Reliability from First Principles We start with the exponential distribution assumption and derive all related metrics. ### Step 1: Define the Hazard Function (Failure Rate) The **conditional probability of failure** in the next infinitesimal interval $dt$, given survival to time $t$: $$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$ ### Step 2: Solve the Differential Equation By definition: $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ Rearranging: $$-h(t)\, dt = \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$ Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$ (with $R(0) = 1$, i.e., the system is working at the start): $$-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(x)} \cdot \frac{dR(x)}{dx}\, dx = \ln[R(t)] - \ln[R(0)]$$ Since $\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0$: $$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)$$ ### Step 3: Apply Constant Failure Rate (Exponential Case) If $h(t) = \lambda = $ constant: $$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda\, dx = \lambda t$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$ ### Step 4: Derive the MTTF $$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$ Using integration by parts with $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$: $$MTTF = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = 0 + \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty}$$ $$MTTF = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Series System Reliability A **series system** functions if and only if **every** component functions. Assuming statistical independence of component failures: $$R_{series}(t) = P(\text{all components survive}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{component } i \text{ survives}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$ For exponential components with rates $\lambda_i$: $$R_{series}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t} = e^{-\lambda_{eq} t}$$ where $\lambda_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ (failure rates add for series systems). ## 3.3 Derivation: Parallel System Reliability A **parallel system** functions if **at least one** component functions. The system fails only if **all** components fail: $$Q_{parallel}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} [1 - R_i(t)]$$ Therefore: $$R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - Q_{parallel}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} [1 - R_i(t)]$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Types | Problem Type | Given | Required Formula | Solution Strategy | |---|---|---|---| | Mission Time | $R(t)$ required, $\lambda$ | $t = \dfrac{-\ln R}{\lambda}$ | Direct substitution | | Failure Rate from Data | $N$ failures, $T$ total hours | $\lambda = \dfrac{N}{T}$ | Divide failures by operating hours | | MTBF to $\lambda$ | $MTBF$ (hours) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Reciprocal | | 2-Component Parallel | $R_1$, $R_2$ | $R_p = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2) = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | Standard formula | | 2-Out-of-3 Voting | $R$ per identical unit | $R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ | Binomial expansion | | Availability | $MTBF$, $MTTR$ | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Direct ratio | | Weibull MTTF | $\eta$, $\beta$ | $MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | Gamma function lookup | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Derivation Example **Problem:** A safety sensor has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures per hour. Calculate (a) the reliability for a 1000-hour mission, (b) the MTTF, and (c) the mission time for $R = 0.99$. **Solution:** ### Part (a): Reliability at $t = 1000$ hr $$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-6})(1000)} = e^{-0.005}$$ $$R(1000) = 0.9950 \;\; (\text{or } 99.50\%)$$ ### Part (b): MTTF $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 200{,}000 \text{ hours} \approx 22.8 \text{ years}$$ ### Part (c): Mission Time for $R = 0.99$ $$0.99 = e^{-\lambda t_m}$$ Taking natural logarithm of both sides: $$\ln(0.99) = -\lambda t_m$$ $$t_m = \frac{-\ln(0.99)}{\lambda} = \frac{-(-0.01005)}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.01005}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$t_m = 2010 \text{ hours} \approx 83.75 \text{ days}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time ```mermaid graph LR A["EARLY LIFE
Infant Mortality
Lambda Decreasing
Burn-in defects"] --> B["USEFUL LIFE
Constant Lambda
Random Failures
Exponential Model"] B --> C["WEAR-OUT PHASE
Lambda Increasing
Aging and Fatigue
Replacement Needed"] style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF style B fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006666,color:#000000 style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4500,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Series Reliability Architecture ```mermaid graph LR INPUT["SYSTEM INPUT"] --> A1["Component 1
R1 equals 0.95"] A1 --> A2["Component 2
R2 equals 0.90"] A2 --> A3["Component 3
R3 equals 0.85"] A3 --> OUT["SYSTEM OUTPUT
Rsys = R1 times R2 times R3"] style A1 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#8B7500 style A2 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#8B7500 style A3 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#8B7500 style OUT fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400 ``` **Critical Rule:** For series systems, **all components must work**. If any single component fails, the entire system fails. ## 4.3 Parallel Redundancy Architecture ```mermaid graph LR INPUT["SYSTEM INPUT"] --> SPLIT{"Reliability
Split Point"} SPLIT --> P1["Path 1
R1 equals 0.80"] SPLIT --> P2["Path 2
R2 equals 0.80"] SPLIT --> P3["Path 3
R3 equals 0.80"] P1 --> MERGE{"Combine
Parallel Paths"} P2 --> MERGE P3 --> MERGE MERGE --> OUT["SYSTEM OUTPUT
Rsys = 1 minus Q1 times Q2 times Q3"] style P1 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#00008B style P2 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#00008B style P3 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#00008B style OUT fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400 ``` **Critical Rule:** For parallel systems, the system succeeds if **at least one** path functions. The system fails only when **all** paths fail. ## 4.4 Reliability Engineering Functional Workflow ```mermaid graph TD DEF["Step 1: Define System
and Mission Time"] --> IDENT["Step 2: Identify Components
and Failure Modes"] IDENT --> DATA["Step 3: Collect Failure Data
or Use Generic Databases"] DATA --> MODEL["Step 4: Select Distribution
Exponential or Weibull"] MODEL --> CALC["Step 5: Compute Reliability
R of t and MTTF"] CALC --> ALLOC["Step 6: Reliability Allocation
Series or Parallel or Voting"] ALLOC --> VALIDATE["Step 7: Validate Against
Target R Required"] VALIDATE --> DECISION{"Target Met?"} DECISION -->|Yes| ACCEPT["Accept Design
Document and Release"] DECISION -->|No| REDESIGN["Redesign: Add Redundancy
Improve Components
Derate Stress"] REDESIGN --> CALC style DEF fill:#E8E8E8 style CALC fill:#FFD93D style VALIDATE fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF style ACCEPT fill:#6BCB77 style REDESIGN fill:#FFA500 ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Comparison — Series vs. Parallel ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SERIES_BLOCK["SERIES CONFIGURATION"] S1["A1"] --> S2["A2"] --> S3["A3"] end subgraph PARALLEL_BLOCK["PARALLEL CONFIGURATION"] P1["B1"] P2["B2"] P3["B3"] end PARALLEL_BLOCK --> FORMULA_S["Rs = Product of Ri
R drops as n increases"] SERIES_BLOCK --> FORMULA_P["Rp = 1 minus Product of 1 minus Ri
R rises as n increases"] style SERIES_BLOCK fill:#FFE5E5 style PARALLEL_BLOCK fill:#E5F5FF ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]** ### Question 1: Define Reliability. Explain the difference between MTTF and MTBF. [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** **Reliability** is the probability that a system or component will perform its intended function under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time $t$, without failure. It is mathematically expressed as $R(t) = P(T > t)$, where $T$ is the random variable representing time-to-failure. **Difference between MTTF and MTBF:** | MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | |---|---| | Applied to **non-repairable** components | Applied to **repairable** systems | | Average time until **first** failure | Average time **between consecutive** failures | | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ for exponential | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | | Example: Light bulb, capacitor | Example: Aircraft engine, pump | ### Question 2: What is the Bathtub Curve? Name its three regions. [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** The **Bathtub Curve** is a graphical representation of the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of a component or system as a function of time. It resembles the cross-section of a bathtub and consists of three regions: 1. **Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in):** High but **decreasing** failure rate, caused by manufacturing defects, poor quality, and substandard materials. Addressed by factory burn-in testing. 2. **Useful Life Region (Normal Operation):** **Constant** failure rate due to random, statistically independent failures. The exponential distribution model applies here. 3. **Wear-Out Region:** **Increasing** failure rate due to aging, fatigue, corrosion, and material degradation. Mitigated by preventive replacement and scheduled overhaul. --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation Pattern]** ### Question A (Choice 1) #### Q.A(a): A complex safety system consists of 3 components in series with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, and $R_3 = 0.85$ for a 500-hour mission. To improve reliability, a parallel redundant unit is added to component 3 only. Calculate: (i) Original series system reliability. (ii) Modified system reliability with parallel redundancy on component 3. (iii) System MTTF assuming constant failure rates. [7 Marks] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Convert Reliabilities to Failure Rates** (using $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ at $t = 500$ hr) $$\lambda_1 = \frac{-\ln(0.95)}{500} = \frac{0.05129}{500} = 1.026 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ $$\lambda_2 = \frac{-\ln(0.90)}{500} = \frac{0.10536}{500} = 2.107 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ $$\lambda_3 = \frac{-\ln(0.85)}{500} = \frac{0.16252}{500} = 3.250 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ **Step 2: Original Series Reliability** $$R_{series} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85$$ $$R_{series} = 0.7268 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 72.68\%$$ **Step 3: Parallel Redundancy on Component 3** When two identical components (each $R_3 = 0.85$) are placed in parallel: $$R_{3,parallel} = 1 - (1 - R_3)(1 - R_3) = 1 - (0.15)^2 = 1 - 0.0225 = 0.9775$$ **Step 4: Modified System Reliability** $$R_{modified} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_{3,parallel} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.9775$$ $$\boxed{R_{modified} = 0.8357 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 83.57\%}$$ **Step 5: System MTTF (using combined failure rate of original series)** For series with constant rates: $$\lambda_{sys} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 1.026 \times 10^{-4} + 2.107 \times 10^{-4} + 3.250 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\lambda_{sys} = 6.383 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ $$MTTF_{system} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{sys}} = \frac{1}{6.383 \times 10^{-4}} = 1566.7 \text{ hours}$$ --- #### Q.A(b): A repairable system has $MTBF = 800$ hours and $MTTR = 10$ hours. Calculate: (i) Steady-state availability. (ii) Unavailability. (iii) The reliability of the system for a 50-hour mission. (iv) The expected number of failures in 1 year of continuous operation (8760 hours). [7 Marks] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Steady-State Availability** $$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{800}{800 + 10} = \frac{800}{810}$$ $$\boxed{A = 0.9877 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 98.77\%}$$ **Step 2: Unavailability** $$U = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9877 = 0.0123 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 1.23\%$$ **Step 3: Failure Rate** For exponential distribution (assumed for repairable systems with random failures): $$\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{800} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ (Note: MTTR is small compared to MTBF, so the approximation holds.) **Step 4: Reliability for 50-hour Mission** $$R(50) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(1.25 \times 10^{-3})(50)} = e^{-0.0625}$$ $$\boxed{R(50) = 0.9394 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 93.94\%}$$ **Step 5: Expected Number of Failures per Year** $$N_{failures} = \frac{T_{total}}{MTBF} = \frac{8760}{800}$$ $$\boxed{N_{failures} = 10.95 \approx 11 \text{ failures per year}}$$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) #### Q.B(a): With a neat sketch, explain the Bathtub Curve. Discuss the failure characteristics in each region and the appropriate reliability improvement strategies. [7 Marks] **Model Answer:** **Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.1 — Mermaid diagram of the Bathtub Curve) **Explanation of Three Regions:** | Region | Time Period | Failure Rate Behavior | Causes | Improvement Strategy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **I — Infant Mortality** | Early life | High, **decreasing** | Manufacturing defects, poor quality, weak components, improper installation | Burn-in testing, environmental stress screening, quality inspection, supplier audits | | **II — Useful Life** | Middle (bulk) life | **Constant** (lowest) | Random stresses beyond design limits, human error, secondary damage | Preventive maintenance, condition monitoring, redundancy, operator training | | **III — Wear-Out** | End of life | **Increasing** | Fatigue, corrosion, wear, creep, material degradation, aging | Scheduled replacement, refurbishment, life-extension programs, design upgrades | **Conclusion:** Effective reliability management requires different strategies for each region. A single maintenance philosophy applied uniformly across all regions is suboptimal. --- #### Q.B(b): Two identical pumps, each with $R = 0.80$ for a 100-hour mission, are connected in parallel as a 1-out-of-2 redundant system. (i) Calculate the parallel system reliability. (ii) If the mission time is increased to 300 hours, what is the reliability of each pump and of the parallel system? (iii) Comment on the trade-off between redundancy and mission duration. [7 Marks] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Convert R to Failure Rate at $t = 100$ hr** $$\lambda = \frac{-\ln(0.80)}{100} = \frac{0.22314}{100} = 2.2314 \times 10^{-3} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ **Step 2: Parallel System Reliability at $t = 100$ hr** For 1-out-of-2 parallel (system works if at least one pump works): $$R_p = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = 1 - (0.20)(0.20) = 1 - 0.04$$ $$\boxed{R_p(100) = 0.96 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 96\%}$$ **Step 3: Individual Pump Reliability at $t = 300$ hr** $$R_1(300) = e^{-\lambda \times 300} = e^{-(2.2314 \times 10^{-3})(300)} = e^{-0.6694}$$ $$R_1(300) = 0.5120 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 51.20\%$$ **Step 4: Parallel System Reliability at $t = 300$ hr** $$R_p(300) = 1 - (1 - 0.5120)(1 - 0.5120) = 1 - (0.4880)^2 = 1 - 0.2381$$ $$\boxed{R_p(300) = 0.7619 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 76.19\%}$$ **Step 5: Comparison and Trade-off Discussion** | Time | $R_{single}$ | $R_{parallel}$ | Improvement Factor | |---|---|---|---| | 100 hr | 0.80 | 0.96 | 1.20× | | 300 hr | 0.5120 | 0.7619 | 1.49× | **Commentary:** As mission time increases, the **absolute reliability** of both single and parallel systems decreases (exponential decay). However, the **relative benefit of redundancy** actually increases with mission duration (1.20× → 1.49×). This demonstrates that parallel redundancy is most valuable for **long-duration missions** where individual component reliability drops significantly. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common mistakes students make in Reliability Engineering problems:** > > 1. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF** — Using $MTBF = 1/\lambda$ for non-repairable components (lose 2 marks). > 2. **Adding reliabilities in series** — Students often write $R_s = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$. **CORRECT FORMULA:** $R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3$ (lose 3 marks). > 3. **Forgetting to convert $R$ to $\lambda$** — When mission times differ, you cannot simply multiply reliabilities. Convert to failure rate first, add, then convert back. (Lose 2 marks.) > 4. **Not specifying units** — Always mention "failures per hour" for $\lambda$ and "hours" for MTBF/MTTF. Unitless answers get partial deductions. > 5. **Using Weibull when not given $\beta$** — If only constant failure rate is implied, use exponential. Weibull requires explicit shape parameter. > 6. **Skipping the assumption statement** — Always write "assuming constant failure rate (exponential model) and statistical independence of component failures" at the start of your solution. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Reliability Engineering** - **Reliability** $R(t)$ is the probability of **no failure** for a stated mission time $t$. - **Unreliability** $Q(t) = 1 - R(t)$ is the probability of failure by time $t$. - **Exponential model** (constant $\lambda$): $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is the most commonly tested form. - **MTTF** (non-repairable) = $1/\lambda$; **MTBF** (repairable) = $1/\lambda$ + MTTR $\approx 1/\lambda$ when MTTR is small. - **Availability** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — long-term fraction of uptime. - **Series system** = ALL components must work: $R_s = \prod R_i$ (reliability DECREASES with more components). - **Parallel system** = At least ONE component must work: $R_p = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ (reliability INCREASES with more components). - **2-out-of-3 voting (identical components):** $R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$. - **Bathtub Curve** has THREE regions: Infant Mortality (decreasing $\lambda$), Useful Life (constant $\lambda$), Wear-Out (increasing $\lambda$). - **Weibull distribution** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ — flexible shape: $\beta < 1$ (decreasing hazard), $\beta = 1$ (exponential), $\beta > 1$ (increasing hazard). - **1 FIT** = 1 failure per $10^9$ device-hours (commonly used unit in electronics). - **Mission time** for target reliability: $t_m = -\ln(R_{required}) / \lambda$. - **Failure rate conversion:** Always use consistent units (per hour, per year, per cycle). - **Statistical independence** is a required assumption for series/parallel formulas. - **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** determination per IEC 61508 uses **PFDavg** (average Probability of Failure on Demand) computed from reliability data. - **Redundancy** can be **active (parallel)** or **standby** — standby redundancy provides even higher reliability than active. - **k-out-of-n** configuration requires at least $k$ out of $n$ components to function; system reliability computed using binomial probability.

topics.title: Basic Concepts - Evaluation of Network Reliability/Unreliability - Series Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts - Evaluation of Network Reliability/Unreliability - Series Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Reliability and Unreliability

In the context of **System Safety Engineering** and **Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA)**, the foundational quantitative metric for any component or system is **Reliability**. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Qualitative and Quantitative Approach in Safety*:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Reliability $R(t)$** is defined as the conditional probability that a component or system will perform its intended function satisfactorily for a specified period of time $[0, t]$, under stated operating conditions, *given that it was operational at time $t = 0$**.
>
> **Unreliability $F(t)$** is the complementary probability that the component or system will *fail* during the same interval $[0, t]$.

Mathematically, the two are linked by the **Law of Total Probability**:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$

Since the system *must* either survive or fail within the interval, these two events form an **exhaustive and mutually exclusive pair**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Distinction (KTU Frequently Asked)**
> - **Reliability** is a *future-looking, time-dependent* survival probability.
> - **Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$** is an *instantaneous* conditional rate of failure, expressed in failures per hour (or per million hours, **FPMH**).
> - **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** is the *expected* lifetime, expressed in hours.

### 1.2 What is a Series System? (The KTU Canonical Definition)

A **Series System** (also called a *weakest-link system* or *non-redundant system*) is one in which **the successful operation of the system requires the successful operation of EVERY component** within it. The system fails if *any single component* fails.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Formal Definition of Series Configuration**
> "Two or more components are said to be connected in **series reliability-wise** if the failure of any one component causes the failure of the entire system, regardless of the state (working or failed) of the other components."

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy: The Bulletproof Chain of a Suspension Bridge**

Imagine a heavy suspension bridge hanging over a deep gorge. The bridge deck is supported by a **single continuous chain of links**, where the entire load is borne by all links together. If *even one* link in that chain snaps — corroded, fatigued, or overloaded — the entire bridge collapses. The bridge is only as strong as its **weakest link**.

This is precisely the operating philosophy of a **Series System** in reliability engineering:
- Every component is *load-bearing* for the system's survival.
- The component with the **lowest reliability** dominates the system's overall reliability.
- Adding more components in series *always decreases* the overall system reliability.

> [!EXAMPLE] **Industrial Examples of Series Systems**
> - A **refrigerator**: Compressor $\rightarrow$ Condenser $\rightarrow$ Expansion Valve $\rightarrow$ Evaporator (any one failing = no cooling).
> - A **spacecraft launch sequence**: Stage 1 $\rightarrow$ Stage 2 $\rightarrow$ Stage 3 (any one failing = mission loss).
> - A **car braking system**: Brake pedal $\rightarrow$ Master cylinder $\rightarrow$ Brake line $\rightarrow$ Wheel cylinder $\rightarrow$ Brake pad.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Units (As per KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $R(t)$ | Reliability at time $t$ | Dimensionless (Probability: $0 \le R \le 1$) |
| $F(t)$ | Unreliability (CDF of failure) | Dimensionless (Probability) |
| $\lambda$ | Constant Failure Rate | Failures / hour (or **FPMH**) |
| $h(t)$ | Instantaneous Hazard Rate | Failures / hour |
| $\text{MTTF}$ | Mean Time To Failure | Hours |
| $f(t)$ | Probability Density of Failure | Per hour |

---

### Key Takeaway from Section 1

A **Series System** has *no redundancy*. Its reliability is governed by the **product rule** of probabilities, and its MTTF is governed by the **additivity of failure rates**. Students must internalize the fact that **adding components in series always reduces reliability**, which is a counterintuitive but critical safety-engineering insight.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Architecture of a Series System

Consider a system consisting of $n$ components arranged in a *series reliability configuration*. The components are indexed as $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$. Let the random variable $T_i$ denote the **time-to-failure** of the $i^{\text{th}}$ component.

The system's time-to-failure $T_s$ is the minimum of all individual component lifetimes:

$$T_s = \min(T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n)$$

This is the **fundamental mathematical statement of a series system**: the system survives only as long as *every* component survives.

### 2.2 Assumptions (Mandatory for KTU Derivations)

For the canonical series system reliability formula to hold, the following **assumptions of statistical independence** and operational conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Component Independence**: The failure of one component does not influence the failure probability of another. Formally, for any subset of indices $S \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$:

$$\Pr\left(\bigcap_{i \in S} A_i\right) = \prod_{i \in S} \Pr(A_i)$$

2. **Constant Operating Conditions**: Stress, temperature, vibration, and load remain within design limits throughout the mission profile.

3. **Non-Repairable / One-Shot Mission**: Components are not repaired during the operational interval $[0, t]$ (typical for space missions, missile flights, and emergency shutdown systems).

4. **Two-State Components**: Each component is either *fully functional* or *fully failed* (binary state assumption).

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' — Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: Define the success event.**
The system $S$ succeeds at time $t$ *if and only if* every component $i$ succeeds at time $t$. Let $S_i$ be the event "Component $i$ survives to time $t$". Then:

$$S_{\text{system}} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} S_i$$

**Step 2: Apply the Multiplication Rule for Independent Events.**
Since we assume statistical independence of component failures:

$$R_{\text{series}}(t) = \Pr(S_{\text{system}}) = \Pr\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} S_i\right) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \Pr(S_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**
Because every $R_i(t) \in [0, 1]$, the product of $n$ such numbers is *always less than or equal to* the smallest of them. This proves algebraically that **series reliability degrades with each added component**.

**Step 4: Compute unreliability.**

$$F_{\text{series}}(t) = 1 - R_{\text{series}}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

> [!NOTE] **Important Engineering Insight**
> For a series system, $F_{\text{series}}(t) \neq \sum F_i(t)$. The unreliabilities **do not add** — they combine in a more complex non-linear fashion through the complement of the product.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every KTU-board-relevant formula** for series systems. Memorize this verbatim for ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Valid For / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Series Reliability (Product Rule)** | $R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Statistically independent, non-repairable components |
| 2 | **Series Unreliability** | $F_s(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Complement of $R_s(t)$ |
| 3 | **Component Reliability (Exponential)** | $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | Constant failure rate $\lambda_i$ |
| 4 | **Series Reliability (Exponential)** | $R_s(t) = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right)$ | All components have constant $\lambda_i$ |
| 5 | **Equivalent Failure Rate (Series)** | $\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Additivity of constant rates |
| 6 | **MTTF of Series System** | $\text{MTTF}_s = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$ | Exponential failure distributions |
| 7 | **Lower Bound Approximation** | $R_s(t) \approx 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i(t)$ | Valid when all $F_i(t) \ll 1$ (rare-event regime) |
| 8 | **Worst-Case Bound** | $R_s(t) \ge \min_i R_i(t)$ | General lower bound for any series system |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Note)**
> The approximation $R_s \approx 1 - \sum F_i$ is **only valid when each $F_i$ is small** (typically $< 0.1$). For highly reliable components (e.g., aerospace), this is the standard engineering approximation. Using it for high-failure-probability scenarios is a common KTU mark-deduction point.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Series system analysis is the **backbone of safety-critical reliability assessment** in:
- **Avionics**: Every flight control computer, sensor, and actuator on a fly-by-wire aircraft is in series for mission success.
- **Nuclear Reactor Safety**: The reactor protection chain (sensors $\rightarrow$ logic $\rightarrow$ scram rods) is a textbook series system.
- **Medical Devices**: A defibrillator's reliability is the product of its battery, capacitor, control circuit, and pad integrity.
- **Process Safety (KTU Chemical Engineering Cross-Application)**: A typical Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) system — rupture disk in series with a relief valve — is a series system for overpressure protection.

---

### Key Takeaway from Section 2

The **multiplicative product rule** is the *defining* mathematical identity of a series system. The **additivity of constant failure rates** is the corollary that makes MTTF analysis tractable. These two facts form the foundation for the derivations in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **exhaustive mathematical derivations** required to answer KTU 14-mark derivations and numerical problems.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Series System Reliability from First Principles

**Given:**
- A system $S$ consists of $n$ components in series.
- Each component $i$ has a reliability function $R_i(t) = \Pr(T_i > t)$.
- All components are statistically independent.

**To Prove:** $R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$.

**Proof:**

By the definition of a series system, the system operates at time $t$ *if and only if* every component operates at time $t$. Let $A_i$ denote the event "Component $i$ operates successfully at time $t$". Then the event "System operates successfully at time $t$" is the intersection of all $A_i$:

$$A_s = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} A_i$$

Taking probabilities of both sides:

$$R_s(t) = \Pr(A_s) = \Pr\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} A_i\right)$$

By the **multiplication rule for statistically independent events**, the joint probability factors into the product of marginal probabilities:

$$\Pr\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} A_i\right) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \Pr(A_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdot R_3(t) \cdots R_n(t)}$$

**$\blacksquare$**

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Additivity of Failure Rates in a Series System

**Given:**
- All $n$ components follow the **exponential failure distribution**.
- Component $i$ has constant failure rate $\lambda_i$.
- The component reliability is $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$.

**To Prove:** The series system has an equivalent constant failure rate equal to the sum of component failure rates.

**Proof:**

Substituting the exponential form into the series reliability product rule:

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

Using the exponential identity $\prod e^{a_i} = e^{\sum a_i}$:

$$R_s(t) = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} t}$$

where the equivalent failure rate is defined as:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \cdots + \lambda_n}$$

**Interpretation:** In a series system, the failure rates of individual components *add directly*, which is a remarkable and elegant result specific to the exponential (constant-rate) assumption. This makes the system itself behave as a single "lumped" component with a single, larger failure rate.

**$\blacksquare$**

### 3.3 Derivation 3: MTTF of a Series System

**Given:** All $n$ components have constant failure rates, so $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} t}$.

**To Prove:** $\text{MTTF}_s = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$.

**Proof:**

The Mean Time To Failure is defined as the **expected value of the time-to-failure random variable** $T_s$:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = E[T_s] = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f_s(t) \, dt$$

Alternatively, using the *survivor-function* formulation (a standard reliability identity):

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_0^{\infty} R_s(t) \, dt$$

Substituting $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} t}$:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} t} \, dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} t}}{-\lambda_{\text{eq}}} \right]_0^{\infty} = \left( 0 - \frac{1}{-\lambda_{\text{eq}}} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \cdots + \lambda_n}}$$

**Key Engineering Consequence:** Because failure rates add, the MTTF of a series system is *always less than* the MTTF of its weakest (lowest-MTTF) component. This is the mathematical proof of the *weakest-link* principle.

**$\blacksquare$**

### 3.4 Worked Example: Quantitative Reliability Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU 14-Mark Type):**

A safety-critical system for an offshore oil platform consists of **3 components in series reliability configuration**, with the following Weibull-distributed reliability functions over a 1-year mission:

- Component A: $R_A(t) = e^{-0.0005 \, t}$ (pressure sensor)
- Component B: $R_B(t) = e^{-0.0008 \, t}$ (logic solver)
- Component C: $R_C(t) = e^{-0.0002 \, t}$ (shutdown actuator)

where $t$ is measured in hours. Calculate:
1. System reliability after 1000 hours.
2. Equivalent system failure rate.
3. MTTF of the system.
4. Probability of system failure in the first year (8760 hours).

**Solution:**

**Part 1: Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours**

Compute each component's reliability:

$$R_A(1000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$R_B(1000) = e^{-0.0008 \times 1000} = e^{-0.8} = 0.4493$$

$$R_C(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

Apply the product rule:

$$R_s(1000) = 0.6065 \times 0.4493 \times 0.8187 = 0.2231$$

**Part 2: Equivalent Failure Rate**

Using the additivity result:

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = 0.0005 + 0.0008 + 0.0002 = 0.0015 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Part 3: Mean Time To Failure**

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{0.0015} = 666.67 \text{ hours} \approx 27.78 \text{ days}$$

**Part 4: Probability of System Failure in 1 Year (8760 hours)**

$$F_s(8760) = 1 - R_s(8760) = 1 - e^{-0.0015 \times 8760} = 1 - e^{-13.14} \approx 1 - 1.97 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$F_s(8760) \approx 0.999998 \text{ (essentially certain failure)}$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Interpretation**
> The system MTTF is only **~28 days**, despite each component having a seemingly low individual failure rate. This starkly illustrates the *weakest-link* principle: a single highly-reliable component (Actuator C, $\lambda = 0.0002$) is dragged down by the less-reliable logic solver (B, $\lambda = 0.0008$). The series configuration is the dominant factor limiting the system's life.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Failure Rate Additivity Proof Verification

| Component | $\lambda_i$ (per hour) | $R_i(1000)$ | Contribution to $\lambda_{\text{eq}}$ | $1/\lambda_i$ (MTTF) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A — Pressure Sensor | 0.0005 | 0.6065 | 33.3% | 2000 hours |
| B — Logic Solver | 0.0008 | 0.4493 | 53.3% | 1250 hours |
| C — Shutdown Actuator | 0.0002 | 0.8187 | 13.4% | 5000 hours |
| **System** | **0.0015** | **0.2231** | **100%** | **666.67 hours** |

The matrix confirms that the system MTTF (666.67 h) is *less than* the MTTF of the worst individual component (Logic Solver at 1250 h), which is the signature of a series configuration.

---

### Key Takeaway from Section 3

The three foundational identities — **(1) product rule for reliability, (2) additivity of failure rates, (3) harmonic-mean-like behavior of MTTF** — are the complete analytical toolkit for any KTU 14-mark problem on series systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Series System Reliability Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a generic 3-component series system, showing the flow of mission success and the multiplication of component-level reliabilities into system-level reliability.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Mission Demand] --> B[Component 1: Lambda 1]
    B --> C[Component 2: Lambda 2]
    C --> D[Component 3: Lambda 3]
    D --> E[System Output Success]

    B -.-> F[R1 of t = exp to power minus Lambda 1 times t]
    C -.-> G[R2 of t = exp to power minus Lambda 2 times t]
    D -.-> H[R3 of t = exp to power minus Lambda 3 times t]

    E -.-> I[System Reliability: R series = R1 x R2 x R3]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I -.-> J[Equivalent Lambda eq = Lambda 1 plus Lambda 2 plus Lambda 3]
    J -.-> K[MTTF system = 1 divided by Lambda eq]
```

**How to Read the Diagram:**

- **Solid arrows** (top row): Represent the *flow of operational success*. Mission demand enters Component 1, must pass through Component 2, then Component 3, before delivering system output.
- **Dotted arrows** (right column): Represent the *analytical relationships* — each component's individual reliability expression contributes multiplicatively to the overall system reliability.
- The final aggregate nodes ($I$, $J$, $K$) summarize the three KTU-board formulas derived in Section 3.

### 4.2 Failure Propagation Diagram (Fault Tree Perspective)

In Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — a key topic in this same KTU Module — a series system corresponds to an **OR-gate** connecting all component failure events at the top level. This is the *dual* representation of the reliability block diagram.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Top[TOP EVENT: System Failure]
    OR1[OR GATE: Any Component Failure Triggers System Failure]
    E1[Event 1: Component 1 Fails]
    E2[Event 2: Component 2 Fails]
    E3[Event 3: Component 3 Fails]
    E4[Event 4: Component N Fails]

    Top --> OR1
    OR1 --> E1
    OR1 --> E2
    OR1 --> E3
    OR1 --> E4

    E1 -.-> P1[F1 of t = 1 minus R1 of t]
    E2 -.-> P2[F2 of t = 1 minus R2 of t]
    E3 -.-> P3[F3 of t = 1 minus R3 of t]
    E4 -.-> P4[Fn of t = 1 minus Rn of t]
```

> [!NOTE] **Cross-Reference to Module 3 Topics**
> This fault-tree representation is *the same* series system, viewed from the failure side. KTU frequently asks students to convert between the **reliability block diagram (success-oriented)** and the **fault tree (failure-oriented)** representations. Both yield identical numerical answers for $R_s$ and $F_s$.

### 4.3 Hazard-Rate Behavior Diagram

For a series system with constant-rate components, the system hazard function is **flat (constant)** over time, as shown in the following schematic.

```mermaid
graph LR
    H[Hazard Rate h of t] --> Const[Constant Value Lambda eq]
    Const --> Sum[Sum of all Lambda i]
    Sum --> MTTF[Inverse gives MTTF]

    H -.-> Bathtub[BATHTUB CURVE CONTRAST: Non-exponential components show h increasing or decreasing with t]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Constant-Rate Caveat**
> The *additivity of failure rates* and the *flat hazard function* are **only valid under the exponential distribution assumption**. If components follow Weibull, lognormal, or gamma distributions, the hazard function is *not* constant, and the system reliability cannot be expressed as a simple exponential. KTU problems usually state explicitly which distribution applies.

---

### Key Takeaway from Section 4

Visual representations help clarify that the **series system is structurally an "all-or-nothing" architecture**, mathematically a *product of probabilities*, statistically an *OR-gate in failure space*, and physically a *sum of failure rates under the exponential assumption*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> Define a **series system** in the context of reliability engineering. State the formula for the reliability of a series system comprising $n$ statistically independent components.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **series system** is a configuration in which the system functions successfully *only if every constituent component functions successfully*. The failure of any single component causes the failure of the entire system.

For $n$ statistically independent components with individual reliabilities $R_1(t), R_2(t), \ldots, R_n(t)$, the system reliability is given by the **product rule**:

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdots R_n(t)$$

Since each $R_i(t) \in [0, 1]$, the system reliability is always less than or equal to the reliability of the weakest component — this is the *weakest-link principle*. **(3 marks: definition 1.5, formula 1.5)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

> For a series system where all components follow the **exponential failure distribution** with constant failure rates $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$, derive the expression for the **equivalent failure rate** and the **MTTF** of the system.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For component $i$ with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$, the reliability is $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$. The series system reliability is:

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} t}$$

Hence, the **equivalent failure rate** is the sum of individual rates:

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

The **Mean Time To Failure** is:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$

**(3 marks: 1.5 for equivalent lambda, 1.5 for MTTF formula)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question carries internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** A safety shutdown system for a chemical reactor consists of **four components connected in series**. The constant failure rates are:
> - Pressure Transmitter: $\lambda_1 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ /hour
> - Solenoid Valve: $\lambda_2 = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ /hour
> - Logic Solver: $\lambda_3 = 8 \times 10^{-5}$ /hour
> - Shutdown Actuator: $\lambda_4 = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ /hour
>
> Calculate:
> 1. The equivalent failure rate of the system.
> 2. The system reliability for a mission time of 500 hours.
> 3. The MTTF of the system.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the above system, calculate:
> 1. The unreliability of the system at $t = 1000$ hours.
> 2. The probability that the system will *not* fail in the first 2000 hours.
> 3. The time at which the system reliability drops to **0.50** (i.e., the *median life* $t_{0.5}$).

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution:**

**(1) Equivalent failure rate** (additivity formula):

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \lambda_4$$

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = (5 \times 10^{-5}) + (2 \times 10^{-4}) + (8 \times 10^{-5}) + (1 \times 10^{-4})$$

Converting to a common power of 10 (all in $10^{-5}$):

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 20 \times 10^{-5} + 8 \times 10^{-5} + 10 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = 43 \times 10^{-5} = 4.3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**(2 marks for equivalent lambda)**

**(2) System reliability at $t = 500$ hours:**

$$R_s(500) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} \times 500} = e^{-(4.3 \times 10^{-4}) \times 500} = e^{-0.215}$$

Using the exponential value $e^{-0.215} \approx 0.8065$:

$$\boxed{R_s(500) \approx 0.8065 \text{ (i.e., 80.65%})}$$

**(3 marks for reliability calculation)**

**(3) Mean Time To Failure:**

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{4.3 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_s \approx 2325.58 \text{ hours} \approx 96.9 \text{ days}}$$

**(2 marks for MTTF)**

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution:**

**(1) Unreliability at $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$F_s(1000) = 1 - R_s(1000) = 1 - e^{-(4.3 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1000} = 1 - e^{-0.43}$$

$$e^{-0.43} \approx 0.6506$$

$$\boxed{F_s(1000) \approx 1 - 0.6506 = 0.3494 \text{ (i.e., 34.94%})}$$

**(2 marks for unreliability)**

**(2) Probability that the system does NOT fail in the first 2000 hours:**

This is precisely $R_s(2000)$:

$$R_s(2000) = e^{-(4.3 \times 10^{-4}) \times 2000} = e^{-0.86}$$

$$e^{-0.86} \approx 0.4232$$

$$\boxed{R_s(2000) \approx 0.4232 \text{ (i.e., 42.32%})}$$

**(2 marks for survival probability)**

**(3) Median life $t_{0.5}$:**

Set $R_s(t_{0.5}) = 0.50$:

$$e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} \, t_{0.5}} = 0.50$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\lambda_{\text{eq}} \, t_{0.5} = \ln(0.50) = -0.6931$$

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{0.6931}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{0.6931}{4.3 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{0.5} \approx 1611.86 \text{ hours} \approx 67.2 \text{ days}}$$

**(3 marks for median life; 1 mark for the ln(0.5) identity)**

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Series Systems**
> - **Most common mark-loss error:** Students compute $\text{MTTF}_i = 1/\lambda_i$ for each component and then *average* them. **This is wrong.** The correct operation is the *harmonic mean*, equivalent to $1/\sum \lambda_i$, which is much smaller than the arithmetic mean.
> - **Second common error:** Confusing $R_s(t)$ with $1 - \sum F_i(t)$. The unreliabilities **do not add directly**; the reliabilities multiply, and unreliability is the complement.
> - **Third common error:** Forgetting to convert units (e.g., failure rate in FPMH vs. per hour) before applying $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with suitable derivations why the **failure rates of components in a series system add up** under the exponential failure distribution assumption. State clearly all underlying assumptions.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A cardiac pacemaker consists of **three components in series**: a battery ($\lambda_1 = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /hour), an electronic control circuit ($\lambda_2 = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /hour), and a lead/electrode assembly ($\lambda_3 = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ /hour). The device is implanted for a mission duration of **5 years (43,800 hours)**. Determine:
> 1. The system MTTF.
> 2. The reliability at the end of 5 years.
> 3. Whether the device satisfies a **95% reliability criterion** at the 5-year mark.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Theoretical Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions** (3 marks for listing clearly):
1. All components follow the **exponential distribution** (constant failure rate).
2. Components are **statistically independent** in their failure behavior.
3. The system is **non-repairable** during the mission.
4. The system fails if and only if any one component fails (series logic).

**Derivation of Failure-Rate Additivity** (4 marks):

For component $i$ with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$:

$$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

By the **product rule for series reliability**:

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

Using the algebraic identity $\prod_i e^{a_i} = e^{\sum_i a_i}$:

$$R_s(t) = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} t}$$

where:

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

Since the system reliability is itself exponential with rate $\lambda_{\text{eq}}$, the system hazard rate is constant and equal to the sum of individual hazard rates. **This completes the proof of failure-rate additivity.**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $\lambda_1 = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /hour (battery)
- $\lambda_2 = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /hour (circuit)
- $\lambda_3 = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ /hour (electrode)
- $t_{\text{mission}} = 5 \text{ years} = 5 \times 8760 = 43,800 \text{ hours}$

**(1) System MTTF** (2 marks):

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = (1 \times 10^{-6}) + (5 \times 10^{-7}) + (2 \times 10^{-7})$$

Converting to a common power of 10:

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = 10 \times 10^{-7} + 5 \times 10^{-7} + 2 \times 10^{-7} = 17 \times 10^{-7} = 1.7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hour}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{1.7 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 588,235.3 \text{ hours} \approx 67.1 \text{ years}$$

**(2) Reliability at 5 years (43,800 hours)** (3 marks):

$$R_s(43800) = e^{-(1.7 \times 10^{-6}) \times 43800} = e^{-0.07446}$$

$$e^{-0.07446} \approx 0.9282$$

**(3) Check against 95% criterion** (2 marks):

$$R_s(43800) \approx 0.9282 < 0.95$$

**Conclusion:** **The device does NOT satisfy the 95% reliability criterion** at the 5-year mark. The probability of survival is only 92.82%, falling short of the required 95% by 2.18 percentage points.

**Engineering Recommendation:** To meet the 95% reliability target, the manufacturer would need to either (i) reduce component failure rates, (ii) introduce **redundancy** (covered in the next module — *Parallel Systems*), or (iii) reduce the mission profile.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Exponential Identity**
> A frequently tested subtlety: students often write $\prod e^{-\lambda_i t}$ and forget to combine the exponents. Always explicitly state the identity $e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}$ and the product-to-sum conversion. Marks are awarded for showing this step clearly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Series Systems**

**Core Definitions:**
- [x] **Series system** = system fails if *any one* component fails.
- [x] **Reliability $R(t)$** = probability of survival to time $t$.
- [x] **Unreliability $F(t)$** = $1 - R(t)$.

**The Four Master Formulas (Memorize These):**

| # | Formula | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | **Product Rule** — reliabilities multiply. |
| 2 | $F_s(t) = 1 - \prod R_i(t)$ | Unreliability is the complement. |
| 3 | $\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | **Additivity** — failure rates add (exponential case). |
| 4 | $\text{MTTF}_s = 1 / \sum \lambda_i$ | MTTF is the *harmonic* sum of inverse rates. |

**Engineering Rules of Thumb:**
- [x] Adding components to a series system **always reduces** system reliability.
- [x] System MTTF $<$ MTTF of the worst individual component.
- [x] System MTTF $= 1/\lambda_{\text{eq}}$ (under exponential assumption only).
- [x] $R_s(t) \ge \min_i R_i(t)$ — the weakest component is the lower bound.
- [x] Approximation $R_s \approx 1 - \sum F_i$ valid only when all $F_i$ are small ($< 0.1$).

**KTU Exam Strategy:**
- [x] Always *state assumptions* before any derivation (independence, exponential, non-repairable).
- [x] Always *show the conversion step* from product to sum of exponents.
- [x] Always *convert units* consistently (hours, FPMH, years).
- [x] Always *interpret* the numerical answer in an engineering context (e.g., "the MTTF is only 28 days, indicating that maintenance intervals should be much shorter").

**Connection to Other Module 3 Topics:**
- [x] Series systems are the *baseline* against which **parallel (redundant) systems** (next sub-topic) are compared.
- [x] Series reliability is used as the **basic event probability** in **Fault Tree Analysis** (OR-gate).
- [x] The exponential assumption links directly to the **Bathtub Curve** and the **useful-life (constant-hazard) region** in Module 2.

**Common Mistake to Avoid:**
- [x] **Never** compute series reliability by adding component reliabilities.
- [x] **Never** compute series unreliability by adding component unreliabilities (use complement of product).
- [x] **Never** apply $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda_{\text{eq}}$ if the components are *not* exponential.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Parallel Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Parallel System (Redundancy Configuration):** > A parallel system (also termed an *active redundant system* or *hot standby system*) is a system-level reliability configuration in which **two or more independent components, subsystems, or units are arranged in parallel branches** such that the system successfully performs its intended function **if and only if at least one of the parallel paths remains operational**. In reliability engineering terminology, the system *fails* only when **ALL** parallel components have failed simultaneously. Mathematically, the system failure event is the **intersection** of the individual component failure events: $$F_{system} = F_1 \cap F_2 \cap F_3 \cap \ldots \cap F_n$$ Equivalently, the system *survives* if at least one path survives, expressed through the **union** of survival events: $$S_{system} = S_1 \cup S_2 \cup S_3 \cup \ldots \cup S_n$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Backup Lifeboats on a Ship":** > Imagine a passenger ship equipped with **three independent lifeboats** (L1, L2, L3). The ship is considered "safe for passengers" as long as **at least one lifeboat is operational and functional**. The ship only "fails" (catastrophic safety failure) when **all three lifeboats** have become non-functional (punctured, swamped, or destroyed). > - L1 works alone → Ship is safe ✓ > - L1 fails but L2, L3 work → Ship is still safe ✓ > - L1, L2 both fail but L3 works → Ship is STILL safe ✓ > - L1, L2, L3 ALL fail → Ship fails ✗ > > This is the exact logic of a parallel system — *redundancy through duplication*. ## 1.3 Key Terminology & Metrics | Terminology | Definition | Standard Symbol | |:---|:---|:---| | **Reliability** | Probability that a component/system performs its intended function under designated conditions for a specified time interval | $R(t)$ | | **Unreliability** | Probability of failure within the time interval, $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | $F(t)$ | | **Failure Rate** | Conditional instantaneous rate of failure for a surviving component | $\lambda$ | | **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure — expected operational lifetime | $MTTF$ | | **Redundancy** | Duplication of critical components to enhance system reliability | $r$ | | **Active Redundancy** | All parallel units operate simultaneously (hot/parallel redundancy) | — | | **Standby Redundancy** | Only one unit operates; others activate upon failure (cold/warm redundancy) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Parallel systems are the *backbone* of quantitative safety engineering. They form the mathematical basis for high-integrity protection systems in **nuclear reactors, aircraft flight controls, chemical process safety, fire protection systems, and emergency shutdown (ESD) systems** — all core application areas of KTU's OESFT837 syllabus. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Theory — Step-by-Step Logic ### Step 1: Probabilistic Foundation - Reliability of a single component over mission time $t$ is $R_i(t) = P(\text{component } i \text{ survives to time } t)$. - The unreliability (failure probability) is $Q_i(t) = 1 - R_i(t)$. - For statistically **independent** components (a key assumption in parallel analysis), joint failure probabilities multiply. ### Step 2: The Failure Condition - A parallel system fails *only* if **every parallel component fails** in the mission time. - Therefore, the system **unreliability** is the product of component unreliabilities: $$Q_{system} = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdot Q_3 \cdot \ldots \cdot Q_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$ ### Step 3: Deriving System Reliability - Since $R_{system} = 1 - Q_{system}$: $$R_{system} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ ### Step 4: The "Why" Behind the Safety Logic - **Why is this used in safety?** Because in safety-critical applications (e.g., SIL-3 / SIL-4 instrumented systems per IEC 61508/61511), a single point of failure is unacceptable. - **How does it achieve safety?** By providing **functional redundancy** — even if one channel fails, the protective function is maintained by the other(s). - **Trade-off:** Parallel redundancy increases reliability, complexity, cost, and may introduce common-cause failures (CCF). ### Step 5: Special Case — Identical Parallel Components When all $n$ components have identical reliability $R$: $$R_{system} = 1 -
