(1 - R)^n$$ ### Step 6: k-out-of-n (kooN) Generalized Parallel System - A more general formulation: the system functions if **at least k** out of **n** parallel components survive. - This is a **k-out-of-n redundancy** (also written $k/n$ or $kooN$). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas use exponential reliability model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ unless stated otherwise.** | # | System Configuration | Reliability Formula | MTTF Formula | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Single Component** | $R_s = R$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | | 2 | **Two Parallel (active, identical)** | $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^2 = 2R - R^2$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ | | 3 | **n Parallel (active, identical)** | $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{i}$ | | 4 | **n Parallel (active, non-identical)** | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | $MTTF = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty R_s(t)\,dt$ | | 5 | **2 Standby (identical, perfect switching)** | $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{2}{\lambda}$ | | 6 | **n Standby (identical, perfect switching)** | $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\dfrac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{n}{\lambda}$ | | 7 | **k-out-of-n system** | $R_s = \displaystyle\sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j}R^j(1-R)^{n-j}$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=0}^{n-k}\dfrac{1}{n-i}$ | | 8 | **Series-Parallel combined** | Apply parallel formula first, then series | Product of subsystem reliabilities | | 9 | **Exponential model substitution** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda = \text{constant failure rate}$ | | 10 | **Parallel failure rate (non-constant)** | $\lambda_s(t) = \dfrac{f_s(t)}{R_s(t)}$ | — | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Exam Pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse the *active* parallel MTTF (harmonic sum) with the *standby* parallel MTTF (linear sum). A common student error is writing $MTTF = n/\lambda$ for active parallel — that is **wrong**. The correct active-parallel MTTF uses the harmonic series $\sum (1/i)$. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Parallel systems are foundational to: - **Aerospace:** Triple-Modular Redundancy (TMR) in flight control computers - **Nuclear Safety:** Redundant reactor protection systems (4-channel 2oo4 voting) - **Process Safety:** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) systems, fire & gas detection (2oo3 voting) - **Railway Signalling:** SIL-4 vital relay logic, ATP/ATC systems - **Medical Devices:** Defibrillators, anaesthesia machines, infusion pumps - **Data Centers:** N+1 redundant power supplies and cooling systems --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Reliability of an n-Component Active Parallel System ### Given: - A parallel system with $n$ statistically independent components. - Each component $i$ has reliability $R_i$ over mission time $t$. - The system functions as long as at least one component functions. ### To Prove: $$R_{system} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ ### Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step): **Step 1 — Define survival and failure events.** For each component $i$, define: - $S_i$ = event that component $i$ survives (probability $R_i$) - $F_i$ = event that component $i$ fails (probability $1 - R_i$) **Step 2 — Express the system survival event.** The system succeeds if at least one component survives. By the addition rule of probability: $$P(S_{system}) = P(S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \ldots \cup S_n)$$ **Step 3 — Apply De Morgan's Law.** The complement of "at least one survives" is "all fail": $$P(S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \ldots \cup S_n) = 1 - P(F_1 \cap F_2 \cap \ldots \cap F_n)$$ **Step 4 — Apply statistical independence.** Since components fail independently: $$P(F_1 \cap F_2 \cap \ldots \cap F_n) = P(F_1) \cdot P(F_2) \cdot \ldots \cdot P(F_n)$$ **Step 5 — Substitute individual failure probabilities.** $$\prod_{i=1}^{n} P(F_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ **Step 6 — Combine the equations.** $$\boxed{R_{system} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)} \qquad \text{Q.E.D.}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: MTTF of n Active Parallel Components (Exponential) ### Given: - $n$ identical, independent, active parallel components. - Each component has a constant failure rate $\lambda$. - Component reliability: $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. ### To Prove: $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \ldots + \frac{1}{n}\right)$$ ### Derivation: **Step 1 — Write the system reliability function.** For $n$ identical components: $$R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$ **Step 2 — Express MTTF as the integral of reliability.** The mean time to failure of any system is: $$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t)\,dt$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and expand using the binomial theorem.** $$R_s(t) = 1 - \sum_{j=0}^{n}\binom{n}{j}(-1)^j e^{-j\lambda t}$$ $$R_s(t) = 1 - \sum_{j=0}^{n}\binom{n}{j}(-1)^j e^{-j\lambda t}$$ **Step 4 — Re-arrange.** $$R_s(t) = 1 - \left[1 - n e^{-\lambda t} + \binom{n}{2}e^{-2\lambda t} - \ldots + (-1)^n e^{-n\lambda t}\right]$$ $$R_s(t) = n e^{-\lambda t} - \binom{n}{2}e^{-2\lambda t} + \ldots + (-1)^{n+1}e^{-n\lambda t}$$ **Step 5 — General term form.** $$R_s(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{n}(-1)^{j+1}\binom{n}{j}e^{-j\lambda t}$$ **Step 6 — Integrate term-by-term.** $$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(-1)^{j+1}\binom{n}{j}e^{-j\lambda t}\,dt$$ $$MTTF = \sum_{j=1}^{n}(-1)^{j+1}\binom{n}{j}\int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-j\lambda t}\,dt$$ **Step 7 — Evaluate the elementary integral.** $$\int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-j\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{j\lambda}$$ **Step 8 — Substitute and simplify using the identity.** Using the combinatorial identity: $$\sum_{j=1}^{n}(-1)^{j+1}\binom{n}{j}\frac{1}{j} = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \ldots + \frac{1}{n} = H_n$$ where $H_n$ is the $n$-th harmonic number: $$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{j} = \frac{H_n}{\lambda}}$$ **Numerical Examples:** | n | Harmonic Sum $H_n$ | MTTF (in units of $1/\lambda$) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 1.000 | $1.000/\lambda$ | | 2 | 1.500 | $1.500/\lambda$ | | 3 | 1.833 | $1.833/\lambda$ | | 4 | 2.083 | $2.083/\lambda$ | | 5 | 2.283 | $2.283/\lambda$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Doubling the number of active parallel components does **NOT** double the MTTF. Diminishing returns are inherent — the marginal reliability benefit of adding redundant units decreases rapidly. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: k-out-of-n System Reliability ### Given: - $n$ identical, independent components, each with reliability $R$. - The system functions iff at least $k$ of the $n$ components function. ### Derivation: **Step 1 — System works if exactly $j$ components work, for $j = k, k+1, \ldots, n$.** **Step 2 — Probability that exactly $j$ components work.** $$P(\text{exactly } j \text{ work}) = \binom{n}{j}R^j(1-R)^{n-j}$$ **Step 3 — Sum over all valid $j$ values.** $$\boxed{R_{k/n} = \sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j}R^j(1-R)^{n-j}}$$ ### Special Cases: - $k = 1$ → Pure parallel system: $R = 1 - (1-R)^n$ ✓ - $k = n$ → Pure series system: $R = R^n$ ✓ - $k = 2, n = 3$ → 2oo3 voting (typical in process safety): $R = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Example 1 (KTU Standard Type):** > A safety-critical pump system has two identical pumps arranged in active parallel. Each pump has an exponential failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. Mission time $t = 100$ hours. Find: > (a) Reliability of a single pump. > (b) Reliability of the parallel system. > (c) MTTF of the parallel system. > (d) Unreliability improvement factor. ### Solution: **(a) Single-pump reliability:** $$R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.001)(100)} = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$$ **(b) Parallel system reliability:** $$R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (0.0952)^2$$ $$R_s = 1 - 0.00906 = 0.99094 \approx 0.9909$$ **(c) MTTF of parallel system:** $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{1.5}{0.001} = 1500 \text{ hours}$$ **(d) Unreliability improvement factor:** $$\text{UIF} = \frac{1 - R}{1 - R_s} = \frac{0.0952}{0.00906} \approx 10.51$$ The parallel system is **~10.5 times less likely to fail** than a single pump. This dramatic improvement is the engineering justification for redundancy in safety systems. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram — Basic Parallel System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input: System Demand] --> B{Parallel Junction} B --> C1[Component 1
Lambda 1] B --> C2[Component 2
Lambda 2] B --> C3[Component 3
Lambda 3] C1 --> D{Output Junction} C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E[Output: System Function] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style C2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style C3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` **Reading the diagram:** The system succeeds if the signal/power can travel from Input (A) to Output (E) via **at least one** of the parallel branches (C1, C2, or C3). All branches must fail for the system to fail. --- ## 4.2 Active vs Standby Redundancy — Comparative Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph ACTIVE["ACTIVE PARALLEL (Hot Redundancy)"] A1[Power Input] --> A2{Splitter} A2 --> A3[Unit 1: RUNNING] A2 --> A4[Unit 2: RUNNING] A2 --> A5[Unit 3: RUNNING] A3 --> A6{Combiner} A4 --> A6 A5 --> A6 A6 --> A7[System Output] end subgraph STANDBY["STANDBY PARALLEL (Cold/Warm Redundancy)"] B1[Power Input] --> B2{Active Switch} B2 -->|Primary Path| B3[Unit 1: RUNNING] B2 -->|Standby| B4[Unit 2: OFFLINE] B2 -->|Standby| B5[Unit 3: OFFLINE] B3 --> B6{Switch} B4 -.->|On Failure| B6 B5 -.->|On Failure| B6 B6 --> B7[System Output] end style ACTIVE fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style STANDBY fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style A3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style A4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style A5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style B3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style B4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style B5 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** > - **Active parallel:** All units consume power and age simultaneously. The system is more reliable but the units wear out faster. > - **Standby parallel:** Only one unit runs. Reserves activate only on failure (requires a *perfect switch* — see Section 5 pitfalls). The system can have up to $n$-times the single-unit MTTF. --- ## 4.3 k-out-of-n Voting System Architecture (e.g., 2oo3) ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Process Variable
Sensor Input] --> V1[Channel 1
Transmitter] S --> V2[Channel 2
Transmitter] S --> V3[Channel 3
Transmitter] V1 --> LOGIC{Majority Voter
2 of 3 Logic} V2 --> LOGIC V3 --> LOGIC LOGIC -->|Trip Signal| ACT[Final Actuator
Shutdown Valve] ACT --> OUT[Safe State] style S fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style V1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style V2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style V3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style LOGIC fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style ACT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style OUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` **Reading the 2oo3 diagram:** The system takes a shutdown action if **any 2 of 3** independent channels demand it. This rejects spurious single-channel failures (avoiding nuisance trips) while still maintaining safety on a real hazard. --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Series-Parallel Combined System ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT[Input Source] --> A1[Subsystem A1
Series Part 1] A1 --> A2[Subsystem A2
Series Part 2] A2 --> PAR{Parallel Block} PAR --> P1[Parallel Unit 1] PAR --> P2[Parallel Unit 2] PAR --> P3[Parallel Unit 3] P1 --> S2[Subsystem S2] P2 --> S2 P3 --> S2 S2 --> S3[Subsystem S3] S3 --> OUTPUT[System Output] style INPUT fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style OUTPUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style PAR fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style P1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style P2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style P3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style A1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c style A2 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c style S2 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c style S3 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c ``` **Analytical strategy for such combined systems:** Always reduce **innermost parallel blocks first**, then compute the overall product of reliabilities along the series chain. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Q1. Define a parallel system in the context of safety engineering. State the general expression for the reliability of an active parallel system with n independent components. **Model Answer:** > A **parallel system** is a redundancy-based configuration in which the system performs its intended safety function as long as **at least one** of the parallel components remains operational. The system fails only when **all** parallel components have failed. > > For $n$ independent components with reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$, the system reliability is: > > $$R_{system} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ > > For $n$ identical components with reliability $R$: > > $$R_{system} = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$ > > *(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks formula)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Q2. Differentiate between active (parallel) and standby redundancy. Which provides higher MTTF for identical component failure rates, and why? **Model Answer:** > | Feature | Active Redundancy | Standby Redundancy | > |:---|:---|:---| > | Operating state | All units run simultaneously | Only one unit runs; others are offline | > | Aging | All units age equally | Reserves do not age (cold) or age slowly (warm) | > | MTTF | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{i}$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{n}{\lambda}$ | > | Switching | Not required | Perfect switch required | > > **Standby redundancy provides higher MTTF** (linear in $n$) compared to active redundancy (harmonic in $n$). For example, with 3 units, standby gives $3/\lambda$ while active gives $1.833/\lambda$. > > *(3 marks: 2 marks differentiation table, 1 mark conclusion with justification)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** A critical safety system consists of **two pumps arranged in active parallel**. Each pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ per hour. Compute: (i) The reliability of each pump over a mission time of $t = 500$ hours. (ii) The reliability of the parallel system for the same mission time. (iii) The MTTF of a single pump and of the parallel system. (iv) Comment on the safety implication of this redundancy. **[7 marks]** **(b)** What is a **k-out-of-n system**? Derive the general reliability expression for a k-out-of-n system with identical, independent components. Hence, show that the formula reduces to (i) pure parallel, and (ii) pure series as special cases. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — Numerical Solution:** **(i) Single-pump reliability** for $\lambda = 0.0005$/hr, $t = 500$ hr: $$R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0005)(500)} = e^{-0.25}$$ $$R = 0.7788$$ **(ii) Parallel system reliability:** $$R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.7788)^2$$ $$R_s = 1 - (0.2212)^2 = 1 - 0.04893$$ $$R_s = 0.9511$$ **(iii) MTTF calculations:** $$MTTF_{single} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$ $$MTTF_{parallel} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{1.5}{0.0005} = 3000 \text{ hours}$$ **(iv) Safety implication:** The system unreliability drops from $0.2212$ (single pump) to $0.0489$ (parallel) — a **4.5× reduction**. The MTTF improves by 50%. This demonstrates that **parallel redundancy is a powerful technique for improving the reliability and safety** of critical systems, especially in applications like emergency cooling, firewater pumping, and process safety isolation, where failure can lead to catastrophic consequences. --- **Part (b) — k-out-of-n Derivation:** A **k-out-of-n system** is a generalized redundant configuration in which the system functions successfully if and only if **at least k** of the **n** parallel components are operational. **Derivation:** Let $X$ be the number of working components. The system works if $X \geq k$. For identical, independent components with reliability $R$, the probability that exactly $j$ components work is given by the binomial distribution: $$P(X = j) = \binom{n}{j}R^j(1 - R)^{n-j}$$ Summing for all $j$ from $k$ to $n$: $$R_{k/n} = \sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j}R^j(1 - R)^{n-j}$$ **Special case (i) — Pure parallel ($k = 1$):** $$R_{1/n} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\binom{n}{j}R^j(1-R)^{n-j} = 1 - \binom{n}{0}R^0(1-R)^n = 1 - (1-R)^n \checkmark$$ **Special case (ii) — Pure series ($k = n$):** $$R_{n/n} = \sum_{j=n}^{n}\binom{n}{j}R^j(1-R)^{n-j} = \binom{n}{n}R^n(1-R)^0 = R^n \checkmark$$ Thus, the k-out-of-n framework generalizes both series and parallel systems. *(7 marks: 1 mark definition, 3 marks derivation, 1 mark each special case, 1 mark conclusion)* --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between **active (parallel) redundancy** and **standby redundancy**. Derive the MTTF for an n-component active parallel system assuming exponential failure distribution. Comment on the trend of MTTF as $n \to \infty$. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A nuclear-grade emergency cooling system has **three identical pumps in active parallel**. Each pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour. Calculate: (i) System reliability for $t = 1000$ hours. (ii) MTTF of the system. (iii) Probability that **at least two pumps** (i.e., 2oo3 voting) survive 1000 hours. (iv) Compare the reliabilities of 1oo3 (parallel) and 2oo3 (k-out-of-n) configurations and discuss the safety trade-off. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — Active vs Standby Redundancy:** *(See Section 4.2 for the schematic.)* | Aspect | Active Parallel | Standby Parallel | |:---|:---|:---| | All units powered? | Yes | No (only primary is online) | | Switchover logic? | Not required | Required (must be highly reliable) | | Failure rate of reserves | Equal to operating unit | Effectively zero (cold) | | MTTF growth with n | Harmonic: $\propto H_n$ | Linear: $\propto n$ | **MTTF Derivation for Active Parallel (see Section 3.2):** $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{i} = \frac{H_n}{\lambda}$$ **Trend as $n \to \infty$:** The harmonic series $H_n$ grows logarithmically: $H_n \approx \ln(n) + \gamma$, where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ is the Euler-Mascheroni constant. Therefore: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} MTTF = \lim_{n \to \infty}\frac{\ln(n) + \gamma}{\lambda} \to \infty$$ Although MTTF grows without bound, the rate of growth is **logarithmic**, not linear. The marginal benefit of adding redundant units diminishes rapidly — a critical engineering trade-off. --- **Part (b) — Nuclear Cooling System Solution:** **Given:** $n = 3$, $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$/hr, $t = 1000$ hr. **(i) System reliability (1oo3):** $$R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$ $$R_{1oo3} = 1 - (1 - 0.8187)^3 = 1 - (0.1813)^3 = 1 - 0.00596 = 0.9940$$ **(ii) MTTF of 3-pump active parallel:** $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{1.8333}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 9166.7 \text{ hours}$$ **(iii) 2oo3 voting reliability:** $$R_{2oo3} = \sum_{j=2}^{3}\binom{3}{j}R^j(1-R)^{3-j}$$ $$R_{2oo3} = \binom{3}{2}R^2(1-R) + \binom{3}{3}R^3$$ $$R_{2oo3} = 3(0.8187)^2(0.1813) + (0.8187)^3$$ $$R_{2oo3} = 3(0.6703)(0.1813) + 0.5488$$ $$R_{2oo3} = 0.3646 + 0.5488 = 0.9134$$ **(iv) Safety Trade-off Discussion:** - **1oo3 reliability (0.9940) > 2oo3 reliability (0.9134).** - 1oo3 is **more reliable** for the function (any one pump suffices). - 2oo3 is **less prone to spurious trips** because it requires 2 of 3 to agree. - **Safety trade-off:** 1oo3 maximizes availability; 2oo3 maximizes *integrity* (rejects single failures, prevents false trips). In nuclear safety, **2oo3 or 2oo4 voting** is preferred because it balances hazard protection with operational reliability — a spurious trip is also a safety-relevant event (loss of cooling). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Parallel System Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing the MTTF formula:** Writing $MTTF = n/\lambda$ for *active* parallel — that formula is for **standby** redundancy. Active parallel uses the **harmonic series**. Loss: **2-3 marks**. > > 2. **Forgetting independence:** The formula $R_s = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ requires statistical **independence** of components. With common-cause failures (CCF), the formula overestimates reliability. Loss: **1-2 marks**. > > 3. **Order of reduction in series-parallel systems:** Always reduce the **innermost (most deeply nested) block first**. Reversing the order gives the wrong answer. Loss: **2 marks**. > > 4. **Incomplete special-case proof:** When asked to show that k-out-of-n reduces to series/parallel, students often skip the algebraic substitution step. Loss: **1-2 marks**. > > 5. **Missing units:** Always state units (hours, failures/hour) in MTTF and failure rate problems. Loss: **0.5 mark** per occurrence. > > 6. **Ignoring the switch reliability in standby:** Standby formulas assume a *perfect* switch. If switch reliability $R_{sw}$ is given, multiply: $R_{s,total} = R_{sw} \cdot R_{s,standby}$. Loss: **2 marks**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parallel Systems** - ✅ **Definition:** System works if *at least one* parallel path works. - ✅ **Core Reliability Formula:** $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ (general) or $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ (identical). - ✅ **Unreliability:** $Q_s = \prod Q_i$ — failure requires *all* components to fail. - ✅ **Active Parallel MTTF:** $\frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{i}$ — **harmonic series**, not linear. - ✅ **Standby Parallel MTTF:** $\frac{n}{\lambda}$ — **linear**, requires perfect switch. - ✅ **Standby Parallel Reliability:** $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ (Poisson form). - ✅ **k-out-of-n Formula:** $R_{k/n} = \sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j}R^j(1-R)^{n-j}$. - ✅ **Special cases:** $k=1 \Rightarrow$ parallel; $k=n \Rightarrow$ series. - ✅ **Exponential model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $MTTF = 1/\lambda$. - ✅ **Diminishing returns:** Doubling parallel units does NOT double reliability or MTTF. - ✅ **Independence assumption:** Required for multiplication of unreliabilities. CCF must be modeled separately. - ✅ **Always reduce innermost parallel blocks first** in series-parallel combinations. - ✅ **Common systems:** 2oo2, 2oo3, 1oo2, 1oo3 — all special cases of k-out-of-n. - ✅ **Engineering uses:** Trip amplifiers, ESD valves, firewater pumps, reactor protection, fly-by-wire, life-support. ---

topics.title: Series-Parallel Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Series-Parallel Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Series-Parallel System** is a reliability block configuration in which two or more components (or subsystems) are interconnected such that their functional arrangement can be decomposed into distinct series blocks and parallel blocks, ultimately reducing to an equivalent single reliability value. In safety engineering, this classification underpins the **Boolean Reliability Modeling** approach used to quantify system availability, Mean Time To Failure (MTTF), and probability of failure on demand (PFD).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A series system functions only when **every** constituent component functions; a parallel (redundant) system functions as long as **at least one** constituent component functions. A series-parallel system is a hybrid configuration built by combining these two basic structures through Boolean algebra reduction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Series System = "Old Christmas Lights":**
> Imagine a string of old-fashioned incandescent Christmas bulbs wired in a single loop. If **any one** bulb blows, the entire string goes dark because the current path is broken. This is exactly how a *series* configuration behaves in safety systems — one weak link brings down the whole chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Parallel System = "Parallel Escalators in a Metro Station":**
> A busy metro station installs **two parallel escalators** between platforms. As long as *at least one* escalator is operating, passengers can move between floors. Only when **both** escalators fail simultaneously does the pathway collapse. This is the essence of *redundancy* in safety-critical systems.

### 1.3 Why This Matters in Safety Engineering

In Quantitative Safety Analysis (as required under IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 / ISO 12100), engineers must compute the **probability of failure** of protective systems (e.g., Emergency Shutdown Systems, Fire & Gas systems, SIL-rated instrumented loops). Series-Parallel reduction is the **first computational tool** used before advancing to more complex methods like Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) or Markov modeling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric Reminder:**
> - **Reliability (R)** = Probability that a component performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval. Value range: **0 ≤ R ≤ 1**.
> - **Unreliability (F or Q)** = 1 − R.
> - **Failure Rate (λ)** = Number of failures per unit time. Unit: **failures/hour** or **FITs (Failures In Time, 10⁻⁹/hr)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Series System — Structural Logic

A series system contains *n* components connected such that the system succeeds **only if all components succeed**.

**Logic Step-by-Step:**
- Component 1 must work **AND** Component 2 must work **AND** … **AND** Component n must work.
- In Boolean terms: System Success = $X_1 \cap X_2 \cap X_3 \dots \cap X_n$
- Because component failures are treated as **statistically independent events** (a standard KTU assumption for Module 3), the joint probability collapses to the product of marginal probabilities.

**Mathematical Outcome:**
$$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \times \dots \times R_n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Inference:** Adding components to a series system **always decreases** total reliability. This is the engineering motivation behind designing protective systems in **parallel (redundant)** configurations.

### 2.2 Parallel System (Active Redundancy) — Structural Logic

A parallel system contains *n* components such that the system succeeds if **at least one** component succeeds.

**Logic Step-by-Step:**
- The system fails only when **all** components fail simultaneously.
- Compute the unreliability first, then subtract from unity.
- $F_{parallel} = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} F_i$

**Mathematical Outcome:**
$$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i) = 1 - F_1 \times F_2 \times \dots \times F_n$$

### 2.3 Series-Parallel Reduction Methodology

For a hybrid network, the KTU-recommended approach is:

1. **Identify** the top-most series/parallel pattern.
2. **Collapse** each leaf-level block into a single equivalent reliability.
3. **Propagate** upward using the series rule and parallel rule alternately.
4. **Terminate** when only one equivalent reliability value remains for the entire system.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Configuration | Reliability Formula | Equivalent Failure Rate (λ_eq) | KTU Board Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Component** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ | Basic |
| **Series (n components)** | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Most Frequent |
| **Parallel (n components)** | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | $\lambda_p \approx \dfrac{\lambda^2}{2}$ for 2 identical | High-Yield |
| **2-out-of-2 Parallel (1oo2)** | $R_p = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$ | — | Safety Loops |
| **2-out-of-3 Voting (2oo3)** | $R = R_1R_2R_3 + (1-R_1)R_2R_3 + R_1(1-R_2)R_3 + R_1R_2(1-R_3)$ | — | SIS Voting |
| **MTBF Series** | $MTBF_s = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_s}$ | Hours | Maintenance |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless | Operations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Vigilance:**
> - $\lambda$ → failures/hour
> - $t$ → hours (always express in matching units)
> - $R$, $F$, $A$ → dimensionless probability

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SIL Determination (IEC 61508):** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) achieves higher SIL through parallel sensor/logic/valve arrangement (e.g., 1oo2, 2oo3 voting).
- **Aviation Redundancy:** Boeing 737 hydraulic systems run in parallel so failure of one pump doesn't ground the aircraft.
- **Nuclear Reactor Protection:** Multiple shutdown rods in parallel-redundant arrangement satisfy single-failure criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Parallel Reliability Formula

**Starting Premise:** A parallel system of two components fails **only** when both components fail simultaneously. Let $F_1 = (1 - R_1)$ and $F_2 = (1 - R_2)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{System Success Probability } R_p &= 1 - P(\text{Both Fail}) \\
&= 1 - P(\text{Comp 1 Fails}) \times P(\text{Comp 2 Fails}) \\
&= 1 - (1 - R_1) \times (1 - R_2) \\
&= 1 - (1 - R_1 - R_2 + R_1 R_2) \\
&= R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** Line 1 expresses the complementary event rule. Line 2 invokes the independence assumption (events don't influence each other). Line 3 substitutes the unreliability terms. Lines 4–5 expand and simplify the binomial product.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Hybrid Series-Parallel Network

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A safety shutdown system consists of **two pressure sensors in parallel**, the output of which feeds a **single logic solver**, which then drives **two solenoid valves in parallel**. Each sensor has reliability $R_S = 0.95$, the logic solver has $R_L = 0.98$, and each valve has $R_V = 0.92$. Calculate the overall system reliability.

**Step 1 — Compute Sensor Pair (Parallel Block)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{S,eq} &= 1 - (1 - R_{S1})(1 - R_{S2}) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95) \\
&= 1 - (0.05)(0.05) \\
&= 1 - 0.0025 \\
&= 0.9975
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Valve Pair (Parallel Block)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{V,eq} &= 1 - (1 - R_{V1})(1 - R_{V2}) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.92)(1 - 0.92) \\
&= 1 - (0.08)(0.08) \\
&= 1 - 0.0064 \\
&= 0.9936
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the Three Equivalent Blocks in Series**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{system} &= R_{S,eq} \times R_L \times R_{V,eq} \\
&= 0.9975 \times 0.98 \times 0.9936
\end{aligned}
$$

Intermediate multiplication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.9975 \times 0.98 &= 0.97755 \\
0.97755 \times 0.9936 &= 0.97129
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R_{system} \approx 0.9713$ (or **97.13%**)

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The original single-channel reliability would have been $0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.92 \approx 0.8566$ (85.66%). Adding one redundant component in each parallel block raised the overall reliability by approximately **11.47 percentage points** — a dramatic safety improvement.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity to Redundancy

Consider a system of **two identical components**, each with $R = 0.90$.

| Configuration | Formula | Reliability | Failure Probability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single component | R | **0.9000** | 0.1000 |
| Series of two | $R^2$ | **0.8100** | 0.1900 |
| Parallel of two | $1 - (1-R)^2$ | **0.9900** | 0.0100 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Adding redundancy in parallel improves reliability **tenfold** in failure reduction (from 10% to 1%), whereas adding the same component in series **degrades** reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Reliability Block Diagram — Series System

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeIn[Input Signal] --> compA[Component A R equals 0.95]
    compA --> compB[Component B R equals 0.92]
    compB --> compC[Component C R equals 0.90]
    compC --> nodeOut[Output to Load]
```

**Interpretation:** Signal flows sequentially from input through Component A, then B, then C. Any single component failure breaks the chain and the output load is unprotected.

### 4.2 Mermaid Reliability Block Diagram — Parallel System (Redundancy)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeIn[Input Signal] --> splitNode{Signal Splitter}
    splitNode --> compA[Component A R equals 0.95]
    splitNode --> compB[Component B R equals 0.95]
    compA --> mergeNode{Signal Combiner}
    compB --> mergeNode
    mergeNode --> nodeOut[Output to Load]
```

**Interpretation:** The signal splitter feeds both Component A and Component B simultaneously. As long as at least one branch delivers the signal, the combiner passes the output forward. This is the architectural foundation of **1oo2 voting** in safety instrumented systems.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hybrid Series-Parallel Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    sigStart[Process Input] --> sensorBank[Sensor Bank Parallel]
    sensorBank --> logicBlock[Logic Solver Series]
    logicBlock --> finalElement[Final Element Bank Parallel]
    finalElement --> sigEnd[Shutdown Action]

    subgraph blockOne [Parallel Block 1 Sensors]
        s1[Sensor 1 R equals 0.95]
        s2[Sensor 2 R equals 0.95]
    end

    subgraph blockTwo [Series Connection]
        ls[Logic Solver R equals 0.98]
    end

    subgraph blockThree [Parallel Block 2 Valves]
        v1[Valve 1 R equals 0.92]
        v2[Valve 2 R equals 0.92]
    end
```

**Reduction Order for the Examiner:**
1. Compute equivalent reliability of `blockOne` using the parallel rule.
2. Treat `blockTwo` as a single series element.
3. Compute equivalent reliability of `blockThree` using the parallel rule.
4. Multiply the three resulting values using the series rule to obtain the system-level reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define a series system in the context of reliability engineering. State the formula for its overall reliability.**

**Model Answer:**
A series system is one in which all components must function successfully for the entire system to function. The failure of any single component causes system failure. The reliability of a series system containing *n* independent components is the product of individual reliabilities:

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \times \dots \times R_n$$

The more components added in series, the lower the overall reliability. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for formula and explanation)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Distinguish between a parallel system and a series system with respect to failure behavior.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Series System | Parallel System |
|---|---|---|
| System functions when | All components work | At least one component works |
| System fails when | Any one component fails | All components fail simultaneously |
| Reliability formula | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ |
| Adding components | Decreases reliability | Increases reliability |
| Application | Non-critical subsystems | Safety-critical redundant systems |

*(3 marks: tabular comparison with at least four distinct points)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**A safety shutdown system consists of three components arranged as follows: Component A (R = 0.90) and Component B (R = 0.85) are in parallel; this parallel combination is in series with Component C (R = 0.95).**

**(a)** Calculate the reliability of the parallel block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hence compute the overall system reliability and identify the weakest block from a safety perspective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Reliability of Parallel Block A-B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{AB} &= 1 - (1 - R_A)(1 - R_B) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.85) \\
&= 1 - (0.10)(0.15) \\
&= 1 - 0.015 \\
&= 0.985
\end{aligned}
$$

**The reliability of the parallel block is 0.985.** *(7 marks: 4 for formula substitution + 3 for arithmetic)*

**(b) Overall System Reliability (Series Combination):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{system} &= R_{AB} \times R_C \\
&= 0.985 \times 0.95 \\
&= 0.93575
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overall system reliability is approximately 0.936 (93.6%).**

**Weakest Block Identification:**
Although Component B has the lowest individual reliability (0.85), the parallel arrangement of A and B elevates its effective reliability to 0.985. The weakest link in the resulting system is therefore **Component C**, because it operates alone in series — its failure directly collapses the entire system. Mitigation recommendation: place a redundant spare of C in parallel to harden the system further. *(7 marks: 3 for multiplication + 2 for interpretation + 2 for recommendation)*

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**A fire and gas detection loop in a hydrocarbon processing plant uses two identical smoke detectors in parallel (each with reliability R = 0.92), feeding into a single control panel (R = 0.97), which then activates two parallel sprinkler valves (each with R = 0.93).**

**(a)** Compute the reliability of the detector pair and the valve pair separately. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the total system reliability and discuss the safety implication of the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detector Pair Reliability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{det} &= 1 - (1 - R_D)^2 \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.92)^2 \\
&= 1 - (0.08)^2 \\
&= 1 - 0.0064 \\
&= 0.9936
\end{aligned}
$$

**Valve Pair Reliability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{valve} &= 1 - (1 - R_V)^2 \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.93)^2 \\
&= 1 - (0.07)^2 \\
&= 1 - 0.0049 \\
&= 0.9951
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 3.5 for each block computation with clear steps)*

**(b) Total System Reliability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sys} &= R_{det} \times R_{panel} \times R_{valve} \\
&= 0.9936 \times 0.97 \times 0.9951
\end{aligned}
$$

Intermediate multiplication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.9936 \times 0.97 &= 0.96379 \\
0.96379 \times 0.9951 &= 0.95911
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total system reliability ≈ 0.9591 (95.91%).**

**Safety Implication Discussion:**
The system delivers approximately 95.9% reliability — a substantial improvement over a non-redundant baseline. The **single-point vulnerability** is the control panel, which sits alone in the series chain. To achieve higher SIL rating, the panel should be duplicated in parallel with a 1oo2 or 2oo2 voting arrangement, eliminating the single point of failure and further raising reliability toward 99.9% or beyond. *(7 marks: 4 for computation + 3 for engineering discussion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Independence Assumption:** If a student forgets to mention that components are *statistically independent*, deduct **1 mark** in Part B derivations. This is the most common KTU deduction point.
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing up reliability with availability in long-form answers invites mark loss. State clearly which quantity you are computing.
> 3. **Wrong Order of Operations:** When solving series-parallel networks, students often multiply individual reliabilities without first reducing the parallel blocks. Show reduction **stepwise** to capture full marks.
> 4. **Missing Units:** Always state reliability as a probability (dimensionless) and failure rate in **failures/hour** or **FITs**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- A **series system** requires **all** components to function; its reliability is the **product** of individual reliabilities.
- A **parallel (redundant) system** requires **at least one** component to function; its unreliability is the **product** of individual unreliabilities.
- **Series-parallel reduction** proceeds from leaf blocks upward using alternating series and parallel rules.
- The reliability **formula sheet** memorized for KTU 2024 ESE must include $R_s = \prod R_i$, $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$, and exponential decay $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **Equivalent failure rate** for series configuration equals the sum of individual failure rates: $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$.
- **Redundancy** is the primary engineering tool for raising reliability of safety-critical loops; adding parallel channels improves reliability while adding series components degrades it.
- For SIL-rated instrumented systems, voting architectures like **1oo2, 2oo2, and 2oo3** are special cases of parallel arrangements — each yields a distinct reliability profile.
- Always declare the **independence assumption** explicitly in derivations to avoid mark deductions in KTU board valuation.
- Distinguish clearly between **reliability (R)**, **availability (A)**, and **probability of failure on demand (PFD)** when framing answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
