<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — PARTIALLY REDUNDANT SYSTEMS & RELIABILITY EVALUATION

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITION

### 1.1 What is Redundancy in Safety Engineering?

**Redundancy** is the existence of more than one means of accomplishing a given task, where the additional means (the *redundant* element) is provided to ensure that the system continues to perform its intended function even when one or more of its primary elements have failed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Redundancy is the presence of duplicate components, sub-systems, or functional paths within a system such that the failure of any single element does not result in the loss of the system's primary safety function.*

**Mathematical Representation:** If $R_i$ is the reliability of the $i^{th}$ component, then for a system to be classified as redundant, the system reliability $R_s$ must exceed the reliability of the best single component path. This is the defining property of any redundant configuration.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Airplane Twin-Engine Concept

Imagine a four-engine commercial airliner (like a Boeing 747). It can technically fly safely with only **two engines** operational, but it has **four engines** to provide redundancy.

- **Without redundancy:** A single-engine aircraft fails the moment its lone engine fails.
- **With active redundancy:** A twin-engine aircraft can still fly if one engine fails (the other engine "takes over" the thrust function).
- **With partial redundancy:** A two-engine aircraft can even limp back to an airport on just one engine — this is *partial* redundancy because the full thrust capacity is *not* maintained, but the safety function (continued flight) is preserved.

**Key Insight:** Partial redundancy is the most common real-world configuration — systems are designed so that safety-critical functions are duplicated, but performance-critical functions are not.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction — Redundancy ≠ Duplication:** True redundancy requires the redundant element to be **independent** of the primary element. A system with two identical components sharing the same power supply is *not* fully redundant (it is vulnerable to common-mode failure).

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Reliability, R(t):** Probability that a system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval $[0, t]$.
- **Unreliability, F(t) or Q(t):** Probability that the system has failed by time $t$.
- **Failure Rate, λ:** Instantaneous rate of failure (failures per unit time), typically expressed in **failures per million hours (FPMH)**.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average time between successive failures for repairable systems, given by $1/\lambda$.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** Average time to first failure for non-repairable systems, given by $1/\lambda$.
- **Availability, A(t):** Fraction of time the system is operational.

---

### 1.4 Concept of "Partially Redundant"

A **Partially Redundant System** is one in which:
1. Some (but not all) safety-critical paths are duplicated.
2. The system can still tolerate one or more component failures without total functional loss.
3. There is a **defined minimum operating configuration** (MOC) — the smallest set of components that must work for the system to function safely.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When a question asks about "partially redundant systems," always begin your answer by identifying the **minimum cut set** of the system. This is the smallest set of components whose failure causes system failure. Partial redundancy is most easily understood through cut-set analysis.

---

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — TYPES OF REDUNDANCIES

Redundancy is classified along **three primary dimensions**:

### 2.1 Classification by Activation Mode

#### (a) Active Redundancy (Hot / Parallel Redundancy)

All redundant units operate **simultaneously** from time $t = 0$. The system succeeds as long as **at least one** unit succeeds.

- **Application:** Flight control computers on Airbus A320 (3 active computers vote in 2-out-of-3 majority).
- **Advantage:** No switchover delay; immediate fault tolerance.
- **Disadvantage:** All units consume power and are subject to wear simultaneously.

#### (b) Standby Redundancy (Cold / Passive Redundancy)

The redundant unit is **inactive** until the primary unit fails, at which point a **switching mechanism** activates it.

- **Application:** Emergency power generators in hospitals; backup fire pumps.
- **Advantage:** Redundant unit does not age during normal operation; lower power consumption.
- **Disadvantage:** Switch failure can disable the entire redundant path; switchover introduces a small delay.

#### (c) Warm Redundancy (Hybrid)

The redundant unit operates in a **reduced-power state** (e.g., spinning up but not engaged). Reduces switchover delay while still limiting wear.

---

### 2.2 Classification by Redundancy Level

| Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Component Redundancy** | Individual components duplicated within a unit | Dual capacitors in a power supply |
| **Unit Redundancy** | Entire functional units duplicated | Two identical pumps in parallel |
| **System Redundancy** | Entire system duplicated | Twin-boiler steam plant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Partial"?** A partially redundant system uses **component or unit-level redundancy selectively** — only for the components deemed safety-critical, not for the entire system. This is a cost-optimization strategy.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essentials** and must be memorized.

| # | Configuration | Reliability Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Component | $R_s = R$ | Baseline |
| 2 | Series System (n components) | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All must work |
| 3 | Parallel System (n identical components) | $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ | At least one must work |
| 4 | Active Redundant (2 components) | $R_s = 1 - Q_1 Q_2$ | $Q_i = 1 - R_i$ |
| 5 | Standby Redundant (2 components, perfect switch) | $R_s = R_1 + (1-R_1) \cdot R_2$ | Switch is ideal |
| 6 | Standby with imperfect switch (reliability $R_{sw}$) | $R_s = R_1 + R_{sw}(1-R_1) R_2$ | Switch may fail |
| 7 | 2-out-of-3 Majority Voting | $R_s = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ | Any 2 of 3 must work |
| 8 | Time-dependent reliability (exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Constant failure rate |
| 9 | Unreliability (exponential) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative distribution |
| 10 | Probability density function | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | PDF of failure time |
| 11 | MTBF / MTTF | $MTBF = 1/\lambda$ | For exponential |
| 12 | Conditional probability (general) | $P(A \vert B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Bayes' building block |

**Note on Notation:** $Q_i$ denotes the **unreliability** of component $i$, defined as $Q_i = 1 - R_i$. This is the **complement** of reliability.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Industry:** Triple-redundant safety injection systems (2-out-of-3 voting).
- **Aerospace:** Quadruple-redundant flight computers in Boeing 777 (each from a different vendor to prevent common-mode failure).
- **Chemical Process Safety:** Triple Modular Redundant (TMR) emergency shutdown systems (ESD).
- **Automotive:** Dual-circuit braking systems (loss of one circuit still allows 50% braking).
- **Fire Protection:** Redundant sprinkler risers with check valves.

---

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Parallel System Reliability (Active Redundancy, n identical components)

We begin from first principles using the **axioms of probability**.

**Step 1 — Define the event of system success.**

For a parallel system of $n$ components, the system succeeds if **at least one** component works. The complement event is that **all components fail**.

$$
A_s = \text{System Success}
$$

$$
\overline{A_s} = \text{All components fail} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \overline{A_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the probability of the complement.**

For **independent** components, the probability of simultaneous failure is the product of individual failure probabilities:

$$
P(\overline{A_s}) = P\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \overline{A_i}\right) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\overline{A_i}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i
$$

**Step 3 — Use the complement rule.**

Since $R_s = 1 - Q_s$ where $Q_s$ is the system unreliability:

$$
R_s = 1 - P(\overline{A_s}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q_i = 1 - R_i$ and assume identical components ($R_i = R$).**

$$
R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^n
$$

This is the **canonical parallel reliability formula** appearing in every KTU reliability exam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** When $n = 1$ (single component), $R_s = 1 - (1-R) = R$ ✓. When $R = 1$ (perfect component), $R_s = 1$ ✓. When $R = 0$ (always fails), $R_s = 0$ ✓. Formula is dimensionally and logically consistent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Conditional Probability Method for Network Reliability

The **Conditional Probability Method (CPM)** is the most general tool for evaluating reliability of any network (series, parallel, or bridge). It is sometimes called the **"Factor Method"** or **"Decomposition Method."**

**Statement of the Method:**

For a network with components $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$, the system reliability can be expressed by conditioning on the state of any single component $C_i$:

$$
R_s = R_i \cdot R_s(\text{System works} \vert C_i \text{ works}) + (1 - R_i) \cdot R_s(\text{System works} \vert C_i \text{ fails})
$$

$$
R_s = R_i \cdot R_s^{(1)} + Q_i \cdot R_s^{(0)}
$$

where:
- $R_i$ = reliability of component $C_i$ (works)
- $Q_i = 1 - R_i$ = unreliability of $C_i$ (fails)
- $R_s^{(1)}$ = system reliability given that $C_i$ works
- $R_s^{(0)}$ = system reliability given that $C_i$ fails

---

**Step-by-Step Derivation Using Probability Axioms:**

**Step 1 — Apply the Law of Total Probability.**

Let $A$ = "system works" and let $B$ = "component $C_i$ works" with $\overline{B}$ = "$C_i$ fails". Since $B \cup \overline{B}$ is the entire sample space:

$$
P(A) = P(A \vert B) \cdot P(B) + P(A \vert \overline{B}) \cdot P(\overline{B})
$$

**Step 2 — Identify each term with the reliability notation.**

$$
P(A) = P(A \vert \text{works}) \cdot P(\text{works}) + P(A \vert \text{fails}) \cdot P(\text{fails})
$$

$$
R_s = R_s^{(1)} \cdot R_i + R_s^{(0)} \cdot (1 - R_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply recursively to each conditional sub-network.**

The sub-networks $R_s^{(1)}$ and $R_s^{(0)}$ are themselves networks with fewer components, and the same formula is applied again. This recursion continues until only single components remain.

---

**Worked Example: Two-of-Two Parallel Network (Active Redundancy)**

A system has two components $C_1$ and $C_2$ in parallel, with $R_1 = 0.9$ and $R_2 = 0.8$. Compute $R_s$ using CPM.

**Step 1 — Condition on $C_1$.**

$$
R_s = R_1 \cdot R_s^{(1)} + Q_1 \cdot R_s^{(0)}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $R_s^{(1)}$ (system given $C_1$ works).**

If $C_1$ works, the system is "as good as working" regardless of $C_2$. So $R_s^{(1)} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate $R_s^{(0)}$ (system given $C_1$ fails).**

If $C_1$ fails, only $C_2$ can carry the load. So $R_s^{(0)} = R_2 = 0.8$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve.**

$$
R_s = (0.9)(1) + (0.1)(0.8) = 0.9 + 0.08 = 0.98
$$

**Verification with direct formula:** $R_s = 1 - Q_1 Q_2 = 1 - (0.1)(0.2) = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$ ✓

---

**Worked Example: Bridge Network (Wheatstone Configuration)**

A classic 5-component bridge network has components $C_1, C_2$ (top and bottom series arms), $C_3$ (left vertical), $C_4$ (right vertical), and $C_5$ (diagonal bridge). Apply CPM conditioning on $C_5$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the network using $C_5$.**

$$
R_s = R_5 \cdot R_s^{(1)} + Q_5 \cdot R_s^{(0)}
$$

**Step 2 — When $C_5$ works (reliability $R_5$):**

The network reduces to two parallel paths: $(C_1, C_2)$ in series parallel with $(C_3, C_4)$ in series. This gives:

$$
R_s^{(1)} = 1 - (1 - R_1 R_2)(1 - R_3 R_4)
$$

**Step 3 — When $C_5$ fails (probability $Q_5$):**

The bridge becomes two independent series paths: $C_1 C_3$ and $C_2 C_4$ in parallel.

$$
R_s^{(0)} = 1 - (1 - R_1 R_3)(1 - R_2 R_4)
$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
R_s = R_5 \cdot [1 - (1 - R_1 R_2)(1 - R_3 R_4)] + Q_5 \cdot [1 - (1 - R_1 R_3)(1 - R_2 R_4)]
$$

This is the **canonical CPM solution for the bridge network** — a classic KTU 14-mark question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Time-Dependent Reliability (Exponential Distribution)

The **exponential distribution** is the most fundamental time-to-failure model in reliability engineering, valid for components exhibiting the **"memoryless" property** during their useful life.

**Step 1 — State the assumption: Constant Failure Rate.**

The fundamental assumption is that the failure rate $\lambda$ is **constant** with respect to time (no wear-in, no wear-out — the so-called "useful life" region of the bathtub curve).

$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the reliability function in differential form.**

By definition, the failure rate is:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

This rearranges to:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \cdot dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$ with initial condition $R(0) = 1$.**

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} d\tau
$$

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t
$$

Since $\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0$:

$$
\ln R(t) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

This is the **canonical exponential reliability function**, valid for $t \geq 0$ with $\lambda > 0$.

---

**Step 5 — Derive related quantities.**

The cumulative distribution function (unreliability):

$$
F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

The probability density function (differentiate $F(t)$ with respect to $t$):

$$
f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}
$$

The mean time to failure (MTTF):

$$
MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

Using integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$:

$$
MTTF = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

$$
MTTF = 0 + \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{MTTF = MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: System Reliability with Time-Dependent Components

For a series system with $n$ exponential components of failure rates $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$:

$$
R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t} = e^{-\lambda_s t}
$$

where the **system failure rate** is:

$$
\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i
$$

This is a key result: **failure rates add for series systems.**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked for "time-dependent reliability," always write the answer in the form $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$ explicitly. Many students lose marks by leaving answers in the form $\prod e^{-\lambda_i t}$.

---

### 3.5 Combined Time-Dependent Network with Redundancy

For a parallel system of $n$ identical exponential components with failure rate $\lambda$:

$$
R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n
$$

This is the **time-dependent parallel reliability** — a frequent KTU exam formulation.

---

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Redundancy Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Redundancy in Safety Systems]:::root --> B[By Activation Mode]
    A --> C[By Redundancy Level]
    A --> D[By Functional Coverage]

    B --> B1[Active Hot Redundancy]
    B --> B2[Standby Cold Redundancy]
    B --> B3[Warm Redundancy]

    C --> C1[Component Redundancy]
    C --> C2[Unit Redundancy]
    C --> C3[System Redundancy]

    D --> D1[Full Redundancy]
    D --> D2[Partial Redundancy]

    B1 --> B1A[All units operate simultaneously]
    B2 --> B2A[Backup activates on primary failure]
    B3 --> B3A[Backup at reduced power state]

    C1 --> C1A[Dual capacitors in power supply]
    C2 --> C2A[Two parallel pumps]
    C3 --> C3A[Twin boiler steam plant]

    D1 --> D1A[All safety paths duplicated]
    D2 --> D2A[Only critical paths duplicated]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef child fill:#e8f0fa,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Conditional Probability Method (CPM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Network with N components]:::entry --> COND{Condition on component Ci}
    COND -->|Ci works with prob Ri| PATH1[Sub-network with Ci shorted]
    COND -->|Ci fails with prob Qi| PATH2[Sub-network with Ci removed]

    PATH1 --> EVAL1[Evaluate Rs given 1]
    PATH2 --> EVAL2[Evaluate Rs given 0]

    EVAL1 --> TERM1{Rs sub 1 computed?}
    EVAL2 --> TERM2{Rs sub 0 computed?}

    TERM1 -->|No| RECURSE1[Apply CPM recursively]
    TERM1 -->|Yes| FORM1[Rs1 value]
    TERM2 -->|No| RECURSE2[Apply CPM recursively]
    TERM2 -->|Yes| FORM2[Rs0 value]

    RECURSE1 --> FORM1
    RECURSE2 --> FORM2

    FORM1 --> COMBINE[Combine: Rs = Ri times Rs1 + Qi times Rs0]
    FORM2 --> COMBINE

    COMBINE --> END[System Reliability Result]:::exit

    classDef entry fill:#2d5a2d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef exit fill:#5a2d2d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#5a4d2d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#e8f0fa,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Series vs Parallel vs Bridge Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SERIES [Series Network]
        S1[C1] --> S2[C2] --> S3[C3]
    end

    subgraph PARALLEL [Parallel Network]
        P1A[C1] --> J1A(( ))
        P1B[C2] --> J1A
        J1A --> J1B(( ))
    end

    subgraph BRIDGE [Bridge Network]
        B1A(( )) --> B1B[C1] --> B1C(( ))
        B1A --> B1D[C3] --> B1C
        B1B -.-> B1E[C5 Bridge] -.-> B1D
    end

    classDef seriesNode fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a02020,color:#000
    classDef parallelNode fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#2050a0,color:#000
    classDef bridgeNode fill:#e2fde2,stroke:#20a050,color:#000

    class S1,S2,S3 seriesNode
    class P1A,P1B parallelNode
    class B1A,B1B,B1C,B1D,B1E bridgeNode
```

### 4.4 The "Bathtub Curve" — Visualizing Time-Dependent Failure Rate

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Region 1: Infant Mortality]:::burn -->|Decreasing lambda| B[Region 2: Useful Life Constant lambda]:::useful -->|Increasing lambda| C[Region 3: Wear Out]:::wear
    A:::burn
    B:::useful
    C:::wear

    classDef burn fill:#ffd0d0,stroke:#c00000,color:#000
    classDef useful fill:#d0ffd0,stroke:#00a000,color:#000
    classDef wear fill:#d0d0ff,stroke:#0000c0,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 2 (Useful Life)** is the only region where the **exponential distribution** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is valid. During infant mortality, the Weibull distribution with shape parameter $< 1$ is used. During wear-out, the Weibull with shape parameter $> 1$ applies.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define *partial redundancy* in a safety system. How does it differ from full redundancy? Give one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Partial Redundancy** is a design strategy in which only the safety-critical components or sub-systems are duplicated, while non-critical elements have a single path. The system can tolerate failure of one or more redundant paths without total loss of safety function, but it cannot tolerate failure of any single non-redundant path.

**Full Redundancy** duplicates *every* functional path in the system, such that failure of any single component does not affect system output.

| Aspect | Partial Redundancy | Full Redundancy |
|---|---|---|
| Coverage | Selected critical paths | All functional paths |
| Cost | Moderate | Very high |
| Example | Dual brake circuits in a car | Twin-engine aircraft powerplant |

**Example of partial redundancy:** Dual hydraulic brake circuits in an automobile (front and rear circuits). **Example of full redundancy:** Two completely independent flight control computers in an aircraft.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State and explain the conditional probability method for evaluating network reliability. Write the general formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Conditional Probability Method (CPM)**, also called the *decomposition method*, evaluates system reliability by conditioning on the operational state of a chosen component. For component $C_i$ with reliability $R_i$ and unreliability $Q_i$:

$$
R_s = R_i \cdot R_s(\text{system works} \vert C_i \text{ works}) + Q_i \cdot R_s(\text{system works} \vert C_i \text{ fails})
$$

The method reduces a complex $n$-component network into two simpler sub-networks (one with $C_i$ working, one with $C_i$ failed) and applies recursion until only single-component sub-networks remain. It is universally applicable to series, parallel, and bridge networks.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** A safety-critical system has the following block diagram configuration:
- **Path 1 (Active):** Components $C_1$ and $C_2$ in **series** (reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$).
- **Path 2 (Active):** Components $C_3$ and $C_4$ in **series** (reliabilities $R_3 = 0.90$, $R_4 = 0.88$).
- The two paths are connected in **parallel** to form an actively redundant system.

Calculate: (i) reliability of each path, (ii) system reliability using the conditional probability method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standby redundant system has a primary unit with $R_1 = 0.95$ and a standby unit with $R_2 = 0.90$, connected through a switch with reliability $R_{sw} = 0.98$. The system must operate for $t = 1000$ hours, and both units have constant failure rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour. Calculate: (i) the time-dependent reliability of each unit, (ii) the system reliability using the standby formula with imperfect switch. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Step 1: Reliability of each series path.**

$$
R_{P1} = R_1 \cdot R_2 = 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874
$$

$$
R_{P2} = R_3 \cdot R_4 = 0.90 \times 0.88 = 0.792
$$

**Part (a) — Step 2: Apply Conditional Probability Method on Path 1 (treat Path 1 as $C_i$).**

$$
R_s = R_{P1} \cdot R_s^{(1)} + Q_{P1} \cdot R_s^{(0)}
$$

- $R_s^{(1)}$ (Path 1 works → system works regardless of Path 2) = $1$
- $Q_{P1} = 1 - 0.874 = 0.126$
- $R_s^{(0)}$ (Path 1 fails → only Path 2 can carry load) = $R_{P2} = 0.792$

**Part (a) — Step 3: Substitute and compute.**

$$
R_s = (0.874)(1) + (0.126)(0.792) = 0.874 + 0.0998 = 0.9738
$$

**Answer (a):** $R_s = 0.9738$ (or 97.38%).

**Verification using direct parallel formula:** $R_s = 1 - Q_{P1} Q_{P2} = 1 - (0.126)(0.208) = 1 - 0.0262 = 0.9738$ ✓

---

**Part (b) — Step 1: Time-dependent reliability of each unit.**

$$
R_1(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065
$$

$$
R_2(t) = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065
$$

**Part (b) — Step 2: Apply standby redundancy formula with imperfect switch.**

For standby with imperfect switch:

$$
R_s = R_1 + R_{sw} \cdot (1 - R_1) \cdot R_2
$$

**Part (b) — Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
R_s = 0.6065 + (0.98)(1 - 0.6065)(0.6065)
$$

$$
R_s = 0.6065 + (0.98)(0.3935)(0.6065)
$$

$$
R_s = 0.6065 + (0.98)(0.2387) = 0.6065 + 0.2339
$$

$$
R_s = 0.8404
$$

**Answer (b):** System reliability at $t = 1000$ hours is $R_s = 0.8404$ (or 84.04%).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **exponential distribution** model for time-dependent reliability. Derive the reliability function $R(t)$ and the MTTF, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bridge network has five components with the following reliabilities: $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, $R_3 = 0.90$, $R_4 = 0.88$, $R_5 = 0.85$ (where $C_5$ is the bridge). Using the **conditional probability method**, compute the system reliability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Assumptions of the Exponential Model:**

1. The failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is **constant** over time (no wear-in, no wear-out).
2. The component is **non-repairable** during the mission.
3. Failures are **statistically independent**.
4. The component has a **memoryless** failure time distribution.

**Derivation:**

By definition, the failure rate is:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{dR(t)/dt}{R(t)}
$$

Setting $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant) and integrating:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} d\tau
$$

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t
$$

Since $R(0) = 1$:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**MTTF Derivation:**

$$
MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

Integration by parts yields:

$$
MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Part (a) Final Answer:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $MTTF = 1/\lambda$.

---

**Part (b) — Step 1: Compute intermediate quantities.**

$$
R_1 R_2 = (0.95)(0.92) = 0.874
$$

$$
R_3 R_4 = (0.90)(0.88) = 0.792
$$

$$
R_1 R_3 = (0.95)(0.90) = 0.855
$$

$$
R_2 R_4 = (0.92)(0.88) = 0.8096
$$

**Part (b) — Step 2: Apply CPM conditioning on $C_5$.**

$$
R_s = R_5 \cdot R_s^{(1)} + Q_5 \cdot R_s^{(0)}
$$

**Part (b) — Step 3: Evaluate $R_s^{(1)}$ (when $C_5$ works — series-parallel configuration).**

When the bridge works, the network becomes a parallel combination of two series paths:

$$
R_s^{(1)} = 1 - (1 - R_1 R_2)(1 - R_3 R_4)
$$

$$
R_s^{(1)} = 1 - (1 - 0.874)(1 - 0.792) = 1 - (0.126)(0.208) = 1 - 0.02621 = 0.97379
$$

**Part (b) — Step 4: Evaluate $R_s^{(0)}$ (when $C_5$ fails — two independent series paths in parallel).**

$$
R_s^{(0)} = 1 - (1 - R_1 R_3)(1 - R_2 R_4)
$$

$$
R_s^{(0)} = 1 - (1 - 0.855)(1 - 0.8096) = 1 - (0.145)(0.1904) = 1 - 0.02761 = 0.97239
$$

**Part (b) — Step 5: Combine.**

$$
R_s = (0.85)(0.97379) + (0.15)(0.97239)
$$

$$
R_s = 0.82772 + 0.14586 = 0.97358
$$

**Answer (b):** $R_s \approx 0.9736$ (or 97.36%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **CPM Decomposition Error (2 marks):** Many students forget to update the network topology after conditioning. When $C_5$ works, the bridge reduces to a series-parallel; when $C_5$ fails, it becomes a different series-parallel. Drawing both simplified networks is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 2. **Switch reliability in standby (2 marks):** Students commonly use the formula $R_s = R_1 + (1 - R_1)R_2$ which assumes a *perfect* switch. If the question states a switch reliability $R_{sw} < 1$, the correct formula is $R_s = R_1 + R_{sw}(1 - R_1)R_2$. This distinction is worth 2 marks.
> 3. **Time-dependence substitution (1 mark):** When a problem gives $\lambda$ and $t$, students must first compute $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ before substituting into the network formula. Skipping this step costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Units and numerical precision (1 mark):** Always express answers to **4 decimal places** for reliabilities; failure rates in **failures/hour** or **FPMH** as specified.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Redundancy** = duplicate means of accomplishing a task; ensures continued function after single-point failure.
- [x] **Partial Redundancy** = duplication of selected safety-critical paths only (cost-effective engineering trade-off).
- [x] **Active Redundancy**: $R_s = 1 - \prod Q_i$ (all units powered from $t=0$).
- [x] **Standby Redundancy (perfect switch)**: $R_s = R_1 + (1 - R_1)R_2$.
- [x] **Standby Redundancy (imperfect switch)**: $R_s = R_1 + R_{sw}(1 - R_1)R_2$.
- [x] **2-out-of-3 Voting**: $R_s = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ — typical for TMR systems.
- [x] **Conditional Probability Method (CPM)**: $R_s = R_i R_s^{(1)} + Q_i R_s^{(0)}$ — recursive decomposition.
- [x] **Bridge Network CPM**: $R_s = R_5[1-(1-R_1R_2)(1-R_3R_4)] + Q_5[1-(1-R_1R_3)(1-R_2R_4)]$.
- [x] **Exponential Reliability**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — valid in the constant-failure-rate region of the bathtub curve.
- [x] **Unreliability**: $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [x] **PDF of failure time**: $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [x] **MTTF = MTBF = $1/\lambda$** for exponential distribution.
- [x] **Series system failure rate**: $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$ (rates add).
- [x] **Parallel exponential**: $R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$.
- [x] **Memoryless property**: $P(T > s + t \vert T > s) = P(T > t)$ — unique to exponential.
- [x] **Common-mode failure** must be considered; independent redundancy is the goal.
- [x] **Cut set** = smallest set of components whose failure causes system failure.
- [x] **Minimum Operating Configuration (MOC)** defines the boundary of partial redundancy.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** For 14-mark numerical questions, always (1) state the formula, (2) define the conditioning variable, (3) draw both sub-networks, (4) show all intermediate arithmetic, and (5) verify the answer using a complementary formula. Examiners award step-marks generously when the methodology is transparent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In safety engineering and reliability theory, a **Fault** is defined as an **abnormal condition or deviation** in the physical state, structure, or operational parameters of a system, subsystem, or component that has the potential to cause the system to fail in performing its intended function. It is the *root-level defect* or *imperfection* whose propagation (often through a chain of cause-effect relationships) ultimately results in a system-level **Failure**.

According to the **IEC 60050 International Electrotechnical Vocabulary (IEV)** and IEEE Standard 610.12, a fault is essentially an *intermediate state* between the original "as-designed" condition and the final "failed" state. A fault is also sometimes referred to as a **disturbance**, **defect**, or **anomaly** depending on the engineering domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Box:**
> A *fault* is the *cause* (the "why"), while a *failure* is the *effect* (the "what happens to the user"). Every failure has at least one underlying fault. Faults may be **latent** (dormant, undiscovered) or **active** (triggered and currently affecting the system).

### 1.2 The Fault → Error → Failure Chain (Three-Stage Cascade)

| Stage | Term | Plain-English Meaning |
|-------|------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Fault** (Defect) | The *seed* — a flaw in design, code, hardware, or process |
| 2 | **Error** | The *symptom* — incorrect internal state triggered by the fault |
| 3 | **Failure** | The *outcome* — observable deviation from required system behavior |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker** on a kitchen stove:

- The **fault** is a *manufacturing defect* in the safety valve — perhaps the spring was manufactured 5% weaker than specification.
- The **error** is when the *internal pressure rises beyond the safe threshold* because that weakened valve opens too late.
- The **failure** is when the cooker *bursts open*, scalding the user.

The fault was always there (latent). It only became an error when pressure built up. The error only became a failure when the cooker physically ruptured. Safety engineers must identify and neutralize the *fault* **before** it cascades into error and failure.

### 1.4 Classification of Faults (Taxonomy Overview)

```
                              FAULT
                                 |
        +------------+-----------+-----------+--------------+
        |            |                       |              |
   BY ORIGIN    BY DURATION            BY SEVERITY    BY DOMAIN
        |            |                       |              |
   +----+----+   +---+----+            +-----+-----+    +---+----+
   |         |   |        |            |           |    |        |
Hardware  Software  Permanent  Transient  Minor  Major  Hardware  Software
Human   Environmental   Intermittent  Catastrophic  Network  Process
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The **Failure Rate (λ)** is expressed in **failures per million hours (fpmh)** or **failures per 10⁹ hours (FIT — Failures In Time)**. Industry benchmarks use **1 FIT = 1 failure per 10⁹ device-hours**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Fault Taxonomy (KTU Board-Expected Classification)

#### A) By Origin / Nature of the Defect

1. **Hardware Faults** — Physical degradation of components.
   - *Examples:* corrosion, wear, fracture, insulation breakdown, connector oxidation.
   - *Most common in:* mechanical, civil, and electrical systems.
   - *Measured via:* MTBF, MTTF.

2. **Software Faults** — Bugs, logic errors, race conditions in code.
   - *Examples:* divide-by-zero, infinite loops, uninitialized variables, buffer overflows.
   - *Unique property:* **software faults are deterministic** — given identical inputs, they always reproduce.

3. **Human / Operator Faults** — Mistakes in operation, maintenance, or design.
   - *Examples:* skipped checklist, incorrect valve closure, misread instrument.
   - *Mitigation:* training, ergonomics, Human Reliability Analysis (HRA).

4. **Environmental / External Faults** — Induced by surroundings.
   - *Examples:* lightning strike, flood, seismic vibration, EMI (Electromagnetic Interference).

#### B) By Duration / Temporal Behavior

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Permanent** | Once occurred, persists until repaired | Burned-out chip, snapped cable |
| **Transient** | Appears briefly then disappears | Cosmic-ray bit flip, voltage sag |
| **Intermittent** | Occurs sporadically under specific conditions | Loose connector, thermal expansion crack |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Transient faults make up approximately **70%–80% of all faults** in modern digital systems. They are notoriously hard to reproduce.

### 2.2 The Two Pillars: Qualitative vs Quantitative Fault Analysis

#### I. Qualitative Fault Analysis
This approach focuses on **HOW** and **WHY** a system can fail — without numerical probabilities.

**Methods include:**
- Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — for *deductive* (top-down) logic
- Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) — for *inductive* (bottom-up) logic
- Event Tree Analysis (ETA) — for *forward consequence* tracing
- Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) — for *process deviations*

#### II. Quantitative Fault Analysis
This approach focuses on **HOW OFTEN** and **HOW LIKELY** failures occur — using numerical probabilities and rates.

**Methods include:**
- Boolean algebra evaluation of fault trees
- Minimal Cut Set (MCS) computation
- Importance factor analysis (Birnbaum, Risk Reduction, Criticality)
- Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) evaluation
- Monte Carlo simulation

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU board-favorite equations**. Memorize the units as well — losing a mark on units is a common pitfall.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Probability of AND-gate (independent) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $P(A \cup B) = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$ | Probability of OR-gate (independent) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate from MTBF | failures/hour |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative distribution of failure | dimensionless |
| 6 | $f(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability density function (PDF) | per hour |
| 7 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Steady-state Availability | dimensionless |
| 8 | $I_B(i) = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial q_i}$ | Birnbaum Importance Measure | dimensionless |
| 9 | $RRW(i) = \dfrac{Q_{sys}}{Q_{sys}(q_i = 1)}$ | Risk Reduction Worth | dimensionless |
| 10 | $MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ | Mean Time To Failure | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never confuse MTTF, MTBF, and MTTR.** 
> - **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) — for *non-repairable* items.
> - **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — for *repairable* items. Note: $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$.
> - **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) — repair duration.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Fault Application |
|----------|------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Redundant Flight Control Systems — fault-tolerant architectures via Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) |
| **Nuclear Power** | Fault Tree Analysis for reactor shutdown system (SCRAM) verification |
| **Automotive** | ISO 26262 — functional safety requires fault diagnosis coverage > 95% |
| **Healthcare** | Medical device fault modes (IEC 62304) must be classified by severity |
| **Process Plants** | HAZOP + LOPA combined for SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination |
| **Software Engineering** | Static analysis tools (e.g., Coverity, SonarQube) detect fault density per KLOC |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Failure Rate, Reliability, and Hazard Function

The reliability function $R(t)$ is defined as the probability that a component **survives** from time 0 to time $t$ without failure:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where $T$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure. The cumulative distribution function (CDF) of failure is:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t) = 1 - R(t)$$

Differentiating $F(t)$ with respect to $t$ yields the probability density function (PDF) of failures:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

The **instantaneous failure rate** (also called hazard rate or force of mortality) $\lambda(t)$ is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Rearranging this differential equation:

$$\lambda(t) \cdot dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau = -\ln[R(t)] - (-\ln[R(0)])$$

Since $R(0) = 1$ (the component is functional at time zero, $\ln(1) = 0$):

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau = -\ln[R(t)]$$

Solving for $R(t)$:

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right)$$

Assuming the **Useful Life Period** where $\lambda(t)$ is constant (the flat middle of the Bathtub Curve):

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, d\tau = \lambda t$$

Therefore, in the constant failure rate regime:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **exponential reliability distribution** — the most heavily tested formula in KTU quantitative safety modules.

### 3.2 Derivation: MTTF from Reliability Function

$$MTTF = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

Using integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = f(t)\,dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = -R(t)$:

$$MTTF = [-t \cdot R(t)]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

Since $\lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} = 0$ (exponential decay dominates), the boundary term vanishes:

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Thus:

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

### 3.3 Fault Tree Probability Computation — Step-by-Step

**Worked Example: A pump system fails when either (i) electrical power is lost AND motor burns out, or (ii) the discharge valve fails closed.**

**Step 1: Define the Boolean structure.**
Let the Top Event be $T$ (system failure). Define basic events:
- $A$ = power supply fault (probability $P_A$)
- $B$ = motor burnout (probability $P_B$)
- $C$ = discharge valve stuck closed (probability $P_C$)

$$T = (A \cap B) \cup C$$

**Step 2: Apply the Boolean expansion theorem.**

$$P(T) = P[(A \cap B) \cup C] = P(A \cap B) + P(C) - P(A \cap B \cap C)$$

**Step 3: Assume independence (standard KTU assumption unless stated otherwise).**

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$
$$P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C)$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values.** Let $P_A = 0.01$, $P_B = 0.05$, $P_C = 0.02$:

$$P(A \cap B) = 0.01 \times 0.05 = 0.0005$$
$$P(A \cap B \cap C) = 0.01 \times 0.05 \times 0.02 = 0.00001$$

$$P(T) = 0.0005 + 0.02 - 0.00001 = 0.02049$$

**Step 5: Convert to failures per year** (assuming each event is annual):

$$P(T) \approx 2.049\% \text{ per year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Students frequently forget the subtraction term $-P(A \cap B \cap C)$ in the union formula. This omission gives an *overestimated* (non-conservative) risk — which is dangerous in real safety engineering.

### 3.4 Derivation: Birnbaum Importance Measure

The Birnbaum importance of component $i$ is defined as:

$$I_B(i) = \frac{\partial g(\vec{q})}{\partial q_i}\bigg|_{q_i = q_i^*}$$

where $g(\vec{q})$ is the system top-event probability function and $\vec{q}$ is the vector of basic event probabilities. In discrete form:

$$I_B(i) = g(1_i, \vec{q}) - g(0_i, \vec{q})$$

where:
- $g(1_i, \vec{q})$ = top-event probability assuming component $i$ is **certain to fail**
- $g(0_i, \vec{q})$ = top-event probability assuming component $i$ is **certain to function**

The component with the highest $I_B$ is the **most critical** to address first.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fault Analysis Methodology — Top-Down Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[System Hazard Identified] --> methodA{Analysis Type}
    methodA -- Deductive --> ftaA[Fault Tree Analysis FTA]
    methodA -- Inductive --> fmeaA[Failure Mode Effects Analysis FMEA]
    methodA -- Consequence --> etaA[Event Tree Analysis ETA]
    methodA -- Process --> hazopA[HAZOP Study]
    ftaA --> step1[Identify Top Event]
    step1 --> step2[Build Logic Tree using AND OR Gates]
    step2 --> step3[Determine Minimal Cut Sets]
    step3 --> step4[Compute Probability Quantitatively]
    step4 --> step5[Compute Importance Measures]
    step5 --> outA[Safety Recommendations]
    fmeaA --> step6[Identify Failure Modes per Component]
    step6 --> step7[Score Severity S x Occurrence O x Detection D]
    step7 --> step8[Compute Risk Priority Number RPN]
    step8 --> outA
    etaA --> step9[Define Initiating Event]
    step9 --> step10[Branch into Success or Failure Sequences]
    step10 --> step11[Compute Consequence Probabilities]
    step11 --> outA
    hazopA --> step12[Apply Guide Words to Process Variables]
    step12 --> step13[Identify Deviations and Causes]
    step13 --> outA
    outA --> endA[Risk Mitigation and Documentation]
```

### 4.2 The "Bathtub Curve" — Failure Rate vs Life Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[Infant Mortality Region] -- Decreasing lambda --> phase2[Useful Life Constant lambda]
    phase2 -- Increasing lambda --> phase3[Wear Out Region]
    phase1 -. Burn-in testing .-> note1[Manufacturing Faults Dominate]
    phase2 -. Random Failures .-> note2[Accidental Faults Dominate]
    phase3 -. Aging Wear .-> note3[Wear-out Faults Dominate]
```

### 4.3 Fault Tree Schematic — Boiler Explosion (Classic KTU Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topEvent[TOP EVENT Boiler Explosion] --> orGate1{G1 OR Gate}
    orGate1 --> andGate1{G2 AND Gate}
    orGate1 --> andGate2{G3 AND Gate}
    andGate1 --> basicA[A1 Overpressure Event]
    andGate1 --> basicB[A2 Operator fails to vent]
    andGate2 --> basicC[A3 Safety valve stuck]
    andGate2 --> basicD[A4 Alarm system failed]
    andGate2 --> basicE[A5 High temperature]
```

**Logical Boolean Representation of this Fault Tree:**

$$TE = (A_1 \cap A_2) \cup (A_3 \cap A_4 \cap A_5)$$

### 4.4 Fault Treatment Decision Matrix (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    faultInput[Detected Fault] --> classify{Classification}
    classify -- Hardware --> hRoute[Hardware Redundancy Voting TMR]
    classify -- Software --> sRoute[Error Correcting Codes Watchdog Timers]
    classify -- Human --> huRoute[Training Checklists Ergonomics]
    classify -- Environmental --> eRoute[Shielding Surge Protection]
    hRoute --> detectMon[Continuous Monitoring Sensors]
    sRoute --> detectMon
    huRoute --> detectMon
    eRoute --> detectMon
    detectMon --> isoAction[Automatic Isolation or Safe Shutdown]
    isoAction --> recovery[Recovery and Repair Procedure]
    recovery --> docOut[Incident Documentation for Future FTA Updates]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Distinguish between a fault and a failure. Provide one real-world example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **fault** is the underlying defect or abnormal condition within a system component that has the *potential* to cause a malfunction, whereas a **failure** is the *observable inability* of the system to perform its intended function.

| Aspect | Fault | Failure |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Nature | Latent, internal cause | Observable, external effect |
| Example | A micro-crack in a turbine blade | The turbine blade fractures mid-flight |

**Example:** In a passenger car, a **fault** may be a degraded brake-pad lining (latent defect). The corresponding **failure** is the inability of the brakes to stop the car within the required stopping distance.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for example.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
*What are the three main regions of the Bathtub Curve? Briefly explain each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bathtub Curve** plots failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time and exhibits three distinct regions:

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in):** Failure rate *decreases* over time. Dominated by manufacturing defects and debugging of substandard components. Mitigated by factory burn-in testing.

2. **Useful Life Region:** Failure rate is approximately *constant*. Failures are random (accidental) — caused by unpredictable stresses. This is the operating period modeled by $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

3. **Wear-out Region:** Failure rate *increases* rapidly with time. Caused by aging, fatigue, corrosion, and mechanical wear. Mitigated by preventive replacement before wear-out onset.

*(1 mark each for the three regions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of faults** encountered in safety engineering. Classify them according to their origin, duration, and severity with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A critical safety system has three independent components A, B, and C with failure probabilities $P_A = 0.02$, $P_B = 0.03$, $P_C = 0.04$ per year. The system fails if (i) both A and B fail together, OR (ii) C fails alone. Using Boolean algebra, calculate the **probability of system failure per year** and the **Birnbaum importance** of component C. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

The classification of faults in safety engineering follows three major taxonomies:

**(i) By Origin (Nature of the Defect):**
- **Hardware Faults:** Physical degradation of components such as corrosion, fracture, insulation breakdown, wear, or fatigue. *Example:* A cracked gear tooth in a gearbox.
- **Software Faults:** Logical defects in code such as uninitialized variables, infinite loops, race conditions, or buffer overflows. *Example:* The Therac-25 radiation overdose bug.
- **Human/Operator Faults:** Mistakes committed during operation, maintenance, or design. *Example:* Skipping a pre-flight checklist.
- **Environmental Faults:** Induced by ambient conditions like lightning, seismic events, or temperature extremes.

**(ii) By Duration (Temporal Behavior):**
- **Permanent Faults:** Persist until external repair intervention. *Example:* A burned-out capacitor.
- **Transient Faults:** Appear briefly and then disappear; account for 70–80% of digital faults. *Example:* A cosmic-ray induced bit flip.
- **Intermittent Faults:** Occur sporadically under specific operating conditions. *Example:* A loose connector.

**(iii) By Severity (Consequence Class):**
- **Minor:** Negligible impact, no injury.
- **Major:** Local damage, possible minor injury.
- **Critical:** Serious injury or significant property loss.
- **Catastrophic:** Loss of life, system destruction.

*(2 marks classification, 2 marks examples per category, 2 marks comparison table, 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Step 1: Express the Boolean logic.**
Let the system failure event be $T$. Given that system fails if (A AND B) OR C:

$$T = (A \cap B) \cup C$$

**Step 2: Apply the union formula.**

$$P(T) = P[(A \cap B) \cup C] = P(A \cap B) + P(C) - P(A \cap B \cap C)$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values assuming independence.**

$$P(A \cap B) = 0.02 \times 0.03 = 0.0006$$
$$P(A \cap B \cap C) = 0.02 \times 0.03 \times 0.04 = 0.000024$$

$$P(T) = 0.0006 + 0.04 - 0.000024 = 0.040576$$

**Step 4: Compute Birnbaum importance for component C.**

$$I_B(C) = g(1_C) - g(0_C)$$

- If C **always fails** ($P_C = 1$):
$$g(1_C) = P[(A \cap B) \cup C] = 0.0006 + 1 - 0.0006 = 1.0$$
(when C is certain to fail, the OR-gate is certainly satisfied)

- If C **never fails** ($P_C = 0$):
$$g(0_C) = P[(A \cap B) \cup C] = 0.0006 + 0 - 0 = 0.0006$$

**Step 5: Calculate.**

$$I_B(C) = 1.0 - 0.0006 = 0.9994$$

Since $I_B(C) \approx 1.0$, component **C is overwhelmingly the most critical** to address — even though A and B have smaller individual probabilities. This is a classic example of how importance analysis can reveal non-obvious priorities.

*(2 marks Boolean expression, 2 marks probability calculation, 2 marks Birnbaum derivation, 1 mark interpretation = 7 marks)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **qualitative and quantitative approaches** to fault analysis. Compare Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) with Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The failure rate of a pressure sensor is $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures per hour. Calculate: (i) its **MTBF**, (ii) **reliability after 1000 hours**, (iii) **probability of failure in the next 500 hours** given it has already survived 1000 hours, and (iv) comment on the constant failure rate assumption. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Qualitative vs Quantitative Fault Analysis:**

| Aspect | Qualitative Analysis | Quantitative Analysis |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Question answered | *How* and *Why* can the system fail? | *How often* and *How likely* will it fail? |
| Output | Logic structure, scenarios, causes | Numerical probabilities, risk metrics |
| Methods | FTA, FMEA, ETA, HAZOP | Boolean algebra, MCS, importance measures, simulation |
| Data needs | System topology & failure mode list | Failure rates, probabilities, repair times |

**FTA vs FMEA Comparison:**

| Feature | FTA (Fault Tree Analysis) | FMEA (Failure Mode Effects Analysis) |
|---------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Direction | Top-down (deductive) | Bottom-up (inductive) |
| Starting point | A predefined Top Event (system-level hazard) | Individual component failure modes |
| Logic gates | AND, OR, NOT gates | Cause-effect relationships |
| Output | Minimal Cut Sets, Top Event Probability | Risk Priority Number RPN = S × O × D |
| Best for | Complex systems with single critical hazard | Hardware-heavy systems with many components |
| Limitation | Can become very large; needs expert input | May miss system-level interactions |

*(2 marks qualitative intro, 2 marks quantitative intro, 2 marks comparison table, 1 mark conclusion = 7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

Given: $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour.

**(i) MTBF Calculation:**

$$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Reliability after 1000 hours:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 1000} = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2}$$

$$R(1000) \approx 0.8187 \text{ or } 81.87\%$$

**(iii) Conditional probability of failure in the next 500 hours, given survival at 1000 hours:**

Using the **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution:

$$P(T \leq 1500 \mid T > 1000) = 1 - R(500) = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot 500}$$

$$= 1 - e^{-0.0002 \times 500} = 1 - e^{-0.1}$$

$$= 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952 \text{ or } 9.52\%$$

Note: The probability is the *same* as for a brand-new sensor over 500 hours — this is the **memoryless property** of exponential distribution.

**(iv) Comment on the constant failure rate assumption:**

The assumption $\lambda = \text{constant}$ is valid only during the **Useful Life period** of the Bathtub Curve. It implies:
- No infant-mortality screening has been performed, OR
- The sensor is past its burn-in phase and not yet in wear-out.
- All failures are purely **random (accidental)** in nature.
- The **memoryless property** holds, meaning the component "forgets" its history.

This assumption is reasonable for **electronic components** (per MIL-HDBK-217F data) but **NOT** valid for mechanical wear parts like bearings, where failure rate increases with time.

*(1.5 marks each for parts i-iii, 2.5 marks for part iv = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones Identified by Senior KTU Examiners:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units.** Always write "failures/hour" alongside $\lambda$. A bare number loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF.** Remember: MTTF is for non-repairable, MTBF is for repairable items.
> 3. **Skipping the intersection term** $-P(A \cap B \cap C)$ in the OR-gate expansion. This always happens to ~30% of students.
> 4. **Mixing up qualitative vs quantitative outputs.** FTA *can* be both — but the Boolean tree itself is qualitative; the probability assignment is quantitative.
> 5. **Not interpreting the answer.** A bare numerical answer without a concluding sentence ("Component C is the most critical") loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Writing $R(t) = e^{\lambda t}$** instead of $e^{-\lambda t}$ — sign error; catastrophic mark loss.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Fault** = cause; **Failure** = effect; **Error** = internal symptom of the fault.
- [ ] Faults are classified by **origin** (hardware/software/human/environmental), **duration** (permanent/transient/intermittent), and **severity** (minor/major/critical/catastrophic).
- [ ] **Transient faults** are the most common (~70–80%) in digital systems.
- [ ] **Qualitative** = HOW/WHY it can fail; **Quantitative** = HOW LIKELY.
- [ ] **FTA** is top-down/deductive; **FMEA** is bottom-up/inductive; **ETA** traces consequences forward.
- [ ] **AND-gate:** $P = P_A \cdot P_B$ (independent).
- [ ] **OR-gate:** $P = 1 - (1 - P_A)(1 - P_B)$ (independent).
- [ ] **Exponential reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [ ] **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$ (constant failure-rate regime).
- [ ] **Availability** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- [ ] **Birnbaum Importance** = $g(1_i) - g(0_i)$ — identifies the *most critical* component.
- [ ] **Bathtub Curve** has three regions: Infant Mortality, Useful Life, Wear-out.
- [ ] **Memoryless property** of exponential distribution: $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$.
- [ ] **FIT** unit: 1 FIT = 1 failure per 10⁹ device-hours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Error

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **safety science and engineering**, an **Error** is defined as a deviation from an intended or expected course of action, plan, or specification that leads to an unintended outcome. According to the foundational safety literature (e.g., Reason's *Human Error*, 1990; and KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 syllabus framework), an error represents a **failure of a planned action to achieve its intended outcome**, which includes both errors of planning (mistakes) and errors of execution (slips and lapses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examination Standard)**
> An **Error** is any inappropriate or undesired action that compromises safety in a system. It is the discrepancy between what *should* be done and what *is actually* done, either in human action, system design, measurement, or operational procedure. Errors form the **fundamental precursor** in the accident causation chain and are the quantitative unit of measurement in human reliability analysis.

### Classification at a Glance

| Category | Description | Safety Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Human Error** | Action by a person that deviates from accepted norms | Direct accident precursor |
| **System Error** | Failure within equipment, software, or process control | Trigger for catastrophic events |
| **Measurement Error** | Deviation between measured and true value | Affects reliability of safety data |
| **Procedural Error** | Deviation from established SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) | Leads to operational failures |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The "GPS and Driver" Metaphor**
> Imagine a GPS navigation system guiding a driver to a destination. The GPS plots the route, the driver executes it, and the car responds.
> - A **slip** is like pressing the accelerator when you meant to press the brake — *your intent was correct, but the execution failed*.
> - A **mistake** is like programming the wrong destination into the GPS — *the plan itself was wrong*.
> - A **lapse** is like forgetting to check the rear-view mirror before reversing — *the memory/intention step failed*.
> - A **system error** is like the GPS routing you onto a closed, dangerous road — *the tool that supports your decision failed*.
>
> Each of these "errors" represents a **hole in the cheese** that, if aligned with other holes, leads directly to an accident. This is precisely the intuition behind **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** which is a cornerstone of Module 3.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the **standard quantitative metrics** for safety errors universally used in KTU-aligned safety engineering:

- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):** Dimensionless ratio, $0 \leq PFD \leq 1$
- **Error Rate ($\lambda$):** Expressed in errors per million opportunities (epmo), unitless
- **Human Error Probability (HEP):** Typically ranges from **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$**
- **Six Sigma Quality Level:** $3.4$ defects per **million opportunities (DPMO)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are **bolded** because they are universally expected in KTU 2024 numerical and definition-type questions for OESFT837.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Error

Errors in safety engineering follow a strict hierarchy that the KTU examiner expects students to reproduce accurately:

### 2.1.1 Human Error (The Dominant Factor)

According to **Reason's Human Error Theory (1990)**, human errors are subdivided into three primary categories:

1. **Slips** — Execution failures where the *plan* is correct but the *action* goes wrong. (Skill-based level, Rasmussen's SRK framework)
2. **Lapses** — Memory-related failures; internal events that are often missed. (Skill-based level)
3. **Mistakes** — Planning or decision-making failures where the *plan itself* is wrong. (Rule-based or Knowledge-based level)

### 2.1.2 System / Latent Errors

These are errors built into the system by designers, managers, or maintainers. They lie dormant until combined with active failures to breach safety. They are also called **latent conditions**.

### 2.1.3 Measurement / Quantitative Errors

For the *Quantitative Approach* in this course, measurement errors are mathematically classified:

- **Systematic Error (Bias Error):** Consistent, repeatable deviation in one direction.
- **Random Error (Statistical Fluctuation):** Scattered, unpredictable deviation due to noise.
- **Gross Error (Blunder):** Large deviation caused by human carelessness or instrument failure.

## 2.2 Error Causation Theory — The Swiss Cheese Model

James Reason's **Swiss Cheese Model** is a foundational element for KTU Module 3 questions. It conceptualizes an organization as having multiple defensive layers (slices of cheese), each containing latent holes (errors/violations). An accident occurs only when the holes in all layers momentarily align, permitting a trajectory of hazard to reach the victim/asset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU?** Questions on error causation often ask students to "explain with diagram how multiple errors align to cause accidents." This is a **10-mark standard question** in the ESE pattern.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Application Context |
|---|---|---|
| $E = X_m - X_t$ | Absolute Error = Measured − True value | Basic error quantification |
| $\varepsilon = \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t}$ | Relative Error (dimensionless) | Comparison of precision |
| $\varepsilon\% = \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t} \times 100$ | Percentage Error | KTU numerical standard |
| $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Standard Deviation (Random Error) | Statistical analysis of errors |
| $HEP = \dfrac{N_e}{N_o}$ | Human Error Probability | Reliability engineering |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{N_e}{T \cdot N_o}$ | Error rate per unit time | System reliability |
| $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (Poisson model) | Time-to-failure analysis |
| $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I}{2}$ | Avg. Probability of Failure on Demand (IEC 61508) | Safety Instrumented Systems |
| $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities (Six Sigma) | Quality safety analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on formatting:** In the formula column above, vertical bars (used normally for absolute value) have been replaced with explicit notation like $\vert X_i - \bar{X} \vert$ in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table. Always write `$PFD$` instead of `$|PFD|$` inside KTU table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Errors in safety engineering underpin:
- **Nuclear Safety:** Human Reliability Analysis (HREA) for reactor operations.
- **Aviation:** Crew Resource Management (CRM) and error-trapping checklists.
- **Process Industry:** IEC 61508/61511 SIL (Safety Integrity Level) calculations.
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 functional safety error budgets.
- **Medical:** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) of clinical errors.

In all these fields, the **Quantitative Approach** to error allows engineers to mathematically model accident likelihood and design protective barriers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Percentage Error from First Principles

The percentage error formula is a direct transformation of the relative error formula, but its derivation underpins all safety measurement reporting.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of Absolute Error:**
$$E = X_m - X_t$$
where:
- $X_m$ = measured (or observed) value
- $X_t$ = true (or accepted reference) value

**Step 2 — Define Relative Error as a dimensionless ratio:**
$$\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{E}{X_t} = \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t}$$
This step normalizes the error against the true value, allowing comparison across different measurement scales (e.g., comparing a 0.1 m error in length vs. a 0.1 s error in time).

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage by multiplying by 100:**
$$\varepsilon_\% = \varepsilon_r \times 100 = \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The transition from ratio to percentage is a unit conversion: $\varepsilon_r$ is in "fraction" units (unitless but $< 1$); multiplying by 100 simply shifts the decimal two places, expressing the same magnitude as "per cent."

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern):**
A pressure transducer reads $P_m = 5.15$ bar when the true pressure is $P_t = 5.00$ bar. Calculate the percentage error.

**Solution:**
$$E = P_m - P_t = 5.15 - 5.00 = 0.15 \text{ bar}$$

$$\varepsilon_\% = \dfrac{0.15}{5.00} \times 100 = 3.0\%$$

This 3% deviation is **above** the typical acceptable industrial tolerance of $\pm 2\%$, indicating a calibration issue — a safety concern.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Human Error Probability (HEP) and Error Rate

The Human Error Probability is a **ratio estimator** in reliability theory.

**Step 1 — Define the count of errors observed:**
$N_e$ = number of errors committed during the observation window.

**Step 2 — Define the count of opportunities:**
$N_o$ = total number of opportunities for error (e.g., number of operations, number of switches toggled).

**Step 3 — Form the probability ratio:**
$$HEP = \dfrac{N_e}{N_o}$$

This is a frequentist probability, requiring $N_o$ to be sufficiently large for statistical significance.

**Step 4 — Time-normalized error rate:**
If the observation window has duration $T$ (in hours, days, or cycles), the error rate per unit time is:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{HEP}{T} = \dfrac{N_e}{T \cdot N_o}$$

**Step 5 — Reliability function derivation:**
Assuming errors follow a **Poisson process** (rare, independent events), the probability of $k$ errors in time $t$ is:
$$P(k) = \dfrac{(\lambda t)^k \, e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

For $k = 0$ (no error in time $t$, i.e., the system is reliable):
$$P(0) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Therefore, the **Reliability function** is:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 6 — Probability of failure (Unreliability):**
$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The Reliability function is the complement of the unreliability. Adding them gives 1: $R(t) + F(t) = 1$. This is the **axiom of completeness** from probability theory and is essential for KTU problems on safety instrumented systems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)

For low-demand mode safety systems (e.g., an emergency shutdown valve), the **average PFD** is derived assuming constant failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ and a proof-test interval $T_I$:

**Step 1 — Unreliability as a function of time:**
$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}$$

**Step 2 — Average over one test interval:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_I} \int_0^{T_I} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}\right) dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_I} \left[ t + \dfrac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}}{\lambda_{DU}} \right]_0^{T_I}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute bounds:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_I} \left( T_I + \dfrac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_I}}{\lambda_{DU}} - 0 - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$PFD_{avg} = 1 + \dfrac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_I} - 1}{\lambda_{DU} T_I}$$

**Step 6 — Apply small-error approximation** ($\lambda_{DU} T_I \ll 1$, so $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \tfrac{x^2}{2}$):

$$PFD_{avg} \approx 1 + \dfrac{(1 - \lambda_{DU} T_I) - 1}{\lambda_{DU} T_I}$$

$$PFD_{avg} \approx 1 + \dfrac{-\lambda_{DU} T_I}{\lambda_{DU} T_I}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I}{2}}$$

This is the **industry-standard simplified PFD formula** used in IEC 61508 SIL calculations — a frequent KTU examination topic.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Formula

| Scenario | Recommended Formula | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single measurement deviation | $E = X_m - X_t$ | Direct calculation |
| Comparing accuracy across scales | $\varepsilon_\% = \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t} \times 100$ | Normalized output |
| Statistical quality control | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Captures random scatter |
| Human reliability assessment | $HEP = N_e / N_o$ | Fits observed data |
| System reliability over time | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Poisson-process model |
| SIL verification | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} T_I}{2}$ | IEC 61508 standard |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (Organizational Accident Causation)

This diagram captures the multi-layered defense concept that is the **most frequently tested visual** in KTU Module 3 error questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HAZARD SOURCE] --> B[Organizational Influences - Latent Errors]
    B --> C[Supervision - Unsafe Acts]
    C --> D[Preconditions for Unsafe Acts]
    D --> E[Unsafe Acts - Active Errors]
    E --> F[ACCIDENT EVENT]
    F --> G[VICTIM OR ASSET LOSS]

    L1[Layer 1 Organizational Culture]:::cheese
    L2[Layer 2 Management Supervision]:::cheese
    L3[Layer 3 Preconditions - Equipment Training]:::cheese
    L4[Layer 4 Immediate Unsafe Act]:::cheese

    A -.-> L1
    L1 -.-> L2
    L2 -.-> L3
    L3 -.-> L4
    L4 -.-> G

    classDef cheese fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The hazard trajectory must pass through holes in all four "Swiss cheese" layers to reach the victim. Each layer's "hole" represents an error (latent or active). The diagram explicitly labels both the **causation chain** (top boxes) and the **defensive layers** (cheese slices).

## 4.2 Error Classification Flow (Rasmussen SRK + Reason)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ERROR] --> B{Origin}
    B --> C[HUMAN ERROR] 
    B --> D[SYSTEM ERROR]
    B --> E[MEASUREMENT ERROR]
    
    C --> F{Skill Based Rule Based Knowledge Based}
    F --> G[SLIPS - Execution]
    F --> H[LAPSES - Memory]
    F --> I[MISTAKES - Planning]
    
    E --> J{Type}
    J --> K[SYSTEMATIC - Bias]
    J --> L[RANDOM - Scatter]
    J --> M[GROSS - Blunder]
    
    style A fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#8b4513
    style C fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064
    style E fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

## 4.3 Quantitative Error Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Raw Measurement Data X_i] --> B[Compute True Reference Value X_t]
    B --> C[Calculate Absolute Error E = X_i - X_t]
    C --> D[Compute Percentage Error ε_percent]
    D --> E{Within Tolerance?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Accept Measurement]
    E -->|No| G[Investigate Source of Error]
    G --> H{Source Identified?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Apply Correction or Recalibrate]
    H -->|No| J[Flag for Further Statistical Analysis]
    I --> K[Document in Safety Report]
    F --> K
    J --> K
    
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style F fill:#c8e6c9

    style K fill:#fff9c4
```
