<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the term "Error" in the context of safety engineering. Differentiate between systematic error and random error.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> In safety engineering, an **error** is defined as any deviation between the intended or true value of a parameter and its observed or measured value, or as an inappropriate human action that compromises safety. (1 mark)

> **Systematic Error** is a consistent, repeatable, and directional deviation caused by faulty calibration, instrument bias, or incorrect procedure. It can be eliminated by recalibration or correction. (1 mark)

> **Random Error** is an unpredictable scatter of measurements around the mean, caused by inherent noise, environmental fluctuations, or operator variability. It is reduced by repeated measurements and statistical averaging but cannot be entirely eliminated. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is Human Error Probability (HEP)? State its typical range.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Human Error Probability (HEP)** is the ratio of the number of errors committed to the total number of opportunities for error during a given operation. It is a dimensionless probability used in human reliability analysis. (2 marks)

$$\text{HEP} = \dfrac{N_e}{N_o}$$

> The typical range of HEP in industrial safety contexts is **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$** (1 mark for range), with very critical operations (e.g., reactor SCRAM) having HEP as low as $10^{-5}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain James Reason's **Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation. With the help of a suitable diagram, describe how the alignment of errors in multiple defensive layers leads to an accident. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure safety valve is required to operate on demand. The dangerous undetected failure rate is $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour, and the proof-test interval is $T_I = 8760$ hours (1 year). Calculate the average Probability of Failure on Demand ($PFD_{avg}$). Comment on whether the system meets SIL 1 requirements (assume $PFD_{avg}$ between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-2}$ for SIL 1). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Swiss Cheese Model (7 marks):**

The **Swiss Cheese Model**, proposed by James Reason in 1990, is a metaphor for organizational accidents. (1 mark)

- Each organizational system has **multiple defensive layers** — organizational policies, management supervision, preconditions (training, equipment), and immediate unsafe acts. (2 marks)
- Each layer is represented metaphorically as a slice of Swiss cheese containing **holes**. The holes represent **latent errors** (organizational/management) and **active errors** (frontline operator actions). (2 marks)
- An accident occurs when the **holes in all layers momentarily align**, creating a trajectory of opportunity for the hazard to reach the victim, asset, or environment. (1 mark)
- The model's key insight is that accidents are rarely caused by a single failure — they are the result of **multiple failures aligning across defensive layers**. (1 mark)

[Refer to diagram in Section 4.1]

**Part (b) — PFD Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $T_I = 8760$ hours

**Step 1 — State the formula:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I}{2} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**
$$5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 5 \times 8760 \times 10^{-6} = 43800 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0438 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 2:**
$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{0.0438}{2} = 0.0219 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} \approx 2.19 \times 10^{-2}}$$

Since $PFD_{avg} = 0.0219$ falls within the SIL 1 range of $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, **the system meets SIL 1 requirements**. (2 marks for commenting correctly)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Classify **human errors** as per Reason's taxonomy. Differentiate between slips, lapses, and mistakes with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety analyst records 12 errors in 5,000 operations performed by workers in a chemical plant over 3 months.
(i) Calculate the Human Error Probability (HEP).
(ii) If each operation takes on average 2 hours, calculate the error rate per hour.
(iii) What is the reliability of the system over 500 hours, assuming Poisson behavior? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Human Error Classification (7 marks):**

Reason classifies human errors into three main types: (1 mark)

1. **Slips** — Errors of execution where the *plan is correct* but the action deviates from it. These occur at the **skill-based level** of behavior. (2 marks)
   *Example:* A pilot presses the flap lever instead of the landing gear lever during approach. (included in 2 marks)

2. **Lapses** — Errors involving **memory failure**, often internal and unnoticed. They are also skill-based. (2 marks)
   *Example:* Forgetting to set the parking brake after parking the vehicle. (included in 2 marks)

3. **Mistakes** — Errors of **planning or decision-making** where the plan itself is faulty. These occur at the rule-based or knowledge-based level. (2 marks)
   *Example:* A doctor prescribing a wrong drug due to misdiagnosis (knowledge-based mistake).

**Part (b) — Quantitative Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) HEP Calculation (2 marks):**
$$HEP = \dfrac{N_e}{N_o} = \dfrac{12}{5000} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(ii) Error Rate per Hour (2 marks):**

Total operation time = $5000 \times 2 = 10{,}000$ hours

$$\lambda = \dfrac{N_e}{T \cdot N_o} = \dfrac{12}{10{,}000} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ errors/hour}$$

(Alternative equivalent computation: $\lambda = HEP / T_{avg} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} / 2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ per hour.)

**(iii) Reliability over 500 hours (3 marks):**

Using the Poisson-derived reliability function:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$R(500) = e^{-(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 500} = e^{-0.6}$$

$$R(500) = 0.5488$$

$$\boxed{R(500) \approx 0.549 \text{ or } 54.9\%}$$

This means there is approximately a **45.1% probability of at least one error** in the next 500 hours — a significant safety concern requiring intervention.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU OESFT837 Module 3 Error Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Slips and Mistakes:** Students frequently interchange these. *Slip = execution error (plan OK); Mistake = planning error (plan wrong).* Examiners specifically allocate 1–2 marks for this distinction.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $PFD_{avg}$:** The "average" PFD formula divides by 2, not 1. Students who write $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} T_I$ will be marked wrong.
> 
> 3. **Unit confusion in $HEP$:** $HEP$ is *dimensionless*. Stating units like "%" or "per hour" is a 0.5 mark deduction.
> 
> 4. **Missing the reliability function form:** Using $R(t) = 1 - \lambda t$ instead of $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is incorrect unless an explicit approximation is justified.
> 
> 5. **Diagram labels in Swiss Cheese:** Examiners expect *both* "latent errors" and "active errors" to be labeled. Skipping one loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Not converting to percentage:** When the question asks for percentage error and the student writes a fraction, 0.5 marks are deducted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Error**

- ☐ **Definition:** Error = deviation between intended/planned and actual/executed action or value.
- ☐ **Three Types (Reason):** Slips (execution), Lapses (memory), Mistakes (planning).
- ☐ **Two Categories (Quantitative):** Systematic Error (bias, fixable) vs Random Error (scatter, statistical).
- ☐ **Swiss Cheese Model:** Multi-layered defense; accidents = alignment of holes in multiple layers.
- ☐ **HEP formula:** $HEP = N_e / N_o$; typical range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$.
- ☐ **Reliability function:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (Poisson assumption).
- ☐ **PFD formula:** $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} T_I / 2$ (IEC 61508 simplified form).
- ☐ **Percentage Error formula:** $\varepsilon_\% = \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t} \times 100$.
- ☐ **SIL 1 Range:** $PFD_{avg}$ between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-2}$.
- ☐ **Standard Deviation (Random Error):** $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$.
- ☐ **Six Sigma metric:** $3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO) is the goal.
- ☐ **Real-world link:** Errors → Latent + Active → Barrier failure → Accident (full chain).
- ☐ **Examiner pet topics:** Reason's Swiss Cheese (often 7-mark question); HEP/PFD calculations (often 7-mark numerical).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Failure Mode** is defined as the physical or functional manifestation by which a system, subsystem, component, or process fails to perform its intended design function. It describes the **observable manner**, **observable condition**, or **observable state** in which a deficiency or breakdown becomes evident to the user, maintainer, or monitoring system.

In the context of qualitative and quantitative safety analysis, a failure mode is the **lowest level of resolution** at which a failure is identified within the system hierarchy. It is the cornerstone term of structured techniques such as **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**, **Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis (FMECA)**, and **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**.

A failure mode is therefore distinct from:
- **Failure Cause**: The *reason* why the failure occurred (e.g., corrosion, operator error).
- **Failure Effect**: The *consequence* of the failure on the system (e.g., loss of braking, overheating).
- **Failure Mechanism**: The *physical, chemical, or biological process* that produced the failure (e.g., fatigue crack propagation, oxidation).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The Family Car**
> Think of your car as a safety-critical system. Your car's *braking subsystem* can fail in many distinct ways:
> - **Failure Mode A**: Complete loss of braking (catastrophic functional failure).
> - **Failure Mode B**: Brake pedal goes soft due to air in the hydraulic line (degraded performance failure).
> - **Failure Mode C**: Brake disc cracks under thermal stress (structural failure).
> - **Failure Mode D**: Brake warning lamp remains lit due to sensor short circuit (incipient failure).
>
> Each of these is a different **Failure Mode**. The car's brake does not simply "fail or not fail" — it can fail in dozens of *specific observable ways*, each requiring a different fix, having a different severity, and a different detectability profile.

### 1.3 Hierarchical Classification of Failure

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard KTU Hierarchy**
> A failure is described by *three nested layers*:
> 1. **Failure Mechanism** → the *internal physics* (e.g., creep, fatigue, wear).
> 2. **Failure Mode** → the *observable symptom* (e.g., fracture, seizure, leakage, drift).
> 3. **Failure Effect** → the *operational consequence* (e.g., loss of thrust, fire hazard).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used With Failure Modes

The following standard safety/reliability metrics govern how failure modes are quantified:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | failures per million hours (FPMH) |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | hours |
| Mean Time To Failure | MTTF | hours |
| Probability of Failure | $P_{f}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Severity | $S$ | ordinal 1–10 scale |
| Occurrence | $O$ | ordinal 1–10 scale |
| Detectability | $D$ | ordinal 1–10 scale |

> [!NOTE] **Severity, Occurrence, and Detectability** are typically rated on a **1 to 10 ordinal scale**, where **1 = negligible / extremely unlikely / almost certain detection** and **10 = catastrophic / very high likelihood / impossible to detect**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The treatment of failure modes in safety engineering follows a structured logical chain:

- **Step 1 — System Decomposition (Functional Breakdown)**
  The parent system is recursively broken into subsystems, assemblies, sub-assemblies, and components. This produces the *Bill of Analysis* (BOA). Why? Because a failure mode cannot be assigned to a black box; granularity is mandatory.

- **Step 2 — Function Definition**
  For every component, the intended design function is stated in measurable terms (e.g., "regulate pressure between **2.0 bar** and **2.5 bar**"). Without a function, a failure mode is meaningless — you cannot fail at something you were never required to do.

- **Step 3 — Failure Mode Enumeration**
  Every conceivable way the component could fail to deliver its function is listed. Standard industry sources (SAE J1739, IEC 60812, MIL-STD-1629A) recommend universal failure-mode dictionaries.

- **Step 4 — Failure Mode Classification**
  Each enumerated mode is categorized into one of five standard families:
  1. **Functional Failure** — component performs no function.
  2. **Degraded Failure** — component performs function but outside tolerance.
  3. **Intermittent Failure** — function is sometimes available, sometimes not.
  4. **Incipient Failure** — function is delivered but degradation is underway.
  5. **Extraneous/Erroneous Function** — component produces an unintended output.

- **Step 5 — Effect and Severity Tracing**
  Each failure mode is propagated upward through the system hierarchy to determine its local, next-higher, and end-effect. A **Severity rating (S)** is assigned.

- **Step 6 — Quantitative Risk Prioritisation**
  The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is computed to rank failure modes for corrective action.

- **Step 7 — Mitigation and Verification**
  Design changes, detection methods, and operational controls are proposed. The RPN is recomputed until acceptable.

### 2.2 Universal List of Standard Failure Modes (Industry Dictionary)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield — Memorize This List**
> Below are the **universal engineering failure modes** used across FMEA libraries:

- **Failure to operate (no function)**
- **Failure to stop (runaway)**
- **Loss of output / partial loss of output**
- **Premature operation (operates when not commanded)**
- **Failure to close / open (stuck)**
- **Leakage (internal or external)**
- **Seizure / binding / jamming**
- **Drift out of specification**
- **Short circuit / open circuit / high impedance**
- **Structural fracture / crack / rupture**
- **Buckling / collapse / deformation**
- **Wear / erosion / corrosion**
- **Cavitation / vibration / noise**
- **Overheating / thermal runaway**
- **Contamination / fouling / plugging**
- **Loss of calibration / scale error**
- **Signal error / wrong reading**
- **Spurious trip / false alarm**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Formula Table**

| Formula # | Formula Name | Equation | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Each term in 1–10 scale, max $RPN = 1000$ |
| 2 | Criticality (MIL-STD-1629A) | $C_{m} = \beta \times \alpha \times \lambda_{p} \times t$ | $\beta$ = conditional failure probability, $\alpha$ = failure mode ratio, $\lambda_p$ = part failure rate |
| 3 | Exponential Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Valid for constant failure rate (useful life) |
| 4 | Probability of Failure | $P_{f}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Complement of reliability |
| 5 | MTBF (constant $\lambda$) | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours, when $\lambda$ is in failures/hour |
| 6 | Hazard Function | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate |
| 7 | Series System Reliability | $R_{s} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}$ | Independence assumed |
| 8 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_{p} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_{i})$ | At least one works |
| 9 | Bathtub Hazard | $\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \alpha t + \dfrac{\beta}{t}$ | Infant mortality, useful life, wear-out |
| 10 | FMECA Criticality Index | $C_{r} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{m,i}$ | Sum across all failure modes of item |

> [!WARNING] **LaTeX Table Pitfall** — In markdown tables, the absolute-value or vertical-bar notation is written as `\vert` or `\mid`, never the bare `\vert x \vert` style that breaks table parsing.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Prevents catastrophic single-point failures in flight control surfaces.
- **Nuclear Power**: Mandatory FMECA on safety-critical Instrumented Systems per IEC 61508 / IEC 61511.
- **Automotive**: ISO 26262 mandates failure mode analysis for ASIL-rated functions.
- **Medical Devices**: FDA 21 CFR Part 820 demands documented failure modes for risk management.
- **Process Industry**: Failure modes of relief valves, interlocks, and pressure vessels are legally required.
- **Railway Signaling**: EN 50129 requires exhaustive failure-mode catalogues to demonstrate safety integrity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reliability Function $R(t)$

We begin with the assumption that the hazard (instantaneous failure rate) $\lambda$ is **constant** during the useful-life region of the bathtub curve.

$$
h(t) = \lambda \quad \text{(assumption: constant hazard)}
$$

By definition, the hazard function is related to the reliability function as follows:

$$
h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Here, $f(t)$ is the probability density function of failure times. Substituting the constant hazard:

$$
-\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda
$$

Rearranging to separate the variables $R(t)$ and $t$:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt
$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$, with initial condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$
\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

Evaluating the integrals:

$$
\ln[R(t)] - \ln(1) = -\lambda t
$$

Since $\ln(1) = 0$:

$$
\ln[R(t)] = -\lambda t
$$

Exponentiating both sides yields the canonical reliability function:

$$
\boxed{\,R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\,}
$$

The probability of failure is then:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{f}(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}\,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

The expected value of the time-to-failure distribution is given by:

$$
MTBF = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt
$$

Using integration by parts, $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$, with $u = t$ and $dv = f(t) \, dt$:

$$
MTBF = \big[t \cdot R(t)\big]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt
$$

Since $\lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot R(t) = 0$ (assuming finite mean) and $R(0) = 1$:

$$
MTBF = 0 - 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

Evaluating:

$$
MTBF = \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \frac{1}{-\lambda}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Series System Reliability

Consider $n$ **independent** components in series. The system functions only if *every* component functions. Using the product rule of independent probabilities:

$$
R_{s} = P(\text{component 1 works} \cap \text{component 2 works} \cap \dots \cap \text{component } n \text{ works})
$$

By independence:

$$
R_{s} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_{i} t} = e^{-(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_{i})t}
$$

The combined equivalent failure rate is:

$$
\boxed{\,\lambda_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_{i}\,}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of RPN (Risk Priority Number)

The RPN is defined operationally as the **triple product** of severity, occurrence, and detectability scores. It is a heuristic ranking index:

$$
\boxed{\,RPN = S \times O \times D\,}
$$

Worked numeric example:
- Severity $S = 8$ (High — major injury possible)
- Occurrence $O = 5$ (Moderate — once per 2000 events)
- Detectability $D = 6$ (Low — only detected by inspection)

$$
RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 6 = 240
$$

An RPN above **100** typically warrants corrective action; above **200** is critical.

### 3.5 Criticality Number Derivation (MIL-STD-1629A)

The classic quantitative criticality index for a given failure mode $m$ of a part is:

$$
C_{m} = \beta \times \alpha \times \lambda_{p} \times t
$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = conditional probability that failure mode $m$ results in the critical effect.
- $\alpha$ = fraction of $\lambda_{p}$ attributable to mode $m$ (modes sum to 1 over a part).
- $\lambda_{p}$ = failure rate of the part (failures per hour).
- $t$ = mission duration (hours).

The total criticality for the part sums over all its failure modes:

$$
C_{r} = \sum_{m=1}^{M} C_{m}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Failure Mode Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 System Decomposition] --> B[Step 2 Function Definition per Component]
    B --> C[Step 3 Failure Mode Enumeration using Standard Dictionary]
    C --> D[Step 4 Classification into Functional Degraded Intermittent Incipient Erroneous]
    D --> E[Step 5 Effect Tracing Local Next Higher End Effect]
    E --> F[Step 6 Severity Occurrence Detectability Scoring]
    F --> G[Step 7 Compute RPN Equals S times O times D]
    G --> H{RPN Above Threshold 100}
    H -->|Yes| I[Step 8 Design Mitigation Detection Control]
    I --> J[Step 9 Recompute RPN]
    J --> K{Acceptable RPN Below 100}
    H -->|No| K
    K -->|Yes| L[Step 10 Document and Close Action Item]
    K -->|No| I
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Failure Mode Categorisation Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Failure Mode Master Set] --> B[Functional Family]
    A --> C[Structural Family]
    A --> D[Environmental Family]
    A --> E[Human Interaction Family]
    B --> B1[Loss of Function]
    B --> B2[Premature Function]
    B --> B3[Intermittent Function]
    B --> B4[Erroneous Function]
    C --> C1[Fracture]
    C --> C2[Wear]
    C --> C3[Corrosion]
    C --> C4[Buckling]
    D --> D1[Thermal Overload]
    D --> D2[Vibration Failure]
    D --> D3[Contamination]
    E --> E1[Operator Error]
    E --> E2[Maintenance Induced Failure]
    E --> E3[Procedural Slip]
```

### 4.3 Bathtub Curve Schematic (ASCII Functional Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infant Mortality Region Lambda Decreasing] --> B[Useful Life Region Lambda Constant]
    B --> C[Wear Out Region Lambda Increasing]
```

### 4.4 RPN Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Failure Mode i] --> Sev[Assign Severity S 1 to 10]
    Sev --> Occ[Assign Occurrence O 1 to 10]
    Occ --> Det[Assign Detectability D 1 to 10]
    Det --> Mul[Compute RPN Equals S times O times D]
    Mul --> Sort[Sort Failure Modes Descending by RPN]
    Sort --> Threshold{RPN Greater Than 100}
    Threshold -->|Yes| Action[Schedule Corrective Action]
    Threshold -->|No| Monitor[Monitor in Operational Use]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Compilation Note** — All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (A, B, C1 etc.) to avoid reserved-keyword collisions. All node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting or HTML tables.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Failure Mode" and distinguish it from "Failure Cause" and "Failure Effect" with one example of each in a pressure relief valve system.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Failure Mode** is the manner in which a system, component, or process fails to perform its intended function — it is the *observable symptom* of failure. For a pressure relief valve:

- **Failure Mode**: *Valve fails to open at set pressure.* (This is the observable behaviour.)
- **Failure Cause**: *Spring fatigue causing loss of preload.* (This is the underlying physical/root reason.)
- **Failure Effect**: *Pressure vessel over-pressurisation leading to catastrophic rupture.* (This is the operational consequence.)

The three concepts form a causal chain: **Cause → Mode → Effect**.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for each correct definition + 1 mark for example.)*

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the Risk Priority Number (RPN) and state its mathematical form, the range of each factor, and its limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a heuristic metric used in FMEA to rank failure modes for prioritisation of corrective actions.

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic)
- $O$ = Occurrence (1 = extremely rare, 10 = very frequent)
- $D$ = Detectability (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = undetectable)

The minimum RPN is $1$, the maximum is $1000$.

**Limitation**: RPN treats the three factors as continuous and equally weighted on a linear scale, even though they are ordinal and often non-linear in real risk. Two failure modes with very different risk profiles (e.g., $S=10, O=1, D=1 \Rightarrow RPN=10$ versus $S=4, O=5, D=5 \Rightarrow RPN=100$) can be ranked counter-intuitively, since the formula does not capture the true severity-weighted risk.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for ranges, 1 mark for limitation.)*

---

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the standard procedure to perform a **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**. List the eight standard steps and the deliverables at each step. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A hydraulic pump in a CNC machine has a failure rate $\lambda = 0.00025$ failures per hour. Compute the reliability $R(t)$, probability of failure $P_f(t)$, and MTBF for an operating period of $t = 1000$ hours. If three such pumps are placed in parallel as redundancy, compute the parallel system reliability at $t = 1000$ hours. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Solution to Question A(a): FMEA Procedure**

**Step 1 — Define the scope and system boundary.** Identify what is included in the analysis (which system, which mission phase, which environment). *Deliverable: Analysis Charter.*

**Step 2 — Construct the block diagram.** Decompose the system hierarchically into subsystems and components. *Deliverable: System hierarchy diagram (BOA — Bill of Analysis).*

**Step 3 — List functions of each item.** State the design intent for each component in measurable terms. *Deliverable: Function matrix.*

**Step 4 — Identify failure modes.** Use a universal dictionary (SAE J1739, IEC 60812) to enumerate possible failure modes per component. *Deliverable: Failure mode list.*

**Step 5 — Determine effects of each failure mode.** Trace the local, next-higher-level, and end effects on mission, safety, and cost. *Deliverable: Effects column in FMEA worksheet.*

**Step 6 — Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detectability (D) scores.** Use standard 1–10 scales. *Deliverable: Scoring columns.*

**Step 7 — Compute RPN and prioritise.** $RPN = S \times O \times D$. Sort descending. *Deliverable: Ranked list.*

**Step 8 — Recommend corrective actions and verify.** Propose design changes, sensors, or procedural controls. Recompute RPN to verify improvement. *Deliverable: Action register and closed-out FMEA.*

---

##### **Solution to Question A(b): Quantitative Reliability Calculation**

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 0.00025$ failures/hour
- $t = 1000$ hours
- 3 identical pumps in parallel redundancy

**Step 1 — Compute $\lambda t$ (the dimensionless hazard product):**

$$
\lambda t = 0.00025 \times 1000 = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Compute reliability of single pump:**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.25}
$$

Using $e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$:

$$
\boxed{\,R_{single}(1000) = 0.7788\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of failure of single pump:**

$$
P_{f}(1000) = 1 - R(1000) = 1 - 0.7788 = 0.2212
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{f}(1000) = 0.2212\,}
$$

**Step 4 — MTBF:**

$$
MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.00025} = 4000 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,MTBF = 4000 \text{ hours}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Parallel system reliability.** For 3 independent identical pumps in parallel, system works if at least one works. Reliability of parallel system:

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R_{single})^{3}
$$

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - 0.7788)^{3} = 1 - (0.2212)^{3}
$$

Computing $(0.2212)^3$:

$$
(0.2212)^{3} = 0.2212 \times 0.2212 \times 0.2212 = 0.04893 \times 0.2212 \approx 0.01082
$$

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - 0.01082 = 0.98918
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_{parallel}(1000) \approx 0.9892 \text{ or } 98.92\%\,}
$$

**Inference:** Parallel redundancy of three pumps raises reliability from 77.88 % to 98.92 % at 1000 hours — a **substantial safety improvement** typical of high-availability CNC hydraulic systems.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks each for $R$, $P_f$, $MTBF$, plus 1 mark for parallel formula setup.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the five standard classifications of failure modes with one engineering example each. Discuss how each category influences the choice of detection method. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A sensor in a chemical reactor safety interlock has the following FMEA profile: Severity $S = 9$, Occurrence $O = 4$, Detectability $D = 7$. The failure rate of the sensor is $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour. Mission time is $t = 2000$ hours. The conditional probability that the failure mode leads to a critical effect is $\beta = 0.6$, and the fraction of failures attributable to this mode is $\alpha = 0.4$. Compute:
- (i) The RPN
- (ii) The Criticality Number $C_m$
- (iii) Comment on whether the design is acceptable (RPN threshold = 200). *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Solution to Question B(a): Five Classifications of Failure Mode**

1. **Functional Failure (Total Loss of Function):** Component completely stops performing. *Example: Pump impeller seizes — no flow.* Detection: Audible/flow-loss alarm.

2. **Degraded Failure:** Function delivered but outside tolerance. *Example: Pressure transmitter reads 5 % low — safety setpoint may be breached.* Detection: Periodic calibration, online diagnostics.

3. **Intermittent Failure:** Function delivered sometimes and lost at other times. *Example: Loose connector in PLC input — random shut-down.* Detection: Event logs, watchdog timers.

4. **Incipient Failure:** Function delivered but degradation is underway. *Example: Bearing with developing spalling — vibration amplitude rising slowly.* Detection: Condition monitoring (vibration spectra).

5. **Erroneous / Extraneous Function:** Component delivers an unintended output. *Example: Solenoid valve opens when commanded closed — over-firing of heater.* Detection: Cross-check with redundant signal.

**Influence on detection method:** Functional failures are easiest (binary alarm); degraded and incipient failures demand *predictive* techniques such as trend analysis; intermittent and erroneous failures require *self-checking* or *redundant comparator* logic.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark per category, 2 marks for detection discussion.)*

---

##### **Solution to Question B(b): RPN and Criticality Calculation**

**Given:**
- $S = 9$, $O = 4$, $D = 7$
- $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour
- $t = 2000$ hours
- $\beta = 0.6$, $\alpha = 0.4$

**(i) RPN Calculation:**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 4 \times 7 = 252
$$

$$
\boxed{\,RPN = 252\,}
$$

**(ii) Criticality Number $C_m$:**

$$
C_{m} = \beta \times \alpha \times \lambda_{p} \times t
$$

$$
C_{m} = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 2000
$$

Step-by-step:
$$
0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24
$$

$$
5 \times 10^{-5} \times 2000 = 0.1
$$

$$
C_{m} = 0.24 \times 0.1 = 0.024
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{m} = 0.024 \text{ failures per million hours equivalent (dimensionless index)}\,}
$$

**(iii) Acceptability Comment:**

- Threshold $RPN_{threshold} = 200$
- Computed $RPN = 252 > 200$ → **NOT acceptable**
- The design requires corrective action: either reduce occurrence (better component, derating), or improve detectability (add redundant sensor with cross-check), or lower severity (add upstream protection).
- Since $C_m$ is relatively low, the *expected frequency* of the critical event is bounded, but the *consequence* (driven by $S=9$) is severe enough to mandate action.

**Recommendation:** Add a redundant sensor with a 2-out-of-2 voting comparator (improves $D$ from 7 → 3) and conduct accelerated stress screening (improves $O$ from 4 → 2). New RPN estimate: $9 \times 2 \times 3 = 54$, well below threshold.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for RPN, 3 marks for $C_m$ arithmetic, 2 marks for comment.)*

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **RPN vs. $C_m$ confusion:** Many students interchange Risk Priority Number and Criticality Number. Remember — RPN is a **qualitative heuristic ranking** (1–1000), whereas $C_m$ (MIL-STD-1629A) is a **quantitative failure rate-based metric**.
> 2. **Series/parallel reliability:** A common error is using $R_{series} = \sum R_i$. The correct form is the **product**, not the sum.
> 3. **Units of $\lambda$:** Always convert failure rates to consistent time units before computing $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Mixing FPMH (failures per million hours) with mission hours is a classic mistake.
> 4. **Severity vs. Occurrence vs. Detectability reversal:** Detectability $D=1$ means *easily detected*, not *hard to detect*. This is the most common inversion in KTU scripts.
> 5. **Parallel redundancy assumption:** Parallel reliability formula $R_p = 1 - (1-R)^n$ assumes **independent** failures and a **k-out-of-n** voting rule. Always state this assumption explicitly to earn the methodology marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Failure Mode** = *manner/observable symptom* of failure (NOT cause, NOT effect).
- ☐ Failure analysis hierarchy: **Cause → Mode → Effect → Consequence**.
- ☐ Five universal failure mode families: **Functional, Degraded, Intermittent, Incipient, Erroneous**.
- ☐ FMEA procedure: **8 steps** — Scope → Decomposition → Function → Mode → Effect → Score → RPN → Action.
- ☐ **RPN = $S \times O \times D$**; each in 1–10; max 1000; threshold typically 100–200.
- ☐ **Reliability (constant $\lambda$):** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **Probability of failure:** $P_f(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **MTBF** = $1 / \lambda$ (in consistent units).
- ☐ **Series reliability:** $R_s = \prod R_i$; **Parallel reliability:** $R_p = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$.
- ☐ **Criticality (MIL-STD-1629A):** $C_m = \beta \alpha \lambda_p t$.
- ☐ Universal failure mode dictionary contains: loss of function, premature function, leakage, seizure, drift, fracture, wear, corrosion, overheating, spurious trip.
- ☐ Severity scale: $S=10$ = catastrophic (death, system loss); $S=1$ = negligible.
- ☐ Detectability: $D=1$ = almost certainly detected; $D=10$ = undetectable.
- ☐ Mitigation priority: **Reduce Occurrence first** (root cause), then **improve Detectability** (sensors), then **contain Severity** (barriers).
- ☐ Standards referenced: IEC 60812 (FMEA), MIL-STD-1629A (FMECA), SAE J1739 (Automotive FMEA), ISO 26262 (Automotive Functional Safety), IEC 61508 (Functional Safety).
- ☐ Always state assumptions: **independence** of failures, **constant $\lambda$** regime, **mission time** boundary.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Failure Classification** is the systematic, multi-axial categorization of equipment, component, structural, or system failures into well-defined taxonomies based on **mode**, **cause**, **mechanism**, **rate of occurrence**, and **severity of consequences**. In the context of qualitative and quantitative safety engineering, failure classification forms the foundational vocabulary for **Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis (FMECA)**, **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, **Reliability Block Diagrams (RBD)**, and **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Failure classification is the gateway concept that feeds every downstream safety quantification tool (FMEA, FTA, Markov analysis, Weibull modelling). Skipping a clean taxonomy guarantees inconsistency in your final-year project and KTU viva.

### 1.2 The Five Primary Classification Axes

| # | Classification Axis | Core Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mode of Failure** | *What did the component look/sound/feel like when it failed?* |
| 2 | **Cause of Failure** | *Why did the failure occur?* (design, material, human, environment) |
| 3 | **Mechanism of Failure** | *What physical/chemical process drove the failure?* |
| 4 | **Rate of Failure** | *When in the lifecycle is failure most likely?* |
| 5 | **Severity of Failure** | *What was the consequence on safety, mission, cost?* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A complete safety report must address **all five axes**. Addressing only the mode (e.g., "fractured") without the mechanism (e.g., "transgranular stress-corrosion cracking") is considered an incomplete engineering diagnosis and is the single most common cause of point deductions in KTU ESE evaluations.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Five-Question Diagnosis"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Patient Diagnosis:**
> Imagine a patient arrives at a hospital. A doctor does **not** just say "the patient is sick". Instead the doctor asks five layered questions:
> 1. **Symptom (Mode):** "What hurts?" → Fractured bone.
> 2. **Cause (Cause):** "Why?" → Car accident.
> 3. **Mechanism (Process):** "How did the bone break?" → Sudden shear load exceeding bone's ultimate tensile strength.
> 4. **Rate (History):** "When in life?" → Was the patient a toddler (infant-mortality fall), middle-aged (random accident), or elderly (osteoporosis wear-out)?
> 5. **Severity (Consequence):** "How bad?" → Hairline (minor) vs. compound (catastrophic).
>
> **Failure Classification = The Engineering Doctor's Five-Question Diagnosis.** Skip one, and the treatment (safety redesign) is guesswork.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Failure Rate ($\lambda$):** measured in **failures per million hours (fpmh)** or **failures per 10⁹ hours (FIT)**.
- **Reliability $R(t)$:** dimensionless, $0 \le R(t) \le 1$.
- **Hazard Rate $h(t)$:** failures per unit time, **1/hour** in SI.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** hours.
- **Availability $A$:** dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme:** The symbol $\lambda$ denotes a **constant failure rate** (only valid in the random-failure region). When failure rate varies with time, we use the hazard function notation $h(t)$ to avoid confusion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification by **Mode of Failure** (The "What")

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode** is the observable symptom — the engineering equivalent of a patient's chief complaint.

| Mode | Description | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Fracture** | Complete separation into two or more parts | Brittle shaft snapping |
| **Deformation (Elastic / Plastic)** | Permanent or recoverable shape change | Yielding beam |
| **Wear** | Gradual material removal | Brake pad thinning |
| **Corrosion** | Chemical/electrochemical material loss | Rusting pipeline |
| **Leakage** | Loss of containment | Valve packing failure |
| **Jamming / Seizure** | Loss of relative motion | Bearing seizure |
| **Loosening** | Loss of preload | Bolt loosening under vibration |
| **Buckling** | Sudden lateral instability | Slender column failure |
| **Cavitation** | Vapor bubble collapse damage | Pump impeller pitting |

## 2.2 Classification by **Cause of Failure** (The "Why")

Causes are grouped under the classic **four-M plus environment** framework:

- **Material:** Wrong alloy, inclusions, defects, porosity.
- **Manufacturing:** Machining errors, heat-treatment errors, residual stress.
- **Mechanical (Design):** Under-sized, stress concentration, mis-loaded.
- **Maintenance:** Lubrication failure, wrong torque, missed inspection.
- **Environment:** Temperature, humidity, radiation, chemical exposure.

## 2.3 Classification by **Mechanism of Failure** (The "How")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism** is the *physical/chemical process* that drives failure. This is the deepest level of analysis — board examiners award full marks only when the student names the *correct* mechanism.

Primary mechanisms taught in KTU Module 3:

1. **Ductile Fracture** — significant plastic deformation before fracture.
2. **Brittle Fracture** — little/no plastic deformation; cleavage along crystallographic planes.
3. **Fatigue** — sub-critical cyclic loading; initiation + propagation.
4. **Creep** — time-dependent deformation at elevated temperature (typically $T > 0.4 T_{m}$).
5. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — synergistic tensile stress + corrosive environment.
6. **Hydrogen Embrittlement** — hydrogen ingress reducing ductility.
7. **Thermal Fatigue** — cyclic thermal stress.
8. **Wear Mechanisms** — adhesive, abrasive, fatigue, corrosive, erosive.

## 2.4 Classification by **Rate of Failure** — The Bathtub Curve

The **bathtub curve** divides component life into three statistical regimes:

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Decreasing failure rate (DFR), $\beta < 1$ in Weibull model. Caused by manufacturing defects, poor installation, commissioning errors.
- **Region II — Useful Life (Random Failures):** Approximately **constant failure rate (CFR)**, $\beta = 1$. Failure is stochastic, independent of age. **This is where most reliability math operates.**
- **Region III — Wear-out:** Increasing failure rate (IFR), $\beta > 1$. Caused by cumulative damage — fatigue, wear, corrosion, creep.

## 2.5 Classification by **Severity** (The "How Bad")

KTU follows the MIL-STD-1629A / IEC 60812 severity scale:

| Category | Effect | Typical Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Catastrophic** | Loss of life / major loss | Safety redesign mandatory |
| **Critical** | Major mission/cost loss, injury | Immediate corrective action |
| **Major** | Significant delay, partial mission loss | Scheduled correction |
| **Minor** | Negotiable, no injury, minor cost | Logging only |
| **Negligible** | Below threshold | No action |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** Every formula below is a potential 14-mark question on the KTU ESE paper.

### Core Probability & Reliability Functions

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f(t)$ | Probability Density of Failure | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | $\text{hour}^{-1}$ |
| $F(t)$ | Cumulative Distribution (CDF) | $F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, d\tau$ | dimensionless |
| $R(t)$ | Reliability | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | dimensionless |
| $h(t)$ | Hazard (Instantaneous Failure Rate) | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | $\text{hour}^{-1}$ |
| $\lambda$ | Constant Failure Rate (Region II) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | $\text{hour}^{-1}$ |
| $R(t)$ | Exponential Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{MTBF}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | hours |

### Weibull Distribution (Parametric Model)

| Symbol | Name | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta$ | Shape Parameter | $\beta < 1$ → infant; $\beta = 1$ → random; $\beta > 1$ → wear-out |
| $\eta$ | Scale (Characteristic Life) | time at which 63.2% have failed |
| $\gamma$ | Location (Guarantee Time) | minimum life before failure can begin |
| $f(t)$ | Weibull PDF | $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t-\gamma)^{\beta}/\eta^{\beta}}$ |
| $R(t)$ | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t-\gamma)^{\beta}/\eta^{\beta}}$ |
| $h(t)$ | Weibull Hazard | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ |
| $t_{0.5}$ | Median Life | $t_{0.5} = \gamma + \eta(\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$ |

### Bathtub Curve & Series Systems

| Concept | Formula |
|---|---|
| Series system reliability | $R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ |
| Series system failure rate (constant) | $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ |
| Availability (1-repair cycle) | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ |
| Mean Time To Failure (Weibull) | $\text{MTTF} = \gamma + \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical-bar symbol `$\vert$` in tables is rendered as `$\mid$` to keep the markdown table structure intact — this is required by KTU's online exam renderer.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Aerospace:** FAA mandates failure classification per AS6081 for every critical component.
- **Nuclear Power:** 10 CFR 50.69 demands severity-graded classification (RISC, NSRST).
- **Oil & Gas:** API 571 catalogues 130+ failure mechanisms in the *Damage Mechanisms* handbook.
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 maps severity into ASIL A–D.
- **Civil:** IS 456 classifies concrete failure as flexural, shear, or bond.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Reliability from the Hazard Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark sub-question** in any KTU Module-3 ESE paper. Memorize every line.

**Starting Definition.** The conditional probability that a component which has survived to time $t$ fails in the next infinitesimal interval $[t, t+dt]$ is the hazard function $h(t)\,dt$.

$$
h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 1 — Rewrite using conditional probability identity.**

$$
P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t) = \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t)}{P(T \ge t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator expansion.** Over an infinitesimal interval, $P(t \le T < t+\Delta t) = f(t)\,\Delta t$.

$$
h(t) = \frac{f(t)\,\Delta t}{R(t)\,\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$.**

$$
h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables.** Multiply both sides by $dt$.

$$
-h(t)\,dt = \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate** from $0$ to $t$, with initial condition $R(0) = 1$.

$$
\int_{0}^{t} -h(\tau)\,d\tau = \int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the right-hand side.**

$$
-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau = \ln R(t) - \ln 1 = \ln R(t)
$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate to obtain the canonical reliability equation.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** If the question states "constant failure rate", then $h(\tau) = \lambda$ and the integral collapses to $\lambda t$, giving $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Always state this simplification explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Weibull Hazard Behaviour Across Bathtub Regions

The Weibull hazard function for the standard two-parameter form ($\gamma = 0$) is:

$$
h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

**Step 1 — Analyse the exponent $(\beta - 1)$.**

- If $\beta - 1 < 0$, i.e., $\beta < 1$, the term $t^{\beta-1}$ is a *decreasing* power of $t$. Hence $h(t)$ **decreases** with time → **Region I (Infant Mortality)**.

- If $\beta - 1 = 0$, i.e., $\beta = 1$, the time-dependence vanishes. Hence $h(t) = \beta / \eta = \text{constant}$ → **Region II (Random Failures)**. Substituting $\beta = 1$ reduces the Weibull PDF to the exponential distribution $f(t) = (1/\eta)e^{-t/\eta}$.

- If $\beta - 1 > 0$, i.e., $\beta > 1$, the term $t^{\beta-1}$ is an *increasing* power of $t$. Hence $h(t)$ **increases** with time → **Region III (Wear-out)**.

**Step 2 — Numerical illustration.** Let $\eta = 1000$ hours.

- For $\beta = 0.5$ (infant mortality), $h(100) = 0.5/1000 \cdot (0.1)^{0.5} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.316 = 1.58 \times 10^{-4}$ hr⁻¹.
- For $\beta = 1$ (random), $h(100) = 1/1000 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ hr⁻¹ (constant).
- For $\beta = 3$ (wear-out), $h(100) = 3/1000 \cdot (0.1)^{2} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.01 = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ hr⁻¹ (low at $t=100$).

This correctly shows that at early times, **wear-out** components have the *lowest* failure rate, while **infant-mortality** components have the *highest* — matching the bathtub intuition.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Series-System Reliability from Component Reliabilities

A system is functionally in series if **any one component failing** brings the system down.

**Step 1 — Define the event.** Let $E_i$ = "Component $i$ survives to time $t$". The system survives if **all** components survive:

$$
E_{\text{sys}} = E_1 \cap E_2 \cap \cdots \cap E_n
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplication rule** assuming statistical independence (a standard KTU assumption unless otherwise stated):

$$
P(E_{\text{sys}}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(E_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
$$

**Step 3 — For constant failure rates**, $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$. Therefore:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-t\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the equivalent system failure rate.**

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Trap:** The series formula assumes **independence of failures**. If components share a common root cause (e.g., common power supply, common operator), this independence is violated and you must use a conditional-probability approach or a fault tree. Examiners will deduct 2–3 marks if you blindly apply the series formula to a common-cause scenario.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How the Five Axes Interact

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix shows that the *same* observable fracture can be classified along five orthogonal axes. A complete KTU answer would name all five.

| Dimension | Question | Example: Broken Drive Shaft |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode** | What does it look like? | Brittle fracture with chevron marks |
| **Cause** | Why did it happen? | Under-sized shaft (design error) |
| **Mechanism** | How did it physically occur? | Torsional overload → cleavage fracture |
| **Rate** | When in life cycle? | Region II (random, MTBF ≈ 5000 h) |
| **Severity** | How bad is the consequence? | Critical (production line halt, $₹$ 50 lakh loss) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
**"Define failure classification. List any two methods of classification with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Failure classification is the systematic categorization of equipment/component failures along five orthogonal axes: **mode, cause, mechanism, rate, and severity**, so that safety analyses (FMEA, FTA, RCA) can be performed consistently.

**Two methods (1.5 marks each):**

- **By Mechanism:** The physical/chemical process is identified. *Example:* Fatigue — failure due to cyclic loading below the ultimate strength.
- **By Rate (Bathtub Curve):** Failure is placed on a time axis (Region I infant mortality, II random, III wear-out). *Example:* A newly commissioned pump failing within 48 hours is Region I (infant mortality).

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]
**"Explain the three regions of the bathtub curve with their physical causes."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The bathtub curve plots hazard rate $h(t)$ versus time and has three regions:

- **Region I – Infant Mortality (DFR, $\beta < 1$):** Failure rate **decreases** with time. Physical causes: manufacturing defects, poor installation, commissioning errors, substandard materials. Countermeasure: **burn-in testing**.
- **Region II – Useful Life (CFR, $\beta = 1$):** Failure rate is approximately **constant**. Physical causes: random, age-independent stochastic events — overloads, human error, environmental shocks. Countermeasure: **redundancy, scheduled inspection**.
- **Region III – Wear-out (IFR, $\beta > 1$):** Failure rate **increases** with time. Physical causes: cumulative damage — fatigue, wear, corrosion, creep. Countermeasure: **preventive replacement** at design life.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, every Module-3 Part-B question provides an **internal choice**. You must attempt **one** of the two offered. Both choices are equally weighted (7 + 7 marks).

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]

**(a) Classify engineering failures along the five primary axes (mode, cause, mechanism, rate, severity). For each axis, give at least two categories with one engineering example.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mode (What):**
- **Fracture:** Shaft breakage due to overload.
- **Leakage:** Valve-packing failure causing fluid escape.
- **Wear:** Brake-pad thinning.

**(ii) Cause (Why):**
- **Design:** Stress concentration at a sharp fillet.
- **Material:** Inclusion in a forging.
- **Manufacturing:** Improper heat treatment.
- **Maintenance:** Missed lubrication.

**(iii) Mechanism (How):**
- **Ductile Fracture:** Cup-and-cone fracture of mild-steel tensile specimen.
- **Brittle Fracture:** Cleavage fracture of cast iron at low temperature.
- **Fatigue:** Aircraft wing spar failure after $10^6$ cycles.
- **Creep:** Turbine blade elongation at $T = 900^\circ$C.
- **Stress Corrosion Cracking:** Brass cartridge case cracking in ammonia.
- **Hydrogen Embrittlement:** Electroplated bolt fracture.

**(iv) Rate (When):**
- **Region I (Infant Mortality):** Pump failure 24 hours after commissioning.
- **Region II (Random):** Random electrical short circuit after 3 years of stable operation.
- **Region III (Wear-out):** Bearing failure after 40 000 hours near design life.

**(v) Severity (How Bad):**
- **Catastrophic:** Aircraft structural failure.
- **Critical:** Plant shutdown; operator injury.
- **Minor:** Cosmetic damage.

**(b) Derive the reliability function $R(t)$ from the hazard function $h(t)$. Hence obtain the exponential model and the mean time between failures (MTBF) when the failure rate is constant.** **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of hazard.**

$$
h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $t$ with $R(0) = 1$.**

$$
-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau = \int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = \ln R(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau}
$$

**Step 4 — For constant failure rate, $h(\tau) = \lambda$.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 5 — MTBF for constant failure rate.**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Numerical example (for viva clarity):** If $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-5}$/h, then $\text{MTBF} = 50\,000$ h, and $R(10\,000) = e^{-0.2} = 0.819$.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]

**(a) What is the Weibull distribution? Derive the Weibull probability density function (PDF), reliability function $R(t)$, and hazard function $h(t)$. Show how the shape parameter $\beta$ identifies infant-mortality, random, and wear-out regions.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition.** The Weibull distribution is a versatile three-parameter lifetime model:

$$
f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t-\gamma)^{\beta}/\eta^{\beta}}
$$

where $\beta$ = shape, $\eta$ = scale (characteristic life), $\gamma$ = location (guarantee time).

**(ii) Derivation of $R(t)$:** Using $R(t) = e^{-\int h(\tau)d\tau}$ with the Weibull hazard:

$$
h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{\tau-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} d\tau = \left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R(t) = e^{-(t-\gamma)^{\beta}/\eta^{\beta}}
$$

**(iii) PDF from $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$:**

$$
f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t-\gamma)^{\beta}/\eta^{\beta}}
$$

**(iv) Shape-parameter interpretation:**

- $\beta < 1$: hazard decreases → **Region I (infant mortality)**.
- $\beta = 1$: hazard constant → **Region II (random; reduces to exponential)**.
- $\beta > 1$: hazard increases → **Region III (wear-out)**.

**(b) A bearing has Weibull parameters $\beta = 2$, $\eta = 5000$ hours, $\gamma = 0$. Calculate (i) reliability at $t = 3000$ h, (ii) hazard rate at $t = 3000$ h, (iii) median life, and (iv) MTTF.** **[7 Marks]**

**Given:** $\beta = 2$, $\eta = 5000$ h, $\gamma = 0$, $t = 3000$ h.

**(i) Reliability:**

$$
R(3000) = e^{-(3000/5000)^{2}} = e^{-0.36} = 0.6977
$$

**(ii) Hazard Rate:**

$$
h(3000) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} = \frac{2}{5000}\left(\frac{3000}{5000}\right)^{1} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.6 = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{h}^{-1}
$$

**(iii) Median Life** (time when $R = 0.5$):

$$
t_{0.5} = \gamma + \eta(\ln 2)^{1/\beta} = 0 + 5000 \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/2} = 5000 \cdot 0.8326 = 4163\ \text{h}
$$

**(iv) MTTF:**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \gamma + \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 0 + 5000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5)
$$

Recall $\Gamma(1.5) = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = 0.8862$.

$$
\text{MTTF} = 5000 \cdot 0.8862 = 4431\ \text{h}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\gamma$ term:** When the question states $\gamma = 0$, students often drop the absolute value of $(t - \gamma)$ inside the Weibull exponent and write $t^\beta/\eta^\beta$. This is correct **only** when $\gamma = 0$. Always verify.
> 2. **Mis-identifying $\beta = 1$:** Many students confuse the exponential distribution's $\lambda$ with Weibull's $\eta$. At $\beta = 1$, the Weibull **mean** equals $\eta$, and the **rate** equals $1/\eta$.
> 3. **Median vs Mean:** Median $\ne$ MTTF for $\beta \ne 1$. Don't substitute $\eta$ for MTTF when $\beta = 2$.
> 4. **Forgetting the integration step** in $R(t)$ derivation: students jump directly to $e^{-\lambda t}$ without showing the integration — losing 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:**

- [ ] Failure classification has **5 axes**: Mode, Cause, Mechanism, Rate, Severity.
- [ ] **Mode** = symptom; **Cause** = why; **Mechanism** = physical process; **Rate** = bathtub region; **Severity** = consequence grade.
- [ ] **Bathtub curve** has **3 regions**: I (DFR, $\beta<1$), II (CFR, $\beta=1$), III (IFR, $\beta>1$).
- [ ] **Core reliability identity:** $h(t) = -R'(t)/R(t)$.
- [ ] **Canonical reliability equation:** $R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau}$.
- [ ] **Exponential model:** valid in Region II; $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Weibull** is the *only* distribution whose shape parameter $\beta$ covers all three bathtub regions.
- [ ] **Weibull reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-(t-\gamma)^{\beta}/\eta^{\beta}}$; **hazard:** $h(t) = (\beta/\eta)(t-\gamma)^{\beta-1}/\eta^{\beta-1}$.
- [ ] **Median life:** $t_{0.5} = \gamma + \eta(\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$.
- [ ] **MTTF (Weibull):** $\gamma + \eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$. Remember $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.8862$.
- [ ] **Series system:** $R_{\text{sys}} = \prod R_i$; $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum \lambda_i$ (only for independent components).
- [ ] **Availability:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- [ ] **Severity scale:** Catastrophic → Critical → Major → Minor → Negligible.
- [ ] **Mechanism vocabulary** expected in viva: ductile, brittle, fatigue, creep, SCC, hydrogen embrittlement, adhesive wear, abrasive wear, thermal fatigue.
- [ ] Always show the **integration step** when deriving $R(t)$ — examiners award full marks only when this is explicit.
- [ ] When asked to "classify", produce answers across **all five axes** — partial answers lose 4–5 marks.
- [ ] Always state the **units** of $\lambda$, $h(t)$, $\text{MTBF}$ explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Cause and Common Mode Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Common Cause and Common Mode Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Cause Failure (CCF):** A Common Cause Failure is the simultaneous or near-simultaneous failure of two or more redundant components or subsystems due to a **single shared root cause**, which defeats the very purpose of redundancy in safety-instrumented systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mode Failure (CMF):** A Common Mode Failure is a specific sub-class of CCF in which multiple components fail in the **same failure mode** (i.e., the same physical mechanism) because they share a common design, manufacturer, operating environment, or maintenance procedure.

In the context of **IEC 61508** and **IEC 61511** (which form the backbone of KTU safety engineering syllabi), the assumption of *statistical independence* between redundant channels is the cornerstone of reliability mathematics. CCF/CMF directly **violates** this assumption.

| Aspect | CCF | CMF |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Scope | Broad category | Sub-class of CCF |
| Trigger | Any shared root cause (internal or external) | Same mode of physical failure |
| Typical Cause | Flood, fire, power loss, software bug | Same batch defect, identical sensor drift |
| Mathematical Treatment | Beta-Factor, Alpha-Factor, MGL Models | Often modelled as a subset using β |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Twin Cars on a Flooded Bridge"**
>
> Imagine you are driving across a flooded bridge in **two identical Toyota cars**, both following the same route. You bought the second car as a "backup" in case the first breaks down.
>
> - If the **first car gets a flat tyre** — that's an **independent failure** (the second car is unaffected).
> - If **flood water sweeps away both cars** — that's a **Common Cause Failure** (one cause, two victims).
> - If **both cars suffer brake failure at the same spot** because they were manufactured in the same batch with the same faulty brake-pad material — that's a **Common Mode Failure** (same cause, same mode, same defect).
>
> The "safety redundancy" of buying a second car is **completely defeated** by the bridge flood and the brake defect.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants / Metrics

The following standard metrics appear repeatedly in KTU board examinations:

- **β (Beta Factor)** → dimensionless, range **[0, 1]**
- **α_k (Alpha Factor)** → dimensionless, Σα_k = 1
- **λ_CCF (Common Cause Failure Rate)** → failures/hour (or /demands)
- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** → dimensionless, range **[10⁻², 10⁻⁵]**
- **DC (Diagnostic Coverage)** → dimensionless, range **[0%, 99.9%]**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why CCF/CMF Matter — The Independence Paradox

In classical reliability engineering, a redundant pair of identical components has a failure probability:

$$Q_{indep} = q_1 \times q_2 = q^2$$

This implies that if each sensor has a failure probability of **10⁻²**, then a pair in parallel gives **10⁻⁴** — a **10,000× improvement**.

However, this calculation **assumes independence**. If 50 % of failures are shared (β = 0.5), the actual system failure probability is:

$$Q_{sys} = (2 - \beta) \cdot q$$

For q = 10⁻² and β = 0.5:

$$Q_{sys} = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$$

This is **150× worse** than the textbook 10⁻⁴ prediction. **CCF/CMF therefore dominate the residual risk budget of any SIL-rated safety loop.**

### 2.2 Categories of CCF Causes

| Category | Examples | Engineering Control |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Internal — Design** | Same circuit topology, same software version | Diversity, different manufacturers |
| **Internal — Manufacturing** | Same batch ICs, same solder flux | Diverse sourcing, screening tests |
| **External — Environmental** | Temperature, humidity, vibration, EMI, flooding | Physical separation, segregation, shielding |
| **External — Operational** | Same maintenance crew, same procedure, calibration drift | Independent maintenance teams, staggered PM |
| **External — Human** | Same operator error (startup, shutdown, reset) | Diverse procedures, training rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often test whether students can **list 5 categories of CCF causes** and **provide one engineering control per category**. Memorise this table.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Beta-Factor — Single Component Failure Prob.** | $Q_{1} = (1-\beta)\,q$ | Independent fraction of component failure |
| 2 | **Beta-Factor — CCF Failure Prob.** | $Q_{CCF} = \beta\,q$ | Shared failure probability |
| 3 | **Beta-Factor — Redundant Pair System** | $Q_{sys} = (2 - \beta)\,q$ | 1oo2 voting logic |
| 4 | **Beta-Factor — 1oo3 System** | $Q_{sys} = (1-\beta)\,q + \beta\,q$ | Single-channel contribution + shared |
| 5 | **Alpha-Factor Definition** | $\alpha_k = \dfrac{N_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} N_j}$ | Fraction of failures in exactly *k* components |
| 6 | **Alpha-Factor Sum Constraint** | $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} \alpha_k = 1$ | Closure condition |
| 7 | **Alpha-Factor — Pair System Failure** | $Q_{sys} = \alpha_1\,q + \alpha_2\,q^2$ | For N = 2 redundant channel |
| 8 | **Mean Downtime for CCF** | $T_{MD} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{2\,\lambda_{D}}$ | Diagnostic-test-related downtime |
| 9 | **Fraction of Safe Failures** | $SFF = 1 - \lambda_{DD}/\lambda_D$ | SFF > 90 % required for SIL 3 |
| 10 | **SIL vs. PFD Mapping (Low-demand)** | $SIL_{n} \leftrightarrow PFD_{avg}$ | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$; SIL 4: $10^{-5}$–$10^{-4}$ |

### 2.4 Why β, α, MGL Models Exist

Because **real-world data on CCF is scarce**, quantitative models have been developed to *bound* the contribution of CCF based on limited operational data. The three most cited models in KTU/PG syllabus are:

- **Beta Factor Model (β)** — assumes ALL multi-component failures happen in **all** components simultaneously (overly pessimistic for k < N).
- **Alpha Factor Model (α_k)** — generalises β by tracking failures involving 1, 2, 3, … N components separately.
- **Multiple Greek Letter (MGL) Model** — further generalises α by allowing distinct fractions for each component in a k-fold failure event.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

CCF/CMF analysis is foundational in:

- **SIL Determination** for Safety Instrumented Functions (SIF) in oil & gas (IEC 61511).
- **Nuclear Reactor Protection Systems** (IEEE 603, IEC 61513).
- **Aerospace Fly-By-Wire** redundancy (DO-178C, DO-254).
- **Automotive ISO 26262** functional safety.
- **Medical Device FDA submissions** (IEC 62304).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Beta-Factor Model for a Redundant Pair (1oo2 Voting)

**Assumption:** Two identical channels *A* and *B*, each with constant per-demand failure probability $q$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the total failure rate $\lambda_D$ into independent and shared fractions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_D &= \lambda_{indep} + \lambda_{CCF} \\[4pt]
\text{where}\quad \lambda_{indep} &= (1-\beta)\,\lambda_D \\[4pt]
\lambda_{CCF} &= \beta\,\lambda_D
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $\beta \in [0,1]$ is the *beta factor*. If $\beta = 0$, no common-cause failure exists (perfect independence). If $\beta = 1$, every failure is shared (worst case).

**Step 2 — Express single-component failure probability:**

For a low-demand system with constant failure rate and test interval $T_i$, the per-demand failure probability is approximated as $q \approx \lambda_D \cdot T_i$.

The **independent** failure probability per channel is:

$$
q_{indep} = (1-\beta)\,q
$$

The **common-cause** failure probability affecting **both** channels simultaneously is:

$$
q_{CCF} = \beta\,q
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the parallel system failure probability:**

The system fails when **either** (a) A fails independently while B fails (CCF or independent), or (b) B fails independently while A fails. Adding both independent contributions plus the CCF contribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sys} &= 2\,(1-\beta)\,q \cdot q \;+\; \beta\,q \\[4pt]
&= 2\,(1-\beta)\,q^2 \;+\; \beta\,q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-q approximation $q^2 \ll q$:**

Since $q$ is typically $\le 10^{-2}$, the squared term $q^2 \le 10^{-4}$ is two orders of magnitude smaller than $q$. Dropping $q^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sys} &\approx 2\,(1-\beta)\,q^2 + \beta\,q \\[4pt]
&\approx \beta\,q \quad \text{when } q^2 \ll q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine into the canonical Beta-Factor formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{sys} \approx (2-\beta)\,q\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check (KTU exam favourite):** Let $q = 10^{-3}$ and $\beta = 0.1$. Then $Q_{sys} = (2 - 0.1) \times 10^{-3} = 1.9 \times 10^{-3}$, compared to the textbook $q^2 = 10^{-6}$. The degradation factor is **1900×**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Alpha-Factor Model for an *N* = 2 Pair

**Step 1 — Define observed failure-event counts:**

Let $N_k$ = number of observed events in which exactly $k$ out of $N$ components failed simultaneously (for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$).

For a redundant pair ($N = 2$): $N_1$ = single-component events, $N_2$ = dual-component events.

**Step 2 — Form the alpha factor for each multiplicity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 &= \frac{N_1}{N_1 + N_2} \\[4pt]
\alpha_2 &= \frac{N_2}{N_1 + N_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Enforce closure:**

$$
\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Relate alpha to system failure probability:**

Each $\alpha_k$ contributes a system-failure term scaled by the appropriate combinatorial factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sys} &= \binom{N}{1}\,\alpha_1\,q \;+\; \binom{N}{2}\,\alpha_2\,q^2 \;+\; \cdots \;+\; \binom{N}{N}\,\alpha_N\,q^N \\[4pt]
&= \alpha_1\,q \;+\; \alpha_2\,q^2 \quad \text{for } N=2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Show the equivalence to Beta Factor when $q^2$ is negligible:**

Setting $\alpha_2 = \beta$ and $\alpha_1 = (1-\beta)$:

$$
Q_{sys} = (1-\beta)\,q + \beta\,q^2 \approx \beta\,q
$$

Wait — this recovers only the CCF contribution. The Alpha model is therefore **more general** than Beta because it preserves the single-channel failure term $\alpha_1 q$ in the system probability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Multiple Greek Letter (MGL) Model — Brief Structure

The MGL model introduces **separate multipliers** $\beta_k$ for each multiplicity $k$:

$$
Q_{sys}^{(N)} = \sum_{k=1}^{N}\binom{N}{k}\,\beta_k\,q^k
$$

with the constraint:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{N}\beta_k = 1
$$

where $\beta_1$ handles single-component events, $\beta_2$ handles dual events, etc. MGL is used when field data is rich enough to estimate each $\beta_k$ separately.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection for KTU Problems

| Operating Regime | Recommended Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Limited field data, single redundancy | **Beta Factor (β)** | One parameter, conservative upper bound |
| Moderate field data, pair redundancy | **Alpha Factor (α₁, α₂)** | Captures single vs. dual failure split |
| Rich operational data, 3–4 channels | **Multiple Greek Letter (MGL)** | Per-multiplicity resolution |
| External hazards dominant | **Shock Model** | Time-dependent environmental stresses |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CCF Propagation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Common Root Cause Source] --> B1[Shared Power Supply Loss]
    A1 --> B2[Shared Environmental Stress]
    A1 --> B3[Shared Maintenance Procedure]
    A1 --> B4[Shared Design Defect]
    B1 --> C1[Channel A Failure]
    B1 --> C2[Channel B Failure]
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    C1 --> D1{Parallel Voter 1oo2}
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Safety Function Fails]
    styleA1[ROOT cause source style fill:#FFB6C1 stroke:#B22222 stroke-width:2px]
    styleE1[SYSTEM failure style fill:#FF6347 stroke:#8B0000 stroke-width:3px color:#FFFFFF]
    styleD1[VOTER style fill:#FFD700 stroke:#DAA520 stroke-width:2px]
    classDef rootStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px
    class A1 rootStyle
    class E1 systemFail
    classDef systemFail fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Conceptual Flow — CCF vs. CMF vs. Independent Failure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Component Failures] --> B[Independent Failure]
    A --> C[Common Cause Failure CCF]
    C --> D[Common Mode Failure CMF]
    C --> E[Other CCF Causes]

    B --> G[Affected Component 2 only]

    E --> I[Affected Component 1 and 2 simultaneously different modes]

```

### 4.3 Mitigation Hierarchy — Defence-in-Depth Against CCF/CMF

```mermaid

    A --> C[Layer 2 Design Diversity]

    A --> F[Layer 5 Diagnostic Coverage]

    D --> H
    E --> H

    G --> I
    H --> J[Improved PFD avg]
```
