
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In all three diagrams, the **red-shaded boxes** indicate failure or threat nodes, while **green-shaded boxes** indicate the desired outcome (reduced PFD). This colour convention mirrors IEC 61508 risk-graph visualisations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A.1. Define Common Cause Failure (CCF) and Common Mode Failure (CMF). State any two engineering measures to mitigate them.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Common Cause Failure (CCF):** A CCF is the simultaneous or near-simultaneous failure of two or more components in a redundant system due to a *single shared root cause* such as a common power supply, common environmental stress (heat, flood, vibration), or a common maintenance error.
>
> **Common Mode Failure (CMF):** A CMF is a special case of CCF in which the affected components fail in the *same physical failure mode* because of identical design, identical manufacturer, identical batch, or identical installation environment.

**Engineering measures (any two):**

1. **Physical separation / segregation** — routing redundant cables through different cable trays, mounting sensors on different sides of a vessel.
2. **Design diversity** — using different measurement principles (e.g., pressure transmitter + differential pressure switch for level interlocks).
3. **Different manufacturers / different batches** — sourcing redundant components from independent suppliers.
4. **Independent maintenance crews** — separate schedules and personnel for redundant channels.
5. **Increased diagnostic coverage (DC)** — automated self-tests reduce the undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$.

---

**Q.A.2. What is the Beta Factor Model? Why is it preferred for SIL determination in low-demand systems?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **Beta Factor Model** quantifies the fraction of component failures that are of common-cause origin, denoted by $\beta$, with $0 \le \beta \le 1$. The CCF failure rate is $\lambda_{CCF} = \beta\,\lambda_D$ and the independent failure rate is $\lambda_{indep} = (1-\beta)\,\lambda_D$.
>
> It is preferred for SIL determination in **low-demand systems** because:
> 1. It requires only **one parameter** ($\beta$), which can be estimated from limited operational feedback databases (e.g., OREDA, IEEE 500).
> 2. It directly links to the **PFD formula** $Q_{sys} = (2-\beta)\,q$ used in IEC 61508/61511 SIL calculations.
> 3. It produces a **conservative upper bound** for CCF, satisfying the safety-instrumented principle of *defensive pessimism*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### ➤ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Q.B.A.** *(a)* Discuss in detail the various **categories of common-cause failures** with one engineering example per category. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A 1oo2 voted safety-instrumented function (SIF) consists of two identical pressure transmitters. Each transmitter has a dangerous-undetected failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr. The proof-test interval is $T_i = 8760$ hr (1 year). Given $\beta = 0.08$, calculate: *(7 marks)*

(i) The per-demand failure probability of a single transmitter.
(ii) The PFD of the 1oo2 system using the Beta-Factor Model.
(iii) Comment on the achievable Safety Integrity Level.

---

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Categories of CCF *(7 marks)*

| # | Category | Example | Control |
|---|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Design CCF** | Two pressure transmitters from the same OEM with identical sensing diaphragm and identical firmware | Use different sensing principles (e.g., capacitance + strain-gauge) |
| 2 | **Manufacturing CCF** | Same batch of ICs in two redundant PLCs; identical lot defect | Source from different suppliers, screen-test incoming batches |
| 3 | **Environmental CCF** | Both sensors mounted on the same pipe section exposed to fire/heat | Physically separate mounting, route through separate conduit |
| 4 | **Operational CCF** | Same operator performs calibration incorrectly on both channels | Independent maintenance teams, four-eyes principle |
| 5 | **Software CCF** | Same firmware version in two redundant controllers with same latent bug | Diverse software, formal verification, version segregation |

*(Each row = 1 mark; add a closing 2 marks for the introductory definition and a 2-mark summary table.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- $T_i = 8760$ hr
- $\beta = 0.08$
- Architecture: 1oo2 (parallel / low-demand)

**(i) Per-demand failure probability of a single transmitter (1 mark):**

For low-demand, $q \approx \lambda_D \cdot T_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (8760) \\[4pt]
q &= 4.38 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) PFD of 1oo2 system using Beta-Factor Model (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{1oo2} &= (2 - \beta)\,q \\[4pt]
&= (2 - 0.08) \times 4.38 \times 10^{-2} \\[4pt]
&= 1.92 \times 4.38 \times 10^{-2} \\[4pt]
&= 8.41 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) SIL classification (3 marks):**

Reference bands (IEC 61508 low-demand mode):

- **SIL 1:** $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$
- **SIL 2:** $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$
- **SIL 3:** $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$

Our result $Q_{1oo2} = 8.41 \times 10^{-2}$ falls in the **SIL 1** band. To upgrade to SIL 2 or SIL 3, we must either **reduce the proof-test interval** $T_i$, **increase diagnostic coverage** (lower $\lambda_{DU}$), or **increase architectural diversity** (lower effective $\beta$).

---

### ➤ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Q.B.B.** *(a)* Explain the **Beta-Factor Model, Alpha-Factor Model, and Multiple Greek Letter (MGL) Model** for quantifying common-cause failures. Compare their data requirements and applicability. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A safety-instrumented system uses 2-out-of-2 redundant transmitters. Field data over 10⁵ operating hours shows: $N_1 = 90$ (single-component failure events) and $N_2 = 10$ (dual-component failure events). Each component has a per-demand failure probability $q = 10^{-3}$. Calculate: *(7 marks)*

(i) The Alpha factors $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$.
(ii) The PFD of the system using the Alpha-Factor Model.
(iii) The equivalent Beta Factor for this dataset.

---

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — CCF Models Comparison *(7 marks)*

| Feature | **Beta-Factor** | **Alpha-Factor** | **MGL** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parameters | 1 (β) | $N-1$ ($\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{N-1}$) | $N-1$ ($\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_{N-1}$) |
| Data Requirement | Very low | Moderate | High |
| Resolution | Single multiplicity | Per-multiplicity split | Per-multiplicity split (component-resolved) |
| Assumption | All multi-failures occur in all N components | Differentiates 1, 2, …, N | Differentiates individual components within each event |
| Best For | Sparse data, pair redundancy | Pair / triple redundancy with modest data | 3–4 channel systems with rich operational data |
| Standard Reference | IEC 61508 Annex B | IEC 61508 Annex B | IAEA TECDOC, IEEE 500 |

*(1 mark for definition of each model + 1 mark per comparison row = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — Alpha-Factor Numerical *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $N_1 = 90$, $N_2 = 10$, $q = 10^{-3}$

**(i) Compute $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 &= \frac{N_1}{N_1 + N_2} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 \\[4pt]
\alpha_2 &= \frac{N_2}{N_1 + N_2} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

Closure check: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 0.90 + 0.10 = 1.00$ ✓

**(ii) PFD using Alpha-Factor Model (3 marks):**

For a redundant pair ($N = 2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sys} &= \alpha_1\,q + \alpha_2\,q^2 \\[4pt]
&= (0.90)(10^{-3}) + (0.10)(10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 9.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \\[4pt]
&\approx 9.0 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Equivalent Beta Factor (2 marks):**

Equating the CCF terms: $\beta_{eq} = \alpha_2 = 0.10$.

Verification via Beta formula: $Q_{1oo2} = (2 - 0.10)(10^{-3}) = 1.9 \times 10^{-3}$, which is **2.1× larger** than the Alpha model result $(9.0 \times 10^{-4})$. The discrepancy arises because the Beta model attributes the full single-component failure rate to the system (no $\alpha_1\,q$ subtraction in the Beta pair formula). Hence, **Beta is more conservative**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CCF/CMF Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the closure constraint** $\sum \alpha_k = 1$ — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $q^2$ for the parallel formula instead of $(2-\beta)q$** — losing up to 3 marks. Examiners *expect* the Beta-Factor expression.
> 3. **Confusing $\lambda_D$ with $\lambda_{DU}$** — many students write $\lambda_{DU}$ in the $q \approx \lambda_D T_i$ formula, which is valid *only* if the proof test detects *all* failures. Otherwise use $\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$.
> 4. **Reporting SIL without comparison to the band table** — examiners want an explicit statement like *"This PFD falls within the SIL 2 band ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$)"*, not just a number.
> 5. **Treating CMF and CCF as identical terms** — examiners award at most ½ credit if the distinction is not drawn.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly)**
>
> - [ ] **CCF** = shared root cause, multiple component failures; **CMF** = sub-class, same failure mode.
> - [ ] **Beta Factor β** ∈ [0, 1]; $\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \lambda_D$.
> - [ ] **Canonical Beta 1oo2 formula:** $Q_{sys} = (2 - \beta)\,q$.
> - [ ] **Alpha factors:** $\alpha_k = N_k / \sum_j N_j$; closure $\sum \alpha_k = 1$.
> - [ ] **Alpha pair system:** $Q_{sys} = \alpha_1 q + \alpha_2 q^2$.
> - [ ] **MGL:** generalisation with $\beta_k$ per multiplicity.
> - [ ] **Per-demand probability:** $q \approx \lambda_D \cdot T_i$ (low-demand mode).
> - [ ] **SIL bands:** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$; SIL 4 = $10^{-5}$–$10^{-4}$.
> - [ ] **5 mitigation categories:** Physical separation, design diversity, manufacturer diversity, independent maintenance, diagnostic coverage.
> - [ ] **IEC 61508 / 61511** are the regulatory anchors for CCF analysis.
> - [ ] **Beta model is conservative**; Alpha/MGL models are more realistic when data is available.
> - [ ] **Always state closure verification** in Alpha/MGL calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Functions f(t)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reliability Functions f(t) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In reliability engineering, the **Reliability Function**, typically denoted as **f(t)**, represents the *probability density function (PDF) of failure times* of a component, system, or piece of equipment. It describes how the likelihood of failure is distributed over time for a non-repairable (or repairable, in the broader sense) item, starting from time t = 0. $$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t+\Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$ Where **T** is the random variable representing the *time-to-failure*. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction (KTU 2024 Module 3 focus):** > - **f(t)** = Probability **Density** Function (PDF) of time-to-failure. *Units: failures per unit time (e.g., 1/hr).* > - **R(t)** = Reliability (Survival) Function. *Dimensionless probability.* > - **F(t)** = Unreliability (CDF). *Dimensionless probability.* > - **λ(t)** = Hazard / Failure Rate Function. *Units: failures per unit time.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Light Bulb" Intuition) Imagine you test **1000 identical electric bulbs** by switching them on at time t = 0 and recording when each one burns out. After the test: - Some bulbs fail in the **first hour** (early failures → manufacturing defects). - Some bulbs fail in the **middle period** (random failures). - Many bulbs last **thousands of hours** (wear-out region). If you plot the **number of bulbs failing per hour** against time, the resulting curve (normalized by total bulbs) gives you **f(t)**. The **total area under this curve** equals **1.0** because *every bulb eventually fails*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **f(t) is NOT a probability itself** — it is a probability **density**. To get a probability, you must integrate over a time interval. For example, the probability that a bulb fails between hour 5 and hour 7 is: > $$P(5 \le T < 7) = \int_{5}^{7} f(t)\, dt$$ > This distinction is a **favourite trap** in KTU valuation. ### 1.3 The Reliability Family — A Group of Five Inter-Related Functions Reliability engineering studies time-to-failure using a **family of five mutually convertible functions**. They all describe the *same physical phenomenon* from different mathematical angles: | Symbol | Name | What it tells you | |---|---|---| | **f(t)** | Probability Density Function | Density of failure at instant *t* | | **F(t)** | Cumulative Distribution Function | Cumulative probability of failure by time *t* | | **R(t)** | Reliability (Survival) Function | Probability of surviving up to time *t* | | **λ(t)** | Failure Rate (Hazard) Function | Instantaneous rate of failure, given survival to *t* | | **H(t)** | Cumulative Hazard Function | Accumulated hazard up to time *t* | **Engineering Utility:** f(t) and its derived functions allow safety engineers to compute *spare-parts inventory*, *preventive maintenance schedules*, *warranty costs*, *system availability*, and *Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)* for industrial equipment, aircraft, nuclear reactors, and process plants. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logical Derivation of the Functions (Step-by-Step Reasoning) The reliability function family emerges from a single random variable **T** (time-to-failure). Each function is a different mathematical lens on **T**. **Step 1 — Define the random variable T:** T = time elapsed until the first failure of the component. T ≥ 0. **Step 2 — Cumulative Distribution Function F(t):** F(t) is the probability that failure occurs *on or before* time t. Because probability is bounded in [0, 1]: - F(t) = 0 at t = 0 (no failure before start) - F(t) → 1 as t → ∞ (eventual certainty of failure for non-repairable items) - F(t) is monotonically non-decreasing **Step 3 — Probability Density Function f(t):** f(t) is the *time-derivative* of F(t). It tells you how rapidly failures are accumulating at instant t. **Step 4 — Reliability Function R(t):** R(t) is the *complement* of F(t). It represents the probability of survival beyond time t. **Step 5 — Failure Rate λ(t):** λ(t) asks: *"Given that the component has survived up to time t, what is the instantaneous rate of failure right now?"* This is a *conditional rate*, which is why R(t) appears in its denominator. **Step 6 — Cumulative Hazard H(t):** H(t) is the time-integral of λ(t). It accumulates the total "exposure to risk" up to time t. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these nine formulas. They form the spine of every numerical problem in KTU ESE Module 3.** | # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ | PDF from CDF | failures / unit time | | 2 | $F(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} f(x)\, dx$ | CDF from PDF | dimensionless (0 ≤ F ≤ 1) | | 3 | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | Reliability | dimensionless (0 ≤ R ≤ 1) | | 4 | $R(t) = \displaystyle\int_{t}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx$ | Reliability as tail-area | dimensionless | | 5 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Failure rate / Hazard | failures / unit time | | 6 | $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx$ | Cumulative hazard | dimensionless | | 7 | $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ | Survival from cumulative hazard | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} t\cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | Mean Time To Failure | time (hours, cycles) | | 9 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | For repairable systems | time | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Computing R(t) of a turbine blade to schedule inspection intervals. - **Nuclear Safety:** Estimating λ(t) of safety-critical pumps to satisfy IAEA reliability targets (e.g., λ ≤ 10⁻⁶ /hr). - **Manufacturing:** Designing burn-in tests using early-life λ(t) to weed out infant-mortality failures. - **Automotive Warranty:** Using Weibull-distributed f(t) to price extended warranty contracts. - **Process Industry:** Calculating SIL (Safety Integrity Level) per IEC 61508/61511 using PFD = 1 − R(t). ### 2.4 The Famous "Bathtub Curve" — λ(t) Across Life The failure rate λ(t) typically follows the **bathtub curve**, which is the qualitative story of how f(t) and λ(t) behave over equipment life: - **Infant Mortality Region:** λ(t) decreases — defective units fail early. - **Useful Life Region:** λ(t) is approximately constant — random failures dominate. - **Wear-Out Region:** λ(t) increases — aging components fail more often. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: From f(t) → F(t) → R(t) We begin with the formal definition of the cumulative distribution function: $$F(t) = P(T \le t)$$ By the fundamental theorem of calculus applied to a probability density: $$F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x)\, dx$$ This is the *definite integral* of the PDF from 0 up to t — geometrically, it is the **area under the f(t) curve** between 0 and t. Because the total probability over all possible failure times must equal certainty: $$\int_{0}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$$ The reliability function is the **complementary tail area**: $$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx$$ **Sanity check:** At t = 0, R(0) = 1 (item starts alive). At t → ∞, R(∞) = 0 (item eventually fails). ✓ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: From R(t) to f(t) (Inverse Relationship) Differentiate R(t) = 1 − F(t) with respect to t: $$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -f(t)$$ Therefore: $$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ This negative derivative **always holds** because R(t) is a *decreasing* function of time (survival probability goes down as t increases). ### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Failure Rate (Hazard) Function λ(t) We want the *conditional probability density of failure at instant t*, given survival up to t: $$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t+\Delta t \,\vert\, T > t)}{\Delta t}$$ Apply the conditional probability identity P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B): $$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t+\Delta t)}{P(T > t)\, \Delta t}$$ The numerator's limit is f(t), and P(T > t) = R(t): $$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$ Substituting f(t) = −dR(t)/dt: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{-1}{R(t)}\cdot\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[\ln R(t)\bigr]$$ ### 3.4 Derivation 4: R(t) = exp(−H(t)) — The Exponential Form Integrate both sides of λ(t) = −d[ln R(t)]/dt from 0 to t: $$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[\ln R(x)\bigr]\, dx$$ The left side is, by definition, the **cumulative hazard function H(t)**: $$H(t) = -\bigl[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\bigr]$$ Since R(0) = 1, ln R(0) = 0: $$H(t) = -\ln R(t)$$ Exponentiating both sides: $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-H(t)}}$$ This is the **single most important formula** in reliability engineering. It links survival probability directly to cumulative hazard. ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) from f(t) The mean of a non-negative random variable T is given by the expectation: $$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\cdot f(t)\, dt$$ Apply integration by parts with u = t and dv = f(t)dt (so du = dt, v = ∫f dt = F(t)): $$\text{MTTF} = \bigl[t\cdot F(t)\bigr]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} F(t)\, dt$$ Since F(∞) = 1 and F(0) = 0, the boundary term becomes ∞·1 − 0·0, but the original integral converges for physical f(t). Using F(t) = 1 − R(t): $$\text{MTTF} = \lim_{t\to\infty} t\cdot\bigl[1-R(t)\bigr] - \int_{0}^{\infty}\bigl[1-R(t)\bigr]\, dt$$ For all *physical* reliability distributions, lim_{t→∞} t·R(t) = 0 (faster than 1/t decay), so: $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$ **This elegant result** says: *the average lifetime equals the area under the reliability curve.* ### 3.6 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style) **Problem:** The PDF of failure for an industrial pump is f(t) = 0.005·e^(−0.005t) for t ≥ 0 (Exponential distribution). Find F(t), R(t), λ(t), and MTTF. **Solution:** F(t): $$F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} 0.005\,e^{-0.005x}\, dx = \left[-e^{-0.005x}\right]_{0}^{t} = 1 - e^{-0.005t}$$ R(t): $$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-0.005t}$$ λ(t): $$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{0.005\,e^{-0.005t}}{e^{-0.005t}} = 0.005\ \text{failures/hour} = \text{constant}$$ MTTF: $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.005t}\, dt = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200\ \text{hours}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** For the exponential distribution, λ(t) is **constant**. This is why it is the *only* distribution with the **memoryless property** — P(T > s+t | T > s) = P(T > t). KTU frequently tests this property. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture: The Reliability Function Family ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Random Variable T
Time To Failure"] startB["Boundary Conditions
F 0 equals 0
F infinity equals 1
R 0 equals 1
R infinity equals 0"] stepA["f of t
Probability Density Function"] stepB["F of t
Cumulative Distribution Function"] stepC["R of t
Reliability Function"] stepD["lambda of t
Failure Rate Function"] stepE["H of t
Cumulative Hazard"] stepF["MTTF and MTBF
Mean Life Metrics"] startA --> stepA startA --> stepB startA --> stepC startA --> stepD startA --> stepE stepA -- integrate 0 to t --> stepB stepB -- complement R equals 1 minus F --> stepC stepA -- divide by R of t --> stepD stepD -- integrate 0 to t --> stepE stepE -- exponentiate R equals exp minus H --> stepC stepA -- compute expectation --> stepF stepC -- compute expectation --> stepF startB -.constrains.-> stepA startB -.constrains.-> stepB startB -.constrains.-> stepC ``` ### 4.2 Schematic: The Bathtub Curve (λ(t) vs t) ```mermaid flowchart LR phase1["Infant Mortality
lambda decreasing
Defects screened out"] phase2["Useful Life
lambda constant
Random failures"] phase3["Wear Out
lambda increasing
Aging dominated"] axisT["Time t increasing left to right"] axisL["lambda of t increasing bottom to top"] phase1 --> phase2 phase2 --> phase3 axisT -.axis.-> phase1 axisL -.axis.-> phase1 ``` ### 4.3 Relationship Map: From f(t) to System Safety Decisions ```mermaid flowchart TD inputA["Equipment Test Data"] inputB["Field Failure Records"] nodeA["Fit f of t Model
Exponential Weibull Normal Lognormal"] nodeB["Compute R of t and lambda of t"] nodeC["Safety Decisions"] out1["Preventive Maintenance Interval"] out2["Spare Parts Inventory"] out3["SIL and PFDavg for IEC 61508"] out4["Warranty Cost Estimation"] inputA --> nodeA inputB --> nodeA nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> out1 nodeC --> out2 nodeC --> out3 nodeC --> out4 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define the **probability density function f(t)** in the context of reliability engineering. State the two essential mathematical properties that f(t) must satisfy. **Model Answer:** The probability density function f(t) describes the *density* of failure occurrences at time t for a population of identical components. Mathematically: $$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{P(t \le T < t+\Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$ The two essential properties are: 1. **Non-negativity:** f(t) ≥ 0 for all t ≥ 0. 2. **Unity of total area:** $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, dt = 1$ (every component fails eventually). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** Distinguish between **R(t)** and **λ(t)** with their mathematical definitions. Why is λ(t) called a *conditional* failure rate? **Model Answer:** - **R(t)** is the *Reliability Function* — the probability that a component survives beyond time t: $R(t) = P(T > t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx$. - **λ(t)** is the *Failure Rate Function* — the instantaneous rate of failure at time t, conditional on survival up to t: $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$. λ(t) is called a **conditional rate** because its value at time t is computed under the *condition* that the component has *already* survived up to that instant. It is not an unconditional probability density; rather, it is the *risk* of failing *right now*, given that failure has not yet occurred. --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between the reliability function R(t) and the cumulative hazard function H(t). Show that for the exponential distribution f(t) = λe^(−λt), the failure rate λ(t) is constant. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The time-to-failure of an electronic control unit follows the distribution f(t) = 0.02·e^(−0.02t) for t ≥ 0 (in years). Calculate: (i) F(10), (ii) R(50), (iii) the probability that it survives beyond 30 years given it has already survived 20 years, and (iv) MTTF. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Derivation: Starting from the failure rate definition: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[\ln R(t)\bigr]$$ Integrating from 0 to t: $$\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(x)\,dx = -\bigl[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\bigr] = -\ln R(t)$$ Since R(0) = 1, the result is: $$H(t) = -\ln R(t) \quad\Rightarrow\quad R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$$ For the exponential distribution f(t) = λe^(−λt), we compute: $$R(t) = 1 - \int_{0}^{t}\lambda e^{-\lambda x}dx = e^{-\lambda t}$$ Therefore: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = \lambda = \text{constant}$$ This constancy is the *defining feature* of the exponential distribution and is known as the **memoryless property**. **(b)** Given λ = 0.02 per year: **(i)** F(10) = 1 − e^(−0.02 × 10) = 1 − e^(−0.2) = 1 − 0.8187 = **0.1813** **(ii)** R(50) = e^(−0.02 × 50) = e^(−1) = **0.3679** **(iii)** By the memoryless property: $$P(T > 30 \mid T > 20) = P(T > 10) = R(10) = e^{-0.2} = \mathbf{0.8187}$$ **(iv)** MTTF = 1/λ = 1/0.02 = **50 years** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]** **(a)** Define MTTF and MTBF. For a repairable system with MTTF = 800 hours and MTTR = 2 hours, compute its steady-state **Availability**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Weibull-distributed component has f(t) = (β/η)(t/η)^(β−1)·e^(−(t/η)^β) with β = 2 and η = 1000 hours. Determine: (i) the reliability at 500 hours, (ii) the failure rate at 500 hours, and (iii) comment on whether the component is in infant-mortality, useful-life, or wear-out region at t = 500. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Definitions: - **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** Average operating time before the *first* failure of a non-repairable (or before next failure of a repairable) component. - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average time interval between *consecutive* failures of a repairable system, including repair time. Availability calculation: $$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 800 + 2 = 802\ \text{hr}$$ $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{800}{802} = \mathbf{0.9975\ or\ 99.75\%}$$ (Equivalently, A = MTTF/MTBF = 800/802.) **(b)** Weibull with β = 2, η = 1000: **(i)** Reliability: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ $$R(500) = e^{-(500/1000)^{2}} = e^{-0.25} = \mathbf{0.7788}$$ **(ii)** Failure rate: $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ $$\lambda(500) = \frac{2}{1000}\left(\frac{500}{1000}\right)^{2-1} = 0.002 \times 0.5 = \mathbf{0.001\ /\text{hr}}$$ **(iii)** Since β = 2 > 1, the failure rate is **increasing** with time. At t = 500 hours the component is in the **Wear-Out Region** of the bathtub curve. The reliability is degrading faster than exponentially, indicating aging-driven failure mechanisms (e.g., fatigue, corrosion, insulation breakdown). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing f(t) with F(t):** f(t) is a *density*, NOT a probability. Never write "f(t) = probability of failure at time t". Integrate first. > 2. **Forgetting the MTTR term:** When asked for "availability" of a *repairable* system, students often write only MTTF/MTBF or only Uptime/(Uptime+Downtime). Use the standard formula A = MTTF/(MTTF+MTTR). > 3. **Sign error in H(t):** H(t) = −ln R(t), NOT +ln R(t). Reliability *decreases* with time, so the cumulative hazard *increases*. > 4. **Units mismatch:** MTTF must be in the *same time units* as the failure rate denominator. If λ is per year, MTTF is in years. > 5. **Memoryless property only for exponential:** Do NOT apply P(T>s+t | T>s) = R(t) to Weibull or Normal distributions — KTU deducts full marks for this. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **f(t)** = PDF of failure; integrate to get probabilities; total area = 1. - ✅ **F(t)** = CDF = cumulative failure probability; F(t) + R(t) = 1. - ✅ **R(t)** = reliability / survival probability; R(0) = 1, R(∞) = 0. - ✅ **λ(t) = f(t)/R(t)** = instantaneous conditional failure rate. - ✅ **H(t) = ∫₀ᵗ λ(x)dx** = cumulative hazard = **−ln R(t)**. - ✅ **Master equation:** R(t) = e^(−H(t)) — memorize verbatim. - ✅ **MTTF** = ∫₀^∞ R(t) dt = ∫₀^∞ t·f(t) dt. - ✅ **MTBF** = MTTF + MTTR (only for repairable systems). - ✅ **Availability** A = MTTF / MTBF = Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime). - ✅ **Exponential distribution** = constant λ(t) = memoryless property. - ✅ **Weibull with β < 1** → infant mortality; **β = 1** → useful life; **β > 1** → wear-out. - ✅ **Bathtub curve** has three regions: infant mortality (decreasing λ), useful life (constant λ), wear-out (increasing λ). - ✅ **f(t) ≠ probability** — f(t)·dt is a probability; f(t) alone is a density (units: 1/time). - ✅ For KTU numericals, **always state units** in the final answer (e.g., 200 hours, 0.005/hr). - ✅ Boundary conditions: F(0)=0, F(∞)=1, R(0)=1, R(∞)=0 — these let you solve for unknown constants.

topics.title: h(t) - Relationship Between These Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** h(t) - Relationship Between These Functions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition In reliability engineering and quantitative safety analysis, the **Hazard Function** $h(t)$, also called the **Failure Rate Function** or **Instantaneous Failure Rate**, is defined as the conditional probability density that a component, system, or item fails at time $t$, given that it has survived up to that instant. Mathematically, $h(t)$ is expressed as the ratio of the failure probability density to the reliability (survival probability): $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$ where: - $f(t)$ = Probability Density Function (PDF) of time-to-failure - $F(t)$ = Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of time-to-failure - $R(t)$ = Reliability Function (Survival Function), where $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The four functions $f(t)$, $F(t)$, $R(t)$, and $h(t)$ are **mutually dependent**. A complete understanding of safety and reliability requires deriving any one function from the others using their inter-relationships. This is a **frequently asked 7 or 14-mark question** in KTU ESE examinations. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The "Lifetime of a Light Bulb" Analogy** > > Imagine you purchase **1,000 identical electric bulbs** for a factory and switch them all on simultaneously at $t = 0$. Let an observer record when each bulb burns out. > > - $R(t)$ answers: *"Out of 1,000 bulbs, how many are STILL glowing at time $t$?"* — this is the **survival fraction**. > - $f(t)$ answers: *"At exactly time $t$, how MANY bulbs (per unit time) are failing?"* — this is the **failure density**. > - $F(t)$ answers: *"By time $t$, what fraction of bulbs have ALREADY failed?"* — this is the **cumulative failure fraction**. > - $h(t)$ answers: *"Among the bulbs that are STILL glowing at time $t$, how fast are they failing RIGHT NOW?"* — this is the **conditional rate of failure**. > > So $h(t)$ is essentially a "**risk per unit time, conditioned on survival**" — much like a doctor asking a patient, *"Given that you are alive today, what is the probability you will die tomorrow?"* ## 1.3 The Five Fundamental Reliability Functions | Symbol | Function Name | Physical Meaning | |:------:|:--------------|:-----------------| | $f(t)$ | Probability Density Function (PDF) | Instantaneous probability of failure at time $t$ | | $F(t)$ | Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) | Total probability of failure up to time $t$ | | $R(t)$ | Reliability (Survival) Function | Probability of survival up to time $t$ | | $h(t)$ | Hazard (Failure Rate) Function | Conditional failure rate at time $t$, given survival | | $H(t)$ | Cumulative Hazard Function | Total accumulated hazard up to time $t$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > All these functions are defined for $t \geq 0$. The boundary conditions are $F(0) = 0$, $R(0) = 1$, $H(0) = 0$, and $f(0) \geq 0$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the Relationships ### Step 1 — Establish the Probability Basis Let $T$ be a **continuous random variable** representing the time-to-failure of a component. The Cumulative Distribution Function is defined as: $$F(t) = P(T \leq t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx$$ This means $F(t)$ is the **area under the PDF curve** from $0$ to $t$. ### Step 2 — Define the Reliability Function Since $F(t)$ gives the probability of failure before $t$, the probability of **survival** beyond $t$ is its complement: $$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$ This is the **fundamental complementarity identity**. Both functions must always sum to unity: $$F(t) + R(t) = 1$$ ### Step 3 — Define the PDF via Differentiation Differentiating $F(t)$ with respect to $t$ recovers the original density: $$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ The negative sign appears because $R(t)$ is a **monotonically decreasing** function (components fail over time, never resurrect). ### Step 4 — Define the Hazard Rate Function The hazard function is the **conditional probability density of failure in the next infinitesimal interval** $[t, t+dt]$, given survival up to $t$: $$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$ Using the definition of conditional probability, this simplifies to: $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$ This is the **central relationship** linking the density, the CDF, and the hazard. ### Step 5 — Define the Cumulative Hazard Function Integrating the hazard rate from $0$ to $t$ yields the cumulative hazard: $$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is $H(t)$ useful?** It transforms any reliability problem into a simple *time-domain integral*, which simplifies parameter estimation in Weibull, exponential, and log-normal distributions. ### Step 6 — The Exponential Bridge Between $R(t)$ and $H(t)$ Differentiating the identity $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ gives $f(t) = -R'(t)$. Substituting into $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$: $$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ Rearranging and integrating: $$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\ln[R(t)] + \ln[R(0)]$$ Since $R(0) = 1$ and $\ln(1) = 0$, we obtain the **master exponential identity**: $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx}}$$ This is the **most important formula** in reliability engineering — it directly links reliability to the hazard function. ### Step 7 — Expressing $f(t)$ in Terms of $h(t)$ Since $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$ and $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$: $$\boxed{f(t) = h(t) \cdot e^{-H(t)} = h(t) \cdot e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx}}$$ This is the **Probability Density in Hazard Form** — required when fitting lifetime data to non-standard distributions. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Master Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU ESE questions on this topic. Memorize all identities and the direction of each derivation arrow. | # | Identity | Mathematical Expression | Domain | Engineering Use | |:-:|:---------|:------------------------|:-------|:----------------| | 1 | Complementarity | $F(t) + R(t) = 1$ | $t \geq 0$ | Convert failure prob. to survival prob. | | 2 | Density from CDF | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ | $t \geq 0$ | Recover density from empirical CDF | | 3 | Density from Reliability | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | $t \geq 0$ | Differentiate empirical survival curve | | 4 | Hazard Definition | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | $t \geq 0$ | Compute instantaneous failure rate | | 5 | Reliability from Hazard | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\,dx\right)$ | $t \geq 0$ | Master exponential identity | | 6 | Density from Hazard | $f(t) = h(t) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\,dx\right)$ | $t \geq 0$ | Density in hazard form | | 7 | Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\,dx$ | $t \geq 0$ | Total accumulated risk | | 8 | Reliability from CDF | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | $t \geq 0$ | Survival probability | | 9 | Hazard from CDF | $h(t) = \dfrac{F'(t)}{1 - F(t)}$ | $t \geq 0$ | Direct differentiation form | | 10 | Hazard from Reliability | $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\big[\ln R(t)\big]$ | $t \geq 0$ | Logarithmic derivative form | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Aerospace Industry:** The hazard function determines the inspection interval for aircraft engines. A rising $h(t)$ triggers scheduled overhaul. > - **Nuclear Plant Safety:** The cumulative hazard $H(t)$ is integrated into **Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA)** to compute core damage frequency. > - **Electronics Manufacturing:** The "bathtub curve" of $h(t)$ distinguishes infant mortality, useful life, and wear-out phases — guiding burn-in testing protocols. > - **Pharmaceutical Reliability:** $h(t)$ is used in clinical survival analysis to compare drug efficacy (Kaplan-Meier hazard estimation). > - **KTU Industry Connect:** Several Kerala-based electronics and process industries (HLL, FACT, Kochi Refineries) use Weibull hazard analysis for plant equipment maintenance scheduling. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Master Identity $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ We begin with the **operational definition** of the hazard function as a conditional probability: $$h(t) \, dt = P(t < T \leq t + dt \mid T > t)$$ **Step 1 — Apply the conditional probability rule.** By the axiom of conditional probability: $$h(t) \, dt = \frac{P(t < T \leq t + dt)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{f(t) \, dt}{R(t)}$$ **Step 2 — Cancel $dt$ from both sides** to obtain the ratio definition: $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$** (from the fundamental identity that density equals the negative derivative of survival): $$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $dt$** and rearrange: $$h(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$ **Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:** $$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dR(x)}{R(x)}$$ **Step 6 — The right-hand integral** is a standard logarithmic integral. By definition $\int \dfrac{du}{u} = \ln u + C$: $$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\Big[\ln R(x)\Big]_{0}^{t} = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$ **Step 7 — Apply boundary condition $R(0) = 1$**, hence $\ln R(0) = 0$: $$H(t) = -\ln R(t)$$ **Step 8 — Exponentiate both sides** to isolate $R(t)$: $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE Module 3. You must show all 8 steps explicitly; skipping any step costs marks. ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Exponential Distribution Let the hazard rate be **constant**: $h(t) = \lambda$ where $\lambda > 0$ (a common model for electronic components during their useful life). **Step 1 — Compute the cumulative hazard:** $$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, dx = \lambda t$$ **Step 2 — Apply the master identity to get reliability:** $$R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the CDF using complementarity:** $$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the PDF by differentiation:** $$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 5 — Verify the hazard relationship:** $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = \lambda \quad \checkmark$$ This confirms that for an **exponential distribution**, the hazard rate is **identically constant**, which is its defining feature. ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Weibull Distribution Let $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$ where $\beta$ is the shape parameter and $\eta$ is the scale parameter. **Step 1 — Compute cumulative hazard:** $$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{x}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} dx$$ Let $u = \dfrac{x}{\eta}$, so $du = \dfrac{dx}{\eta}$, hence $dx = \eta \, du$. The integration limits change from $x = 0 \Rightarrow u = 0$ and $x = t \Rightarrow u = t/\eta$: $$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t/\eta} \frac{\beta}{\eta} u^{\beta-1} \cdot \eta \, du = \int_{0}^{t/\eta} \beta \, u^{\beta-1} \, du$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the integral** using $\int u^{\beta-1} du = \dfrac{u^{\beta}}{\beta}$: $$H(t) = \beta \left[\frac{u^{\beta}}{\beta}\right]_{0}^{t/\eta} = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$ **Step 3 — Reliability:** $$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$ **Step 4 — CDF:** $$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$ **Step 5 — PDF (by differentiation using the chain rule):** $$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$ **Step 6 — Verification:** $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} \quad \checkmark$$ ## 3.4 Conversion Matrix — Pick Any Two Functions, Compute the Rest Given any one function, the entire reliability framework is recoverable. The KTU examiner often asks to "**derive $R(t)$ given $f(t)$**" or vice versa. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Pathways (Directed Graph):** > > 1. $f(t) \xrightarrow{\int_0^t} F(t) \xrightarrow{1 - \,\cdot\,} R(t) \xrightarrow{\int_0^t h(x)\,dx\,=\,-\ln(\cdot)} H(t)$ > > 2. $h(t) \xrightarrow{\int_0^t} H(t) \xrightarrow{e^{-H(t)}} R(t) \xrightarrow{1 - \,\cdot\,} F(t) \xrightarrow{d/dt} f(t)$ > > 3. $R(t) \xrightarrow{-d/dt \ln(\cdot)} h(t) \xrightarrow{\int_0^t} H(t)$ > > 4. $F(t) \xrightarrow{F'(t)/[1-F(t)]} h(t) \xrightarrow{\int_0^t} H(t)$ ## 3.5 Worked Example — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) The **MTTF** is the expected lifetime, given by: $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$ **For an Exponential Distribution** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$: $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ This is the famous **"reciprocity rule"**: for exponential lifetime, $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{h}$ since $h(t) = \lambda$ is constant. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Relationship Network — Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete interplay** between all five reliability functions. Each block represents a function, and the arrows represent **mathematical transformations** (integrate, differentiate, exponentiate, complement). ```mermaid flowchart LR A["f of t PDF Density"] -->|Integrate from 0 to t| B["F of t CDF Cumulative Failure"] B -->|Subtract from 1| C["R of t Reliability Survival"] C -->|Multiply with f of t ratio| D["h of t Hazard Rate"] B -->|F prime over 1 minus F| D D -->|Integrate from 0 to t| E["H of t Cumulative Hazard"] E -->|Exponentiate negative| C C -->|Negative log derivative| D A -->|f over R ratio| D C -->|Differentiate negative| A B -->|Differentiate| A ``` ## 4.2 Sequential Derivation Pipeline — Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Start: Given one function"] --> Q1{"Which function is given?"} Q1 -->|Given f of t| P1["Step 1: Integrate f to get F"] P1 --> P2["Step 2: R equals 1 minus F"] P2 --> P3["Step 3: h equals f divided by R"] P3 --> P4["Step 4: Integrate h to get H"] P4 --> End["All five functions derived"] Q1 -->|Given h of t| Q2["Step 1: Integrate h to get H"] Q2 --> Q3["Step 2: R equals exponential negative H"] Q3 --> Q4["Step 3: F equals 1 minus R"] Q4 --> Q5["Step 4: Differentiate F to get f"] Q5 --> End Q1 -->|Given R of t| R1["Step 1: F equals 1 minus R"] R1 --> R2["Step 2: f equals negative dR by dt"] R2 --> R3["Step 3: h equals negative d of ln R by dt"] R3 --> R4["Step 4: Integrate h to get H"] R4 --> End style Start fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000 style End fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#27AE60,color:#000 style Q1 fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F39C12,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 The "Bathtub Curve" — Hazard Behavior Over Equipment Life ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Phase1["Phase I: Infant Mortality"] D1["Decreasing h of t"] end subgraph Phase2["Phase II: Useful Life"] C1["Constant h of t equals lambda"] end subgraph Phase3["Phase III: Wear Out"] I1["Increasing h of t"] end Phase1 --> Phase2 Phase2 --> Phase3 Note1["Burn-in testing
removes early failures"] -.-> Phase1 Note2["Random failures
exponential model"] -.-> Phase2 Note3["Scheduled replacement
Weibull with beta greater than 1"] -.-> Phase3 style Phase1 fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B,color:#000 style Phase2 fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2874A6,color:#000 style Phase3 fill:#F5B7B1,stroke:#922B21,color:#000 style D1 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C1 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,color:#000 style I1 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Conversion Operations Legend > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** > > - A solid arrow `→` means "**apply this operation**" (e.g., integrate, differentiate, exponentiate). > - The **yellow decision diamond** indicates the entry point based on which function is initially specified in a problem. > - The **green end block** confirms that **all four other functions** can be derived by following the arrows in sequence. > - This diagram is the **mnemonic backbone** for solving KTU 14-mark derivation questions. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the hazard function $h(t)$ and state its relationship with the reliability function $R(t)$ and probability density function $f(t)$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The hazard function $h(t)$ is defined as the **instantaneous conditional probability density of failure** at time $t$, given that the component has survived up to time $t$. It is also called the **failure rate function**. Mathematically, the hazard rate is the ratio of the probability density function to the reliability function: $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$ Here, $f(t)$ is the probability density of failure at time $t$, and $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ is the reliability (survival probability) up to time $t$. The function $h(t)$ provides the **rate of failure per unit time** among the components that are still operational at time $t$. --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State and prove the relationship between the reliability function $R(t)$ and the cumulative hazard function $H(t)$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The relationship between $R(t)$ and $H(t)$ is given by the **master exponential identity**: $$R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$$ **Proof:** Starting from $h(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$, we have: $$h(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$ Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$ and using $R(0) = 1$: $$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\ln R(t)$$ Exponentiating yields: $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Define the following reliability functions: probability density function $f(t)$, cumulative distribution function $F(t)$, reliability function $R(t)$, hazard function $h(t)$, and cumulative hazard function $H(t)$. State **any four** mathematical relationships among them. **(b)** [7 Marks] For a system with constant hazard rate $\lambda = 0.002$ per hour, derive expressions for $F(t)$, $R(t)$, $f(t)$, and $H(t)$. Compute the reliability and probability of failure at $t = 500$ hours. **Model Solution:** **(a) Definitions and Four Relationships [7 Marks]:** - **$f(t)$ — Probability Density Function:** Gives the instantaneous probability of failure at time $t$. It satisfies $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) \, dt = 1$. - **$F(t)$ — Cumulative Distribution Function:** Total probability of failure up to time $t$. $F(t) = P(T \leq t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx$. - **$R(t)$ — Reliability Function:** Probability of survival up to time $t$. $R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$, with boundary $R(0) = 1$. - **$h(t)$ — Hazard Function:** Conditional failure rate at time $t$, given survival up to $t$. - **$H(t)$ — Cumulative Hazard:** Total accumulated hazard up to time $t$. **Four Key Relationships:** $$(i) \quad F(t) + R(t) = 1$$ $$(ii) \quad f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ $$(iii) \quad h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$ $$(iv) \quad R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)$$ **(b) Derivation and Calculation for $\lambda = 0.002$ per hour [7 Marks]:** Given $h(t) = \lambda = 0.002$ hr$^{-1}$ (constant). **Step 1 — Cumulative Hazard:** $$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} 0.002 \, dx = 0.002t$$ **Step 2 — Reliability Function (using the master identity):** $$R(t) = e^{-0.002t}$$ **Step 3 — CDF:** $$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-0.002t}$$ **Step 4 — PDF (by differentiating $F(t)$):** $$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = 0.002 \cdot e^{-0.002t}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation at $t = 500$ hours:** $$R(500) = e^{-0.002 \times 500} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$ $$F(500) = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$$ **Conclusion:** At $t = 500$ hours, the system has a **36.79% probability of survival** and a **63.21% probability of failure**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the relationship $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)$ starting from the definition of hazard rate. **(b)** [7 Marks] The hazard rate of a mechanical component follows $h(t) = \dfrac{1}{100} + \dfrac{t}{10000}$ per hour. Determine $R(t)$, $f(t)$, and the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF). **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:** By definition, $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$. Since $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$, we substitute: $$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ Multiplying both sides by $dt$: $$h(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$ Integrating from $0$ to $t$: $$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\big[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\big]$$ Since $R(0) = 1$ implies $\ln R(0) = 0$: $$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\ln R(t)$$ Exponentiating both sides: $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx}}$$ **(b) Linear Hazard Component [7 Marks]:** Given $h(t) = \dfrac{1}{100} + \dfrac{t}{10000}$ hr$^{-1}$. **Step 1 — Cumulative Hazard:** $$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \left(\frac{1}{100} + \frac{x}{10000}\right) dx = \frac{t}{100} + \frac{t^{2}}{20000}$$ **Step 2 — Reliability:** $$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{100} - \frac{t^{2}}{20000}\right)$$ **Step 3 — PDF:** $$f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t) = \left(\frac{1}{100} + \frac{t}{10000}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{100} - \frac{t^{2}}{20000}\right)$$ **Step 4 — MTTF:** $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{100} - \frac{t^{2}}{20000}\right) dt$$ This integral has no closed-form solution; it must be evaluated numerically using Gaussian quadrature or Simpson's rule. Using numerical integration: $$\text{MTTF} \approx 79.3 \text{ hours}$$ (Students may show the numerical setup and table for full marks.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Errors:** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** when writing $f(t) = -dR/dt$. Marks deducted if positive sign is used. > 2. **Omitting boundary condition** $R(0) = 1$ in the derivation. This causes $\ln R(0)$ term to be dropped incorrectly. > 3. **Confusing $h(t)$ with $f(t)$** — they are dimensionally different! $h(t)$ has units of [time]$^{-1}$, while $f(t)$ has units of [time]$^{-1}$ but represents probability density, not rate. Both have same units but different interpretations. > 4. **Wrong exponent sign** — students often write $R(t) = e^{+H(t)}$ instead of $e^{-H(t)}$. This is a critical error. > 5. **Not stating the units** in numerical answers. Always write "hr$^{-1}$" or "per hour" for hazard rates. > 6. **Skipping intermediate steps** in 7-mark derivations. KTU examiners require **every algebraic step** explicitly written. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: h(t) and Its Relationships** - **Definition:** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — hazard is the **conditional** failure rate, not the absolute one. - **Five Functions:** $f(t)$, $F(t)$, $R(t)$, $h(t)$, $H(t)$ — all interlinked, only one is independent. - **Master Identity:** $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ — the most important formula of this module; memorize and be able to derive it from scratch in **under 3 minutes**. - **Complementarity:** $F(t) + R(t) = 1$ — always true for any lifetime distribution. - **Differentiation Rules:** - $f(t) = +dF/dt = -dR/dt$ - $h(t) = -d[\ln R(t)]/dt$ - **Integration Rules:** - $F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx$ - $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx$ - $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ - **Boundary Conditions:** $F(0) = 0$, $R(0) = 1$, $H(0) = 0$, $f(0) \geq 0$. - **Exponential Special Case:** If $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant), then $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$. - **Weibull Special Case:** If $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$, then $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ — covers infant mortality ($\beta < 1$), random failures ($\beta = 1$), and wear-out ($\beta > 1$). - **Common Mistake:** Writing $R(t) = e^{H(t)}$ (positive exponent). The correct form is $e^{-H(t)}$. - **Conversion Direction:** Given any ONE function, the other four can be derived systematically via integrate, differentiate, exponentiate, or complement. - **Engineering Relevance:** Used in PSA (Probabilistic Safety Assessment), FMEA, Weibull analysis, bathtub curve modeling, and preventive maintenance scheduling. - **KTU Exam Weightage:** This topic regularly appears in **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition and in **Part B (7–14 marks)** as a derivation or numerical problem. Mastery of the 8-step derivation of $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ is **non-negotiable** for scoring above 80%.

topics.title: Bathtub Curve - Exponential Failure Density and Distribution Functions - Expected Value and Standard Deviation of Exponential Distribution - Measures of Reliability - MTTF
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Reliability Mathematics — The Bathtub Curve & Exponential Failure Model

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bathtub Curve - Exponential Failure Density and Distribution Functions - Expected Value and Standard Deviation of Exponential Distribution - Measures of Reliability - MTTF

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Bathtub Curve — Formal Definition

The **Bathtub Curve** is the canonical reliability engineering model that describes the *hazard (failure) rate* $\lambda(t)$ of a population of components, machines, or systems as a function of operating time. As the name suggests, the shape of the curve visually resembles the cross-section of a bathtub: a steeply descending left wall, a long flat bottom, and a rising right wall.

It is composed of three distinct phases, each governed by different physical failure mechanisms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Anchor Concept**
> The bathtub curve is the foundational qualitative model that connects *lifecycle phase* to *quantitative failure rate*. In the **Useful Life (Phase II)** region, the failure rate is **constant**, which mathematically forces the time-to-failure $T$ to follow an **Exponential Distribution**. This single observation links qualitative intuition to the entire module's quantitative machinery.

| Phase | Name | Failure Rate Trend | Dominant Failure Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase I** | Infant Mortality (Burn-in) | Decreasing ($\lambda$ falls) | Manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, latent flaws |
| **Phase II** | Useful Life (Random Failures) | **Constant** ($\lambda$ = constant) | Random overstress events, unpredictable shocks |
| **Phase III** | Wear-out (Aging) | Increasing ($\lambda$ rises) | Fatigue, corrosion, wear, material degradation |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Human Life, A Light Bulb, A Car

**Real-world analogy — Three regimes of human health:**

- **Infancy (Phase I):** A newborn baby has a high probability of dying from congenital defects, infections, or birth complications. As the child grows and the weakest infants are eliminated, the death rate falls sharply.
- **Adulthood (Phase II):** A healthy adult aged 20–40 years has an approximately *constant* low annual probability of dying — most deaths are due to unpredictable accidents (random events), not "scheduled" aging.
- **Old age (Phase III):** Beyond 60–70 years, organ systems degrade, joints wear out, and the annual death rate climbs steeply.

The exact same logic applies to a machine: defective parts fail early, the remaining population then fails at a steady random rate, and finally the components physically wear out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A manufacturer can "burn in" equipment before shipping to eliminate Phase I failures. Preventive maintenance / replacement is scheduled *before* the system enters Phase III. The middle flat region is where we *operate* the asset and where the **exponential distribution** is the correct mathematical model.

### 1.3 The Exponential Distribution — Formal Definition

The **Exponential Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution that models the time elapsed between successive *Poisson-process* events. In reliability engineering, it is the distribution of the time-to-first-failure $T$ of a non-repairable component operating in its useful-life phase, where the failure rate is **constant**.

**Probability Density Function (PDF):**

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0,\ \lambda > 0$$

**Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):**

$$F(t) = P(T \le t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0$$

**Reliability (Survival) Function:**

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Here $\lambda$ is the **constant failure rate** with units of *failures per unit time* (often written as $\text{fit} = \text{failures}/10^9 \text{ hours}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memoryless Property** (the defining trait of the exponential distribution):
> $$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$
> A component that has already survived $s$ hours has *no memory* of that survival — its future failure probability is the same as that of a brand-new component. This is exactly the behaviour we observe in the flat Phase II region of the bathtub curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Bathtub Curve — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define the hazard function.**
The instantaneous failure rate is mathematically defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the lifecycle.**
For a heterogeneous population, the total observed failure rate is the *superposition* of three sub-populations:

$$\lambda_{\text{total}}(t) = \lambda_{\text{infant}}(t) + \lambda_{\text{random}} + \lambda_{\text{wear}}(t)$$

- $\lambda_{\text{infant}}(t) = A \cdot e^{-Bt}$ (Weibull-like, decreasing)
- $\lambda_{\text{random}} = \lambda_0$ (constant → exponential regime)
- $\lambda_{\text{wear}}(t) = C \cdot t^{D}$ (increasing, Weibull/gamma-type)

**Step 3 — Identify the operational region.**
During Phase II, $\lambda_{\text{infant}} \to 0$ and $\lambda_{\text{wear}} \approx 0$, leaving $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0$. The PDF that produces a *constant* hazard is uniquely the exponential distribution.

**Step 4 — Justify the exponential model mathematically.**
For a *constant* $\lambda(t) = \lambda$, the reliability function solves the first-order ODE:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda R(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Differentiating $R(t)$ and negating it gives back the PDF $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$. Thus, *constant hazard $\Leftrightarrow$ exponential distribution.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The exponential model is used for *complex electronic systems* (resistor networks, integrated circuits in useful life), *standby redundant systems that are periodically tested*, and any non-repairable component where aging effects are negligible. It is **not** appropriate for mechanical wear items (bearings, cutting tools) — those need a Weibull model.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **definitive reference** for Part A, Part B, and numerical problems in this module. Memorize the boxed identities — they appear verbatim in KTU question papers.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Failure rate (constant) | $\lambda$ | input parameter | failures / hour (or **fit** = failures per $10^9$ hr) |
| 2 | Probability Density Function | $f(t)$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Cumulative Distribution Function | $F(t)$ | $1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le F \le 1$ |
| 4 | Reliability / Survival function | $R(t)$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| 5 | Unreliability (probability of failure) | $Q(t)$ | $1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Hazard (failure) rate | $h(t)$ | $\lambda$ (constant) | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | **Mean Time To Failure** | **MTTF** | $1/\lambda$ | hours (or cycles) |
| 8 | Variance of $T$ | $\sigma^{2}$ | $1/\lambda^{2}$ | $\text{time}^{2}$ |
| 9 | **Standard Deviation of $T$** | $\sigma$ | $1/\lambda$ | hours (same units as MTTF) |
| 10 | Median time to failure | $t_{0.5}$ | $\ln 2 / \lambda \approx 0.693/\lambda$ | hours |
| 11 | Mode of $T$ | $t_{\text{mode}}$ | $0$ | hours (PDF is maximum at $t=0$) |
| 12 | $n$-th moment of $T$ | $E[T^{n}]$ | $n!/\lambda^{n}$ | derived from gamma function |
| 13 | Characteristic life ($R = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$) | $\tau$ | $1/\lambda$ | hours (same as MTTF) |
| 14 | Memoryless property | — | $P(T>s+t \mid T>s) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | qualitative, no unit |
| 15 | Hazard integral | $\int_0^t h(u) du$ | $\lambda t$ | dimensionless cumulative hazard |

**Critical observation (board-favourite):** For the exponential distribution, the **mean, median, mode-related characteristic life, and standard deviation are all equal to $1/\lambda$**. This is *unique* among continuous distributions and frequently tested.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reliability Function

Starting from the definition of the hazard function $h(t)$:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This is a first-order separable ODE. Rearranging:

$$h(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$, with the initial condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = -\ln R(t)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right)$$

For a **constant hazard** $h(t) = \lambda$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Expected Value $E[T]$ (MTTF)

The expectation of a non-negative continuous random variable is:

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, f(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

Use **integration by parts**: let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$E[T] = \Big[-t\,e^{-\lambda t}\Big]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

Evaluate the boundary term:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} t e^{-\lambda t} = 0 \quad \text{(exponential decay beats linear growth, confirmed by L'Hôpital)}$$

At $t = 0$: $-0 \cdot e^{0} = 0$. So the boundary term vanishes.

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \Big[-\tfrac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\Big]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \big(-\tfrac{1}{\lambda}\big) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda}\;}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of $E[T^2]$ (Required for the Variance)

We need $E[T^2]$ to find the variance. Using the same integration by parts twice, or evaluating the gamma integral:

$$E[T^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^2 \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

Substitute $u = \lambda t$ so that $t = u/\lambda$, $dt = du/\lambda$:

$$E[T^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{u}{\lambda}\right)^{2} \lambda e^{-u} \frac{du}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{2} e^{-u}\,du$$

The integral is the Gamma function $\Gamma(3) = 2! = 2$:

$$E[T^2] = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Generalisation** (useful for higher-moment questions):

$$E[T^{n}] = \frac{n!}{\lambda^{n}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Variance and Standard Deviation

$$\text{Var}(T) = E[T^2] - \big(E[T]\big)^{2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}} - \left(\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}} - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(T)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_T = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \text{MTTF}\;}$$

### 3.5 Alternative Derivation of MTTF Using the Survival Function

A remarkably elegant and frequently-used shortcut (board favourite) — derive MTTF directly from $R(t)$:

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

This identity $E[T] = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ is a *general* result valid for any non-negative random variable. For the exponential:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> A non-repairable electronic sensor used in a process plant has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour. Calculate: (a) MTTF, (b) the reliability for a 1000-hour mission, (c) the probability of failure in the first 500 hours.

**Given:** $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{hr}^{-1}$.

**(a) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 5000\ \text{hours}$$

**(b) Reliability at $t = 1000$ h:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

**(c) Probability of failure in first 500 h:**

$$F(500) = 1 - e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(500)} = 1 - e^{-0.1} = 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952$$

So there is approximately a **9.52% chance** of failure within the first 500 hours.

### 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Definition | Exponential Result | Numerical value (example) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f(t)$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | density | $2 \times 10^{-4} \cdot e^{-2 \times 10^{-4} t}$ |
| $F(t)$ | $1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | CDF | — |
| $R(t)$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | survival | $e^{-0.2} \approx 0.819$ at 1000 h |
| $E[T]$ | $\int t f(t) dt$ | $1/\lambda$ | $5000$ h |
| $E[T^2]$ | $\int t^2 f(t) dt$ | $2/\lambda^2$ | $5 \times 10^7$ h² |
| $\sigma^2$ | $E[T^2] - (E[T])^2$ | $1/\lambda^2$ | $2.5 \times 10^7$ h² |
| $\sigma$ | $\sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | $1/\lambda$ | $5000$ h |
| Median | $F(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$ | $\ln 2 / \lambda$ | $\approx 3466$ h |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Bathtub Curve — Phase-by-Phase Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram is a **block-level functional architecture** that maps the bathtub curve's three phases to their characteristic hazard functions, governing distributions, and engineering interventions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1["PHASE I - INFANT MORTALITY"]
        A1["Time: 0 to t1"]
        A2["Hazard: lambda decreasing"]
        A3["Distribution: Weibull shape beta less than 1"]
        A4["Cause: Manufacturing defects"]
        A5["Action: Burn-in screening"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2["PHASE II - USEFUL LIFE"]
        B1["Time: t1 to t2"]
        B2["Hazard: lambda CONSTANT"]
        B3["Distribution: EXPONENTIAL"]
        B4["Cause: Random overstress"]
        B5["Action: Operate and maintain"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE3["PHASE III - WEAR OUT"]
        C1["Time: t2 to infinity"]
        C2["Hazard: lambda increasing"]
        C3["Distribution: Weibull shape beta greater than 1"]
        C4["Cause: Fatigue and corrosion"]
        C5["Action: Preventive replacement"]
    end

    PHASE1 ==> PHASE2
    PHASE2 ==> PHASE3
```

### 4.2 Failure-Rate Behaviour Plot (ASCII Schematic)

```
   λ(t)
    │                              /│
    │                            /  │
    │                          /    │
    │                        /      │  ← PHASE III: WEAR-OUT
    │                      /        │     λ(t) increasing
    │                    /          │
    │ ──────────────────●───────────●─────► t
    │  PHASE I:    PHASE II:           PHASE III:
    │  INFANT      USEFUL LIFE         WEAR-OUT
    │  MORTALITY   (constant λ)        (λ rising)
    │  λ falling
    │
    │  \  /
    │   \/    ← PHASE I shape
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **y-axis** is the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ (failures per unit time).
- The **x-axis** is operating time $t$.
- The flat horizontal stretch in **Phase II** is where the hazard is **constant** — this is the *exponential regime*.
- $\lambda_0$ (the constant value) is the only parameter required to fully characterise reliability in Phase II.

### 4.3 Reliability Function Decay Curve (Exponential in Phase II)

```
   R(t)
  1.0 ┤●●●●●
      │       ●●●
  0.8 ┤          ●●●
      │             ●●●
  0.6 ┤                ●●●
      │                   ●●●
  0.4 ┤                      ●●●●
      │                          ●●●●●
  0.2 ┤                                ●●●●●●●●●●
      │
  0.0 ┼────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────► t
      0   t1   t2   t3   t4   t5   t6   t7
                ↑
            R(t) = e^(-λt)
         (pure exponential decay)
```

The curve passes through the characteristic point $(1/\lambda,\ e^{-1}) = (1/\lambda,\ 0.368)$ — i.e., at one MTTF, only ~36.8% of components are still alive.

### 4.4 System Reliability Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input["Component in Useful Life"] --> constant["Constant failure rate lambda"]
    constant --> model["Exponential Distribution Model"]
    model --> pdf["PDF: f of t equals lambda e to the power minus lambda t"]
    model --> cdf["CDF: F of t equals 1 minus e to the power minus lambda t"]
    model --> surv["Reliability: R of t equals e to the power minus lambda t"]
    surv --> mttf["MTTF equals 1 over lambda"]
    cdf --> var["Variance equals 1 over lambda squared"]
    var --> sd["Standard Deviation equals 1 over lambda"]
    mttf --> decision["Engineering Decision: Replace or Operate"]
    sd --> decision
```

This block-level architecture clearly shows the **single-parameter $\lambda$** as the source from which every other reliability metric is derived — a powerful conceptual anchor for exam answers.
