## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*State and explain the "memoryless property" of the exponential distribution. Why is this property essential in reliability engineering?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The memoryless property states that for a component with exponentially distributed time-to-failure $T$:

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad s, t \ge 0$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** A component that has already operated for $s$ hours has *no statistical memory* of its past — its remaining-life distribution is identical to that of a brand-new component. The probability of surviving an additional $t$ hours depends *only* on $t$, not on $s$.

**Significance in reliability engineering (2 marks):**
1. It mathematically models the *random-failure regime* (Phase II of the bathtub curve) where failures are caused by unpredictable external shocks, not by accumulated operating history.
2. It enables **simple replacement policies** — a component that has just been repaired (or replaced) is "as good as new," so reliability calculations restart from $R(0) = 1$ without complicated conditioning terms.
3. It drastically simplifies system-level reliability computations for series, parallel, and standby configurations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*List the three phases of the bathtub curve. For each phase, state (i) the trend of failure rate, (ii) one typical cause of failure, and (iii) the appropriate engineering action.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Phase | Trend | Cause | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Infant Mortality** | Decreasing | Manufacturing defects, latent flaws | Burn-in / factory screening |
| **II — Useful Life** | **Constant** | Random overstress, accidents | Operate, schedule inspection |
| **III — Wear-out** | Increasing | Fatigue, corrosion, material aging | Preventive replacement |

(Marks: 1 mark for correct list, 1 mark for trend identification, 1 mark for engineering action.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** confuse MTTF (non-repairable, mean time to *first* failure) with **MTBF** (repairable, mean time *between* failures) or **MTTR** (mean time *to* repair). Wrong abbreviation = -2 marks minimum.
> - When computing standard deviation of an exponential random variable, **state the units explicitly** (e.g., *hours*). KTU examiners specifically check for unit consistency; -1 mark if omitted.
> - In derivation questions, examiners want to see the **integration by parts step** written out. A bare final answer `MTTF = 1/λ` with no working = **0 marks** for that sub-part in ESE. Show all integration boundaries and limits.
> - When asked "show that the variance is $1/\lambda^2$", you *must* compute $E[T^2]$ first, then subtract $(E[T])^2$. Skipping $E[T^2]$ is a common losing-marks trap.
> - Always convert the failure rate to **matching time units** before substituting. If $\lambda$ is in failures per *hour* and $t$ is in *days*, multiply $t$ by 24 first. Unit mismatch is the #1 numerical error in KTU papers.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, OESFT837 Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the **mean time to failure (MTTF)** and the **standard deviation** of the time-to-failure random variable $T$ when $T$ follows an exponential distribution with constant failure rate $\lambda$. Show every step of the integration clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety-critical relay in a refinery shutdown system has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ failures per hour. Calculate:
    (i) MTTF of the relay.
    (ii) The reliability $R(2000)$ for a 2000-hour mission.
    (iii) The probability that the relay fails within the first 1000 hours of operation.
    (iv) The median time to failure.
    (v) Comment on the suitability of the exponential model for this relay if field data shows a steadily increasing failure rate after year 7 of service. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (Question A):**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The exponential PDF is $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ for $t \ge 0$, $\lambda > 0$.

**Step 1: MTTF (mean) — (3 marks)**

By definition:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

Apply integration by parts with $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$:

$$du = dt,\qquad v = -e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \Big[\,-t\,e^{-\lambda t}\,\Big]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

**Boundary term:** $\lim_{t\to\infty} t e^{-\lambda t} = 0$ (by L'Hôpital's rule, exponential dominates linear). At $t=0$: $-0 \cdot e^0 = 0$. So the boundary term is **0**.

**Remaining integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\;} \qquad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Variance — (2 marks)**

We need $E[T^2]$:

$$E[T^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^2 \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

Let $u = \lambda t$, so $t = u/\lambda$ and $dt = du/\lambda$:

$$E[T^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{u^2}{\lambda^2}\,e^{-u}\frac{du}{\lambda}\cdot\lambda = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\int_{0}^{\infty} u^2 e^{-u}\,du = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\,\Gamma(3) = \frac{2!}{\lambda^2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}$$

Variance:

$$\sigma^{2} = E[T^{2}] - (E[T])^{2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}} - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Standard deviation — (2 marks)**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \text{MTTF}\;}$$

**Key remark (1 mark, for completeness):** The standard deviation of an exponentially distributed time-to-failure equals the MTTF — a unique and important property used in safety-instrumented-system design.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 marks):**

Given: $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{failures/hour}$.

**(i) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 20{,}000\ \text{hours}$$

(≈ 2.28 years of continuous operation) — **(1 mark)**

**(ii) Reliability at $t = 2000$ h:**

$$R(2000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-5})(2000)} = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$$

So the relay has a **90.48%** chance of completing 2000 hours without failure. — **(1 mark)**

**(iii) Probability of failure within 1000 h:**

$$F(1000) = 1 - R(1000) = 1 - e^{-(5 \times 10^{-5})(1000)} = 1 - e^{-0.05} = 1 - 0.9512 = 0.0488$$

So **4.88%** probability of failure in the first 1000 hours. — **(1 mark)**

**(iv) Median time to failure:**

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.6931}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 13{,}863\ \text{hours}$$

(≈ 1.58 years) — **(1 mark)**

**(v) Engineering commentary — (3 marks):**

The exponential model assumes a **constant** failure rate, which corresponds to the *Useful Life* (Phase II) of the bathtub curve. If field data shows the failure rate is **steadily increasing** after year 7, the system has entered **Phase III — Wear-out**. Under these conditions:

- The exponential model **is no longer valid**; it will underestimate the true probability of failure.
- A more appropriate model is the **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta > 1$ (often 2–4 for electromechanical components), or the **lognormal** distribution.
- Recommended action: **preventive replacement** of the relay at a fixed interval *before* year 7 (e.g., at 5 years of service) to keep the operating point in Phase II.
- For a *safety-instrumented system* (SIS) in a refinery, this may require proof-test interval revision per IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 to maintain the required Safety Integrity Level (SIL).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, OESFT837 Module 4]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **bathtub curve**. For each of its three phases, identify the failure mechanism and state whether the exponential distribution is appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two identical safety valves are arranged in a **parallel (1-out-of-2) configuration** to provide redundancy. Each valve has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour. The system fails only when *both* valves fail (i.e., the system succeeds if at least one valve works). Calculate:
    (i) The reliability of each valve for a 5000-hour mission.
    (ii) The unreliability of the parallel system at $t = 5000$ h.
    (iii) The MTTF of the parallel system (use $R_{\text{sys}}(t) = 2R(t) - R(t)^2$ and integrate).
    (iv) Comment on the reliability gain achieved by parallel redundancy compared to a single valve. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (Question B):**

**Part (a) — Bathtub Curve (7 marks):**

**Sketch (1 mark):** Draw the bathtub curve with $\lambda(t)$ on the y-axis and $t$ on the x-axis, showing the three regions clearly (decreasing, constant, increasing).

| Phase | Mechanism | Exponential Suitable? |
|---|---|---|
| **I — Infant Mortality** | Defects in material, workmanship, quality-control lapses. Failures are *early-life* and their probability decreases as weak units are eliminated. | **No.** A Weibull with $\beta < 1$, or a lognormal, is appropriate. |
| **II — Useful Life** | Random external stresses, voltage spikes, operator errors, environmental shocks. Failures are *memoryless* and time-independent. | **Yes.** Constant $\lambda \Rightarrow$ exponential model is exact. |
| **III — Wear-out** | Mechanical fatigue, corrosion, dielectric breakdown, bearing wear. Failures are *aging-driven* and their probability rises. | **No.** A Weibull with $\beta > 1$, or lognormal, is appropriate. |

**Explanation (1 mark per phase, 3 marks total):**
- The hazard rate $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ is a decreasing function in Phase I, a constant in Phase II, and an increasing function in Phase III.
- The bathtub shape arises because three different sub-populations of failure mechanisms dominate in turn.

**Why exponential fits Phase II (2 marks):**
- A *constant* hazard $h(t) = \lambda$ uniquely yields $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ via the differential equation $dR/dt = -\lambda R$.
- The memoryless property $P(T>s+t \mid T>s) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is consistent with random, history-independent shocks.
- Therefore the exponential is the *only* distribution that models constant failure rate.

---

**Part (b) — Parallel Redundancy Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $\lambda = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{hr}^{-1}$, $t = 5000$ h.

**(i) Reliability of a single valve:**

$$R_1(5000) = R_2(5000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(1.5 \times 10^{-4})(5000)} = e^{-0.75} \approx 0.4724$$

— **(1 mark)**

**(ii) Unreliability of the 1-out-of-2 parallel system:**

For independent components in parallel:

$$R_{\text{sys}}(t) = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$$

With $R_1 = R_2 = R$:

$$R_{\text{sys}}(5000) = 2R - R^2 = 2(0.4724) - (0.4724)^2 = 0.9448 - 0.2232 = 0.7216$$

Unreliability:

$$Q_{\text{sys}}(5000) = 1 - R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - 0.7216 = 0.2784$$

— **(2 marks)**

**(iii) MTTF of the parallel system:**

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{sys}} = \int_0^{\infty} R_{\text{sys}}(t)\,dt = \int_0^{\infty} \big(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\big)\,dt$$

$$= 2\int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt - \int_0^{\infty} e^{-2\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{2}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{4 - 1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{sys}} = \frac{3}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3}{3 \times 10^{-4}} = 10{,}000\ \text{hours}$$

— **(2 marks)**

**(iv) Commentary on reliability gain — (2 marks):**

- For a **single valve:** $R(5000) = 0.4724$ and MTTF = $1/\lambda \approx 6667$ h.
- For the **1-out-of-2 system:** $R(5000) = 0.7216$ and MTTF = $10{,}000$ h.

The parallel redundancy **raises** the mission reliability from 47.24% to 72.16% and **increases** MTTF by 50% (a factor of $3/2$). However, this reliability gain comes at the cost of **doubling the hardware, maintenance, and proof-testing burden**. In safety-instrumented systems, parallel redundancy is justified only when the *target* SIL (per IEC 61508) cannot be met by a single channel.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it 30 minutes before the exam. The bullets below cover every KTU-frequent concept from Module 4.**

- ✅ **Bathtub curve** has **3 phases**: Infant Mortality (↓ $\lambda$), Useful Life (constant $\lambda$), Wear-out (↑ $\lambda$).
- ✅ **Exponential distribution** is the *only* distribution with a **constant failure rate** — it models **Phase II** of the bathtub curve.
- ✅ PDF: $\ f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$. CDF: $\ F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$. Reliability: $\ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ✅ **MTTF = 1/λ**, **Variance = 1/λ²**, **Standard Deviation = 1/λ** (i.e., MTTF = σ for exponential — unique property).
- ✅ **Median time to failure = (ln 2) / λ ≈ 0.693 / λ**.
- ✅ **Mean $\neq$ Median** for exponential: mean = $1/\lambda$, median = $0.693/\lambda$. The mean is *larger*.
- ✅ **Memoryless property:** $P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — the future does not depend on the past.
- ✅ **Characteristic life** (where $R = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$) is also $1/\lambda$.
- ✅ At $t = \text{MTTF}$, the reliability has dropped to **36.8%** — a board-exam favourite.
- ✅ **Reliability after one MTTF:** $R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$.
- ✅ **Reliability after two MTTF:** $R(2 \cdot \text{MTTF}) = e^{-2} \approx 0.135$.
- ✅ **Reliability after three MTTF:** $R(3 \cdot \text{MTTF}) = e^{-3} \approx 0.050$ — the "3-σ rule of thumb" for useful-life operation.
- ✅ **MTTF applies to NON-repairable** systems; **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** for repairable systems.
- ✅ **MTTR** = mean time to repair; **MTBF** = mean time between failures (repairable).
- ✅ **Availability** $A = \text{MTTF} / (\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR})$ — dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$.
- ✅ **Series system:** $R_{\text{sys}} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n$ (system fails if *any* component fails).
- ✅ **Parallel system (1-out-of-2):** $R_{\text{sys}} = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ (system fails only if *all* components fail).
- ✅ **Standby redundant system:** inactive component has *zero* failure rate until switched in.
- ✅ The **exponential model is inappropriate** in Phase I (use Weibull $\beta < 1$) and Phase III (use Weibull $\beta > 1$ or lognormal).
- ✅ **Units consistency:** always ensure $\lambda$ and $t$ use the same time unit before computing $\lambda t$ (dimensionless exponent).
- ✅ **failures-in-time (fit) unit:** $1\ \text{fit} = 10^{-9}$ failures per hour; convert to per-hour when using $e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ✅ **Memoryless property** is **unique** to the geometric (discrete) and exponential (continuous) distributions — KTU loves this.
- ✅ For an exponential random variable, **$\sigma$ = MTTF**, which means the **coefficient of variation is 1** — a fingerprint of exponential behaviour.
- ✅ Integration-by-parts derivation: $\int_0^\infty t\lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = 1/\lambda$ — must be shown in full in derivations.
- ✅ Reliability block diagrams for **k-out-of-n** systems use the binomial expansion.
- ✅ For mission times $t \ll 1/\lambda$, the approximation $R(t) \approx 1 - \lambda t$ (linearization) is often used in quick design checks.
- ✅ KTU Module 4 final takeaway: **the bathtub curve is qualitative; the exponential distribution is its quantitative engine in Phase II.**

---
