topics.title: MTTR
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MTTR

# Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) – Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** is the standard quantitative reliability and maintainability metric that represents the *expected average time required to restore a failed system, equipment, or component back to fully operational status*. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (NEP 2020 aligned) under *Qualitative and Quantitative Approaches in Safety*, MTTR is classified as a **Quantitative Safety Metric (QSM)** that directly governs the *Inherent Availability* of safety-critical systems.

Mathematically, MTTR is the arithmetic mean of all repair times observed for a population of identical (or similar) repairable items.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Official Terminology:** MTTR is broadly grouped under the domain of **Maintainability Engineering** (IEC 60300-3-12 / MIL-HDBK-472 frameworks). It is a first-order metric used by safety engineers to compute **Operational Availability ($A_o$)** and **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** verification for Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS).

The generic governing expression is:

$$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{repair},i}}{N} $$

where $T_{\text{repair},i}$ is the downtime of the $i^{\text{th}}$ failure event, and $N$ is the total number of failure events.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – Plain English

Imagine you own a **car**. Over five years, it breaks down (puncture, battery failure, brake issue, overheating, alternator replacement). Each time it fails, the garage takes a different number of hours/days to fix it. Now, if you add up **all the days the car spent in the garage** and divide it by the **number of breakdowns**, you get the *average time your car is "sick" before it heals*.

That average is exactly what **MTTR** is for an industrial plant, a turbine, a fire-fighting pump, or a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC).

| Real-World Analogy | Industrial Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Car breaks down | Equipment/system failure |
| Days spent in garage | Downtime ($T_{\text{repair}}$) |
| Garage mechanic | Maintenance crew |
| Number of breakdowns | Number of failures ($N$) |
| **Average healing time** | **MTTR** |

## 1.3 MTTR – Sub-Components of Repair Time

For accurate KTU-level analysis, the total repair time $T_{\text{repair}}$ is the sum of four sequential sub-phases:

> [!NOTE]  
> **Decomposition of $T_{\text{repair}}$:**
> 1. **Detection Time ($T_D$):** Time elapsed from the actual failure to the moment an alarm/operator notices the failure.
> 2. **Diagnosis Time ($T_{Di}$):** Time taken by the maintenance team to identify the *root cause* (using diagnostics, schematics, etc.).
> 3. **Logistics / Spare Procurement Time ($T_L$):** Time to fetch spare parts, tools, and reach the site.
> 4. **Active Repair / Replacement Time ($T_R$):** Time for hands-on physical repair, replacement, or reconfiguration.
> 5. **Re-commissioning & Testing Time ($T_C$):** Time to restart, calibrate, and verify the system is operational.

Therefore:

$$ T_{\text{repair}} = T_D + T_{Di} + T_L + T_R + T_C $$

When computing MTTR, **all five sub-phases are included** in the summation. This is a common KTU pitfall — students often only count $T_R$ (hands-on repair) and ignore detection & logistics time.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Some literature defines **MTTR** (narrow) and **MDT (Mean Down Time)** (broad) differently. MTTR in the *strict maintainability sense* is only the *active repair time* (excluding logistics & administrative delay). However, in the **KTU 2024 Safety Engineering context**, the broader operational definition is preferred, where *MTTR ≈ MDT*. Always check the wording of the question.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Highlighted)

The key standard units and metrics associated with MTTR in KTU 2024 Safety curriculum:

- **MTTR Unit:** **hours (h)** or **minutes (min)**
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Uptime metric
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** For non-repairable items
- **Availability ($A$):** A dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) or percentage
- **Failure Rate ($\lambda$):** Failures per hour, **units: 1/h**
- **Repair Rate ($\mu$):** Reciprocal of MTTR, **units: 1/h**
- **Standard Reference:** **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 (Functional Safety)**, **MIL-HDBK-472 (Maintainability)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why MTTR is a Pillar of Quantitative Safety

In quantitative safety analysis, every accident scenario has a *frequency* and a *consequence*. MTTR directly controls how *frequently* a safety-critical protective layer (e.g., an emergency shutdown valve, ESDV) is in a *failed-but-undiscovered* state. The longer a safety system is in repair, the higher the probability that a *demand* (a hazardous event requiring protection) will occur while the safety layer is unavailable.

This is the basis of the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** calculations in **SIL (Safety Integrity Level) verification**:

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{SIS}} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} $$

where $\lambda_{DU}$ is the *dangerous undetected failure rate* and $t_{CE}$ is the **Channel Equivalent Downtime**, which is **numerically equal to one-half of the MTTR** for a redundant system with proof testing:

$$ t_{CE} = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{2} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Linking MTTR to SIL:** Reducing MTTR from 24 hours to 4 hours can drop PFD by a factor of 6, often improving the SIL rating of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) without any hardware change. This is a **favourite KTU Part B (14-mark) question topic**.

## 2.2 Logical Breakdown – The Operational Concept

**Step 1 – Failure Occurs**
A component reaches its wear-out point or suffers a random failure. The system transitions from *Operational* to *Failed* state.

**Step 2 – Detection & Diagnosis**
Sensors, alarms, or operators detect the failure. Time $T_D + T_{Di}$ elapses.

**Step 3 – Mobilization**
Maintenance crew arrives, spare parts are obtained. Time $T_L$ elapses.

**Step 4 – Active Repair**
Hands-on work is performed. Time $T_R$ elapses.

**Step 5 – Verification**
System is restarted, tested, and certified. Time $T_C$ elapses.

**Step 6 – Return to Service**
The clock for the *next* MTBF cycle begins. Total downtime = $T_{\text{repair}}$.

**The "Why" Behind MTTR Reduction:**
- Higher **System Availability** $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- Lower **Safety Unavailability**
- Lower **Production Loss** in operational plants
- Lower **Hazard Exposure Time** in chemical/process plants

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_{\text{repair},i}}{N}$ | Fundamental definition | hours |
| 2 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Reciprocal of repair rate | hours |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent Availability | 0 to 1 (dimensionless) |
| 4 | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}$ | Operational Availability (MDT $\geq$ MTTR) | 0 to 1 |
| 5 | $\text{PFD} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{2}$ | Probability of Failure on Demand (1oo1) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{MDT} = \dfrac{T_{\text{repair}}}{N}$ | Mean Down Time (broader) | hours |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure rate relationship | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $A \approx e^{-\lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}}$ | Exponential approximation (low $\lambda$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $t_{CE} = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{2}$ | Channel Equivalent Downtime | hours |
| 10 | $\text{MTTR}_{\text{system}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{MTTR}_j$ | Series (non-redundant) system MTTR | hours |
| 11 | $\text{MTTR}_{\text{parallel}} = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}_1 \cdot \text{MTTR}_2}{\text{MTTR}_1 + \text{MTTR}_2}$ | Parallel redundant MTTR | hours |
| 12 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For non-repairable items | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Absolute Value Rule (Anti-Break):** In the markdown formula table above, every absolute-value or magnitude symbol has been written using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert A \vert$) — never the raw `vert` pipe character — to preserve the markdown table structure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of MTTR |
|---|---|
| **Process Safety** (Oil & Gas) | Designing SIL of Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves |
| **Nuclear Safety** | Availability of reactor protection systems |
| **Aviation Safety** | AOG (Aircraft on Ground) recovery time, MRO scheduling |
| **Manufacturing** | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) calculation |
| **Healthcare Safety** | Mean downtime of life-support / imaging equipment |
| **Fire Safety Engineering** | Restoration of sprinkler pumps, hydrant networks |
| **Automotive Functional Safety** | ISO 26262 hazard analysis, fault tolerant time interval |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – MTTR from the Repair Rate ($\mu$)

The repair process is classically modelled as a **memoryless (Markovian) exponential process**, just like failure. The probability that a repair takes longer than time $t$ is the *survival function* of the repair time:

$$ S_{\text{repair}}(t) = e^{-\mu t} $$

The expected value (mean) of the repair time distribution gives MTTR. For an exponential distribution with parameter $\mu$:

$$ E[T_{\text{repair}}] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt $$

where the probability density function is $f(t) = \mu e^{-\mu t}$.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the expectation integral.

$$ E[T_{\text{repair}}] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \mu e^{-\mu t} \, dt $$

**Step 2:** Use integration by parts. Let:
- $u = t \quad \Rightarrow \quad du = dt$
- $dv = \mu e^{-\mu t} \, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = -e^{-\mu t}$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$.

$$ E[T_{\text{repair}}] = \left[ -t e^{-\mu t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\mu t}) \, dt $$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the boundary term. As $t \to \infty$, $t e^{-\mu t} \to 0$. At $t = 0$, $t e^{-\mu t} = 0$. So the boundary term vanishes:

$$ \left[ -t e^{-\mu t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 $$

**Step 5:** Simplify the remaining integral.

$$ E[T_{\text{repair}}] = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\mu t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\mu} e^{-\mu t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} $$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the limits.

$$ E[T_{\text{repair}}] = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{\mu} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} $$

**Step 7 (Conclusion):** Therefore,

$$ \boxed{\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}} $$

This is the formal link between MTTR and the exponential repair rate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Availability from MTBF and MTTR

Consider a system that alternates between *Operational* (Up) and *Repair* (Down) states. Over a long mission time $T$, the total Up time is $T \cdot A$ and total Down time is $T \cdot (1-A)$, where $A$ is the **Inherent Availability**.

**Step 1:** Express Up time in terms of MTBF.

The system, on average, operates for MTBF hours between failures:

$$ \text{Up time} = T \cdot A = N \cdot \text{MTBF} $$

where $N$ is the average number of Up cycles.

**Step 2:** Express Down time in terms of MTTR.

$$ \text{Down time} = T \cdot (1 - A) = N \cdot \text{MTTR} $$

**Step 3:** Divide the two equations.

$$ \frac{T \cdot A}{T \cdot (1 - A)} = \frac{N \cdot \text{MTBF}}{N \cdot \text{MTTR}} $$

**Step 4:** Cancel $T$ and $N$.

$$ \frac{A}{1 - A} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTTR}} $$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiply.

$$ A \cdot \text{MTTR} = \text{MTBF} - A \cdot \text{MTBF} $$

**Step 6:** Collect $A$ terms on one side.

$$ A \cdot \text{MTTR} + A \cdot \text{MTBF} = \text{MTBF} $$

$$ A \cdot (\text{MTTR} + \text{MTBF}) = \text{MTBF} $$

**Step 7 (Final):**

$$ \boxed{A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Link Between MTTR and Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)

For a single-channel (1oo1) Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), the **average unavailability** over a proof-test interval $T_{\text{proof}}$ is computed by averaging the failure probability over the interval.

**Step 1:** Failure rate for a dangerous undetected failure is $\lambda_{DU}$. For a small interval $\Delta t$, the probability of failure is $\lambda_{DU} \cdot \Delta t$.

**Step 2:** The *average* unavailability over the proof test interval is the mean time the system spends failed, divided by the interval length.

**Step 3:** For a single failure event that is detected and repaired, the average downtime is MTTR/2 (since the failure could occur anywhere uniformly in the interval, averaging to half the maximum MTTR):

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{\text{MTTR}}{2} $$

**Step 4:** For a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) redundant SIF, the unavailability is the product of both channels' unavailabilities:

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \left( \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{\text{MTTR}}{2} \right)^{2} $$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump in a chemical plant has been observed for 8000 hours. It failed 6 times. The recorded downtimes (in hours) for each failure are: 4, 6, 3, 8, 5, 2. Compute the MTTR, MTBF, and Inherent Availability.

### Solution

**Step 1 – Compute MTTR:**

$$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{4 + 6 + 3 + 8 + 5 + 2}{6} = \frac{28}{6} = 4.667 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 2 – Compute total operating (Up) time:**

$$ \text{Up time} = \text{Total time} - \text{Down time} = 8000 - 28 = 7972 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 3 – Compute MTBF:**

$$ \text{MTBF} = \frac{7972}{6} = 1328.67 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 4 – Compute Inherent Availability:**

$$ A = \frac{1328.67}{1328.67 + 4.667} = \frac{1328.67}{1333.33} = 0.9965 \text{ (or 99.65\%)} $$

**Step 5 – Compute repair rate:**

$$ \mu = \frac{1}{\text{MTTR}} = \frac{1}{4.667} = 0.2143 \text{ h}^{-1} $$

**Step 6 – Interpretation for Safety:**

The pump is available 99.65% of the time. The PFD for a dangerous undetected failure with $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ h}^{-1}$ would be:

$$ \text{PFD} = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times \frac{4.667}{2} = 1.167 \times 10^{-5} $$

This corresponds approximately to **SIL-2** range ($10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$) in IEC 61508.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lifecycle Block Diagram of MTTR

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional state-transition architecture** of a system with MTTR, MTBF, and Availability as its governing parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Operational] -->|Continuous Operation| B[System Operational]
    B -->|Random Failure Event| C[Failed State Entry]
    C -->|Detection and Diagnosis| D[Diagnosis Phase]
    D -->|Spare and Crew Mobilization| E[Logistics Phase]
    E -->|Active Hands on Repair| F[Active Repair Phase]
    F -->|Restart and Verification| G[Re-commissioning Phase]
    G -->|Restored to Service| A
    A -.->|Average Duration MTBF| B
    C -.->|Total Downtime T repair| G
    A -->|Compute Inherent Availability A| H[Availability Metric]
    H -->|Inherent Availability Formula| I[A equals MTBF divided by MTBF plus MTTR]
    F -->|Mean Duration| J[MTTR Metric]
    J -->|Reciprocal| K[Repair Rate Mu]
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (A → B → C → ... → A) represents one full mission cycle of a repairable system.
- The **top edge** (A → B) represents the *Up state* governed by **MTBF**.
- The **lower path** (C → D → E → F → G) represents the *Down state* governed by **MTTR**.
- The **dashed arrows** indicate the *metric* governing the corresponding phase.

## 4.2 Relationship of MTTR with Adjacent Safety Metrics

```mermaid
graph LR
    MTBF[MTBF Mean Time Between Failures] --> AVAIL[Inherent Availability A]
    MTTR[MTTR Mean Time To Repair] --> AVAIL
    AVAIL --> PFD[Probability of Failure on Demand PFD]
    PFD --> SIL[Safety Integrity Level SIL 1 to 3]
    MTBF --> LAMBDAD[Lambda Failure Rate]
    MTTR --> MU[Mu Repair Rate]
    LAMBDAD --> PFD
    MU --> PFD
    SIL --> SIS[Safety Instrumented System Design]
```

**Reading guide:**
- MTBF and MTTR are the **two parent metrics** that determine Availability.
- Availability and Failure Rate jointly determine PFD.
- PFD determines the **SIL rating** of the safety function.
- This chain is the **heart of IEC 61508/61511 Functional Safety design**.

## 4.3 Exponential Decay Behaviour of Repair Probability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time equals 0] -->|Repair Begins| T1[Time equals MTTR]
    T1 -->|63.2 percent probability Repair Complete| T2[Time equals 2 times MTTR]
    T2 -->|86.5 percent probability Repair Complete| T3[Time equals 3 times MTTR]
    T3 -->|95.0 percent probability Repair Complete| T4[Time equals 4 times MTTR]
    T4 -->|98.2 percent probability Repair Complete| T5[Time equals 5 times MTTR]
```

**Key takeaway (memoryless property):**
- At $t = \text{MTTR}$, ~63.2% of repairs are complete.
- At $t = 2 \cdot \text{MTTR}$, ~86.5% are complete.
- This is a common **3-mark KTU question**: *"What percentage of repairs are completed within one MTTR?"* (Answer: ~63.2%).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define MTTR. How is it distinguished from MDT (Mean Down Time)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** It is the average time taken to restore a failed system to fully operational status, computed as the total corrective maintenance time divided by the number of failures. It is a *maintainability metric* in the strict sense, including only **active repair and re-commissioning time** (about 1.5 marks).

**Distinction from MDT:** MDT is a *broader* operational metric that includes MTTR **plus** administrative delay, logistics time, and waiting time. Mathematically, MDT $\geq$ MTTR. In KTU 2024 safety assessments, MTTR is often used synonymously with MDT for simplicity (about 1.5 marks).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**A pressure safety valve has a failure rate of $2 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour and an MTTR of 10 hours. Compute the Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) for a 1oo1 architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The formula for PFD of a 1oo1 SIF (about 1 mark):

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \times \frac{\text{MTTR}}{2} $$

Substituting $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ h}^{-1}$ and MTTR = 10 h (about 1 mark):

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo1} = (2 \times 10^{-5}) \times \frac{10}{2} = 1 \times 10^{-4} $$

The PFD is $1 \times 10^{-4}$, which falls in the SIL-1 range ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$) as per IEC 61508 (about 1 mark).

---

## Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the five sequential sub-phases of repair time used to compute MTTR. How does reducing MTTR affect the Safety Integrity Level (SIL) of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A critical fire-water pump in an LNG terminal is observed over 12,000 hours. It fails 8 times. The recorded downtimes (in hours) are: 12, 18, 9, 24, 6, 15, 21, 11. The dangerous undetected failure rate is $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ h}^{-1}$.
> Compute: (i) MTTR, (ii) MTBF, (iii) Inherent Availability, (iv) PFD for a 1oo1 SIF, and (v) classify the SIL.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – 7 Marks**

The five sequential sub-phases of repair time are (about 1 mark each):

1. **Detection Time ($T_D$):** Interval between actual failure and operator/system recognition. Reduced by better alarm systems and condition monitoring.

2. **Diagnosis Time ($T_{Di}$):** Time spent identifying the *root cause* using diagnostic tools, schematics, and expert analysis.

3. **Logistics Time ($T_L$):** Time to obtain spares, tools, and mobilize crew to the work site.

4. **Active Repair Time ($T_R$):** Hands-on physical work — replacement, welding, re-calibration, etc.

5. **Re-commissioning Time ($T_C$):** Verification, testing, and certification before returning the system to service.

**Effect of MTTR Reduction on SIL:** Since PFD = $\lambda_{DU} \times \frac{\text{MTTR}}{2}$ for 1oo1 (about 2 marks):

- PFD is *directly proportional* to MTTR. Halving MTTR halves the PFD.
- A reduction in PFD can move the SIF from one SIL bracket to a higher (safer) one, without any hardware change.
- For example, reducing MTTR from 24 h to 4 h reduces PFD by a factor of 6 — a SIL-2 SIF ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$) can be re-classified as SIL-3 ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$) for the same failure rate.
- This makes **logistics optimization, condition monitoring, and predictive maintenance** critical levers for safety (about 1 mark).

**Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**(i) MTTR (1 mark):**

$$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{12 + 18 + 9 + 24 + 6 + 15 + 21 + 11}{8} = \frac{116}{8} = 14.5 \text{ hours} $$

**(ii) MTBF (2 marks):**

Total downtime = 116 hours. Total Up time = $12000 - 116 = 11884$ hours.

$$ \text{MTBF} = \frac{11884}{8} = 1485.5 \text{ hours} $$

**(iii) Inherent Availability (1.5 marks):**

$$ A = \frac{1485.5}{1485.5 + 14.5} = \frac{1485.5}{1500} = 0.9903 \text{ (or 99.03%)} $$

**(iv) PFD (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \times \frac{\text{MTTR}}{2} = (3 \times 10^{-6}) \times \frac{14.5}{2} $$

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo1} = (3 \times 10^{-6}) \times 7.25 = 2.175 \times 10^{-5} $$

**(v) SIL Classification (1 mark):**

Since PFD = $2.175 \times 10^{-5}$, it falls in the range $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$, which corresponds to **SIL-2** as per IEC 61508.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not** use the total observation time (12,000 h) directly as MTBF numerator. MTBF uses *Up time* only.
> 2. **Do not** forget to divide MTTR by 2 in the PFD formula. This is the most common error costing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Always** state the SIL bracket ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$, etc.) explicitly. Vague answers lose the final 1 mark.
> 4. **Watch** the units — MTTR must be in hours, $\lambda$ in h$^{-1}$, PFD is dimensionless.

---

### Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice) – **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between MTTR, MTBF, and Inherent Availability. Explain how availability is a function of MTTR with reference to safety-critical systems.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A pressure transmitter in a refinery has $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ h}^{-1}$. The maintenance crew takes an average of 8 hours to repair it. Compare the PFD and SIL of the SIF for (i) 1oo1 architecture, (ii) 1oo2 architecture. What inference do you draw about the role of redundancy?

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – 7 Marks**

**Derivation of Availability (about 4 marks):** Refer to Derivation 2 in Section 3.2 above, and reproduce the 7-step derivation:

1. Up time = $T \cdot A = N \cdot \text{MTBF}$
2. Down time = $T \cdot (1 - A) = N \cdot \text{MTTR}$
3. Divide: $\frac{A}{1-A} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTTR}}$
4. Cross-multiply, simplify, isolate $A$:

$$ \boxed{A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}} $$

**MTTR-Availability Relationship (about 3 marks):**
- $A$ is *inversely related* to MTTR for a given MTBF.
- A 50% reduction in MTTR significantly increases $A$.
- For safety-critical systems (nuclear, chemical, aviation), even a small MTTR reduction can shift the SIF from a lower SIL to a higher SIL.
- Example: If MTBF = 10,000 h and MTTR = 100 h, $A = 0.99$. If MTTR drops to 10 h, $A = 0.999$, a tenfold reduction in unavailability.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**(i) PFD for 1oo1 (about 2 marks):**

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \times \frac{\text{MTTR}}{2} = (1 \times 10^{-5}) \times \frac{8}{2} = 4 \times 10^{-5} $$

This falls in the range $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ → **SIL-2**.

**(ii) PFD for 1oo2 (about 3 marks):**

For 1oo2 (one-out-of-two), both channels must fail for system failure:

$$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \left( \lambda_{DU} \times \frac{\text{MTTR}}{2} \right)^{2} = (4 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 1.6 \times 10^{-9} $$

This falls in the range $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ → **SIL-4** (or at least SIL-3 high-demand boundary).

**(iii) Inference on Redundancy (about 2 marks):**

- Redundancy reduces PFD by *several orders of magnitude* — a 1oo1 with PFD $4 \times 10^{-5}$ becomes a 1oo2 with PFD $1.6 \times 10^{-9}$, a reduction by a factor of 25,000.
- This is the **fundamental justification for hardware redundancy** in SIL-3 and SIL-4 applications.
- However, redundancy also requires **common cause failure analysis** (beta-factor model) for realistic SIL verification.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):**
> 1. **Squaring** in the 1oo2 PFD is a common mistake — students often *add* the two PFDs instead of *multiplying* them. 1oo2 requires BOTH channels to fail, hence multiplication.
> 2. **Do not** use the formula $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot \text{PFD}_{1oo1}$. That is wrong. The correct form is $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \text{PFD}_{1oo1}^2$ for *independent* identical channels.
> 3. **State both PFD value AND SIL bracket** for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Quick-Recall Bullet Box for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ **MTTR = Total Repair Time / Number of Failures** — fundamental equation.
- ✅ **MTTR = 1 / $\mu$** — where $\mu$ is the repair rate.
- ✅ **MTTR unit is hours** (sometimes minutes in textbooks).
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- ✅ **PFD (1oo1) = $\lambda_{DU} \cdot \text{MTTR} / 2$**.
- ✅ **PFD (1oo2) = $(\lambda_{DU} \cdot \text{MTTR} / 2)^2$**.
- ✅ **MTTR has 5 sub-phases:** Detection, Diagnosis, Logistics, Active Repair, Re-commissioning.
- ✅ **MTTR $\leq$ MDT** (MTTR is strict; MDT is broad).
- ✅ **63.2%** of repairs are completed within one MTTR (memoryless exponential).
- ✅ **Redundancy drastically reduces PFD** — squaring effect for 1oo2.
- ✅ **MTTR reduction is a "free" SIL upgrade** — no hardware change needed, only maintenance optimization.
- ✅ **Standards to cite in answers:** **IEC 61508**, **IEC 61511**, **MIL-HDBK-472**, **ISO 26262**.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Forgetting the division by 2 in PFD formula — *always half of MTTR*.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Using total observation time (instead of Up time) for MTBF denominator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MTBF - Evaluation of Network Reliability/Unreliability of Simple Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MTBF - Evaluation of Network Reliability/Unreliability of Simple Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is the expected elapsed time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system under defined operating conditions. It is the most fundamental reliability metric used in quantitative safety engineering and is mathematically expressed as the reciprocal of the constant failure rate in a system that follows the **exponential distribution**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *MTBF is the arithmetic mean of the time intervals between successive failures of a repairable component or system, computed as the total operational time divided by the number of failures observed during that period.*

## 1.2 Network Reliability — Conceptual Meaning

In reliability block diagrams, a **Network** refers to a logical arrangement of components such that system success depends on the successful functioning of these components in a defined configuration. The two elementary configurations are:

1. **Series Configuration** — All components must function for the system to function.
2. **Parallel Configuration** — At least one path must remain functional for system success.

**Network Reliability (R)** is the probability that the network performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time. Conversely, **Network Unreliability (Q)** is the probability of network failure.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weakest Link Chain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a **chain holding a heavy load**. The chain has multiple links (components) joined end-to-end (series). The chain holds the load **only if every single link holds**. If even one link breaks, the entire chain fails and the load falls.
> - The **weakest link** dictates the strength of the chain.
> - Adding more links does not make the chain stronger — it actually *reduces* the overall reliability because there are now *more* things that could fail.
> - This is exactly how a **series reliability network** behaves: $R_{system} < R_{component}$ always.

## 1.4 Key Reliability Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability | $R(t)$ | Probability of survival up to time $t$ | Dimensionless |
| Unreliability | $Q(t)$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ | Dimensionless |
| Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | Failures per unit time | Failures/hour |
| MTBF | $MTBF$ | Mean time between failures | Hours |
| MTTF | $MTTF$ | Mean time to failure (non-repairable) | Hours |
| MTTR | $MTTR$ | Mean time to repair | Hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Relationship for Repairable Systems:**
> $$MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$$
> For a **constant failure rate** regime (the exponential reliability region), this simplifies to:
> $$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Exponential Reliability Model — Foundation

Most reliability calculations for series networks assume the **exponential distribution**, valid when:
- The system has a **constant failure rate** ($\lambda$ = constant).
- The component has passed its infant-mortality (burn-in) phase.
- The component has not yet reached its wear-out phase.
- Failures are **statistically independent** and **memoryless**.

Under this model, the fundamental reliability function for a single component is:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This single equation is the **cornerstone** of all series network MTBF derivations.

## 2.2 Building the Series Network — Logic Flow

The series reliability model is built on three foundational assumptions:

1. **Independence of Failures** — The failure of one component does not physically cause or prevent the failure of another.
2. **Functionality Requirement** — *All* components must work for the system to work.
3. **Multiplicative Law of Probability** — For independent events, the joint probability equals the product of individual probabilities.

## 2.3 Derivation Logic — Series Reliability

Applying the multiplication rule of probability to $n$ independent components in series:

**Step 1:** System functions → All components must function.

**Step 2:** Probability of joint success = Product of individual success probabilities.

**Step 3:** For exponential reliability of each component $i$: $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$.

**Step 4:** Substituting yields the series reliability function.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for Series Network MTBF & Reliability**

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | Single-component exponential reliability | Constant $\lambda$, memoryless |
| 2 | $Q_i(t) = 1 - R_i(t)$ | Single-component unreliability | Always valid |
| 3 | $R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Series reliability (independent components) | $n \geq 1$ |
| 4 | $R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\lambda_s t}$ | Series reliability in exponential form | Series in exponential regime |
| 5 | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Equivalent series failure rate (additive) | Constant $\lambda_i$ per component |
| 6 | $MTBF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$ | Series MTBF | Valid when $\lambda_s$ is constant |
| 7 | $Q_s(t) = 1 - R_s(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_s t}$ | Series unreliability | $0 \leq Q_s \leq 1$ |
| 8 | $MTBF_i = \frac{1}{\lambda_i}$ | Individual component MTBF | Component $i$ in constant-rate region |
| 9 | $\frac{1}{MTBF_s} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{MTBF_i}$ | Reciprocal-form series MTBF | Equivalent to Formula 6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Reciprocal Rule" (Formula 9):** This is a KTU examiner favourite. It allows you to combine component MTBFs directly without first converting to failure rates. The reciprocals of MTBFs add up to give the reciprocal of the system MTBF.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Systems** — Aircraft flight control systems are analyzed as series chains because a single sensor failure can compromise the entire control loop.
- **Nuclear Reactor Safety** — Emergency shutdown systems use series logic (AND-gates) to ensure no single failure can defeat the protective function.
- **Manufacturing Production Lines** — A conveyor line is a series system; the line's MTBF depends on the sum of failure rates of every machine on the belt.
- **Medical Devices** — Patient monitoring circuits must be modeled in series because any sensor dropout is a critical failure.
- **Telecommunication Backbones** — Long-haul fiber links are series-connected repeaters; the link MTBF is governed by the **weakest** (highest $\lambda$) repeater.

## 2.6 Key "Why" Behind the Additive Failure Rate Rule

The reason failure rates *add* in series is intuitive: in a fixed time interval $dt$, the probability that *any* one of the $n$ components fails is approximately the sum of individual failure probabilities (for small $\lambda_i dt$), since cross-failures (two components failing in the same infinitesimal interval) have negligible probability of order $(dt)^2$.

$$\lambda_s \, dt \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \, dt$$

Dividing both sides by $dt$ gives $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of Series Reliability Function

**Given:** $n$ independent components with constant failure rates $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$ arranged in a series configuration.

**To Prove:** $R_s(t) = \exp\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \, t\right)$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s(t) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) & &\text{(Multiplicative law for independent events in series)} \\[4pt]
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} & &\text{(Each component follows exponential law } R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t} \cdots e^{-\lambda_n t} & &\text{(Explicit expansion of the product)} \\[4pt]
&= e^{-\lambda_1 t - \lambda_2 t - \cdots - \lambda_n t} & &\text{(Property of exponents: } e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= e^{-\left(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots + \lambda_n\right) t} & &\text{(Factor out } t \text{ from exponent)} \\[4pt]
&= e^{-\lambda_s t} & &\text{where } \lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Therefore:** The series system also follows an exponential reliability law with equivalent failure rate equal to the sum of individual failure rates.

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation of Series MTBF

**Starting Point:** The Mean Time Between Failures of a system is defined as the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable $T$.

$$MTBF = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, f(t) \, dt$$

For an exponential distribution with parameter $\lambda_s$, the probability density function is:

$$f(t) = \lambda_s \, e^{-\lambda_s t}$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTBF_s &= \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda_s \, e^{-\lambda_s t} \, dt & &\text{(Definition of expected value)} \\[4pt]
&= \lambda_s \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, e^{-\lambda_s t} \, dt & &\text{(Pull constant out of integral)} \\[4pt]
&= \lambda_s \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda_s^{2}} & &\text{(Use standard integral } \int_0^\infty t e^{-at} dt = \frac{1}{a^2}\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{\lambda_s} & &\text{(Cancellation of } \lambda_s\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i} & &\text{(Substitute the series equivalent failure rate)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{MTBF_i}} & &\text{(Reciprocal-form using } \lambda_i = 1/MTBF_i\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,MTBF_s = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i} = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{MTBF_i}}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Standard Format

> **Problem Statement:**
> A safety-critical alarm system consists of **three independent components** connected in series:
> - Component A: $MTBF_A = 2000$ hours
> - Component B: $MTBF_B = 5000$ hours
> - Component C: $MTBF_C = 10000$ hours
>
> **Calculate:**
> (i) The reliability of the system for a mission time of $t = 100$ hours.
> (ii) The unreliability of the system for $t = 100$ hours.
> (iii) The MTBF of the entire series network.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute individual failure rates from MTBFs.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_A &= \frac{1}{MTBF_A} = \frac{1}{2000} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hr} \\[4pt]
\lambda_B &= \frac{1}{MTBF_B} = \frac{1}{5000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hr} \\[4pt]
\lambda_C &= \frac{1}{MTBF_C} = \frac{1}{10000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute equivalent series failure rate (additive).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_s &= \lambda_A + \lambda_B + \lambda_C \\[4pt]
&= (5.0 + 2.0 + 1.0) \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
&= 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute system MTBF (Part iii).**

$$MTBF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 1250 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4: Compute reliability at $t = 100$ hours (Part i).**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s(100) &= e^{-\lambda_s \cdot t} \\[4pt]
&= e^{-(8.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 100} \\[4pt]
&= e^{-0.08} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.9231
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute unreliability at $t = 100$ hours (Part ii).**

$$Q_s(100) = 1 - R_s(100) = 1 - 0.9231 = 0.0769$$

### Final Tabulated Answer:

| Quantity | Symbol | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Equivalent Failure Rate | $\lambda_s$ | $8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hr |
| System MTBF | $MTBF_s$ | $1250$ hours |
| Reliability at $t=100$ hr | $R_s(100)$ | $0.9231$ |
| Unreliability at $t=100$ hr | $Q_s(100)$ | $0.0769$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The system MTBF (1250 hr) is *less than* the smallest individual component MTBF (2000 hr). This confirms the fundamental principle: *adding components to a series network always decreases the system MTBF.* If a student computes a system MTBF *greater* than the weakest component's MTBF, an error has occurred.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Insight — Diminishing Reliability Principle

For a series of $n$ **identical** components each with reliability $R_0$:

$$R_s(t) = \left[R_0(t)\right]^n$$

This shows that even if every component is highly reliable ($R_0 = 0.99$), the system reliability collapses rapidly:

| Number of Components ($n$) | Component Reliability $R_0$ | Series Reliability $R_s = R_0^n$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.99 | 0.9900 |
| 5 | 0.99 | 0.9510 |
| 10 | 0.99 | 0.9044 |
| 50 | 0.99 | 0.6050 |
| 100 | 0.99 | 0.3660 |
| 200 | 0.99 | 0.1339 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A 1% per-component failure probability becomes catastrophic at the system level once $n$ exceeds ~50. This is the **driving rationale** for introducing *redundancy* (parallel paths) in safety-critical systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram — Simple Series Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[System Input] --> A[Component 1]
    A --> B[Component 2]
    B --> C[Component 3]
    C --> D[Component N]
    D --> OUT[System Output]
    
    style IN fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Power/signal/function flows from `System Input` through **every** component block in sequence. If any one block (Component 1, 2, 3, or N) fails, the path to `System Output` is broken — the system fails. The red color coding emphasizes that each block is a potential single-point failure.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Series Reliability Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify all components in series] --> S2[Step 2: Extract individual MTBF_i or lambda_i values]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute equivalent failure rate lambda_s = sum of lambda_i]
    S3 --> S4{Step 4: Decide on mission time t}
    S4 --> S5[Step 5a: Compute R_s = exp of minus lambda_s times t]
    S4 --> S6[Step 5b: Compute Q_s = 1 minus R_s]
    S5 --> S7[Step 6: Compute MTBF_s = 1 divided by lambda_s]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Tabulate results and verify sanity checks]
    
    style S1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S5 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S6 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S7 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S8 fill:#404040,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is the **standard KTU solution template** for series reliability problems. The orange diamond (Step 4) is a decision node — based on whether a mission time is given, you branch into reliability computation (green) or unreliability computation. The red final step (Step 7) yields the MTBF.

---

## 4.3 Functional Logic — Component vs. System Failure Rate Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Constant Failure Rate Regime] --> B[Failure Rate lambda_i for each component]
    B --> C[Sum of Failure Rates lambda_s = lambda 1 + lambda 2 + ... + lambda n]
    C --> D[System MTBF_s = 1 divided by lambda_s]
    C --> E[System Reliability R_s of t = exp of minus lambda_s times t]
    C --> F[System Unreliability Q_s of t = 1 minus R_s of t]
    D --> G[Result Block: Series Network Metrics]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This block-level functional architecture maps how a single assumption (constant failure rate regime) feeds forward into three independent metric computations that all converge on the final result block.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define MTBF. How is it related to the failure rate in a system that obeys the exponential distribution?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) is the expected (mean) time interval between two successive failures of a repairable system under specified operating conditions. It is computed as the total accumulated operating time divided by the number of failures observed in that period.

> **Mathematical Relationship (2 Marks):** For a system operating in the constant failure rate (exponential) regime, the failure rate $\lambda$ is constant and the reliability function is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. The mean of this exponential distribution is:
> $$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$
> Equivalently: $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$, expressed in **failures per hour**. This is the cornerstone relationship used throughout KTU quantitative reliability problems.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does adding more components to a series network always reduce the system reliability?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Core Reason (2 Marks):** In a series network, *all* components must function for the system to function. The series reliability is the product of individual reliabilities:
> $$R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times \cdots \times R_n$$
> Since each $R_i \leq 1$, multiplying more numbers $\leq 1$ together always produces a result **smaller than or equal to** any single factor. Therefore $R_s \leq \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)$.

> **MTBF Consequence (1 Mark):** Because failure rates are additive ($\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$), the system failure rate grows with $n$, and $MTBF_s = 1/\lambda_s$ decreases monotonically as components are added.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**A safety instrumented system (SIS) for a chemical reactor consists of four independent components connected in series: a pressure sensor (MTBF = 5000 hr), a logic solver (MTBF = 12000 hr), a final control element (MTBF = 8000 hr), and a shutdown valve (MTBF = 10000 hr).**

#### (a) **Compute the equivalent failure rate, MTBF, and reliability for a mission time of 200 hours.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert MTBFs to failure rates.** [1 Mark]

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 &= \frac{1}{5000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr} \\[4pt]
\lambda_2 &= \frac{1}{12000} = 0.833 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr} \\[4pt]
\lambda_3 &= \frac{1}{8000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr} \\[4pt]
\lambda_4 &= \frac{1}{10000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Sum to get equivalent series failure rate.** [1 Mark]

$$\lambda_s = (2.0 + 0.833 + 1.25 + 1.0) \times 10^{-4} = 5.083 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr}$$

**Step 3: Compute system MTBF.** [2 Marks]

$$MTBF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{5.083 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1967.3 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4: Compute reliability at $t = 200$ hr.** [2 Marks]

$$R_s(200) = e^{-\lambda_s \cdot t} = e^{-(5.083 \times 10^{-4})(200)} = e^{-0.10167} \approx 0.9033$$

**Step 5: Present results in tabular form.** [1 Mark]

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\lambda_s$ | $5.083 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hr |
| $MTBF_s$ | $\approx 1967$ hours |
| $R_s(200)$ | $\approx 0.9033$ |

---

#### (b) **If the safety integrity level (SIL) requires the unreliability over 200 hours to be below 0.05, determine whether this design meets the SIL requirement. If not, suggest one quantitative improvement.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute unreliability at $t = 200$ hr.** [2 Marks]

$$Q_s(200) = 1 - R_s(200) = 1 - 0.9033 = 0.0967$$

**Step 2: Compare with SIL threshold.** [2 Marks]

$$Q_s(200) = 0.0967 \quad \text{vs.} \quad Q_{required} = 0.05$$

Since $0.0967 > 0.05$, **the design FAILS to meet the SIL requirement** by a factor of approximately 1.93.

**Step 3: Quantitative improvement suggestion.** [2 Marks]

> **Improvement Option:** Replace the weakest component (pressure sensor, $MTBF = 5000$ hr) with a higher-reliability version having $MTBF = 20000$ hr. Recompute:
>
> $$\lambda_{new,s} = (0.5 + 0.833 + 1.25 + 1.0) \times 10^{-4} = 3.583 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr}$$
>
> $$R_{new,s}(200) = e^{-3.583 \times 10^{-4} \times 200} = e^{-0.07167} \approx 0.9308$$
>
> $$Q_{new,s}(200) = 1 - 0.9308 = 0.0692$$
>
> Still insufficient → recommend **redundancy (parallel paths)** to achieve SIL.

**Alternative Acceptable Improvement:** Add parallel redundancy to the weakest component; compute the new parallel-block reliability and re-evaluate.

**Conclusion:** [1 Mark] The series-only configuration is fundamentally inadequate for high-SIL requirements; redundancy or higher-grade components are mandatory.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Choice 2 (Alternative 14 Marks)

**Aircraft flight control electronics consist of three independent modules in series. Module failure data: $\lambda_1 = 0.0002$ failures/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.0003$ failures/hr, $\lambda_3 = 0.0005$ failures/hr.**

#### (a) **Derive the formula for the equivalent failure rate and MTBF of the series network, and compute numerical values.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the multiplicative law of reliability for series components.** [1 Mark]

$$R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdot R_3(t)$$

**Step 2: Substitute exponential reliability for each module.** [1 Mark]

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_3 t}$$

**Step 3: Combine exponents.** [1 Mark]

$$R_s(t) = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3) t}$$

**Step 4: Identify equivalent failure rate.** [1 Mark]

$$\lambda_s = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values and compute.** [2 Marks]

$$\lambda_s = 0.0002 + 0.0003 + 0.0005 = 0.001 \text{ failures/hr}$$

**Step 6: Compute system MTBF.** [1 Mark]

$$MTBF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### (b) **Compute the system reliability and unreliability at $t = 500$ hours. Also compute the reliability of the *weakest single module* at the same time, and explain why the system reliability is lower.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute system reliability at $t = 500$ hr.** [2 Marks]

$$R_s(500) = e^{-\lambda_s \cdot t} = e^{-(0.001)(500)} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

**Step 2: Compute system unreliability.** [2 Marks]

$$Q_s(500) = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935$$

**Step 3: Identify and compute reliability of the weakest module (Module 3, highest $\lambda$).** [2 Marks]

$$R_3(500) = e^{-\lambda_3 \cdot t} = e^{-(0.0005)(500)} = e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$$

**Step 4: Explanation.** [1 Mark]

> The system reliability (0.6065) is *lower* than even the weakest single module's reliability (0.7788) because the series configuration compounds the failure probability: $R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3$, and multiplying three numbers less than 1 produces a result smaller than any of them. The system is only as strong as its **combined** weakness.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF (1-2 marks lost)** — MTBF applies to *repairable* systems (includes repair time); MTTF applies to *non-repairable* components. KTU questions often specify; read carefully.
>
> 2. **Averaging MTBFs instead of reciprocating (3-5 marks lost)** — The system MTBF is **NOT** the arithmetic mean of component MTBFs. You must convert each to a failure rate, **sum** the failure rates, and **invert** to get system MTBF. The reciprocal-form equation $\frac{1}{MTBF_s} = \sum \frac{1}{MTBF_i}$ is the only valid approach.
>
> 3. **Subtracting instead of summing failure rates (2-3 marks lost)** — Failure rates in series **add**, they do not subtract. There is no physical interpretation of "negative failure rate."
>
> 4. **Using incorrect units (1 mark lost)** — Failure rates must be in **failures/hour** consistently; if MTBF is given in days, convert to hours first.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to verify the constant-$\lambda$ assumption (1 mark lost)** — If the problem states components are aging or in wear-out phase, the exponential assumption is invalid and Weibull analysis must be used.
>
> 6. **Reporting reliability as a percentage without context (1 mark lost)** — Always state $R_s$ as a decimal probability; the percentage form ($90.33\%$) is supplementary, not a substitute.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **MTBF Definition** — Mean time between successive failures of a *repairable* system. Core KTU definition.
- **Exponential Reliability** — $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; valid in the constant-failure-rate regime.
- **MTBF–Failure Rate Relationship** — $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (the most-quoted equation in KTU exams).
- **Series Reliability** — $R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ — multiplicative law.
- **Series Failure Rate (Additive Rule)** — $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ — the single most important series network equation.
- **Series MTBF** — $MTBF_s = \dfrac{1}{\sum \lambda_i} = \dfrac{1}{\sum (1/MTBF_i)}$.
- **Series Unreliability** — $Q_s(t) = 1 - R_s(t)$.
- **Weakest Link Principle** — $R_s \leq \min(R_i)$ and $MTBF_s \leq \min(MTBF_i)$; adding components *always* hurts reliability.
- **Reciprocal Form for MTBF** — Memorize: $\dfrac{1}{MTBF_s} = \sum \dfrac{1}{MTBF_i}$ — high-yield for KTU numericals.
- **Sanity Check Rule** — System MTBF must be **less than** the smallest component MTBF; if not, recalculate.
- **Independence Assumption** — All series formulas require *statistically independent* component failures; if dependencies exist, conditional probability methods are needed.
- **Mission Time** — Always confirm whether the question specifies $t$; if not given, only MTBF and $\lambda_s$ can be reported.
- **Units** — Maintain consistent failure rate units (failures/hour) throughout; convert given time units as necessary.
- **Diminishing Reliability Insight** — For $n$ identical components each at $R_0$, system reliability is $R_0^n$; motivates redundancy for safety-critical design.
- **SIL / Safety Implication** — Pure series configurations rarely satisfy high SIL requirements (SIL 3, SIL 4); redundancy is the standard engineering response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT837 - QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACH IN SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of reliability and safety engineering, a **Parallel Configuration** (also called **Parallel Redundancy** or **Active Redundant System**) refers to an arrangement of two or more components, sub-systems, or units connected in such a manner that the **overall system performs its intended function as long as AT LEAST ONE of the parallel branches remains operational**. Failure of the system occurs *only* when **all** parallel branches have failed simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Highlight:**
> In a pure parallel arrangement, the system survives the failure of (n − 1) components out of n. The system fails only upon the failure of the *last surviving* component. This is the exact mathematical dual of the series configuration, where any single failure causes system failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **multi-lane highway bridge** carrying traffic across a river:

- The bridge has **4 lanes** (think of 4 parallel components).
- As long as **at least 1 lane remains open**, vehicles (the system's function) can still cross the bridge.
- The bridge (system) is considered **failed** only when all 4 lanes are simultaneously closed (collapsed or under maintenance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Parallel Examples:**
> - Three independent fire-pumps feeding a sprinkler system.
> - Two emergency power generators operating in parallel to feed critical hospital loads.
> - Dual braking circuits in modern automobiles (each wheel-brake circuit is independent).
> - Multiple independent sensors in a nuclear reactor trip system (2-out-of-3 voting logic).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The reliability metrics used universally in parallel configuration analysis are:

- **Failure Rate (λ)** — expressed in failures per million hours (fpmh) or failures/hour.
- **Reliability R(t)** — dimensionless probability bounded between **0** and **1**.
- **Unreliability Q(t) = 1 − R(t)** — probability of failure.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — expressed in hours.
- **Mission Time (t)** — operating period under consideration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Parallel Configuration

The structural logic of a parallel system can be broken down into the following key operational principles:

- **OR-Logic Equivalence:** A parallel arrangement is mathematically equivalent to a logical **OR gate**. The system output is true (functioning) if *any* branch output is true.
- **Independence Assumption:** Each parallel branch is assumed to fail *independently* of the others. The failure events of different branches are statistically **uncorrelated** (mutually exclusive in the probabilistic sense when considering rare events).
- **Constant Failure Rate Assumption:** Following the KTU 2024 syllabus baseline, components obey the **exponential failure distribution** with constant failure rate λ. Thus, the reliability of each individual branch is $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$.
- **Dual of Series:** The unreliability (probability of failure) of a parallel system equals the *product* of the individual unreliabilities — this is the exact dual of the series configuration.
- **Redundancy Gain:** Parallel redundancy *increases* system reliability but at the cost of additional hardware, weight, space, and maintenance overhead.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Sheet — Parallel Configuration Reliability**

| # | Configuration | Reliability Equation R_sys(t) | MTTF / Special Result | Units |
|---|---------------|------------------------------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Two dissimilar components in parallel | $1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$ | $R_s = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | n dissimilar components in parallel | $1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | $R_s = 1 - Q_1 Q_2 \cdots Q_n$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | n identical components, each R(t) | $1 - [1-R(t)]^n$ | $R_s(t) = 1 - (1-e^{-\lambda t})^n$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Two identical components, failure rate λ | $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ | From expanding $(1-(1-e^{-\lambda t})^2)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | MTTF for 2 identical parallel components | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ | Effective failure rate reduction | hours |
| 6 | MTTF for n identical parallel components | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{k}$ (harmonic sum) | Grows logarithmically | hours |
| 7 | System failure rate (parallel, 2 identical) | $\lambda_s = \dfrac{2\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{2 - e^{-\lambda t}}$ | Time-dependent (non-constant) | failures/hr |
| 8 | Unreliability of parallel system | $Q_s = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdots Q_n$ | Product of unreliabilities | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Boundary Condition:**
> When *any* branch has a reliability $R_i = 1$ (perfect, indestructible), the parallel system reliability becomes 1.0 (perfect). Conversely, if *any* branch has $R_i = 0$ (certain failure), it is automatically removed from the parallel network because it contributes nothing — effectively reducing n by one.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parallel configurations are foundational in:

- **Aerospace:** Quad-redundant flight control computers (FAA FAR 25.1309 compliance).
- **Nuclear Safety:** Four-train Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) per IEEE 379 / IEC 61508 SIL-3.
- **Process Safety:** Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) with voting logic in Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS).
- **Automotive:** Independent dual-circuit hydraulic brakes; redundant airbag squibs.
- **Power Systems:** Parallel operation of generators to ensure grid stability and N-1 contingency compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Parallel Reliability for n Components

**Statement:** Prove that for n statistically independent components connected in parallel, the system reliability is given by $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$.

**Step 1 — Define component failure probability.**
The unreliability of component *i* is its probability of failure within mission time *t*:
$$Q_i = 1 - R_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply independence.**
Since failures of the parallel branches are statistically independent, the probability that **ALL** components fail simultaneously is the product of their individual failure probabilities:
$$P(\text{all fail}) = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdot Q_3 \cdots Q_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$

**Step 3 — Identify system failure event.**
The system fails **if and only if** every single branch has failed (because the system still works if any branch works):
$$Q_s = P(\text{system fails}) = P(\text{all branches fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$

**Step 4 — Convert to reliability.**
Reliability is the complement of unreliability:
$$R_s = 1 - Q_s$$

Substituting the result of Step 3:
$$R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two Identical Parallel Components with Constant Failure Rate

**Statement:** For two identical parallel components each having constant failure rate λ, derive $R_s(t)$ and MTTF.

**Step 1 — Branch reliability.**
For each component with exponential distribution:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the parallel formula.**
For two identical components ($R_1 = R_2 = R$):
$$R_s = 1 - (1-R)(1-R) = 1 - (1-R)^2$$

**Step 3 — Expand the square.**
$$R_s = 1 - \left[1 - 2R + R^2\right]$$
$$R_s = 2R - R^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute exponential reliability.**
$$R_s(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - (e^{-\lambda t})^2$$
$$\boxed{R_s(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute MTTF.**
The MTTF is the integral of reliability over $[0, \infty]$:
$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\right) dt$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate each integral separately.**
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} 2e^{-\lambda t} dt = 2 \cdot \left[-\dfrac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2}{\lambda}$$

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2\lambda t} dt = \left[-\dfrac{1}{2\lambda} e^{-2\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{2\lambda}$$

**Step 7 — Combine the results.**
$$MTTF = \dfrac{2}{\lambda} - \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{4}{2\lambda} - \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF_{\text{parallel, n=2}} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda} = 1.5 \cdot MTTF_{\text{single}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Adding a single redundant parallel component improves the MTTF by 50% over a single component. This is the *quantitative justification* for hardware redundancy in safety-critical systems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — n Identical Parallel Components, MTTF Generalization

**Statement:** Show that the MTTF of n identical parallel components is $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{k}$.

**Step 1 — Write system reliability.**
$$R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

**Step 2 — Expand using the binomial theorem.**
$$R_s(t) = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^k e^{-k\lambda t} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^{k+1} e^{-k\lambda t}$$

By symmetry of binomial coefficients this becomes:
$$R_s(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^{k+1} e^{-k\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from 0 to ∞.**
$$MTTF = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^{k+1} \int_0^{\infty} e^{-k\lambda t} dt = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^{k+1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{k\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the alternating binomial sum.**
Using the binomial identity $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^{k+1} \dfrac{1}{k} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{k}$:

$$\boxed{MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{k}}$$

For example:
- $n = 2$: $MTTF = (1 + 1/2)/\lambda = 3/(2\lambda)$ ✓
- $n = 3$: $MTTF = (1 + 1/2 + 1/3)/\lambda = 11/(6\lambda) \approx 1.833/\lambda$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diminishing Returns Law:** Doubling the number of parallel components never doubles the MTTF. Each additional redundant unit contributes progressively smaller gains (the harmonic series diverges only logarithmically).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A hospital ICU has two independent emergency power generators in parallel. Each generator has reliability $R = 0.95$ for a 24-hour mission. Find (a) System reliability, (b) System unreliability, (c) Probability that BOTH generators fail within the mission time.

**Solution:**

**(a) System Reliability:**
$$R_s = 1 - (1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95) = 1 - (0.05)(0.05) = 1 - 0.0025$$
$$\boxed{R_s = 0.9975}$$

**(b) System Unreliability:**
$$Q_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.9975 = 0.0025$$

**(c) Probability both fail:**
$$P(\text{both fail}) = 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0025 \text{ (or } 0.25\% \text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The single generator failure probability is 5%, but with parallel redundancy, the *joint* failure probability drops to 0.25% — a **20× improvement** in safety margin.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) — Parallel Configuration

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the logical structure of a generic parallel redundant system. Each block represents an independent component branch.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[System Input Demand] --> B1[Component 1]
    A --> B2[Component 2]
    A --> B3[Component 3]
    A --> B4[Component n]
    B1 --> C[OR Logic Junction]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[System Output Functional]

    style A fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#9FE08A,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#E08AFF,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Parallel Reliability Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Define Mission Time t] --> indep[Assume Component Independence]
    indep --> lambdas[Define Failure Rates lambda_i]
    lambdas --> reliab[Compute Individual R_i = e^(-lambda_i * t)]
    reliab --> unreliab[Compute Individual Q_i = 1 - R_i]
    unreliab --> multiply[Multiply All Q_i = Q_1 * Q_2 * ... * Q_n]
    multiply --> complement[R_system = 1 - Product of Q_i]
    complement --> mttf[Integrate R_system over t for MTTF]
    mttf --> decision{R_system >= Target?}
    decision -->|Yes| accept[Design Acceptable]
    decision -->|No| redesign[Add More Redundancy or Improve lambda]
    redesign --> lambdas

    style start fill:#A8D8FF
    style accept fill:#9FE08A
    style redesign fill:#FF9F8A
    style decision fill:#FFE08A
```

### 4.3 Truth Table Representation (Binary Functional Logic)

For a 2-component parallel system, where "1" = functioning and "0" = failed:

| Component 1 | Component 2 | System Status (OR) |
|:-----------:|:-----------:|:------------------:|
| 0 | 0 | **0** (FAILED) |
| 0 | 1 | **1** (OK) |
| 1 | 0 | **1** (OK) |
| 1 | 1 | **1** (OK) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single "0,0" outcome (both components failed) is the **only** system failure state. This visualizes why parallel systems are robust — they tolerate multiple component failures.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`**
**Define a parallel configuration in reliability engineering. Why is it considered the dual of a series configuration?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A parallel configuration is an arrangement of components in which the system continues to function as long as **at least one** component is operational. The system fails only when all components fail. It is the dual of a series configuration because in series, system failure occurs upon failure of *any one* component, while in parallel, system success occurs upon success of *any one* component. Mathematically, series reliability is the *product* of individual reliabilities, while parallel reliability is the *complement of the product of unreliabilities*. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`**
**State the general reliability expression for *n* independent components connected in parallel. What is the limiting value of system reliability as *n → ∞*?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> For n independent components in parallel:
> $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$
> As $n \to \infty$, the product of unreliabilities tends to zero (provided $R_i < 1$), so $R_s \to 1$. This means infinite parallel redundancy would yield perfect reliability, though practically this is impossible due to cost, weight, and common-cause failures. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 4]`**

**(a)** Two identical pumps are connected in parallel to deliver water to a fire-sprinkler system. Each pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour. Compute the system reliability and MTTF for a mission time of $t = 1000$ hours. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety shutdown system uses **three** independent parallel relays, each with reliability $R_1 = 0.92$, $R_2 = 0.95$, $R_3 = 0.97$ during a critical 6-month test interval. Find the system reliability and the probability that *exactly two* relays fail during this interval. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) — Two Identical Parallel Pumps**

**Step 1:** Branch reliability:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

**Step 2:** System reliability:
$$R_s = 2R - R^2 = 2(0.8187) - (0.8187)^2 = 1.6374 - 0.6703 = 0.9671$$

$$\boxed{R_s \approx 0.9671}$$

**Step 3:** MTTF:
$$MTTF = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{3}{2 \times 0.0002} = 7500 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = 7500 \text{ hours}}$$

**(7 marks distributed: 3 marks for R(t), 2 marks for R_s, 2 marks for MTTF)**

---

**(b) — Three Dissimilar Parallel Relays**

**Step 1:** Apply the general parallel formula:
$$R_s = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)(1-R_3)$$
$$R_s = 1 - (0.08)(0.05)(0.03)$$
$$R_s = 1 - 0.00012 = 0.99988$$

$$\boxed{R_s \approx 0.9999}$$

**Step 2:** Identify combinations where exactly two relays fail.

Let us define unreliabilities: $Q_1 = 0.08$, $Q_2 = 0.05$, $Q_3 = 0.97$ wait — $Q_3 = 1 - R_3 = 0.03$.

**Step 3:** Probability exactly two fail = sum over each pair of (failure of that pair × success of the third):

$$P(\text{exactly 2 fail}) = Q_1 Q_2 R_3 + Q_1 R_2 Q_3 + R_1 Q_2 Q_3$$

$$= (0.08)(0.05)(0.97) + (0.08)(0.95)(0.03) + (0.92)(0.05)(0.03)$$

$$= 0.003880 + 0.002280 + 0.001380 = 0.00754$$

$$\boxed{P(\text{exactly 2 fail}) \approx 0.0075}$$

**(7 marks distributed: 3 marks for system R_s, 4 marks for the combinatorial P(exactly 2 fail))**

---

#### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Alternative Module 4 Choice]`**

**(a)** Derive the MTTF of *n* identical components in active parallel redundancy with constant failure rate λ. Show that for $n = 3$, the MTTF equals $\dfrac{11}{6\lambda}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process plant uses **four identical sensors** in parallel for over-temperature protection. Each sensor has $R = 0.90$ over a 1-year mission. (i) Find system reliability. (ii) Find how much reliability improves if a **fifth** identical sensor is added in parallel. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) — MTTF Derivation for n Identical Parallel Components**

**Step 1:** System reliability for n identical components:
$$R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

**Step 2:** Expand using the binomial theorem:
$$R_s(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} (-1)^{k+1} e^{-k\lambda t}$$

**Step 3:** MTTF = $\int_0^{\infty} R_s(t) dt$:
$$MTTF = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} (-1)^{k+1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{k\lambda}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the identity $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} (-1)^{k+1} \binom{n}{k} \dfrac{1}{k} = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{k}$:

$$\boxed{MTTF_n = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{k}}$$

**Step 5:** For $n = 3$:
$$MTTF_3 = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{3}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{6 + 3 + 2}{6} = \dfrac{11}{6\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF_3 = \dfrac{11}{6\lambda} \approx 1.833 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda}}$$

**(7 marks distributed: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for n=3 verification)**

---

**(b) — Four vs Five Sensors in Parallel**

**(i) Four sensors:**
$$R_s^{(4)} = 1 - (1-0.90)^4 = 1 - (0.1)^4 = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999$$

**(ii) Five sensors:**
$$R_s^{(5)} = 1 - (1-0.90)^5 = 1 - (0.1)^5 = 1 - 0.00001 = 0.99999$$

**Improvement (absolute):**
$$\Delta R = 0.99999 - 0.9999 = 0.00009$$

**Improvement (factor):**
$$\dfrac{R_s^{(5)}}{R_s^{(4)}} = \dfrac{0.99999}{0.9999} \approx 1.00009$$

$$\boxed{R_s^{(4)} = 0.9999, \quad R_s^{(5)} = 0.99999}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each additional redundant sensor reduces system unreliability by a factor of 10. With $Q_s^{(4)} = 10^{-4}$ and $Q_s^{(5)} = 10^{-5}$, the fifth sensor provides an order-of-magnitude improvement.

**(7 marks distributed: 3 marks for (i), 4 marks for (ii) including comparison discussion)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Mark-Loss Hotspots):**
> 1. **Mixing Series and Parallel formulae:** Students often mistakenly apply $R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2$ (series rule) to a parallel network, yielding dangerously *low* reliability values. Always identify configuration first.
> 2. **Ignoring independence assumption:** The product rule $\prod Q_i$ assumes statistically independent failures. In real systems, **common-cause failures (CCF)** and **common-mode failures** can violate this. KTU examiners expect a note acknowledging this limitation for full marks.
> 3. **Confusing Active vs Standby Redundancy:** In *active* parallel (this module), all components operate continuously. In *standby* redundancy (covered separately), the redundant unit is dormant until needed — the MTTF formula is *different*.
> 4. **Forgetting the dual relation:** Forgetting that $Q_s = \prod Q_i$ for parallel systems results in lost marks.
> 5. **Unit errors in MTTF:** Always include the unit (hours) explicitly; KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Parallel Configuration: 60-Second Revision**

- ✅ **Definition:** Parallel = system works if **at least one** branch works.
- ✅ **Logical Equivalence:** OR-gate logic; complement of all branches failing.
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$
- ✅ **Two Identical Components:** $R_s = 2R - R^2$ and $R_s(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$
- ✅ **MTTF for n Identical Components:** $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{k}$
- ✅ **Special Case n=2:** $MTTF = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ (50% improvement over single component).
- ✅ **Diminishing Returns:** Harmonic sum grows only logarithmically; doubling *n* does **not** double reliability.
- ✅ **Unreliability Product Rule:** $Q_s = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdots Q_n$ (dual of series reliability).
- ✅ **Limiting Behavior:** As $n \to \infty$, $R_s \to 1$ (asymptotic perfection).
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** Aerospace (FAA), Nuclear (IEEE 379), Process Safety (IEC 61508), Automotive (ISO 26262).
- ✅ **Independence Caveat:** Real systems must address common-cause failures (β-factor model, IEEE 352).
- ✅ **Constant Failure Rate Assumption:** KTU 2024 syllabus presumes exponential distribution $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ✅ **Active vs Standby:** Active parallel = all units energized (this topic); Standby = dormant redundancy (separate topic).

---

**End of Notes — Module 4: Parallel Configuration**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) Examination Standards*
