# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the power rule of integration. Apply it to compute $\int_{0}^{4} x^3\,dx$.**

**Model Answer:**

The power rule states that for $n \neq -1$:

$$\int x^n \,dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$$

Applying with $n = 3$:

$$\int_{0}^{4} x^3\,dx = \left[ \frac{x^4}{4} \right]_{0}^{4} = \frac{4^4}{4} - 0 = \frac{256}{4} = 64$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{64 \text{ (units}^4\text{)}}$

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Evaluate $\int e^{-0.05t}\,dt$ using trivial integration. Mention its application in safety reliability.**

**Model Answer:**

Using the exponential formula with $a = -0.05$:

$$\int e^{-0.05t}\,dt = \frac{1}{-0.05} e^{-0.05t} + C = -20\,e^{-0.05t} + C$$

**Application in Safety Reliability:** This integral arises in the **exponential reliability model** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, which is the foundation for computing the probability of survival of safety-critical components (valves, sensors, pressure relief devices) over a mission time $t$. Integrating this function yields the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**, which is central to preventive maintenance scheduling and SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination per IEC 61508.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using trivial integration, evaluate the following:
$$\int_{0}^{5} \left( 3t^2 + 4t + 6 \right) dt$$
Interpret the result if the integrand represents the instantaneous power output (in MW) of a safety back-up generator as a function of time $t$ (in seconds).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A toxic gas sensor in a refinery detects concentration $C(t) = 200\,e^{-0.4t}$ ppm, where $t$ is in minutes. Calculate the total dose received by a worker over a 10-minute exposure window using:
$$D = \int_{0}^{10} C(t)\,dt$$

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (a)

**Step 1 — Apply linearity to split the integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{5} 3t^2\,dt + \int_{0}^{5} 4t\,dt + \int_{0}^{5} 6\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term using the power rule / constant rule:**

First term:
$$3 \int_{0}^{5} t^2\,dt = 3 \left[ \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{5} = \left[ t^3 \right]_{0}^{5} = 125 - 0 = 125$$

Second term:
$$4 \int_{0}^{5} t\,dt = 4 \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{5} = 2 \left[ t^2 \right]_{0}^{5} = 2 \times 25 = 50$$

Third term:
$$6 \int_{0}^{5} 1\,dt = 6 \left[ t \right]_{0}^{5} = 6 \times 5 = 30$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions:**

$$I = 125 + 50 + 30 = 205$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**

The integral of instantaneous power over time equals **total energy delivered**:

$$E = 205 \text{ MW} \cdot \text{s} = 205 \text{ MJ}$$

In a safety context, this represents the **total energy reserve** of the back-up generator over the 5-second window — a critical parameter for ensuring that emergency systems (firewater pumps, evacuation lighting, fire suppression) can operate through the full response time of primary power restoration.

$$\boxed{E = 205 \text{ MJ}}$$

#### Model Solution to B-A (b)

**Step 1 — Set up the integral:**

$$D = \int_{0}^{10} 200\,e^{-0.4t}\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Pull out the constant and apply the exponential formula:**

$$D = 200 \int_{0}^{10} e^{-0.4t}\,dt = 200 \left[ \frac{1}{-0.4} e^{-0.4t} \right]_{0}^{10}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at the bounds:**

$$D = 200 \left[ -2.5\,e^{-0.4t} \right]_{0}^{10} = 200 \left[ \left(-2.5\,e^{-4}\right) - \left(-2.5\,e^{0}\right) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Numerical computation:**

$e^{-4} = 0.01832$, so $-2.5 \times 0.01832 = -0.0458$

$$D = 200 \left[ -0.0458 - (-2.5) \right] = 200 \times 2.4542$$

$$\boxed{D = 490.84 \text{ ppm} \cdot \text{min}}$$

**Interpretation:** This cumulative dose is then compared against the **Occupational Exposure Limit (OEL)** or **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health)** threshold to determine whether evacuation, respiratory protection, or additional engineering controls are required.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative to B-A) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** State the linearity property of integration. Using it, evaluate:
$$\int \left( 5x^4 - 3x^2 + \frac{2}{x} \right) dx$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** The hazard rate of a mechanical component is given by $h(t) = 0.01\,t$ (failures per year per component) over $[0, 10]$ years. The cumulative hazard is:
$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau$$
Compute $H(10)$ and the reliability $R(10) = e^{-H(10)}$.

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (a)

**Step 1 — State the Linearity Property:**

The linearity property of integration states that for constants $\alpha, \beta$ and functions $u(x), v(x)$:

$$\int \left[ \alpha \, u(x) + \beta \, v(x) \right] dx = \alpha \int u(x)\,dx + \beta \int v(x)\,dx$$

**Step 2 — Apply linearity to split the integral:**

$$\int 5x^4\,dx - \int 3x^2\,dx + \int \frac{2}{x}\,dx$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term:**

First term (power rule, $n = 4$):
$$\int 5x^4\,dx = 5 \cdot \frac{x^5}{5} = x^5$$

Second term (power rule, $n = 2$):
$$\int 3x^2\,dx = 3 \cdot \frac{x^3}{3} = x^3$$

Third term (logarithm rule, $\int 1/x\,dx = \ln \vert x \vert$):
$$\int \frac{2}{x}\,dx = 2 \ln \vert x \vert$$

**Step 4 — Combine and add the constant of integration:**

$$\boxed{\int \left( 5x^4 - 3x^2 + \frac{2}{x} \right) dx = x^5 - x^3 + 2\ln \vert x \vert + C}$$

#### Model Solution to B-B (b)

**Step 1 — Set up the cumulative hazard integral:**

$$H(10) = \int_{0}^{10} 0.01\,\tau\,d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Pull the constant out and apply the power rule:**

$$H(10) = 0.01 \int_{0}^{10} \tau\,d\tau = 0.01 \left[ \frac{\tau^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{10}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at the bounds:**

$$H(10) = 0.01 \times \frac{100}{2} = 0.01 \times 50 = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute the reliability:**

$$R(10) = e^{-H(10)} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

After 10 years, the probability of survival of the component is approximately **60.65%**, and the cumulative hazard exposure is **0.5**. In a safety context, this is used to plan **proof-test intervals** for Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) and to verify that the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** remains within the SIL target range.

$$\boxed{H(10) = 0.5, \quad R(10) \approx 0.6065}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Trivial Integration (ESE Valuation):**
> 1. **Sign error in exponential antiderivative (–2 marks):** Students frequently write $\int e^{-0.1t}\,dt = e^{-0.1t} + C$ instead of $-10 e^{-0.1t} + C$. The negative sign from $1/a$ with $a<0$ is the **most common** sign error in the KTU exam paper.
> 2. **Wrong power (–2 marks):** Writing $\int x^2\,dx = x^2 + C$ instead of $x^3/3 + C$ — examiners explicitly check the exponent increment.
> 3. **Swapped integration limits (–1 mark):** For definite integrals, $F(\text{lower}) - F(\text{upper})$ instead of the correct order. Result has opposite sign.
> 4. **Omitting $C$ (–1 mark):** For **indefinite** integrals, the constant of integration is mandatory. For definite integrals, $C$ is not required.
> 5. **No units in the final answer (–0.5 to 1 mark):** KTU 2024 explicitly deducts marks for answers without dimensional units.
> 6. **Skipping the linearity step (–1 mark):** For sum-of-terms integrands, explicitly show the splitting step before evaluating each term.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Trivial Integration (Module 4):**
> - **Definition:** Trivial integration = direct application of standard antiderivative formulas to elementary functions (polynomial, exponential, log, trigonometric). No advanced techniques (by parts, partial fractions) are required.
> - **Power rule (foundation):** $\int x^n\,dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$, valid for $n \neq -1$.
> - **Reciprocal rule (special case):** $\int \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx = \ln \vert x \vert + C$ — this is **not** obtained from the power rule with $n = -1$ (that would be undefined).
> - **Exponential rule:** $\int e^{ax}\,dx = \dfrac{1}{a} e^{ax} + C$. The $\dfrac{1}{a}$ factor is **mandatory**.
> - **Linearity property:** $\int [\alpha f(x) + \beta g(x)]\,dx = \alpha \int f(x)\,dx + \beta \int g(x)\,dx$ — use this to break down complex integrands.
> - **Definite integral evaluation:** Always $F(\text{upper limit}) - F(\text{lower limit})$. Sign-check after evaluation.
> - **Indefinite integral:** Always add $+C$.
> - **Safety applications:** Cumulative release mass, MTTF, cumulative hazard, dose calculation, aggregate risk, total energy.
> - **Dimensional analysis:** Check that the final answer carries appropriate units — KTU examiners explicitly check this.
> - **Sanity check:** Verify the order of magnitude by comparing with the constant-rate approximation.
> - **MTTF shortcut:** For constant failure rate $\lambda$, MTTF $= 1/\lambda$ — this is a special case of $\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$.
